topics.title: History of Model Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** History of Model Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Model Testing** in the context of Marine Hydrodynamics refers to the systematic experimental investigation of the hydrodynamic behavior of a ship, offshore structure, or marine vehicle by studying a geometrically scaled replica (the *model*) under controlled laboratory conditions. The data obtained is then extrapolated to the *prototype* (full-scale vessel) using the principles of **Dimensional Analysis** and **Dynamic Similarity**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PENST865**, the term encompasses:

- The reproduction of a ship's hull form, appendages, and superstructure at a reduced scale.
- The conduct of experiments in specialized facilities such as **towing tanks**, **circulating water channels**, **cavitation tunnels**, **seakeeping basins**, and **manoeuvring basins**.
- The application of **scaling laws** (Froude, Reynolds, Weber, Cauchy, Euler) to convert model-scale measurements into reliable full-scale predictions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The history of model testing is *not* merely a chronological narrative. It is the evolutionary foundation of **similitude theory**. Every historical milestone corresponds to a refinement in our understanding of which non-dimensional group (Froude number, Reynolds number, etc.) dominates the physics being modeled.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to know whether a newly designed paper aeroplane will glide well, but you cannot test it indoors due to space constraints. You could:

1. **Scale it down** to fit in a corridor.
2. **Increase the air density** slightly using a fan, OR **scale the wind speed** correctly.
3. **Fly the smaller version** and observe its trajectory.

By carefully scaling, you mimic the *flow conditions* even though the *physical size* is different. This is exactly what marine model testing does — except instead of paper and corridor air, we deal with **steel hulls, water, and basins up to 300 m long**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Model testing is *not* a scaled-down version of reality. It is a **carefully engineered surrogate experiment** in which dimensionless parameters are preserved. Two different scales can represent the *same* full-scale ship if the non-dimensional groups match.

## 1.3 The Core "Why" of Model Testing

Before computers became powerful, and even today despite CFD advances, model testing remains the **gold standard** for:

- **Resistance and propulsion prediction** (effective power, hull efficiency).
- **Manoeuvring derivatives** (yaw, sway, roll coupling).
- **Seakeeping** (motion responses in waves).
- **Slamming, green water, and wet deck impact** studies.
- **Validation data** for numerical solvers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** A typical ship model is built to a **length scale ($\lambda$)** between **1:15 and 1:40**, while high-speed craft models can go as small as **1:5** to preserve Reynolds number in cavitation tunnels.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Evolution of Model Testing — A Structured View

The history of model testing can be broken down into **five logical epochs**, each driven by a specific scientific need:

### Epoch 1 — The Era of Empirical Guessing (Pre-1869)
- Ship resistance was estimated using **purely empirical rules** (e.g., the "Boucher's rule", the *Système des Navires* tradition).
- No consistent physical basis existed. Builders relied on **trial-and-error** and parent hull forms.
- **Why this failed:** Each new hull form was unique; no transfer of learning was possible.

### Epoch 2 — The Birth of Dimensional Reasoning (1869–1898)
- **William Froude's** pioneering work at Torquay, England introduced the concept that **resistance scales with weight** (gravitational force) and **not with viscosity**.
- The **frictional resistance** component was identified as the part that *did not* scale like wave resistance.
- Froude proposed the famous **frictional resistance correlation** based on flat-plate experiments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from Froude:** A ship model's wave-making resistance can be scaled to the prototype *only if* the **Froude number** of the model equals that of the prototype. The frictional part must be *subtracted* and *re-added* using empirical plate friction.

### Epoch 3 — The Froude Number Era (1898–1930s)
- The systematic use of the **Froude scaling law**:
$$F_r = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$$
- **Towing tanks** became purpose-built scientific instruments. Notable early facilities:
  - **William Froude's Torquay Tank (1871)** — 90 m long.
  - **The Denny Tank (1884)** at Dumbarton, Scotland — the first commercial ship model basin.
  - **The Admiralty Experimental Works (AEW) Tank at Haslar (1911)**.
- **George P. Baker** at the **David Taylor Model Basin (DTMB), Washington D.C. (1899)** formalized the procedure of resistance extrapolation.

### Epoch 4 — The Reynolds Number Awakening (1930s–1960s)
- Engineers realized that for **frictional resistance**, the **Reynolds number** is the correct scaling parameter:
$$R_n = \frac{VL}{\nu}$$
- However, matching **both** $F_r$ and $R_n$ simultaneously is **impossible** in a single tank (because $F_r$ fixes $V/\sqrt{gL}$ and $R_n$ fixes $VL/\nu$, leaving a contradiction for fixed fluids).
- The **ITTC 1957** (International Towing Tank Conference) established the **standard friction line** $C_f = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$ to handle this dichotomy.

### Epoch 5 — The Modern, Multi-Physics Era (1970s–Present)
- Recognition of **additional non-dimensional groups**:
  - **Weber number** $W_e = \rho V^2 L / \sigma$ — for **cavitation and spray**.
  - **Cauchy number** $C_a = \rho V^2 / E$ — for **structural deflection**.
  - **Euler number** $E_u$ — for **pressure distribution**.
- Specialized facilities: **cavitation tunnels, pressurized tunnels, cryogenic facilities** (e.g., the **MEP** at Martec, Norway) to partially recover Reynolds scaling.
- Coupled with **CFD validation** and **RIB** (Resistance, Integrity, Ballast) studies.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Typical Range / Unit |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| $\lambda$ | Length scale ratio ($L_m / L_p$) | $\lambda = L_m / L_p$ | 0.02 to 0.20 |
| $F_r$ | Froude number | $F_r = V / \sqrt{g L}$ | 0.10 to 0.50 (ships) |
| $R_n$ | Reynolds number | $R_n = V L / \nu$ | $10^6$ to $10^9$ |
| $W_e$ | Weber number | $W_e = \rho V^2 L / \sigma$ | $10^2$ to $10^5$ |
| $C_a$ | Cauchy number | $C_a = \rho V^2 / E$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| $C_T$ | Total resistance coefficient | $C_T = R_T / \left(\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S\right)$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| $C_f$ | Frictional coefficient (ITTC-57) | $C_f = 0.075 / \left(\log R_n - 2\right)^2$ | dimensionless |
| $C_r$ | Residuary (wave) coefficient | $C_r = C_T - C_f$ | dimensionless |
| $V_m$ | Model speed | $V_m = V_p \sqrt{\lambda}$ | m/s |
| $R_{Tm}$ | Model total resistance | $R_{Tm} = R_{Tp} \, \lambda^3$ | N |
| $E P$ | Effective power | $E P = R_T \cdot V_p$ | W or kW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These formulas are the *lingua franca* of every towing tank test report. Whether you are at **HSVA Hamburg, MARIN Wageningen, SSPA Gothenburg, IIHR Iowa, or NAL Bengaluru**, the symbol conventions are identical — a deliberate outcome of international cooperation since the first ITTC in 1933.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Froude Scaling Law (Velocity & Time)

The Froude number equates gravitational and inertial forces:

$$F_r = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{g L_p}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\frac{V_p^2}{g L_p} = \frac{V_m^2}{g L_m}$$

The gravitational acceleration $g$ cancels (it is identical at model and prototype scales because both are tested on Earth):

$$\frac{V_p^2}{L_p} = \frac{V_m^2}{L_m}$$

Solving for the model speed:

$$V_m = V_p \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_p}} = V_p \sqrt{\lambda}$$

This is the **Froude speed scale** — a direct consequence of gravity similitude.

**Conversion logic step:** $L_m / L_p$ is the geometric scale ratio $\lambda$, hence the model must be towed at $\sqrt{\lambda}$ times the prototype speed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Resistance Scaling (Force and Power)

The total resistance scales as the product of dynamic pressure and wetted area:

$$R_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^2 \, S \, C_T$$

Since $C_T$ is preserved under Froude similitude, the ratio of model to prototype resistance is:

$$\frac{R_{Tm}}{R_{Tp}} = \frac{\rho_m V_m^2 S_m}{\rho_p V_p^2 S_p} = \left(\frac{V_m}{V_p}\right)^2 \left(\frac{S_m}{S_p}\right)$$

because $\rho_m = \rho_p$ (same fluid, fresh or salt water) and $S$ scales as $L^2$:

$$\frac{R_{Tm}}{R_{Tp}} = \lambda \cdot \lambda^2 = \lambda^3$$

Therefore:

$$R_{Tm} = R_{Tp} \, \lambda^3$$

**Conversion logic step:** Velocity scales as $\lambda^{1/2}$ and area as $\lambda^2$, so resistance scales as $\lambda^{1/2 \times 2 + 2} = \lambda^{1+2} = \lambda^3$. This cubic scaling is why **a 1/40 scale model weighs roughly 1/64 000th of the prototype** — and yet the *forces* are 1/64 000th of full scale, which is why a $50$ N tow force on the model corresponds to **3.2 MN** on a VLCC.

The **Effective Power** is $EP = R_T \cdot V$, so:

$$\frac{EP_m}{EP_p} = \lambda^3 \cdot \lambda^{1/2} = \lambda^{7/2}$$

$$EP_m = EP_p \, \lambda^{7/2}$$

**Conversion logic step:** Power combines force (cubic) and velocity (square-root), yielding the $7/2$ exponent.

---

## 3.3 The Froude-Havelock Form for Total Resistance

In historic form (and still used in KTU reference material), the total resistance is decomposed:

$$R_T = R_F + R_W + R_A$$

Where:
- $R_F$ = frictional resistance (uses $C_f$ from ITTC-57 plate friction).
- $R_W$ = wave-making resistance (Froude-scaled from model).
- $R_A$ = correlation allowance (accounts for scale effects and form deviations).

Converting each component to prototype:

$$R_{Fp} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_p^2 S_p \, C_f(R_{np})$$

$$R_{Wp} = R_{Wm} \left(\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_m}\right) \left(\frac{V_p}{V_m}\right)^2 \left(\frac{L_p}{L_m}\right)^2 \left(\frac{C_{wm}}{C_{wp}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical note:** This decomposition was the breakthrough that allowed Froude to **subtract** the frictional part and only **scale** the wave part. The procedure is taught verbatim at KTU in PENST865 because it appears in almost every End Semester Examination.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Historical Milestones and Their Scaling Implication

| Year | Contributor / Facility | Contribution | Resulting Scaling Law |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **1869** | William Froude (England) | Began systematic ship model experiments at Torquay | Empirical friction line + Froude's law (later formalised) |
| **1871** | William Froude | Built the **first dedicated towing tank** (90 m long) | Froude similitude for wave resistance |
| **1884** | William Denny (Scotland) | Established the **Denny Tank** — first commercial test basin | $\lambda = 1/16$ to $1/24$ model standards |
| **1898** | D.W. Taylor (USA) | Built the **Experimental Model Basin (DTMB)** | $C_T = R_T / (0.5 \rho V^2 S)$ standardised |
| **1933** | First **ITTC** (International Towing Tank Conference) | Standardised resistance test procedures | Foundation of ITTC-57 friction line |
| **1957** | ITTC | Adopted $C_f = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$ | Reynolds-number-based frictional extrapolation |
| **1960s** | NSRDC, NSMB, BMT | Cryogenic and pressurised tunnels to recover $R_n$ scaling | Cavitation / Reynolds-corrected testing |
| **1970s–Today** | ITTC, MARIN, HSVA, IIHR | Manoeuvring, seakeeping, slamming, CFD-coupling | Multi-parameter similitude |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Historical Evolution Flow of Model Testing

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **chronological-functional architecture** of the development of model testing, mapping how each historical era contributed to the modern procedure.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Pre 1869: Empirical Era] --> B[1869: Froude Begins Torquay Experiments]
    B --> C[1871: First Towing Tank Torquay 90m]
    C --> D[1884: Denny Tank Commercial Basin]
    D --> E[1899: DTMB Washington DC Built]
    E --> F[1933: First ITTC Conference]
    F --> G[1957: ITTC 57 Friction Line Adopted]
    G --> H[1960s: Cryogenic and Pressurised Tunnels]
    H --> I[1970s to 2000s: Specialised Basins]
    I --> J[Modern Era: CFD Coupled Model Testing]
    J --> K[Future: Digital Twins and Hybrid Simulators]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Model Test Campaign

The diagram below shows how a **modern model test campaign** is structured — directly traceable to the historical evolution above.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Client Specifications] --> B[Hull Form Definition and Lines Plan]
    B --> C[Model Construction at Scale lambda]
    C --> D[Calibration of Towing Carriage]
    D --> E[Resistance Test in Towing Tank]
    E --> F[Frictional Extrapolation via ITTC 57]
    F --> G[Wave Resistance via Froude Scaling]
    G --> H[Propulsion Test Self Propulsion Point]
    H --> I[Wake and Thrust Deduction]
    I --> J[Full Scale Effective Power Prediction]
    J --> K[Validation Against CFD]
    K --> L[Final Report to Shipyard]
```

## 4.3 Similitude Hierarchy Map

The figure below maps the **dominant non-dimensional groups** to the **historical facility** that addresses them.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Froude Number Fr] --> F1[Towing Tank]
    P2[Reynolds Number Rn] --> F2[Pressurised or Cryogenic Tunnel]
    P3[Weber Number We] --> F3[Cavitation Tunnel]
    P4[Cauchy Number Ca] --> F4[Slamming Test Rig]
    P5[Euler Number Eu] --> F5[Pressure Paint and PIV Basins]
    F1 --> OUT[Full Scale Performance]
    F2 --> OUT
    F3 --> OUT
    F4 --> OUT
    F5 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Each terminal box represents a *physical facility*. The arrows indicate the *parameter* being matched. This figure is the standard pedagogical tool used in **KTU PENST865 lectures** to illustrate why a single tank cannot solve all problems — hence the historical diversification of testing facilities.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Why is it impossible to satisfy both Froude and Reynolds similarity simultaneously in a single towing tank test?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Froude similarity requires $V_m / V_p = \sqrt{\lambda}$, fixing the velocity ratio.
- (1 mark) Reynolds similarity requires $V_m L_m / \nu_m = V_p L_p / \nu_p$. With the same fluid, $\nu_m = \nu_p$, leading to $V_m / V_p = 1/\lambda$, which **contradicts** the Froude requirement unless $\lambda = 1$.
- (1 mark) Hence a single tank can match **only one** of these; in practice, Froude is matched (wave-making) and the Reynolds deficit is **corrected** empirically using the ITTC-57 friction line.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Name the first dedicated towing tank in history and its builder. What was its length?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The first dedicated towing tank was built by **William Froude**.
- (1 mark) Location: **Torquay, England**, year **1871**.
- (1 mark) Length: approximately **90 m**, width **9 m**, depth **3 m**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Trace the **historical evolution of model testing in marine hydrodynamics** from pre-1869 to the present day, highlighting the key contributions of **Froude, Taylor, and the ITTC**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A full-scale ship of length $L_p = 200$ m is to be tested at a model scale of $\lambda = 1/25$ in a towing tank. The prototype speed is $V_p = 10$ m/s. Calculate the **model speed**, the **resistance scale factor**, and the **effective power scale factor**. Assume sea water density at model and prototype. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

1. **(1 mark) Pre-1869 Empirical Era:** Resistance was estimated by builder's rules of thumb (e.g., *Boucher's rule*). No scientific basis. Different hull forms yielded no transferable learning.
2. **(1 mark) Froude's Contribution (1869–1871):** William Froude's pioneering work identified that ship resistance had two components — **frictional** (viscous) and **residual** (mostly wave-making). He built the first towing tank at Torquay in 1871 and introduced the **frictional resistance correlation** based on flat-plate experiments.
3. **(1 mark) Froude's Law:** Wave resistance scales with the **Froude number**, $F_r = V / \sqrt{gL}$. By ensuring $F_r$ equality between model and prototype, wave resistance can be reliably extrapolated.
4. **(1 mark) Taylor's Contribution (1899):** D. W. Taylor established the **David Taylor Model Basin (DTMB)** in Washington D.C., one of the world's largest facilities. He standardised the **resistance coefficient** $C_T = R_T / (0.5 \rho V^2 S)$, which is now the universal symbol in naval architecture.
5. **(1 mark) The ITTC Era (1933 onwards):** The International Towing Tank Conference was founded in 1933 to harmonise test procedures across nations. The **ITTC-1957** standard friction line $C_f = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$ was adopted to handle the **Reynolds number mismatch** when Froude similitude is enforced.
6. **(1 mark) Modern Diversification (1960s–Present):** Pressurised tunnels, cryogenic facilities (e.g., the **MEP Martec** in Norway using LN$_2$ vapour to boost $R_n$), cavitation tunnels, and seakeeping basins emerged. The present day combines **physical testing with CFD** for validation.
7. **(1 mark) Summary:** The historical trajectory moved from empiricism → dimensional reasoning → standardised similitude → multi-parameter facility specialisation. Each step was driven by a specific **engineering need** (predict resistance, predict cavitation, predict seakeeping, etc.).

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $L_p = 200$ m, $\lambda = L_m / L_p = 1/25$, $V_p = 10$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Model speed under Froude scaling (2 marks):**
Using $F_{rm} = F_{rp}$:

$$V_m = V_p \sqrt{\lambda} = 10 \times \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{25}}$$

$$V_m = 10 \times \tfrac{1}{5} = 2.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Resistance scale factor (2 marks):**

$$\frac{R_{Tm}}{R_{Tp}} = \lambda^3 = \left(\tfrac{1}{25}\right)^3 = \tfrac{1}{15\,625}$$

**Step 3 — Effective power scale factor (2 marks):**

$$\frac{EP_m}{EP_p} = \lambda^{7/2} = \left(\tfrac{1}{25}\right)^{3.5} = 25^{-3.5} = \tfrac{1}{4882.81}$$

$$\frac{EP_m}{EP_p} \approx 2.048 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check (1 mark):**
For example, if $EP_p = 10$ MW, then $EP_m = 2.048$ kW, which is a typical load on a model towing dynamometer. This confirms the calculation is consistent with engineering practice.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **Froude method of resistance extrapolation** from model to prototype. Clearly state why the frictional component is treated separately. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of historical model testing** and explain how the **ITTC-1957 standard friction line** was developed to address one of these limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

1. **(1 mark) The Decomposition:** Froude proposed that total resistance $R_T$ be split as $R_T = R_F + R_R$, where $R_F$ is frictional and $R_R$ is residuary (mostly wave-making).
2. **(1 mark) Frictional Component — Empirical:** $R_F$ is computed from the **flat-plate friction line** at the model Reynolds number, not by Froude scaling.
3. **(1 mark) Residuary Component — Froude Scaled:** $R_R$ scales with $\rho V^2 S$, with $C_R$ preserved by Froude similitude. The model is towed, $R_{Tm}$ measured, then $R_F$ is subtracted to obtain $R_{Rm}$.
4. **(1 mark) Extrapolation to Prototype:**
   - Compute $R_{Fp}$ using the same friction formula but at the **prototype Reynolds number**.
   - $R_{Rp} = R_{Rm} \cdot (\rho_p V_p^2 S_p) / (\rho_m V_m^2 S_m) = R_{Rm} \lambda^3$.
   - $R_{Tp} = R_{Fp} + R_{Rp}$.
5. **(1 mark) Why Separately:** Because **frictional resistance is governed by viscous (Reynolds) scaling**, not gravity (Froude) scaling. Combining them in one formula would require an **impossible simultaneous** match of both $F_r$ and $R_n$.
6. **(1 mark) Schoenherr's vs ITTC-57:** Historically, the **Schoenherr friction line** (1932) was used; this was later superseded by **ITTC-57** for better high-$R_n$ accuracy.
7. **(1 mark) Correlation Allowance:** Modern procedure adds a **correlation allowance $C_A$** to account for hull form roughness, appendage effects, and model-to-prototype scale effects that pure similitude cannot capture.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

1. **(1 mark) Limitation — Reynolds Mismatch:** Froude scaling only addresses wave-making; frictional drag cannot be simultaneously scaled, leading to errors of **5–15%** in frictional extrapolation.
2. **(1 mark) Limitation — Surface Roughness:** Model surfaces are smoother than prototype hulls, which may have been in service. Paint roughness differs by an order of magnitude.
3. **(1 mark) Limitation — Cavitation and Ventilation:** Standard towing tanks operate at atmospheric pressure; cavitation inlets and appendages cannot be reproduced at scale.
4. **(1 mark) Development of ITTC-57:** A joint committee analysed towing tank data from **18 leading basins** worldwide and fit a single logarithmic curve to the data, valid for $R_n$ from $10^6$ to $10^9$.
5. **(1 mark) The Formula:** $C_f = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$ — note the **$-2$ in the denominator** reflects the turbulence transition.
6. **(1 mark) Why It Solves the Problem:** It provides a **common, internationally agreed** frictional reference. Even if the model $R_n$ is far below the prototype's, the same formula extrapolates the drag correctly.
7. **(1 mark) Modern Updates:** The ITTC has since refined the line (e.g., **ITTC-78**), and the current procedure includes a **form factor $(1+k)$** and roughness allowance $C_A$ — but the 1957 form remains the foundation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps on "History of Model Testing" Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention ITTC-1957 by name.** It is *not* enough to say "a standard formula is used" — the examiner expects the specific form $C_f = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$. **(Lose up to 2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing length scale $\lambda$ with the model-to-prototype ratio direction.** Always write $\lambda = L_m / L_p$, *not* $L_p / L_m$. Many students invert this and lose the **derivation chain** marks. **(Lose up to 3 marks)**
> 3. **Not explaining *why* Froude and Reynolds cannot be matched together.** A common answer says "it's impossible" without the algebraic contradiction — this is worth only partial credit. **(Lose up to 2 marks)**
> 4. **Omitting the year 1871 / Froude's name / Torquay location** in the short answer — these are the exact "keyword" items KTU examiners tick on the mark sheet.
> 5. **Misquoting the power scale** as $\lambda^3$ instead of $\lambda^{7/2}$. This is a frequent slip; remember: **Power = Force $\times$ Velocity**, so exponents **add** ($3 + 0.5$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **William Froude (1869–1871)** is universally credited as the **father of model testing**; his Torquay tank (1871) was the **first purpose-built facility**.
- **D.W. Taylor (1899)** built DTMB and standardised the resistance coefficient $C_T$.
- **Froude number** $F_r = V / \sqrt{gL}$ governs **wave-making similitude** — the dominant physics for surface ships.
- **Reynolds number** $R_n = VL / \nu$ governs **viscous similitude**; it cannot be matched with $F_r$ in a single tank.
- **ITTC-1957 formula:** $C_f = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$ — the *single most tested equation* in KTU examinations.
- **Speed scale:** $V_m = V_p \sqrt{\lambda}$. **Resistance scale:** $R_{Tm} = R_{Tp} \, \lambda^3$. **Power scale:** $EP_m = EP_p \, \lambda^{7/2}$.
- **Geometric scale** $\lambda = L_m / L_p$ must always be **less than 1** for a model; typical values are **1/20 to 1/40** for displacement ships.
- **Froude's decomposition** $R_T = R_F + R_R$ is the bedrock of all extrapolation — and is a **favourite 7-mark question** in Part B.
- **ITTC** stands for **International Towing Tank Conference**, founded **1933**, headquartered in Europe.
- **Modern era** integrates **CFD + physical testing** under **ITTC quality systems** — examiners may ask about this as a "describe" item.
- **Memorise the three formulas**, the three names (Froude, Taylor, ITTC), the three eras (empirical → Froude → modern), and the three exponents ($\lambda^{1/2}, \lambda^3, \lambda^{7/2}$). These nine items cover the bulk of KTU question papers on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Towing Tanks Including Instrumentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components of Towing Tanks Including Instrumentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Towing Tank Facility

A **towing tank** is a long, narrow, rectangular basin of still water equipped with a computer-controlled carriage system used to determine the hydrodynamic performance of ship models, propeller models, and other submerged bodies under controlled laboratory conditions. It is the **primary experimental facility** in ship hydrodynamics laboratories worldwide, used to measure **resistance, propulsion, wave patterns, and maneuvering characteristics** of marine vehicles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Standardized):** A towing tank is an enclosed or open-type rectangular water basin fitted with a towing carriage, wave-generation systems, absorption beaches, and precision instrumentation that allows the controlled experimental determination of hydrodynamic forces, moments, and free-surface effects on towed or self-propelled models.

**Standard Towing Tank Dimensions (Industry Reference):**

| Facility Type | Length (m) | Width (m) | Depth (m) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Small Teaching Tank | 30 – 50 | 2 – 3 | 1.5 – 2 |
| Standard Research Tank | 100 – 150 | 5 – 8 | 3 – 5 |
| Large Deep Tank (e.g., MARIN, IIHR) | 270 – 400 | 10 – 15 | 6 – 12 |

> [!NOTE]
> The Froude number similarity criterion demands a tank length sufficient to avoid wave reflections from the beach interfering with the model during testing. This is why **major towing tanks are typically 100 m or longer**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Aquatic Racetrack"

Imagine a giant **swimming pool with a moving bridge** running along its length on rails. An engineer attaches a small ship model to this moving bridge and pulls it through the calm water. As the model moves, sensors measure the **drag force** (how hard the water resists the model's motion), and cameras/windows in the sidewalls record the **wave patterns** generated.

In essence, the towing tank reverses the problem: instead of making water flow past a stationary model (as in a wind tunnel), the **model is moved through stationary water** — exactly mimicking how a real ship moves through the ocean. The carriage is the "ship-puller's hand," and the **instrumentation is the scientific translator** that converts physical phenomena (forces, pressures, motions) into electrical signals we can record and analyze.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Tip:** Think of the towing tank as a **giant ruler filled with water** — every meter the carriage travels can be timed to the millisecond, every Newton of resistance can be measured to within grams, and every millimeter of wave height can be recorded to micron precision. It is the **gold standard** of ship-model testing because the model experiences real gravity, real water, and real free-surface effects — something no computer simulation can fully replicate.

---

## 1.3 Major Component Classification Overview

A modern towing tank consists of the following **functional subsystems**:

```
1.  Civil Structure (basin, walls, viewing windows)
2.  Towing Carriage (mechanical + propulsion)
3.  Wave-Maker (wave generator)
4.  Wave-Beach (absorption system)
5.  Model Support & Dynamometer
6.  Instrumentation & Sensors
7.  Data Acquisition System (DAQ)
8.  Control & Drive Electronics
9.  Auxiliary Systems (filtration, temperature control)
```

Each of these components plays a critical, interdependent role. We will dissect each in Sections 2 and 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component 1 — Towing Tank Civil Structure

The basin is a reinforced concrete or steel tank with:
- **Smooth, flat sidewalls** to minimize secondary reflections.
- **Glass viewing windows** (typically 1.5 m × 1.0 m acrylic panels) on one or both sides for flow visualization and wave measurement.
- **Rails** along the top edges on which the towing carriage rides.
- **Guide rails** at the bottom in deeper tanks for X-Y probe traverses.
- **Overhead gantry cranes** for model installation.

**Critical Design Ratios:**

$$\frac{L_{tank}}{L_{model}} \geq 15 \quad \text{(to avoid wake interference from carriage)}$$

$$\frac{W_{tank}}{B_{model}} \geq 3 \quad \text{(to avoid sidewall/blockage effects)}$$

$$\frac{H_{tank}}{T_{model}} \geq 3 \quad \text{(depth ratio for shallow-water elimination)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blockage Correction:** When the tank width is less than 5× the model beam, **blockage effects** artificially increase resistance. KTU examiners frequently test the student's awareness of these **wall and bottom proximity corrections**.

---

## 2.2 Component 2 — Towing Carriage

The **towing carriage** is the heart of the facility. It is a motorized bridge spanning the width of the tank and riding on precision-machined rails.

**Sub-components:**
- **Main frame (steel truss):** Carries instruments, dynamometer, and operator cabin.
- **Drive system:** Servo-controlled AC motor with rack-and-pinion or wire-rope drive.
- **Speed range:** Typically **0.01 m/s to 10 m/s** (covering Froude numbers 0.05 to 1.5 for a 3-m model).
- **Speed accuracy:** ±0.1% of set speed (e.g., ±1 mm/s at 1 m/s).
- **Position accuracy:** ±1 mm over the full tank length.
- **Acceleration/braking:** Controlled ramps to avoid water-surface disturbances.

**Carriage Velocity Equation (Fundamental):**

$$U = \sqrt{g \cdot L_{pp}} \cdot Fr$$

Where:
- $U$ = carriage velocity (m/s)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $L_{pp}$ = length between perpendiculars of the model (m)
- $Fr$ = Froude number (dimensionless)

---

## 2.3 Component 3 — Wave Maker

A **wave maker** generates regular or irregular waves for seakeeping experiments.

**Common Types:**

| Type | Mechanism | Frequency Range |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Flap-type | Pivoted bottom flap oscillates | 0.3 – 2.0 Hz |
| Piston-type | Vertical paddle moves horizontally | 0.2 – 1.5 Hz |
| Wedge-type | Wedge plunges vertically | 0.3 – 1.0 Hz |
| Snake-type (piston array) | Multiple pistons phased | 0.1 – 3.0 Hz |

**Wave Maker Theory (Havelock Formulation):**

The transfer function relating paddle stroke $S$ to wave height $H$ at depth $d$ is:

$$\frac{H}{S} = \frac{4 \sinh^2(kd)}{2kd + \sinh(2kd)}$$

Where $k$ is the wave number satisfying the dispersion relation:

$$\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** The wave maker is essential for **seakeeping tests** — measuring ship motions (heave, pitch, roll) in waves, added resistance in waves, and parametric roll studies.

---

## 2.4 Component 4 — Wave Beach (Absorber)

At the downstream end of the tank, a **wave absorber** (beach) dissipates incoming wave energy to prevent re-reflection back into the test section.

**Types of Beaches:**

1. **Sloping solid beach** (slope 1:10 to 1:20) — uses gradual depth reduction to break waves.
2. **Porous beach** — wire mesh, perforated plates, or expanded metal sheets that dissipate energy via viscous resistance.
3. **Active absorption beach** — computer-controlled paddles with real-time wave-elevation feedback to cancel reflected waves.
4. **Parabolic beach** — shaped to absorb a broad frequency range.

**Reflection Coefficient (performance metric):**

$$K_r = \frac{H_{reflected}}{H_{incident}}$$

A well-designed beach achieves $K_r < 0.10$ (i.e., >90% absorption).

---

## 2.5 Component 5 — Force Dynamometer

The **dynamometer** is the load-measuring instrument connecting the model to the carriage.

**Two Primary Architectures:**

| Type | Measures | Application |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Planar-motion mechanism (PMM)** | Forces + moments (X, Y, Z, Mx, My, Mz) | Manoeuvring tests |
| **Strut dynamometer** | Resistance (X), Side force (Y), Yaw moment (Nz) | Straight-line towing |
| **Cavitometer / Submerged balance** | All 6 components underwater | Cavitation tunnel |

**Typical Load Cell Configuration:**

A modern dynamometer uses **strain-gauge load cells** mounted in a Wheatstone bridge configuration. The output voltage $V_o$ is proportional to the applied force:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R / R}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

Where:
- $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage (typically 5–10 V)
- $GF$ = gauge factor (~2.0 for constantan)
- $\varepsilon$ = strain (dimensionless)
- $\Delta R / R$ = fractional resistance change

**Sensitivity of a typical towing-tank dynamometer:** 5 N with ±0.1% accuracy (full-scale 500 N).

---

## 2.6 Component 6 — Instrumentation Suite

### (a) Wave-Height Probes

**Resistance-type probes:** Two parallel stainless-steel wires partially immersed; resistance varies linearly with submersion depth.

$$h(t) = h_0 + \frac{V(t) - V_0}{m}$$

Where $m$ is the calibration slope (V/m).

**Capacitance-type probes:** Higher accuracy (±0.3 mm), immune to water conductivity changes.

**Ultrasonic probes:** Non-contact, suitable for harsh environments.

### (b) Pressure Transducers

Used on hull surfaces to measure **local pressure distribution** during resistance or wave-interaction tests.

$$p(t) = p_{ref} + \frac{V_{out}(t) - V_{offset}}{Sensitivity}$$

Typical sensitivity: 1–10 mV/kPa with frequency response up to 1 kHz.

### (c) Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)

Laser-based system that measures instantaneous **2D or 3D velocity fields** in a plane illuminated by a thin laser sheet. Used for wake-flow and boundary-layer studies.

### (d) Flow Visualization Tools

- **Dye injection** (potassium permanganate or food coloring) for streamline visualization.
- **Hydrogen-bubble technique** for boundary-layer flow visualization.
- **Tuft screens** for qualitative flow separation studies.

### (e) Motion Capture (for free-running models)

- **Camera-based optical tracking** (e.g., Vicon, Qualisys).
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)** combining accelerometers, gyroscopes, and magnetometers.

---

## 2.7 Component 7 — Data Acquisition System (DAQ)

The DAQ system converts analog sensor signals to digital form for storage and analysis.

**Typical Specifications:**
- **Sampling rate:** 100 Hz to 10 kHz (depending on signal type).
- **Resolution:** 16-bit or 24-bit ADC.
- **Anti-aliasing filter:** Low-pass filter at half the sampling rate (Nyquist).
- **Channels:** 32 to 256 simultaneous channels.

**Nyquist Sampling Criterion:**

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

Where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component in the signal.

**Time-Averaged Resistance (post-processing):**

$$R_{mean} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} R(t)\, dt$$

$$R_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} [R(t) - R_{mean}]^2\, dt}$$

---

## 2.8 Component 8 — Control Electronics

The carriage controller maintains constant speed during the test run:

$$U_{error}(t) = U_{set} - U_{measured}(t)$$

$$U_{command}(t) = K_p \cdot U_{error}(t) + K_i \int U_{error}(t)\, dt$$

This is a **PI (Proportional-Integral) controller** — the industry standard for towing carriages.

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Froude Number | $Fr = U/\sqrt{gL_{pp}}$ | Speed scaling |
| 2 | Reynolds Number | $Re = UL_{pp}/\nu$ | Viscous scaling |
| 3 | Wave Dispersion | $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$ | Wave-maker design |
| 4 | Havelock Transfer | $H/S = 4\sinh^2(kd)/[2kd+\sinh(2kd)]$ | Wave height calibration |
| 5 | Reflection Coefficient | $K_r = H_r/H_i$ | Beach performance |
| 6 | Nyquist Criterion | $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$ | DAQ setup |
| 7 | Strain-Gauge Output | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R/R \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ | Load cell calibration |
| 8 | Mean Resistance | $R_{mean} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T R(t)\,dt$ | Data reduction |
| 9 | Blockage Ratio | $W/B_{model} \geq 3$ | Tank sizing |
| 10 | Blockage Correction (ITTC) | $\Delta C_F = C_F \cdot [0.6(B/H)^2]$ | Wall proximity |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the **dispersion relation** and the **Havelock transfer function** — these appear in nearly every KTU paper on wave generation.

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Towing Tank Application |
|:---|:---|
| Commercial Shipping | Resistance optimization, hull-form development |
| Naval / Defense | Submarine hydrodynamics, signature reduction |
| Offshore Renewables | Wave-energy converters, floating wind platforms |
| High-Speed Craft | Planning hulls, air-cushion vehicles |
| Yacht Design | Calm-water resistance and wave patterns |
| Autonomous Marine Vehicles | Maneuvering and control-surface design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ITTC (International Towing Tank Conference)** sets standardized procedures for towing-tank testing, including calibration, blockage correction, roughness allowance, and form-factor derivation. KTU examiners expect awareness of these standards.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Froude Similarity and Speed Scaling

**Statement:** For dynamic similarity between model and full-scale ship, the Froude numbers must be equal.

### Step 1 — Definition of Froude Number

The Froude number is the ratio of inertial force to gravitational force:

$$Fr = \frac{U}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Similarity Condition

For model (subscript $m$) and prototype (subscript $s$):

$$Fr_m = Fr_s$$

$$\frac{U_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{U_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Model Speed

$$U_m = U_s \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}}$$

Or, equivalently:

$$\frac{U_m}{U_s} = \sqrt{\lambda}$$

Where $\lambda = L_m / L_s$ is the length scale.

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

A 200 m full-scale ship is tested at 15 knots (7.72 m/s) using a 1:25 scale model. Find the model test speed.

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{25}$$

$$U_m = 7.72 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{25}} = \frac{7.72}{5} = 1.544 \text{ m/s}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wave Dispersion Relation Derivation

**Statement:** Show that the dispersion relation $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$ governs linear water waves.

### Step 1 — Governing Equations

For inviscid, incompressible, irrotational flow, the velocity potential $\phi(x,z,t)$ satisfies Laplace's equation:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

### Step 2 — Boundary Conditions

- **Bottom (z = −d):** $\partial \phi / \partial z = 0$
- **Free surface (z = 0):**
  - Kinematic: $\partial \eta / \partial t = \partial \phi / \partial z$
  - Dynamic: $\partial \phi / \partial t + g\eta = 0$

### Step 3 — Separable Solution

Assume:

$$\phi(x,z,t) = A \cosh[k(z+d)] \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

This satisfies Laplace and bottom BC automatically.

### Step 4 — Apply Surface BC

Combining the two surface BCs eliminates $\eta$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} + g \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{at } z=0$$

Substituting:

$$-\omega^2 \cosh(kd) + gk \sinh(kd) = 0$$

$$\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Havelock Wave-Maker Transfer Function

### Step 1 — Setup

Consider a flap-type wave maker hinged at the bottom, oscillating with small angular amplitude $\theta_0$ and frequency $\omega$. Paddle stroke at the free surface: $S = 2\theta_0 d$.

### Step 2 — Match Boundary Conditions

Match the wave field with the paddle's horizontal velocity at $z=0$. Solving the Laplace equation with moving boundary:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0} = -i\omega \theta_0 (z+d) \quad \text{for } -d \leq z \leq 0$$

### Step 3 — Velocity Potential Expansion

Express the radiated wave field as:

$$\phi = \frac{A g}{\omega} \cosh[k(z+d)] \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Matching Condition

At $x=0$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{A g k}{\omega} \cosh[k(z+d)] \cos(\omega t)$$

Setting equal to paddle velocity and integrating over depth yields:

$$A = \frac{2\theta_0 \omega}{gk} \cdot \frac{1}{\cosh(kd)}$$

### Step 5 — Wave Amplitude

The free-surface displacement amplitude is:

$$\eta = \frac{\omega A}{g} \cosh(kd) = \frac{2\theta_0}{k}$$

Since paddle stroke $S = 2\theta_0 d$:

$$H = 2\eta = \frac{4\theta_0}{k}$$

The transfer function (wave height per unit stroke):

$$\boxed{\frac{H}{S} = \frac{4\sinh^2(kd)}{2kd + \sinh(2kd)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters:** A wave-maker operator must know this $H/S$ ratio to dial in the correct stroke setting to achieve a desired wave height at the model location.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Strain-Gauge Load Cell Calibration

### Step 1 — Wheatstone Bridge Equation

A load cell uses four strain gauges in a full bridge. The output voltage is:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left(\frac{R_1 + \Delta R_1}{R_1 + \Delta R_1 + R_2 + \Delta R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}\right)$$

For a balanced bridge with two gauges in tension, two in compression:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} = -\frac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} = \frac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} = -\frac{\Delta R_4}{R_4}$$

### Step 2 — Simplification

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

### Step 3 — Strain Relation

Strain-gauge gauge factor:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

Where $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is the strain.

### Step 4 — Stress-Strain Relation

For a beam under bending stress $\sigma = F/A$ and elastic modulus $E$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

### Step 5 — Final Output Equation

$$\boxed{V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \frac{F}{A \cdot E}}$$

Solving for force:

$$F = \frac{V_o \cdot A \cdot E}{V_{ex} \cdot GF}$$

**Numerical Example:** A load cell with $A = 10^{-4}$ m², $E = 200$ GPa, $GF = 2.0$, $V_{ex} = 10$ V produces $V_o = 2$ mV. Find the force.

$$F = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-3})(10^{-4})(2 \times 10^{11})}{(10)(2.0)} = \frac{4 \times 10^{4}}{20} = 2000 \text{ N}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Mean Resistance from Time-Series Data

### Step 1 — Discrete Sampling

The DAQ samples $N$ values of resistance $R_i$ at sampling interval $\Delta t$.

### Step 2 — Time-Average

$$R_{mean} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i$$

### Step 3 — RMS Fluctuation

$$R_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (R_i - R_{mean})^2}$$

### Step 4 — Trim Correction

Often only the central portion (free from acceleration/braking transients) is averaged:

$$R_{mean} = \frac{1}{N_2 - N_1} \sum_{i=N_1}^{N_2} R_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** Most KTU lab questions test the student's ability to extract mean resistance from raw time-series data while correctly identifying the **steady-state window**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — ITTC Standard Corrections

| Correction | Formula | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Blockage | $C_F^{corr} = C_F [1 - 0.6(B/H)^2]$ | Sidewall effect |
| Shallow water | $C_F^{corr} = C_F [1 - 0.6(T/H)^2]$ | Bottom proximity |
| Form factor | $k = (C_T - C_F)/C_F$ | Viscous-pressure split |
| Roughness allowance | $\Delta C_F = 0.044 \left[ (k_s/L)^{1/3} - 10 \cdot Re^{-1/3}\right] \times 10^{-3}$ | Hull roughness scaling |
| Spray & Ventilation | Empirical tables | High-speed craft |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Problem — Full Carriage Test Analysis

**Problem:** A 4 m ship model ($\lambda = 1:30$) is towed at 2.5 m/s in a 120 m towing tank. The dynamometer records the following time-series (N): 28.5, 29.1, 28.8, 29.3, 28.7, 29.0, 28.9, 29.2, 28.6, 29.1. The wave-height probe at midships shows a regular wave of amplitude 12 mm.

**(a) Calculate the model Froude number.**

$$Fr_m = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 4}} = \frac{2.5}{6.264} = 0.399$$

**(b) Compute the full-scale speed.**

$$U_s = U_m \cdot \sqrt{1/\lambda} = 2.5 \times \sqrt{30} = 2.5 \times 5.477 = 13.69 \text{ m/s} \approx 26.6 \text{ knots}$$

**(c) Compute the mean resistance.**

$$R_{mean} = \frac{28.5+29.1+28.8+29.3+28.7+29.0+28.9+29.2+28.6+29.1}{10}$$

$$\sum = 289.2$$

$$R_{mean} = 28.92 \text{ N}$$

**(d) Compute the standard deviation.**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10}(R_i - 28.92)^2}$$

Differences: 0.42, 0.18, -0.12, 0.38, -0.22, 0.08, -0.02, 0.28, -0.32, 0.18
Squared: 0.1764, 0.0324, 0.0144, 0.1444, 0.0484, 0.0064, 0.0004, 0.0784, 0.1024, 0.0324
Sum: 0.636

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.0636} = 0.252 \text{ N}$$

**(e) Compute the full-scale resistance using $C_F$ scaling.**

Assume $C_F$ (model) = 0.0030. By Froude scaling:

$$R_{full} = R_m \cdot \lambda^3 \cdot \frac{C_{Fs}}{C_{Fm}}$$

With $Re_s = U_s L_s / \nu$ and ITTC-57 line:

$$C_{Fs} = 0.075 / (\log Re_s - 2)^2$$

For $Re_s = 13.69 \times 120 / (10^{-6}) = 1.643 \times 10^9$:

$$C_{Fs} = 0.075 / (\log(1.643 \times 10^9) - 2)^2 = 0.075/(9.216 - 2)^2 = 0.075/52.0 = 0.00144$$

$$R_{full} = 28.92 \times 27000 \times (0.00144/0.0030) = 28.92 \times 27000 \times 0.48 = 374,803 \text{ N} \approx 375 \text{ kN}$$
