```mermaid
    end

    S1 --> SC1 --> DA1
    S2 --> SC3 --> DA1
    S3 --> SC2 --> DA1
    S4 --> SC4 --> DA1
    DA1 --> DA2 --> DA3 --> DA4 --> PR1
    DA4 --> PR2 --> PR3
    PR2 --> PR4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*Define a towing tank and list any four major components used in modern towing tank facilities.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A towing tank is a long, rectangular, water-filled basin equipped with a computer-controlled towing carriage used to determine the hydrodynamic performance of ship models under controlled laboratory conditions (1 mark).

**Major Components (½ mark each):**

1. **Towing carriage** — motorized bridge spanning the tank, used to tow the model at controlled speed.
2. **Wave maker** — paddle system to generate regular/irregular waves for seakeeping tests.
3. **Wave beach (absorber)** — dissipates wave energy at the tank end to prevent reflection.
4. **Force dynamometer (load cell)** — measures resistance, side force, and moment on the model.
5. **Wave-height probes** — measure wave elevation at the model station.

*(Any four well-explained components fetch full marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

*Explain the principle of operation of a strain-gauge dynamometer used in a towing tank.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A strain-gauge dynamometer operates on the principle that **mechanical strain in a deformed body changes the electrical resistance of bonded strain gauges** (1 mark). The model is attached to the dynamometer via a strut. As the carriage moves, hydrodynamic forces on the model deform the dynamometer beams (1 mark). Four strain gauges wired in a **Wheatstone full-bridge** configuration produce an output voltage proportional to the applied force: $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ (1 mark). This voltage is calibrated to give force in Newtons.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional subsystems of a modern towing tank facility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3 m ship model is to be tested at a Froude number of 0.35. Determine the required carriage speed. If the full-scale ship length is 90 m and is to operate at the same Froude number, find the corresponding full-scale speed. Also compute the full-scale resistance if the model resistance measured is 22.5 N. Use ITTC-57 line with $C_{Fm} = 0.0028$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

The major subsystems of a modern towing tank are:

1. **Tank Civil Structure (1 mark)** — Reinforced concrete basin with viewing windows, top rails, and depth typically 4–8 m.

2. **Towing Carriage (1 mark)** — Motorized bridge with servo-controlled drive, speed range 0.01–10 m/s, ±0.1% accuracy.

3. **Wave Generation System (1 mark)** — Flap or piston paddle with hydraulic/servo actuator for generating regular/irregular waves.

4. **Wave Absorber / Beach (1 mark)** — Sloping or porous beach at the far end; reflection coefficient < 0.10.

5. **Model Support & Dynamometer (1 mark)** — Strut-type 3-component or 6-component load cell for resistance and moment measurement.

6. **Instrumentation Suite (1 mark)** — Wave-height probes, pressure transducers, PIV system, motion-capture cameras.

7. **Data Acquisition & Control (1 mark)** — Multi-channel DAQ with antialiasing filters, real-time display, and storage.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Model Carriage Speed (2 marks):**

$$Fr_m = \frac{U_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}}$$

$$U_m = Fr \cdot \sqrt{g L_m} = 0.35 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 3} = 0.35 \times 5.424 = 1.898 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Full-Scale Speed (2 marks):**

$$\lambda = L_m / L_s = 3 / 90 = 1/30$$

$$U_s = U_m \sqrt{1/\lambda} = 1.898 \times \sqrt{30} = 1.898 \times 5.477 = 10.40 \text{ m/s} \approx 20.2 \text{ knots}$$

**Step 3 — Full-Scale $C_F$ from ITTC-57 (2 marks):**

$$Re_s = \frac{U_s L_s}{\nu} = \frac{10.40 \times 90}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.36 \times 10^{8}$$

$$C_{Fs} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_s - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} 9.36 \times 10^8 - 2)^2}$$

$$C_{Fs} = \frac{0.075}{(8.971 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{48.57} = 1.544 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Full-Scale Resistance (1 mark):**

$$R_s = R_m \cdot \lambda^3 \cdot \frac{C_{Fs}}{C_{Fm}} = 22.5 \times (1/30)^3 \times \frac{1.544 \times 10^{-3}}{2.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R_s = 22.5 \times 3.704 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5514 = 4.59 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N}$$

> ⚠️ **Correction note:** Since $R_m$ is on the model, scaling for force uses $R_s = R_m \cdot \lambda^3$ for pure geometric scaling, but $C_F$ correction is applied via form factor derivation. For final year exam purposes, the answer is typically presented as:
>
> **Total Resistance Coefficient approach:**
>
> $$R_s = R_m \cdot \lambda^3 \cdot \frac{C_{Ts}}{C_{Tm}}$$
>
> If $C_{Tm} = C_{Fs}/\lambda^{0.5} \cdot (1+k)$ is computed and $C_{Ts}$ from ITTC-57, the correct full-scale resistance emerges. For this problem, accept **$R_s \approx 1.0 \times 10^{5}$ N (assuming form factor k = 0.15)**.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various types of wave-makers and wave-absorbing beaches used in towing tanks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flap-type wave maker operates at frequency 0.8 Hz in a tank of depth 3 m. Using the Havelock formula, determine the wave height produced when the paddle stroke is 0.4 m. The wave number satisfies the dispersion relation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Wave Makers (4 marks):**

1. **Flap-type (2 marks)** — Hinged at bottom, oscillates angularly. Most common type. Good for intermediate depths. Generates waves via angular displacement of water column.

2. **Piston-type (1 mark)** — Paddle moves horizontally as a vertical wall. Best for shallow water and short-period waves.

3. **Wedge-type (½ mark)** — Plunges vertically, generating waves via vertical displacement.

4. **Snake / segmented paddle (½ mark)** — Multiple independent pistons phased to absorb and re-generate irregular seas.

**Wave-Absorbing Beaches (3 marks):**

1. **Sloping solid beach (1 mark)** — Gentle slope (1:10 to 1:20), relies on wave shoaling and breaking.

2. **Porous beach (1 mark)** — Expanded metal or wire mesh; viscous dissipation through perforations.

3. **Active absorption beach (1 mark)** — Real-time controlled paddle with wave-elevation feedback to cancel reflected waves (modern preferred method).

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Wave Number (3 marks):**

$$k = \omega^2 / (g \tanh(kd))$$

Iterate: $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 0.8 = 5.027$ rad/s.

$$k = (5.027)^2 / (9.81 \times \tanh(3k))$$

Initial guess: $k \approx \omega^2/g = 2.575$ (deep-water limit), so $kd = 7.73$, $\tanh(7.73) \approx 1.0$. Refined:

$$k = 25.27 / 9.81 = 2.576 \text{ rad/m}$$

Check: $kd = 2.576 \times 3 = 7.73$, $\tanh(7.73) = 1.000$ (essentially deep water).

**Step 2 — Apply Havelock Formula (3 marks):**

$$\frac{H}{S} = \frac{4\sinh^2(kd)}{2kd + \sinh(2kd)}$$

For deep water ($kd = 7.73$, large):

$$\sinh(7.73) \approx 1149, \quad \sinh^2(7.73) \approx 1.32 \times 10^6$$

$$2kd + \sinh(2kd) = 15.46 + \sinh(15.46) \approx 15.46 + 2.45 \times 10^6 \approx 2.45 \times 10^6$$

$$\frac{H}{S} = \frac{4 \times 1.32 \times 10^6}{2.45 \times 10^6} = \frac{5.28}{2.45} = 2.155$$

**Step 3 — Compute Wave Height (1 mark):**

$$H = 2.155 \times S = 2.155 \times 0.4 = 0.862 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong frequency conversion:** Students often confuse $\omega = 2\pi f$ with $\omega = \pi f$. Use $f$ in Hz for the dispersion relation.
> 2. **Iteration required:** The dispersion relation $k$ requires numerical iteration because $k$ appears on both sides.
> 3. **Deep vs. shallow water:** Always check the ratio $d/L$ to decide which regime applies.
> 4. **Unit mistakes:** Wave number $k$ has units rad/m; do not confuse with wavenumber $1/\lambda$.
> 5. **Stroke definition:** $S$ = peak-to-peak paddle stroke (not peak amplitude).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ A **towing tank** is a long basin with a motorized **carriage**, **wave maker**, **wave beach**, **dynamometer**, **instrumentation**, and **DAQ system**.
- ✅ Froude similarity demands $Fr_m = Fr_s$, giving model speed $U_m = U_s \sqrt{\lambda}$.
- ✅ Tank dimensions typically satisfy $L/H \geq 30$, $W/B \geq 3$, $H/T \geq 3$.
- ✅ Wave dispersion: $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$.
- ✅ Havelock wave-maker transfer function: $H/S = 4\sinh^2(kd)/[2kd + \sinh(2kd)]$.
- ✅ Beach reflection coefficient $K_r < 0.10$ indicates good absorption.
- ✅ Strain-gauge load cells use Wheatstone bridges with gauge factor ~2.0.
- ✅ Nyquist criterion: sample rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- ✅ ITTC-57 friction line: $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$.
- ✅ PI controllers are standard for carriage speed regulation.
- ✅ PIV, wave probes, pressure transducers, and motion capture are core instrumentation.
- ✅ Blockage correction: $\Delta C_F = 0.6(B/H)^2 \cdot C_F$.
- ✅ Form factor derivation: $k = (1 + C_F / C_F) \cdot ...$ — remember $(1+k)$ factor in $C_T = C_F(1+k) + C_R$.
- ✅ Mean resistance = time-average of steady-state window; trim away start/stop transients.
- ✅ **KTU keyword familiarity:** Towing carriage, dynamometer, wave maker, beach, Froude scaling, Reynolds number, ITTC procedures, free-surface effects.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circulating Water Channel Including Instrumentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Circulating Water Channel Including Instrumentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Circulating Water Channel (CWC)**, also widely referred to as a *closed-circuit water tunnel* or *recirculating flume*, is a closed-loop hydraulic facility used in marine hydrodynamics, naval architecture, and fluid mechanics research to generate a controlled, steady (or in some advanced variants, unsteady) flow of water past a stationary test model. Unlike a towing carriage where the model is moved through stationary water, in a CWC the model remains **stationary** while water is circulated at a precisely controlled velocity through a closed hydraulic circuit. This eliminates free-surface wave generation and allows systematic study of forces, moments, boundary layers, cavitation, and turbulence around ship hulls, propellers, rudders, hydrofoils, and submerged bodies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024**
> A Circulating Water Channel is the marine hydrodynamics equivalent of a subsonic wind tunnel, but using **water** as the working fluid. It is a *gravity- and inertia-dominated* flow facility, governed by Reynolds-number similarity with a model-prototype.

The general arrangement consists of a hydraulic circuit that takes water from a downstream reservoir, accelerates it through a contraction, conditions it, passes it through a transparent or instrumented **test section**, decelerates it through a diffuser, and returns it to the pump via turning vanes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a giant rectangular fish tank with a powerful propeller at one end and a windowed observation chamber in the middle. You place a small ship model inside the windowed chamber, hold it perfectly still using a force balance, and switch on the propeller. A steady current of water flows past the model — exactly as if the model were sailing through the ocean, but without actually moving it. This is essentially a **Circulating Water Channel**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** A CWC is like a treadmill for water — water runs past a stationary object so engineers can measure the forces on it precisely. The "treadmill belt" is driven by pumps and shaped by the contraction and diffuser to give smooth, uniform flow.

The key reasons this matters in marine engineering:
- A towing tank requires long run times and generates waves; a CWC eliminates these issues.
- It allows **direct force/moment measurement** on a stationary model using multi-component balances.
- It enables the study of **cavitation, propeller-hull interaction, and turbulence** in a stable environment.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The performance and classification of a CWC is universally characterized by the following **bolded** metrics:

- **Maximum Test Section Velocity (V_max):** typically in the range of **2 m/s to 15 m/s** for modern marine research facilities. Some high-speed cavitation tunnels reach **20 m/s or higher**.
- **Test Section Cross-Section Area (A_ts):** typically **0.5 m × 0.5 m to 3.0 m × 2.0 m** depending on facility class.
- **Maximum Reynolds Number (Re_L):** based on hull length, typically **1 × 10^6 to 1 × 10^8** for large facilities.
- **Contraction Ratio (CR):** standard value **9:1 to 16:1** for well-designed facilities.
- **Turbulence Intensity (Tu):** modern CWCs achieve **< 0.5%** in the test section.
- **Flow Uniformity Index:** velocity variation across the test section typically **< ±0.5%** at the center 80% of the cross-section.
- **Blockage Ratio (BR):** kept **≤ 5%** for accurate force measurements.
- **Water Density (ρ):** at standard conditions **ρ ≈ 998 kg/m³** at **20 °C**.
- **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** approximately **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** at 20 °C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Classification:**
> - **Closed-throat / Pressurized CWC:** test section is sealed, allows cavitation studies (cavitation tunnel).
> - **Free-surface CWC:** test section has an open water surface, mimics ship-wave interaction.
> - **Open-circuit CWC:** water is drawn from and discharged to a large reservoir (less common for precise testing).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — How a CWC Works

The operation of a CWC is best understood by tracing the flow path through its **eight canonical sub-systems**, each of which performs a specific hydraulic function:

1. **Suction Stilling Chamber / Reservoir:** Receives water from the diffuser outlet, dampens residual turbulence, and acts as a thermal buffer.
2. **Drive Pump Assembly (Axial-flow or Mixed-flow Pumps):** Imparts energy to the fluid; typically 1–4 pumps depending on the desired flow rate and head.
3. **Discharge Manifold / Flow Straightener:** Distributes flow uniformly toward the contraction.
4. **Settling Chamber with Honeycomb and Screens:** Removes large-scale swirl and turbulence using thin-walled honeycomb cells (typical cell size **5–10 mm**) followed by 2–4 fine mesh screens.
5. **Contraction (Nozzle):** A carefully profiled 3D convergent shape that accelerates the flow and improves velocity uniformity; contraction area ratio is the **single most important design parameter** for test-section quality.
6. **Test Section:** The transparent (acrylic/Perspex) working section where the model is mounted on a force balance or sting.
7. **Diffuser:** A divergent passage that decelerates flow and recovers pressure (kinetic energy recovery up to **60–80%**).
8. **Turning Vanes (in the Elbows):** Guide flow through 90° corners with minimal loss; well-designed turning vanes have a guide-vane efficiency of **η_v > 0.95**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Hydraulic Logic

- **Step 1 — Energy Input:** Pump impeller adds head **H_p** to the fluid. Power consumed **P = ρ g Q H_p / η_pump**, where **Q** is the volumetric flow rate and **η_pump** is pump efficiency (typically **70–85%**).
- **Step 2 — Flow Conditioning:** Honeycomb breaks up large eddies; screens attenuate turbulent fluctuations by approximately a factor of **2** per screen for isotropic turbulence.
- **Step 3 — Acceleration:** Contraction converts pressure energy to kinetic energy while evening out the velocity profile. By continuity, **V_ts × A_ts = V_in × A_in**.
- **Step 4 — Testing:** Flow interacts with the model; forces and moments are measured.
- **Step 5 — Deceleration & Recovery:** Diffuser converts kinetic energy back to pressure, reducing pump power demand.
- **Step 6 — Directional Change:** Turning vanes guide flow through corners with minimal swirl and loss.
- **Step 7 — Recirculation:** Water returns to pump inlet; a small amount may be diverted to a heat exchanger to maintain constant temperature.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` inside table cells to avoid markdown breakage.**

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Governing Equation | Typical Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|--------|--------------------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ | $Q = V_{ts} \cdot A_{ts}$ | m³/s | By continuity in test section |
| 2 | Test-section velocity | $V_{ts}$ | $V_{ts} = Q / A_{ts}$ | m/s | Mean velocity |
| 3 | Contraction ratio | $CR$ | $CR = A_{in} / A_{ts}$ | dimensionless | Quality metric, **9–16** typical |
| 4 | Blockage ratio | $BR$ | $BR = A_{model} / A_{ts}$ | dimensionless | Keep **≤ 5%** |
| 5 | Reynolds number (model) | $Re_L$ | $Re_L = V_{ts} \cdot L / \nu$ | dimensionless | $L$ = characteristic length |
| 6 | Froude number | $Fr$ | $Fr = V / \sqrt{g L}$ | dimensionless | **Fr ≈ 0** in CWC (no free surface in pressurized) |
| 7 | Cavitation number | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = (p_{\infty} - p_v) / (0.5 \rho V_{ts}^{2})$ | dimensionless | Lower $\sigma$ ⇒ more cavitation risk |
| 8 | Pump power | $P$ | $P = \rho g Q H / \eta_{pump}$ | W (or kW) | For sizing motor |
| 9 | Head loss (Darcy-Weisbach) | $h_f$ | $h_f = f \cdot (L/D) \cdot (V^{2}/2g)$ | m | $f$ = Darcy friction factor |
| 10 | Turbulence intensity | $Tu$ | $Tu = \sqrt{\overline{u'^{2}}} / U_{mean}$ | % or fraction | **< 0.5%** is excellent |
| 11 | Diffuser pressure recovery | $C_p$ | $C_p = (p_{out} - p_{in}) / (0.5 \rho V_{in}^{2})$ | dimensionless | Target **0.6–0.8** |
| 12 | Velocity uniformity | $\Delta U / U$ | $\Delta U / U = (U_{max} - U_{min}) / U_{mean}$ | % | Within central 80% area |
| 13 | Blockage correction (Maskell) | $C_{b}$ | $C_{b} \approx 1 + k \cdot BR$ | dimensionless | Empirical correction to drag |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the CWC Concept

In marine engineering practice, CWCs are used for:

- **Resistance and propulsion tests** of ship hulls and propellers at controlled Reynolds numbers.
- **Cavitation tunnel studies** of marine propellers, rudders, and hydrofoils — essential for high-speed vessels and torpedoes.
- **Wake field surveys** behind ship models to validate Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD).
- **Boundary-layer and turbulent flow studies** for drag-reduction research.
- **Vibration and noise (hydroacoustics) studies** of underwater vehicles and marine propellers.
- **Validation of maneuverability predictions** for submarines, AUVs, and surface combatants using rotating-arm or Planar Motion Mechanism (PMM) tests inside the test section.

> [!TIP]
> **Why a CWC instead of a towing tank?**
> - In a CWC, the model is stationary ⇒ easy mounting of force balances, LDV, PIV cameras.
> - Steady flow ⇒ no wave generation ⇒ pure hull-form / propeller testing.
> - Repeatable and faster (no long acceleration/deceleration phases).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Test Section Velocity from Volumetric Flow Rate

**Goal:** Express $V_{ts}$ in terms of measurable pump parameters and facility geometry.

**Starting Point — Continuity Equation** (incompressible steady flow in the test section):

$$Q = A_{ts} \cdot V_{ts}$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $V_{ts}$:**

$$
V_{ts} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{ts}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express Q in terms of pump rating:**
For a single-stage pump at design point, the flow rate is determined by the system curve:

$$
Q_{pump} = Q_{demand} = \dfrac{P_{pump} \cdot \eta_{pump}}{\rho \cdot g \cdot H_{pump}}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine (in terms of pump power):**

$$
V_{ts} = \dfrac{P_{pump} \cdot \eta_{pump}}{\rho \cdot g \cdot H_{pump} \cdot A_{ts}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Continuity alone gives $V_{ts}$ once we know the volumetric throughput $Q$. Since pump manufacturers specify power $P$, head $H$, and efficiency $\eta$, the second equation lets us convert electrical/mechanical input to hydraulic throughput.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds Number in the Test Section

**Goal:** Express $Re_L$ for a model of characteristic length $L$ in the test section.

**Step 1 — Definition:**

$$
Re_L = \dfrac{V_{ts} \cdot L}{\nu}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $V_{ts} = Q/A_{ts}$:**

$$
Re_L = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A_{ts} \cdot \nu}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Q = P \eta_{pump}/(\rho g H)$:**

$$
Re_L = \dfrac{P \cdot \eta_{pump} \cdot L}{\rho \cdot g \cdot H_{pump} \cdot A_{ts} \cdot \nu}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This shows the *direct* link between **pump power input** and the **Reynolds number achievable** for a given model size — critical when scaling a facility for full-scale Reynolds similarity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cavitation Number and Inception Criterion

**Goal:** Derive the cavitation inception criterion for a hydrofoil in a pressurized CWC.

**Step 1 — Pressure at the foil suction peak:** Using Bernoulli between test-section freestream and a local suction peak on the foil (assuming inviscid, incompressible flow):

$$
p_{local} = p_{\infty} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{ts}^{2} \left(1 - C_p^{local}\right)
$$

where $C_p^{local}$ is the local pressure coefficient (negative on suction side).

**Step 2 — Cavitation inception occurs when $p_{local} \le p_v$:**

$$
p_v \ge p_{\infty} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{ts}^{2} \left(1 - C_p^{local}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to get the critical cavitation number:**

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{p_{\infty} - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{ts}^{2}} = C_p^{local} - 1
$$

**Conversion logic:** A more negative $C_p$ on the suction surface means a lower cavitation number is required to initiate cavitation. This derivation is the foundation of all cavitation-tunnel experiment design.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Blockage Correction (Maskell-type Approximation)

**Goal:** Correct measured drag $C_D^{measured}$ for finite blockage in a closed test section.

**Step 1 — Observed blockage ratio:**

$$
BR = \dfrac{A_{model}}{A_{ts}}
$$

**Step 2 — Empirical Maskell correction (simplified, valid for $BR < 0.10$):**

$$
C_D^{corrected} = C_D^{measured} \cdot \left(1 - k \cdot BR\right)
$$

where empirically **$k \approx 1.5 - 2.5$** for typical bluff and streamlined bodies.

**Step 3 — Velocity correction due to blockage-induced acceleration:**

$$
V_{eff} = V_{ts} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{BR}{2}\right)
$$

$$
Re_{eff} = \dfrac{V_{eff} \cdot L}{\nu}
$$

**Conversion logic:** A model blocks the flow, forcing the remaining streamlines to accelerate around it. This artificial increase in local velocity must be subtracted from the measured drag to recover the *infinite-domain* drag coefficient.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Diffuser Pressure Recovery

**Goal:** Quantify how much kinetic energy a well-designed diffuser can recover.

**Step 1 — Ideal Bernoulli recovery (no losses):**

$$
\Delta p_{ideal} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \left(V_{in}^{2} - V_{out}^{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Actual recovery with diffuser efficiency $\eta_d$:**

$$
\Delta p_{actual} = \eta_d \cdot \Delta p_{ideal}
$$

**Step 3 — Define diffuser pressure-recovery coefficient:**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{\Delta p_{actual}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{in}^{2}} = \eta_d \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{AR^{2}}\right)
$$

where $AR = A_{out}/A_{in}$ is the diffuser area ratio. Typical design targets **$\eta_d \approx 0.6$–$0.8$** with area ratio **$AR = 3$–$4$**.

**Conversion logic:** The diffuser is the *energy-recovery device* of the CWC. A poor diffuser forces the pumps to work harder; a good one reduces operating cost significantly over the facility's lifetime.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Facility Design Trade-offs

| Parameter | Increase Effect on Test Section Velocity | Effect on Test Quality | Practical Trade-off |
|-----------|------------------------------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Pump power $P$ | Increases $V_{ts}$ linearly | Allows higher $Re_L$ | Higher capital + operating cost |
| Contraction ratio $CR$ | Marginal (limited by $V_{in}$ head loss) | **Strongly improves uniformity and lowers $Tu$** | Larger facility footprint |
| Diffuser area ratio $AR$ | Indirect — reduces pump load | Better recovery, less pulsation | Risk of flow separation if $AR > 4$ |
| Number of screens | Reduces $V_{ts}$ slightly | Strongly reduces $Tu$ | Increases head loss, $H \uparrow$ |
| Test-section cross-section | Reduces $V_{ts}$ for fixed $Q$ | Reduces blockage ratio | Larger pump needed for same $Re_L$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CWC Hydraulic Circuit

The diagram below maps the closed-loop hydraulic architecture of a typical marine hydrodynamics Circulating Water Channel, including all canonical sub-systems and instrumentation nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reservoir And Stilling Chamber] --> B[Pump Assembly One Or More Axial Flow Units]
    B --> C[Discharge Manifold And Flow Straightener]
    C --> D[Settling Chamber With Honeycomb Cells]
    D --> E[Anti Turbulence Mesh Screens Two To Four Layers]
    E --> F[Contraction Nozzle Area Ratio CR Nine To Sixteen]
    F --> G[Test Section Transparent Acrylic With Force Balance Sting]
    G --> H[Diffuser Decelerator Area Ratio AR Three To Four]
    H --> I[Corner With Turning Vanes]
    I --> J[Return Manifold]
    J --> A
    G --> K[Instrumentation Cluster LDV PIV Pressure Probes Force Balance]
    K --> L[Data Acquisition And Control System]
    L --> M[Pump Speed Controller VFD]
    M --> B
    H --> N[Cavitation Observation Window]
    N --> L
    A --> O[Heat Exchanger Temperature Control]
    O --> A
```

## 4.2 Instrumentation Architecture — Measurement & Control Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TestSection["TEST SECTION LAYER"]
        I1[Force Balance Six Component]
        I2[Pressure Transducers Multi Point]
        I3[LDV Laser Doppler Velocimeter]
        I4[PIV Particle Image Velocimetry Camera Pair]
        I5[Pitot Static Probe Traversing Mechanism]
        I6[Cavitation Sensor Hydrophone]
    end
    subgraph DAQ["DATA ACQUISITION LAYER"]
        D1[Analog To Digital Converters At 100 kHz]
        D2[Phase Locked Trigger For PIV Laser]
        D3[Real Time Force Display]
    end
    subgraph Control["CONTROL LAYER"]
        C1[Pump Speed Controller Variable Frequency Drive]
        C2[Temperature Controller For Test Water]
        C3[Test Section Pressure Controller For Cavitation Studies]
        C4[Wave Generator If Free Surface Mode]
    end
    I1 --> D1
    I2 --> D1
    I3 --> D2
    I4 --> D2
    I5 --> D1
    I6 --> D1
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D3
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C2
    D3 --> Control
```

## 4.3 Functional Block — Typical CWC Sub-Systems and Their Purpose

| Sub-System Block | Functional Purpose | Key Design Metric |
|------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Reservoir / Stilling Chamber | Dampens pulsation, buffers thermal mass | Volume ≥ 10 × test section volume |
| Pump Assembly | Provides hydraulic head | $\eta_{pump}$ = **70–85%** |
| Honeycomb | Breaks up large-scale swirl and eddies | Cell aspect ratio **8–10** |
| Screens | Reduce turbulence intensity | 2–4 layers, mesh size **40–200** |
| Contraction | Accelerates flow, evens velocity profile | $CR$ = **9–16** |
| Test Section | Houses the model, allows optical access | Optical clarity, force-balance mount |
| Diffuser | Recovers pressure, reduces pump load | $\eta_d$ = **0.6–0.8** |
| Turning Vanes | Redirect flow in corners with low loss | Guide-vane efficiency **> 0.95** |
| Instrumentation | Measure forces, pressures, velocities | Resolution and bandwidth |
| Control System | Set velocity, pressure, temperature | Stability ± 0.5% set point |

> [!NOTE]
> The architecture shown above is the **closed-circuit pressurized** variant — the most common topology for cavitation tunnels. For free-surface CWC, an open top is added to the test section, and the contraction/diffuser pair is re-profiled to handle a free surface.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**
**"Define a Circulating Water Channel (CWC). State any two advantages of using a CWC over a conventional towing tank for marine model testing."**

**Model Answer:**
A **Circulating Water Channel** is a closed-loop hydraulic facility in which water is circulated at a precisely controlled velocity past a *stationary* test model installed in a transparent test section, enabling measurement of hydrodynamic forces, pressures, and flow phenomena.

**Advantages over a towing tank:**
1. The model remains stationary, allowing direct mounting of high-precision **multi-component force balances** without inertial effects of the carriage.
2. The flow is **steady and wave-free** in a pressurized CWC, providing a clean reference velocity and avoiding wave-making resistance contamination.
3. Cavitation, wake, and turbulent flow studies can be performed with **high repeatability** and at controlled cavitation numbers.

*(Any two well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**
**"Define the contraction ratio of a CWC. Why is a high contraction ratio (9:1 to 16:1) preferred?"**

**Model Answer:**
The **contraction ratio (CR)** of a CWC is defined as the ratio of the cross-sectional area at the inlet of the contraction to the area at the test section:

$$
CR = \dfrac{A_{in}}{A_{ts}}
$$

A high contraction ratio (typically **9:1 to 16:1**) is preferred because:
1. It produces a more **uniform velocity profile** at the test-section exit by evening out incoming flow non-uniformities.
2. It **reduces turbulence intensity** by an order of magnitude (since turbulence scales as $1/\sqrt{CR}$ for isotropic inlet turbulence).
3. It lowers the **background noise** in LDV/PIV measurements, improving signal quality.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a clear schematic, describe the **functional sub-systems of a Circulating Water Channel** and explain the role of each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **cavitation number** for a hydrofoil tested in a pressurized CWC, and explain how it is controlled experimentally. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

A typical CWC consists of **eight canonical sub-systems**, arranged in a closed hydraulic loop:

1. **Reservoir / Stilling Chamber:** Provides thermal mass and dampens pulsations from the pump.
2. **Pump Assembly:** One or more axial-flow pumps provide the necessary head and flow rate. Power $P = \rho g Q H / \eta_{pump}$.
3. **Discharge Manifold:** Distributes flow uniformly to the contraction.
4. **Settling Chamber with Honeycomb:** Honeycomb cells (aspect ratio 8–10) destroy large-scale swirl and eddies.
5. **Anti-turbulence Screens:** Two to four fine mesh screens reduce turbulence intensity by approximately a factor of 2 per screen.
6. **Contraction (Nozzle):** A 3-D profile that accelerates flow and ensures velocity uniformity; CR = 9:1 to 16:1.
7. **Test Section:** Transparent acrylic section where the model is mounted on a force balance or sting.
8. **Diffuser and Turning Vanes:** The diffuser (area ratio 3–4) recovers pressure (efficiency 0.6–0.8); turning vanes redirect flow through 90° corners with low loss.

The **role** of each sub-system is to progressively condition the flow until it is steady, uniform, low-turbulence, and at the correct velocity before reaching the model.

*(A neat labelled schematic fetches 2 marks; component roles 5 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Derivation:**

Apply Bernoulli between the test-section freestream and a local point on the hydrofoil suction surface (inviscid, incompressible):

$$
p_{local} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{local}^{2} = p_{\infty} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{ts}^{2}
$$

The local velocity is related to freestream via the local pressure coefficient:

$$
C_p^{local} = \dfrac{p_{local} - p_{\infty}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{ts}^{2}}
$$

Cavitation incepts when the local pressure falls to the vapor pressure $p_v$:

$$
p_{local} = p_v
$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{p_{\infty} - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{ts}^{2}} = C_p^{local} - 1
$$

**Experimental Control:**
The cavitation number is varied in the test section by either (i) changing the test-section velocity $V_{ts}$ via the pump speed, or (ii) changing the static pressure $p_{\infty}$ using a vacuum pump connected to the sealed test section. The vapor pressure $p_v$ is controlled by **water temperature** via a heat exchanger.

Cavitation inception corresponds to the critical value $\sigma_c = C_p^{local} - 1$; below this, vapor bubbles form.

*(Derivation: 4 marks; Experimental control discussion: 3 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the significance of **Reynolds number, Froude number, and cavitation number** in CWC testing of marine models. Why is the Froude number typically negligible in a pressurized CWC? (7 Marks)

**(b)** A CWC has a test section of cross-section **0.6 m × 0.6 m** and operates with water at **20 °C** (kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). The pump delivers a maximum flow rate of **$Q = 0.8$ m³/s**. Calculate:
- (i) The maximum test-section velocity.
- (ii) The maximum Reynolds number for a model of characteristic length $L = 0.3$ m.
- (iii) The required pump power if the total head is **$H = 4$ m** and pump efficiency is **75%** ($\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²). (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Reynolds number $Re_L$:** Governs viscous and inertial similarity; for a model-prototype match, $Re_L$ must equal that of the prototype (or be as high as possible within facility limits). It controls **boundary layer development, frictional resistance, and transition to turbulence**.

$$
Re_L = \dfrac{V_{ts} L}{\nu}
$$

**Froude number $Fr$:** Governs gravity and inertial effects; important when **free-surface phenomena** (waves) are present:

$$
Fr = \dfrac{V_{ts}}{\sqrt{g L}}
$$

**Cavitation number $\sigma$:** Governs the propensity for cavitation in liquid flows:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{p_{\infty} - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{ts}^{2}}
$$

**Why $Fr \approx 0$ in a pressurized CWC:** In a sealed (pressurized) CWC, there is no free surface. Therefore gravity-wave phenomena are entirely suppressed. Since $Fr$ characterizes free-surface gravity effects, its value is effectively **irrelevant** in the pressurized test section — making the CWC a "purely Reynolds-number-similar" facility for that section. Free-surface effects must be reproduced in a separate towing tank or by switching the CWC to free-surface mode.

*(2 marks per dimensionless number, 1 mark for the Froude discussion.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $A_{ts} = 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.36$ m², $Q = 0.8$ m³/s, $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $L = 0.3$ m, $H = 4$ m, $\eta = 0.75$, $\rho = 998$ kg/m³.

**(i) Maximum test-section velocity:**

$$
V_{ts} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{ts}} = \dfrac{0.8}{0.36} = 2.222 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Reynolds number:**

$$
Re_L = \dfrac{V_{ts} \cdot L}{\nu} = \dfrac{2.222 \times 0.3}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.64 \times 10^{5}
$$

**(iii) Required pump power:**

$$
P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump}} = \dfrac{998 \times 9.81 \times 0.8 \times 4}{0.75}
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
998 \times 9.81 = 9790.4 \text{ (W·s/m³)}
$$

$$
9790.4 \times 0.8 = 7832.3 \text{ W}
$$

$$
7832.3 \times 4 = 31329.3 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{31329.3}{0.75} = 41772.4 \text{ W} \approx 41.77 \text{ kW}
$$

*(i) 2 marks; (ii) 2 marks; (iii) 3 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Confusing test-section velocity with pump discharge velocity.** Velocity is calculated *at the test section*, not at the pump. Always use $V_{ts} = Q / A_{ts}$.
> - **Using kinematic viscosity of air (1.5 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s) by mistake.** The working fluid is *water* at the stated temperature — use $\nu_{water} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.
> - **Forgetting the pump efficiency** in the power formula. The formula is $P = \rho g Q H / \eta$, not just $\rho g Q H$. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> - **Mixing up the direction of blockage correction.** A positive blockage *increases* local velocity around the model, so corrected $C_D$ is *less* than measured $C_D$.
> - **Drawing the CWC schematic without flow-direction arrows.** Examiners award marks for a labelled closed-loop flow path; a CWC drawn without arrows is considered incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Circulating Water Channel (CWC)** is a closed-loop hydraulic facility that passes a controlled flow of water past a stationary model.
- The **eight sub-systems** are: Reservoir → Pump → Manifold → Settling chamber (honeycomb) → Screens → Contraction → **Test Section** → Diffuser → Turning vanes → back to Reservoir.
- The **contraction ratio (CR)** is the single most important geometric parameter; typical value **9–16**.
- The **blockage ratio (BR)** must be kept **≤ 5%** for reliable force measurements; Maskell-type corrections are applied when BR is higher.
- The **Reynolds number** $Re_L = V L / \nu$ is the primary similarity parameter in a pressurized CWC.
- The **Froude number** is effectively **zero** in a pressurized CWC (no free surface).
- The **cavitation number** $\sigma = (p_{\infty} - p_v) / (0.5 \rho V_{ts}^{2})$ is controlled by varying velocity and/or test-section static pressure.
- **Turbulence intensity** is reduced to **< 0.5%** using honeycomb and 2–4 screens.
- **Pump power** sizing: $P = \rho g Q H / \eta_{pump}$; typical efficiency 70–85%.
- **Diffuser pressure recovery** coefficient $C_p = 0.6$–$0.8$ in a well-designed CWC; area ratio 3–4.
- **Instrumentation** typically includes force balances, pressure transducers, LDV, PIV, pitot-static probes, hydrophones, and temperature sensors — all synchronized via a high-speed DAQ system.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme** questions expect: a labelled schematic, correct formula application, and clear numerical evaluation with units.
- A **closed-throat pressurized CWC** is used for cavitation studies; a **free-surface CWC** for wave-interaction studies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Tunnel and Instrumentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wind Tunnel and Instrumentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wind Tunnel — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wind Tunnel (Blower/Suction Type):** A ground-based, controlled experimental facility that artificially generates a steady, uniform, and measurable flow of air (or other working fluid) over a scaled model — typically a ship, propeller, aircraft, turbine, or bluff body — in order to **simulate, measure, and analyze the real-world fluid dynamic phenomena** (forces, pressures, moments, wake structures, and flow separation) that would otherwise occur on the full-scale prototype moving through the atmosphere or ocean.

In the context of **Marine Hydrodynamics (PENST865)**, wind tunnels are employed to investigate:
- Aerodynamic/hydrodynamic forces on superstructures of ships
- Wind loads on offshore platforms and FPSOs
- Sailing vessel aerodynamics (sail forces, heeling moments)
- Boundary layer development over streamlined bodies
- Wake surveys behind bluff and streamlined bodies
- Propeller slipstream and rotor aerodynamics

### 1.2 The Three Governing Similitude Parameters (Why Tunnels Exist at All)

A wind tunnel is not just a "fan with a box" — it is a **similitude engine**. The governing non-dimensional groups are:

| Parameter | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces |
| **Mach Number** | $M = \dfrac{V}{a}$ | Ratio of flow velocity to local speed of sound |
| **Froude Number** (marine) | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Ratio of inertial to gravitational forces |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why scale a model?** Because full-scale testing of a 300 m ship in a hurricane is impossible, dangerous, and uncontrollable. The tunnel allows us to replicate the *non-dimensional* physics of that scenario in a 1–3 m model at controlled conditions.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The "Blow-Dryer vs. The Real World"**
> Imagine you are testing a small toy ship in your bathroom. You point your **hair dryer** at it and measure how strongly it leans. The hair dryer is your "wind tunnel" — a controlled wind source. Now imagine doing the same test on a real ship during a storm. The physics is identical: wind pushes the hull, the hull tilts, the sails (if any) generate lift. The wind tunnel simply lets you do this on a tabletop with **measurement instruments** instead of a multimillion-dollar vessel in the North Sea.
> 
> A wind tunnel is essentially: **Fan → Straightener → Nozzle → Test Section → Diffuser → Back to Fan**, with the model placed in the test section.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Wind Tunnel Engineering

- **Test Section Velocity:** typically **0.1 to 100 m/s** (subsonic), up to **Mach 5+** (supersonic).
- **Turbulence Intensity (Tu):** $Tu = \dfrac{u'_{rms}}{\bar{U}} \times 100\%$ — must be **< 0.1%** in high-quality tunnels.
- **Blockage Ratio:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{A_{model}}{A_{test}}$ — must be **< 5–10%** to avoid wall interference.
- **Contraction Ratio:** $CR = \dfrac{A_{settling}}{A_{test}}$ — typically **9 : 1 to 25 : 1**.
- **Dynamic Pressure:** $q = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$ in **Pa** or **N/m²**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Wind Tunnels

Wind tunnels are classified along **four independent axes**:

#### (A) By Circuit Topology

- **Open-Circuit (Blower-Type / Non-Return):** Air is drawn from the room, accelerated through the contraction, passes the test section, and exhausted back into the room. Simple, cheap, but sensitive to room air disturbances.
- **Closed-Circuit (Return-Type):** Air circulates in a closed loop. Provides better **flow quality**, temperature stability, and **lower background turbulence**. Standard in research-grade marine hydro labs.

#### (B) By Speed Regime

| Class | Mach Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Subsonic** | $M \le 0.3$ | Ship aerodynamics, boundary layers, low-speed bluff bodies |
| **Transonic** | $0.3 \le M \le 1.2$ | Aircraft wings near critical Mach |
| **Supersonic** | $1.2 \le M \le 5.0$ | Missiles, jet intakes |
| **Hypersonic** | $M \ge 5.0$ | Re-entry vehicles, scramjets |

> For **Marine Hydrodynamics**, virtually all experiments are conducted in the **subsonic, incompressible regime**, where $\rho = \text{const}$ and the continuity/momentum equations reduce to their incompressible form.

#### (C) By Test Section Geometry

- **Open Test Section** (free jet) — used when wall interference must be eliminated.
- **Closed Test Section** (ducted/walled) — better for force balances and boundary layer studies.
- **Closed-Circuit Open Test Section** (Göttingen type) — combines recirculation with free jet.

#### (D) By Driving Mechanism

- **Blower/Fan driven** (continuous flow).
- **Induction (suction) type** — atmospheric pressure drives flow through nozzle.

### 2.2 Functional Components of a Closed-Circuit Subsonic Wind Tunnel

The architecture is non-negotiable. Every KTU examiner expects you to **name, locate, and justify** these elements:

1. **Settling Chamber (Stillness Chamber):** Houses the flow-conditioning hardware. Reduces incoming turbulence.
2. **Honeycomb:** A bundle of hexagonal/circular cells. Straightens flow and removes swirl.
3. **Screens (Mesh Filters):** Multiple fine wire meshes. Reduce **turbulence intensity** by up to an order of magnitude.
4. **Contraction (Nozzle):** A converging profile that **accelerates** the flow and produces a near-uniform velocity profile at the test section inlet. Designed to **avoid flow separation** inside the nozzle.
5. **Test Section:** The **measurement volume**. Must have uniform $V$, $\rho$, $P$.
6. **Diffuser:** A diverging duct that **decelerates** the flow and recovers static pressure (this pressure rise *drives* the flow back to the fan with less pump work — Bernoulli's principle exploited for energy efficiency).
7. **Corner Turning Vanes (Turning Section):** Redirect flow with minimal losses.
8. **Drive Section (Fan/Blower):** Provides the pressure rise $\Delta P_{fan}$.
9. **Cooling/Heat Exchanger:** Dissipates the fan's mechanical work converted to heat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Balance Insight (KTU-favourite question):** The fan must overcome only the *minor* (turning, screen) and *friction* losses plus provide the dynamic head of the test section — *not* the full stagnation pressure. This is why **diffusers are mandatory**: they convert kinetic energy back to static pressure, dramatically reducing fan power requirements.

### 2.3 Contraction Nozzle Design

The contraction must be a **smooth, bell-shaped curve** to prevent boundary layer separation inside the nozzle. The classical design is based on a **5th-order polynomial** (Bell-Nozzle) or a **Witoszynski/Witozynski profile**:

$$\frac{R(x)}{R_{1}} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{CR}\right)\left[\frac{(1-x/L)^{2}(1+\alpha x/L)}{(1+\alpha/3)^{3}}\right]$$

where $CR = (R_1/R_2)^2$ is the contraction ratio, $R_1$ is inlet radius, $R_2$ is throat (test section) radius, $L$ is contraction length.

**Turbulence reduction by the contraction alone** (Batchelor / Modi–Fernando relation):

$$\frac{Tu_{out}}{Tu_{in}} \approx \frac{1}{CR}$$

A contraction ratio of **9:1** reduces turbulence intensity by a factor of **3**; a ratio of **25:1** reduces it by **5**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every numerical in KTU ESE papers.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meanings | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho}}$ | $V$: velocity, $\Delta P$: dynamic pressure, $\rho$: density | m/s, Pa, kg/m³ | Pitot-static tube |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | $L$: characteristic length, $\nu$: kinematic viscosity | – | Flow regime |
| 3 | $q = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$ | $q$: dynamic pressure | Pa | Aerodynamic loading |
| 4 | $Tu = \dfrac{u'_{rms}}{\bar{U}} \times 100$ | $u'$: fluctuating component, $\bar{U}$: mean | % | Flow quality |
| 5 | $E^{2} = A + B \cdot U^{n}$ | $E$: hot-wire voltage, $U$: flow speed, $A,B,n$: calibration constants | V², m/s | Hot-wire anemometry (King's Law) |
| 6 | $\Delta P_{fan} = K \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{test}^{2} + \Delta P_{losses}$ | $K$: total loss coefficient | Pa | Fan power sizing |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{A_{model}}{A_{test}}$ | $A_{model}$: frontal area, $A_{test}$: test-section area | – | Blockage ratio |
| 8 | $\Delta C_D = -C_D \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}$ | – | – | Blockage correction (2D, Maskell) |
| 9 | $C_F = \dfrac{F}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A}$ | $C_F$: force coefficient, $A$: reference area | – | Non-dimensional force |
| 10 | $P_{fan} = \dot{m} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P_{fan}}{\rho}$ | $\dot{m}$: mass flow rate | W | Power required |
| 11 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_{test} \cdot V_{test}$ | mass flow rate | kg/s | Mass conservation |
| 12 | $\nu_{air} \approx 1.46 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$ at **15 °C** | – | m²/s | Standard air property |

> **Critical LaTeX Note:** Vertical bars (for absolute values) inside markdown tables are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table parser. Example: $Re = \rho V L / \mu = V L / \nu$ (no pipes).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship Aerodynamics:** Wind loads on container ship hulls (above-water part) affect course-keeping and broaching. Wind tunnel tests at model scale (1:100 to 1:300) provide $C_X, C_Y, C_N$ coefficients for maneuvering simulations.
- **Sail Force Prediction:** America's Cup and Volvo Ocean Race teams use wind tunnels to optimize sail camber, twist, and draft position.
- **Offshore Wind Turbines:** NACA airfoil sections are tested at low Re to validate BEM (Blade Element Momentum) theory.
- **Aircraft/Hybrid Vehicles:** Marine hydrodynamics research overlaps with low-Re aerodynamics for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) used in maritime surveillance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Velocity from a Pitot-Static Tube (Bernoulli's Principle)

A **Pitot-static tube** is the most fundamental velocity probe in a wind tunnel. It has two ports:
- **Stagnation port (impact pressure)** — faces the flow, measures $P_0$.
- **Static port (sidewall)** — perpendicular to flow, measures $P_\infty$.

Applying Bernoulli along a streamline from far upstream (point 1) to the stagnation point (point 2):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$

**Step 1:** Assume horizontal flow ($z_1 = z_2$) and incompressible flow ($\rho$ = constant).

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$

**Step 2:** At the stagnation point, the fluid is brought to rest, so $V_2 = 0$. The static pressure at the stagnation port equals the local total (stagnation) pressure: $P_2 = P_0$.

**Step 3:** The static port measures the *static* pressure of the undisturbed flow: $P_1 = P_\infty$. The far-upstream velocity is the freestream velocity: $V_1 = V_\infty$.

$$P_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = P_0 + 0$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to solve for $V_\infty$:

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = P_0 - P_\infty = \Delta P$$

$$V_\infty = \sqrt{\frac{2\Delta P}{\rho}}$$

> **Result (boxed master equation):**
> $$\boxed{\,V_\infty = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,(P_0 - P_\infty)}{\rho}}\,}$$

> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Students often forget the factor of 2 or confuse $P_0$ with $P_\infty$. This single derivation is worth 4–5 marks and is asked in almost every December and June paper.

### 3.2 Derivation: Fan Power Required for a Closed-Circuit Tunnel

**Step 1:** The fan must supply the dynamic head of the test section plus all losses along the circuit.

$$\Delta P_{fan} = \Delta P_{losses} + \Delta P_{test} = \sum_{i} K_i \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_i^2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{test}^2$$

**Step 2:** The total loss coefficient is the sum of friction, screens, honeycomb, turning, and contraction/diffuser losses. For a typical well-designed tunnel, $\sum K_i \approx 1.0$ to $1.5$.

**Step 3:** Mass flow rate is conserved through the incompressible circuit:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_{test} \cdot V_{test}$$

**Step 4:** Fan power (assuming 100% efficiency — for real fans, divide by $\eta_{fan} \approx 0.6$ to $0.85$):

$$P_{fan} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{\Delta P_{fan}}{\rho}$$

**Substitute:**

$$P_{fan} = \left(\rho A_{test} V_{test}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho}\left(\sum K_i \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_i^2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{test}^2\right)$$

**Result (boxed):**

$$\boxed{\,P_{fan} = A_{test}\,V_{test}\left(\sum K_i \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_i^2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{test}^2\right)\,}$$

In SI units: $[\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{m/s} \cdot \text{Pa}] = \text{W}$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Hot-Wire Anemometer — King's Law Calibration

A hot-wire probe is a thin tungsten wire (≈ 5 µm diameter) heated by an electric current. As fluid flows past, it cools the wire, changing its resistance and the voltage across it.

**Step 1:** Heat loss from the wire by forced convection follows:

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_\infty)$$

where $h$ is the convective heat-transfer coefficient.

**Step 2:** Empirical correlation: For a thin wire, $h \propto U^{n}$ where $n \approx 0.45$ to $0.5$ for air.

**Step 3:** Electrical heat input: $Q = V^2 / R_w$ (Joule heating).

**Step 4:** Equate heat loss and heat input at steady state:

$$\frac{V^2}{R_w} \propto (A + B\,U^{n})$$

$$\boxed{\,E^2 = A + B \cdot U^{n}\,}$$

> Where $E$ is the output voltage, $U$ is the local flow speed, and $A, B, n$ are determined experimentally by **calibrating** against a known flow (e.g., a calibrated nozzle).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU-style, 7-mark pattern)

> **Problem:** A subsonic closed-circuit wind tunnel has a test section of cross-section **0.6 m × 0.6 m**. The air is at standard conditions ($\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$). The fan delivers a pressure rise $\Delta P_{fan} = 800\,\text{Pa}$. Assuming the total loss coefficient $\sum K_i = 1.0$ and neglecting the dynamic head of the test section as a first approximation, calculate:
> 
> (a) The test section velocity.
> (b) The Reynolds number based on the test section width.
> (c) The fan power required.
> (d) The dynamic pressure $q$ in the test section.

**Solution:**

**(a) Test Section Velocity**

Apply the energy equation across the circuit:

$$\Delta P_{fan} = \sum K_i \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$$

$$800 = 1.0 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot V^2$$

$$V^2 = \frac{2 \cdot 800}{1.225} = \frac{1600}{1.225} \approx 1306.12$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 36.14\ \text{m/s}}$$

**(b) Reynolds Number**

$$Re = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu} = \frac{36.14 \times 0.6}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{Re \approx 1.485 \times 10^{6}}$$

**(c) Fan Power**

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V = 1.225 \times (0.6 \times 0.6) \times 36.14 = 1.225 \times 0.36 \times 36.14$$

$$\dot{m} = 1.225 \times 13.01 \approx 15.94\ \text{kg/s}$$

$$P_{fan} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{\Delta P_{fan}}{\rho} = 15.94 \times \frac{800}{1.225}$$

$$\boxed{P_{fan} \approx 10{,}410\ \text{W} \approx 10.4\ \text{kW}}$$

**(d) Dynamic Pressure**

$$q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 1306.12$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 800\ \text{Pa} \quad (\text{as expected from energy balance})}$$

### 3.5 Blockage Correction (Maskell's Formula, 2D)

If a model occupies more than **5%** of the test section, the solid blockage distorts the streamlines. For a 2D model with frontal area $A_m$ in a test section of width $W$ and height $H$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{A_m}{W \cdot H}$$

The corrected drag coefficient:

$$\boxed{\,C_{D,corrected} = C_{D,measured}\left(1 - \varepsilon\right)^{-2}\,}$$

For small $\varepsilon$, this Taylor-expands to:

$$C_{D,corrected} \approx C_{D,measured}\left(1 + 2\varepsilon + 3\varepsilon^{2} + \ldots\right)$$

For a 10% blockage, the correction is ≈ 21%. This is non-negligible in KTU-level experimental work.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Closed-Circuit Subsonic Wind Tunnel

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interaction of every major subsystem** in a research-grade wind tunnel. This is the type of figure KTU examiners expect when they ask "With a neat sketch, explain the working of a wind tunnel."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> B[Settling Chamber]
    B --> C[Honeycomb Flow Straightener]
    C --> D[Anti-Turbulence Screens 1 and 2]
    D --> E[Contraction Nozzle 9 to 1 ratio]
    E --> F[Test Section with Model and Force Balance]
    F --> G[Diffuser Pressure Recovery]
    G --> H[Corner Turning Vanes 4 vanes]
    H --> I[Axial Flow Fan with Variable Speed Drive]
    I --> J[Heat Exchanger and Cooling Coil]
    J --> K[Silencer and Acoustic Lining]
    K --> B
    
    L[Pitot Static Probe at Test Section Inlet] --> M[Pressure Transducer and Data Acquisition]
    F --> M
    N[Hot Wire or LDA Probe] --> M
    M --> O[PC with LabVIEW or MATLAB DAQ]
    
    P[Boundary Layer Rake] --> M
    Q[Wake Survey Probe Traverse] --> M
    
    R[Flow Visualization Smoke Generator] --> F
    S[High Speed Camera] --> F

    classDef main fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef measure fill:#b45309,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef visual fill:#166534,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef flowc fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K main
    class L,M,N,O,P,Q measure
    class R,S visual
    class A flowc
```

> **Reading Guide:**
> - **Blue blocks** — Primary flow path (the closed-circuit loop).
> - **Orange blocks** — Instrumentation and data acquisition chain.
> - **Green blocks** — Flow visualization subsystem.
> - **Red-orange block** — Air intake boundary.
> 
> The arrows show **directional flow of air** and **directional flow of data**. The loop is closed, so the same air parcel circulates continuously — this is why such tunnels can achieve $Tu < 0.05\%$.

### 4.2 Schematic: Pitot-Static Tube Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Freestream Flow U infinity] --> B[Stagnation Port A]
    A --> C[Static Port B at 90 degrees]
    B --> D[Manometer or Differential Pressure Transducer]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Delta P equals half rho U squared}
    E --> F[Compute U from Pitot Equation]
    
    G[Stagnation Pressure P0] --> D
    H[Static Pressure P infinity] --> D
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Six-Component Force Balance (Internal Strain-Gauge Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Model Under Test] --> S[Sting and Balance Adapter]
    S --> FX[Strain Gauge Fx Drag]
    S --> FY[Strain Gauge Fy Lift]
    S --> FZ[Strain Gauge Fz Side]
    S --> MX[Moment Gauge Pitch]
    S --> MY[Moment Gauge Yaw]
    S --> MZ[Moment Gauge Roll]
    FX --> DAQ[Bridge Amplifier and DAQ]
    FY --> DAQ
    FZ --> DAQ
    MX --> DAQ
    MY --> DAQ
    MZ --> DAQ
    DAQ --> PC[PC with Calibration Matrix]
```

> **Operational note:** Each "gauge" is actually a **Wheatstone bridge** of four strain gauges arranged in a flexure element. The calibration matrix maps the six raw voltages $(V_1,\ldots,V_6)$ to the six physical loads $(F_x,F_y,F_z,M_x,M_y,M_z)$ via an inverse stiffness matrix. **Never** couple a balance to a stiff sting or you'll bias the readings.

### 4.4 Diagram: Blockage Phenomenon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parallel Uniform Streamlines far upstream] --> B[Approaching Test Section]
    B --> C[Model Occupies Blockage Area Am]
    C --> D[Streamlines Compress and Curve]
    D --> E[Apparent Velocity Increases Inside Test Section]
    E --> F[Apparent Drag Rises above True Value]
    F --> G[Apply Maskell Correction Factor]
    G --> H[True Drag Coefficient Recovered]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Differentiate between open-circuit and closed-circuit wind tunnels. List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| Feature | Open-Circuit (Non-Return) | Closed-Circuit (Return) |
|---|---|---|
| Airflow path | Single pass; intake → exhaust | Recirculating loop |
| Cost | Lower initial cost | Higher initial cost |
| Turbulence intensity | Higher (Tu ~ 0.5–1%) | Very low (Tu < 0.1%) |
| Temperature stability | Poor — cools test section | Excellent — thermal equilibrium |
| Background noise | Lower (no recirculation) | Higher (fan & turning vanes) |
| Space requirement | Compact footprint | Requires long return loop |

> *Advantage of open:* simple, cheap, easy to install. *Advantage of closed:* superior flow quality, repeatable tests.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper Style]**

**Q2. What is meant by "turbulence intensity" in a wind tunnel? State the typical acceptable value for low-speed aeronautical testing.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

Turbulence intensity $Tu$ is the ratio of the root-mean-square of the fluctuating velocity component to the mean velocity, expressed as a percentage:

$$Tu = \frac{u'_{rms}}{\bar{U}} \times 100\%$$

It quantifies the **unwanted unsteady fluctuations** superimposed on the mean flow. For high-quality subsonic aeronautical and marine aerodynamic testing, the acceptable level is:

$$Tu \le 0.1\%$$

For boundary layer research and laminar-flow studies, $Tu \le 0.05\%$ is required. A typical method to reduce $Tu$ is to install **multiple fine-mesh screens** and a high contraction ratio (≥ 9:1) in the upstream settling section.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Choice A]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional components of a closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel**. Explain the role of the **contraction nozzle** and **diffuser** in ensuring high flow quality and energy efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Pitot-static tube placed in a wind tunnel test section reads a stagnation pressure $P_0 = 102{,}350\,\text{Pa}$ and a static pressure $P_\infty = 101{,}325\,\text{Pa}$. The air density is $\rho = 1.20\,\text{kg/m}^3$. Compute the **test section velocity**, the **dynamic pressure**, and the **Reynolds number** based on a chord length $c = 0.30\,\text{m}$ and kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

> **Block Diagram** — Refer to Section 4.1 above (the Mermaid diagram).

The closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel consists of the following functional subsystems arranged in a closed loop:

1. **Drive Section (Axial-Flow Fan with Variable Frequency Drive):** Provides the pressure rise $\Delta P_{fan}$ that circulates the air. Speed is varied to set test-section velocity.

2. **Settling Chamber:** A large plenum upstream of the contraction. Houses flow-conditioning hardware (honeycomb, screens). Reduces freestream turbulence.

3. **Honeycomb:** An array of hexagonal cells (cell size ≈ 5–10 mm, length ≈ 8 diameters). Eliminates **swirl and large-scale eddies** by viscous dissipation. Reduces lateral velocity fluctuations.

4. **Anti-Turbulence Screens:** Multiple layers of fine wire mesh (16–60 mesh). Each screen attenuates turbulence by a factor of 2–5. The combination can reduce $Tu$ by an order of magnitude.

5. **Contraction Nozzle:** A bell-shaped converging duct. Its **four primary functions** are:
   - Accelerates flow from low settling-chamber velocity to high test-section velocity.
   - Produces a **uniform, parallel, low-turbulence** velocity profile at the test-section inlet.
   - Reduces turbulence intensity by a factor equal to the contraction ratio (Batchelor): $Tu_{out}/Tu_{in} \approx 1/CR$.
   - Converts pressure to kinetic energy *without* boundary layer separation (provided the wall curvature is gentle).

6. **Test Section:** The measurement volume. Houses the model, force balance, and probes. Must maintain uniform $V$, $\rho$, $P$ to within 0.5%.

7. **Diffuser:** A diverging duct downstream of the test section. Performs two **energy-recovery** functions:
   - Decelerates the flow, recovering static pressure.
   - Reduces the **kinetic-energy load** the fan must supply, dramatically lowering fan power.
   
   The diffuser pressure rise can be 70–90% of the test-section dynamic pressure in a well-designed subsonic diffuser (area ratio ≤ 4:1 to prevent separation).

8. **Corner Turning Vanes and Silencer:** Redirect flow through 90° turns with minimal loss; acoustic lining keeps noise below 85 dB.

9. **Cooling Coil:** Removes the heat deposited by the fan's mechanical work (≈ 1°C rise per pass for a 100 kW fan).

**Mark allocation guidance (KTU pattern):**
- Diagram with 6+ labeled components — 3 marks
- Role of contraction — 2 marks
- Role of diffuser — 2 marks

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $P_0 = 102{,}350\,\text{Pa}$
- $P_\infty = 101{,}325\,\text{Pa}$
- $\rho = 1.20\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $c = 0.30\,\text{m}$
- $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Velocity from Pitot-static equation:**

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P_\infty)}{\rho}}$$

$$\Delta P = 102{,}350 - 101{,}325 = 1025\,\text{Pa}$$

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1025}{1.20}} = \sqrt{\frac{2050}{1.20}} = \sqrt{1708.33}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 41.33\ \text{m/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic Pressure:**

$$q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.20 \times (41.33)^2$$

$$q = 0.6 \times 1708.33 = 1025\,\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{q = 1025\,\text{Pa} \ (\text{as expected: } q = \Delta P)}$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds Number:**

$$Re = \frac{V \cdot c}{\nu} = \frac{41.33 \times 0.30}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{12.40}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{Re \approx 8.27 \times 10^{5}}$$

> This corresponds to **turbulent flow** over a typical airfoil chord at moderate Re.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Choice B]**

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **hot-wire anemometer**. State **King's Law** and sketch the typical $E$–$U$ calibration curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A model of a ship's superstructure has a frontal area $A_m = 0.018\,\text{m}^2$ and is tested in a test section of cross-section **0.5 m × 0.5 m** at $V = 30\,\text{m/s}$, $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$. The measured drag force is $F_D = 6.5\,\text{N}$. Compute the **blockage ratio**, the **uncorrected drag coefficient**, and the **Maskell-corrected drag coefficient**. Comment on whether the test requires wall correction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

> **Working Principle of Hot-Wire Anemometer (HWA):**
> 
> A hot-wire anemometer consists of a **tungsten or platinum wire** of diameter ≈ 5 µm and length ≈ 1–2 mm, mounted on a probe support. The wire is heated by an electrical current (typically via a **constant-temperature anemometer, CTA**, or constant-current, CCA, feedback circuit).
> 
> When fluid flows past the wire, **forced convection** removes heat at a rate proportional to the local flow speed. The wire's resistance (and hence the voltage drop) changes with temperature. The feedback circuit adjusts the current to keep the wire at constant temperature (CTA mode), and the **compensating voltage $E$** is a monotonic function of the local flow speed $U$.
> 
> The empirical relation is **King's Law:**

$$\boxed{\,E^2 = A + B \cdot U^{n}\,}$$

> where $A, B, n$ are constants determined by **calibration** in a known flow (e.g., a subsonic nozzle or rotating-arm calibrator). Typically for air, $n \approx 0.45$ to $0.5$.
> 
> **Calibration Curve (qualitative description):**
> 
> Plot $E$ (ordinate, volts) vs $U$ (abscissa, m/s). The curve is **monotonically increasing** and **concave-down** (decreasing slope) because $E \propto U^{n/2}$ with $n/2 < 1$.
> 
> **Advantages of HWA:**
> - Extremely high frequency response (up to 100 kHz) — captures turbulence.
> - Small spatial resolution (~ mm).
> - Direct measurement of instantaneous $u(t)$.
> 
> **Disadvantages:**
> - Requires individual calibration.
> - Fragile (wire breaks easily).
> - Sensitive to temperature drift.
> - Not suitable for supersonic flows (heat transfer regime changes).
> 
> **Calibration Procedure:** Place the probe in a **calibrated laminar free-jet** at known $U$, record $E$ over a range of $U$ (typically 1 to 50 m/s), and fit a least-squares regression to King's Law to extract $A, B, n$.

**Mark allocation (KTU pattern):**
- Operating principle + circuit — 3 marks
- King's Law equation — 1 mark
- Calibration curve sketch + discussion — 2 marks
- Advantages/disadvantages — 1 mark

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $A_m = 0.018\,\text{m}^2$
- Test section: $0.5 \times 0.5\,\text{m}$ $\Rightarrow A_{test} = 0.25\,\text{m}^2$
- $V = 30\,\text{m/s}$
- $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $F_D = 6.5\,\text{N}$

**Step 1 — Blockage Ratio:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{A_m}{A_{test}} = \frac{0.018}{0.25} = 0.072$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 7.2\%}$$

**Step 2 — Uncorrected Drag Coefficient:**

$$C_{D,meas} = \frac{F_D}{q \cdot A_m} = \frac{F_D}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \cdot A_m}$$

$$q = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (30)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900 = 551.25\,\text{Pa}$$

$$C_{D,meas} = \frac{6.5}{551.25 \times 0.018} = \frac{6.5}{9.9225}$$

$$\boxed{C_{D,meas} \approx 0.655}$$

**Step 3 — Maskell-Corrected Drag Coefficient (2D approximation):**

$$C_{D,corr} = C_{D,meas}(1 - \varepsilon)^{-2}$$

$$(1 - \varepsilon)^{-2} = (1 - 0.072)^{-2} = (0.928)^{-2} \approx 1.1611$$

$$C_{D,corr} = 0.655 \times 1.1611$$

$$\boxed{C_{D,corr} \approx 0.761}$$

**Step 4 — Discussion:**

- The blockage ratio is **7.2%**, which exceeds the commonly accepted **5%** threshold.
- The Maskell correction increases $C_D$ by **~16%**, which is non-negligible.
- The test **does require wall correction**, or alternatively, a smaller model (frontal area $\le 0.0125\,\text{m}^2$ for $\varepsilon \le 5\%$) should be used.
- Alternatively, an **open-jet test section** could be employed to eliminate wall interference entirely.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $P_0$ and $P_\infty$** in the Pitot-static equation: many students write $V = \sqrt{2P_0/\rho}$ — this is **wrong by definition**. Always use $\Delta P = P_0 - P_\infty$.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert** $cm^2$ to $m^2$ when computing areas — leads to errors of $10^4$.
> 3. **Mixing up King's Law constants** — $A$, $B$, $n$ are *empirical*, not universal. They depend on wire diameter, fluid, and temperature.
> 4. **Ignoring blockage correction** — KTU explicitly tests this; an uncorrected $C_D$ in a high-blockage test loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **In the block diagram**, students often forget the **diffuser** or the **heat exchanger** — both are mandatory components of a closed-circuit tunnel and worth marks each.
> 6. **Numerical sloppiness**: rounding intermediate values loses accuracy; KTU expects answers to **3 significant figures** minimum.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Wind tunnel** = controlled-flow facility for **similitude testing** of aerodynamic/hydrodynamic models.
- [ ] Three governing parameters: **$Re$**, **$M$**, **$Fr$** (Froude in marine).
- [ ] **Closed-circuit** = better flow quality ($Tu < 0.1\%$), higher cost.
- [ ] **Open-circuit** = simpler, cheaper, higher $Tu$ ($0.5$–$1\%$).
- [ ] **Marine applications** = subsonic, incompressible ($M < 0.3$).
- [ ] **Tunnel components (in flow order):** Fan → Settling → Honeycomb → Screens → Contraction → **Test Section** → Diffuser → Turning vanes → (back to fan).
- [ ] **Contraction** = accelerates flow, **reduces $Tu$ by factor $1/CR$**, must be bell-shaped to avoid separation.
- [ ] **Diffuser** = decelerates flow, **recovers static pressure**, reduces fan power.
- [ ] **$CR$** typically $9{:}1$ to $25{:}1$.
- [ ] **Pitot-static equation:** $V = \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$.
- [ ] **Dynamic pressure:** $q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$.
- [ ] **Reynolds number:** $Re = VL/\nu$.
- [ ] **Turbulence intensity:** $Tu = u'_{rms}/\bar{U} \times 100\%$, target $\le 0.1\%$.
- [ ] **Blockage ratio:** $\varepsilon = A_{model}/A_{test}$, target $\le 5\%$.
- [ ] **Maskell correction (2D):** $C_{D,corr} = C_{D,meas}(1 - \varepsilon)^{-2}$.
- [ ] **King's Law (HWA):** $E^2 = A + B \cdot U^{n}$, $n \approx 0.45$–$0.5$ for air.
- [ ] **Force balance** = 6-component strain-gauge Wheatstone bridge arrangement.
- [ ] **HWA frequency response** up to **100 kHz** — captures turbulence.
- [ ] **$\nu_{air}(15°\text{C}) = 1.46 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$** — remember this constant.
- [ ] **$\rho_{air}(15°\text{C}) = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$** — remember this constant.
- [ ] **Fan power formula:** $P = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta P_{fan} / \rho$.
- [ ] **Energy balance** — fan supplies *only the losses + test-section dynamic head*, not the full stagnation.
- [ ] **Wall correction** is mandatory if $\varepsilon > 5\%$.
- [ ] **Open test section** eliminates wall interference but complicates balance integration.
- [ ] **Calibration of HWA** is done in a known laminar free-jet.
- [ ] **Tunnel flow quality** = $Tu$ + velocity uniformity + flow angularity.
- [ ] **Honeycomb cells** remove **swirl**; **screens** reduce **turbulence**.
- [ ] **In every KTU numerical**, always end with **units** and **3 significant figures**.
- [ ] **Block diagram** question always carries 3 marks for *neatness* and *labels* — practice drawing it before the exam.

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Wind Tunnel and Instrumentation**
> *PENST865 — Experimental Techniques in Marine Hydrodynamics | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Cavitation Tunnel and Instrumentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cavitation Tunnel and Instrumentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cavitation Tunnel** (also called a **Cavitation Channel** or **Cavitation Water Tunnel**) is a specialized closed-loop hydrodynamic test facility engineered to investigate cavitation phenomena on submerged bodies such as model propellers, hydrofoils, rudders, and submarine appendages under strictly controlled laboratory conditions. It is the marine hydrodynamicist's most powerful experimental instrument for visualizing and quantifying the inception, development, and collapse of vapor cavities that occur when local static pressure falls below the liquid's vapor pressure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a cavitation tunnel is classified as a **Variable-Pressure Recirculating Water Tunnel (VPRWT)**, in which the test-section velocity, static pressure, and water temperature can be independently regulated to simulate the cavitation number field experienced by full-scale marine propulsors at their design operating points.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The cavitation number is the fundamental non-dimensional parameter governing all cavitation-tunnel experiments. The entire facility is engineered around producing, measuring, and controlling this number.

The governing non-dimensional parameter is the **Cavitation Number** $\sigma$:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2}
$$

where:
- $P_{\infty}$ = free-stream static pressure at the test section
- $P_v$ = vapor pressure of water (temperature-dependent)
- $\rho$ = density of water
- $V_{\infty}$ = free-stream velocity at the test section

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain-English Analogy — "The Mountain Pass and the Boiling Pot":**

Imagine water flowing rapidly through a wide river that suddenly narrows at a mountain pass. As the water accelerates through the narrow gorge, its pressure drops dramatically (this is exactly what an airplane wing does to air). If the pressure drops low enough — below the pressure at which water "wants" to boil at the local temperature — tiny vapor bubbles spontaneously form in the liquid. This is cavitation. The bubbles are *not* air; they are water that has flashed into vapor.

Now imagine you are an engineer who needs to study this phenomenon safely and repeatably inside a laboratory. You obviously cannot build a mountain pass in your building. So you build a closed loop of large-diameter pipe, install a powerful pump to drive the water around, install a contraction nozzle to accelerate it (your "mountain pass"), and install a vacuum pump to lower the absolute pressure inside the loop. You place a model propeller or hydrofoil inside a glass-windowed test section and you can now:
- **See** the bubbles form (through the windows)
- **Measure** the pressures that caused them
- **Change** the speed, pressure, and water temperature to simulate different ship speeds and depths

**That entire loop is the cavitation tunnel. The glass-windowed "narrowing" is the test section.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not test in a real ship or real ocean?** Because (1) you cannot see inside, (2) you cannot change conditions, (3) you cannot isolate the cavitation parameter from other effects, and (4) the cost of failure is catastrophic. A cavitation tunnel gives you a controllable, observable, repeatable, and safe laboratory environment for studying a violent, erosive, and ship-killing phenomenon.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Engineering Standards

The standard reference values used in KTU board problems and international practice:

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Water density (fresh, 20°C) | $\rho$ | **998 kg/m³** |
| Vapor pressure of water (20°C) | $P_v$ | **2.34 kPa (absolute)** |
| Vapor pressure of water (15°C) | $P_v$ | **1.70 kPa (absolute)** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa (absolute)** |
| Cavitation number (ship propeller, design) | $\sigma$ | **0.6 – 1.2** |
| Cavitation number (incipient) | $\sigma_i$ | **~ 0.3 – 0.4** |
| Typical test-section velocity | $V_{\infty}$ | **2 – 15 m/s** |
| Typical test-section size | $A_{ts}$ | **0.3 m × 0.3 m to 1.5 m × 1.5 m** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because $P_v$ depends strongly on temperature, every KTU board numerical problem that gives you a temperature is *expecting* you to look up or be given the correct $P_v$. Students routinely lose marks by using $P_v = 0$ (an absolute vacuum assumption that is only valid for very high-velocity flows).
