# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Cavitation Tunnel — The Five Logical Zones

A modern cavitation tunnel is engineered as a sequence of five distinct hydraulic zones. Each zone has a single, well-defined purpose, and the design choices in each zone directly determine the quality of the cavitation experiment.

### Zone 1 — The Settling Chamber and Flow Conditioning
**Purpose:** To deliver a flow to the test section that is uniform, low-turbulence, and free of swirl.

**Components:**
- **Honeycomb** — a bundle of small hexagonal or square tubes that break up large-scale vortices and swirl by viscous dissipation.
- **Flow straighteners (screens / perforated plates)** — fine wire meshes that further reduce the turbulent intensity $Tu$ to typically less than **0.5% – 2%**.
- **Antivortex baffles** — to prevent residual angular momentum.

**Why it matters:** Cavitation inception is highly sensitive to free-stream turbulence. Even small turbulent fluctuations ($Tu > 2\%$) can artificially lower $\sigma_i$ (the incipient cavitation number) and give misleading results. A properly designed settling chamber will give a **turbulence intensity $Tu < 0.5\%$** at the test-section inlet.

### Zone 2 — The Contraction Nozzle
**Purpose:** To accelerate the flow smoothly to the test-section velocity $V_{\infty}$ while keeping the velocity profile uniform and the streamlines parallel.

**Engineering detail:** The contraction is designed with a **fifth-order polynomial or a Witozinsky profile** to give a uniform exit velocity profile with a contraction ratio (area of inlet / area of test section) typically between **6 : 1 and 9 : 1**. The minimum pressure recovery is dictated by the **vena contracta** at the nozzle exit.

### Zone 3 — The Test Section (the heart of the tunnel)
**Purpose:** To host the model and provide optical access.

**Components:**
- **Optical viewing windows** — usually flat or slightly crowned tempered glass, designed to resist the full pressure differential.
- **Model support strut** — a streamlined stinger or sting balance to hold the model from downstream (or above) with minimum interference.
- **Instrumentation ports** — pressure tappings, force balance wiring, high-speed camera mounts.
- **Free-surface control** (in free-surface tunnels) — to set the submergence depth and simulate free-surface effects.

### Zone 4 — The Diffuser and Return Loop
**Purpose:** To recover pressure (and thus reduce pumping power) before returning flow to the pump.

**Engineering detail:** A well-designed diffuser has a **divergence half-angle between 3° and 7°**. Beyond ~7° the boundary layer separates and the diffuser stalls, *increasing* the total head loss. The pressure recovery coefficient of a good diffuser is **0.6 – 0.8**, meaning 60–80% of the dynamic head is recovered as static pressure.

### Zone 5 — The Pump, Vacuum, and Heat-Exchanger System
**Purpose:** To provide the driving head, regulate the absolute pressure, and maintain temperature.

- **Axial-flow or centrifugal pump** (driven by a variable-speed motor, typically **75 – 750 kW** for research facilities).
- **Vacuum pump** connected to a sealed de-aeration reservoir — used to lower $P_{\infty}$ to as low as **15 – 30 kPa (absolute)**.
- **Heat exchanger** — to maintain water temperature within $\pm 0.5°$C. This is critical because $P_v$ varies by roughly **1.7 kPa per 10°C**, so a 5°C drift can change $\sigma$ by a measurable amount.
- **De-aeration tank** — to strip dissolved gases (especially nitrogen) that would otherwise form spurious "gas cavitation" bubbles and contaminate the experiment.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas. They appear in nearly every KTU 2024 board question on cavitation tunnels.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cavitation Number | $\sigma = \dfrac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of pressure suppression to dynamic pressure |
| 2 | Incipient Cavitation Number | $\sigma_i$ | dimensionless | Value of $\sigma$ at which bubbles first appear |
| 3 | Critical Cavitation Number (bubble collapse) | $\sigma_c$ | dimensionless | Value of $\sigma$ at which supercavitation begins |
| 4 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | Viscous scaling of model |
| 5 | Froude Number (free-surface tunnels) | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | dimensionless | Gravity / inertia scaling |
| 6 | Test-section dynamic pressure | $q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2$ | Pa | Pressure available to suppress cavitation |
| 7 | Vapor pressure (Antoine equation, simplified) | $\log_{10} P_v = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | Pa | Strong function of temperature |
| 8 | Pump power requirement | $\dot{W} = \rho g Q H_{total}$ | W | Hydraulic power for circulation |
| 9 | Turbulence intensity | $Tu = \dfrac{\sqrt{\overline{u'^2}}}{U}$ | dimensionless | RMS of velocity fluctuations / mean |
| 10 | Contraction ratio | $CR = A_{in} / A_{ts}$ | dimensionless | Settling-chamber area / test-section area |
| 11 | Head loss in tunnel (Darcy-Weisbach) | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | Friction loss in the loop |
| 12 | Force balance — Thrust | $T = \dot{m}(V_{in} - V_{out})$ | N | Measured via sting balance |
| 13 | Force balance — Torque | $Q$ | N·m | Measured via torque transducer |
| 14 | Open-water efficiency | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{T V_A}{2 \pi n Q}$ | dimensionless | Propeller open-water efficiency |
| 15 | NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head) | $NPSH = \dfrac{P_{in} - P_v}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{in}^2}{2g}$ | m | Available pressure head above vapor |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Vertical Pipes in Markdown:** In the formulas above, the absolute-value or "magnitude" bars that you would normally write as $\vert x \vert$ must be written as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX. This is because the bare pipe character `|` is a markdown table separator. The same applies to the "such that" symbol (use `\mid` not `\vert` in set notation).

## 2.3 Why a Cavitation Number? — The Physical Logic

The cavitation number is the *single* parameter that controls *whether* cavitation happens. Here is the rigorous chain of reasoning a KTU examiner expects:

**Step 1 — Pressure Field Around a Body:** When a body moves through a fluid, the local static pressure on its surface varies. The lowest surface pressure is $P_{min}$, which by Bernoulli equals $P_{\infty} - C_p^{min} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2$, where $C_p^{min}$ is the minimum pressure coefficient (a function of body shape, Reynolds number, and angle of attack).

**Step 2 — The Inception Condition:** Cavitation incepts when the local surface pressure first drops to the vapor pressure: $P_{min} = P_v$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and Rearrange:**

$$
P_{\infty} - C_p^{min} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2 = P_v
$$

$$
C_p^{min} = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2} = \sigma
$$

**The remarkable result:** Cavitation incepts when $\sigma = C_p^{min}$, i.e., when the cavitation number *equals* the minimum pressure coefficient of the body. The cavitation number is therefore not arbitrary — it is a *body-property* threshold for any given geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** If you measure $\sigma_i$ for a particular propeller in a tunnel, you are effectively measuring its $C_p^{min}$ at that Reynolds number. This is why cavitation-tunnel tests are *fundamental* to propeller design — they are essentially pressure-coefficient measurements of the most complex geometry in marine engineering.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine and Ocean Engineering

1. **Propeller Design and Selection:** Every commercial ship propeller is cavitation-tested before being fitted. Cavitation causes erosion (pitting of bronze blades), noise (the most common source of underwater-radiated noise on a ship), and loss of efficiency.
2. **Submarine Appendages:** Sonar domes, propeller shrouds, and rudder cavitation are studied.
3. **Hydrodynamic Foils for High-Speed Craft:** Hydrofoils, control surfaces, and torpedoes experience supercavitation at high speed; the tunnel is used to map the supercavity shape.
4. **Tidal and Hydrokinetic Turbines:** Cavitation on runner blades limits the maximum tip speed.
5. **Validation of CFD Codes:** Modern RANS and LES codes are routinely validated against tunnel-measured cavity length, $\sigma_i$, and unsteady sheet behavior.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bernoulli to the Cavitation Number

We will now derive the cavitation number from first principles, in a manner acceptable as a full KTU 2024 ESE board answer.

**Starting Point — Bernoulli's Equation Along a Streamline:**

For an incompressible, inviscid, steady flow along a streamline, Bernoulli's theorem (no body forces) states:

$$
\frac{P}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} = \text{constant along a streamline}
$$

Applied between the free stream (at infinity) and a point on the body's surface where the local velocity is $V$ and the local static pressure is $P$:

$$
\frac{P_{\infty}}{\rho} + \frac{V_{\infty}^2}{2} = \frac{P}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to express $P$:**

$$
P = P_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2 - \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2
$$

**Step 2 — Define the local velocity coefficient and the dynamic head:**

Let $q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2$ (the free-stream dynamic pressure). Then:

$$
P = P_{\infty} + q_{\infty} - \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the local velocity ratio $k = V / V_{\infty}$:**

$$
P = P_{\infty} + q_{\infty} (1 - k^2)
$$

**Step 4 — Define the pressure coefficient $C_p$:**

By convention, $C_p = \dfrac{P - P_{\infty}}{q_{\infty}}$:

$$
C_p = \frac{P - P_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2} = 1 - k^2
$$

**Step 5 — Cavitation inception condition:** The minimum pressure on the body occurs at the point of maximum velocity, i.e., where $k$ is maximum and $C_p$ is minimum (and most negative). At inception, this minimum equals the vapor pressure:

$$
P_{min} = P_v
$$

$$
P_{\infty} + q_{\infty} C_p^{min} = P_v
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $C_p^{min}$:**

$$
C_p^{min} = \frac{P_v - P_{\infty}}{q_{\infty}} = - \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{q_{\infty}}
$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the cavitation number:**

By definition,

$$
\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{q_{\infty}}
$$

**Step 8 — Conclude the inception criterion:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_i = - C_p^{min}}
$$

Or, equivalently, the cavitation number at inception is the *negative* of the body's minimum pressure coefficient. In a KTU board answer, the student should clearly state:

> *Cavitation incepts on a body when the free-stream cavitation number equals the negative of the body's minimum pressure coefficient.*

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (typical KTU 2-mark sub-question):** A hydrofoil in a cavitation tunnel experiences a free-stream velocity of 12 m/s. The water temperature is 25°C. If the static pressure in the test section is 35 kPa (absolute), determine the cavitation number. Take $\rho = 998$ kg/m³ and $P_v(25°C) = 3.17$ kPa (absolute).

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**

$$
V_{\infty} = 12 \text{ m/s}, \quad P_{\infty} = 35 \text{ kPa}, \quad P_v = 3.17 \text{ kPa}, \quad \rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the free-stream dynamic pressure:**

$$
q_{\infty} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 998 \times (12)^2
$$

$$
q_{\infty} = \frac{1}{2} \times 998 \times 144
$$

$$
q_{\infty} = 499 \times 144 = 71,856 \text{ Pa} = 71.856 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cavitation number:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{q_{\infty}} = \frac{35 - 3.17}{71.856} = \frac{31.83}{71.856}
$$

$$
\sigma = 0.4430
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:**

A cavitation number of **0.443** means that the pressure suppression available is only about **44%** of the free-stream dynamic pressure. For a hydrofoil with $C_p^{min} \approx -0.45$ (typical of a cambered foil at 6° angle of attack), we have $\sigma \approx -C_p^{min}$, and so the hydrofoil would be operating just at the threshold of incipient cavitation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error #1:** Forgetting to convert kPa to Pa consistently. The answer will be off by a factor of 1000.
> **Common Student Error #2:** Using gauge pressure (e.g., writing $P_{\infty} = 35 - 101.325 = -66.3$ kPa gauge) when the problem gives absolute pressure. In a sealed cavitation tunnel, the test-section pressure is *almost always* below atmospheric, so absolute pressure is the relevant quantity.
> **Common Student Error #3:** Using $P_v = 0$ (an idealized assumption valid only for very high-speed flows). For low-speed tunnel work, the temperature-corrected $P_v$ must be used.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reverse Problem (Test-Section Pressure Required)

**Problem:** A propeller with $C_p^{min} = -0.8$ is to be tested at a free-stream velocity of 8 m/s in a tunnel where the water temperature is 20°C. Find the test-section absolute pressure required for incipient cavitation. Take $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $P_v(20°C) = 2.34$ kPa.

**Step 1 — Inception criterion:**

$$
\sigma_i = -C_p^{min} = -(-0.8) = 0.8
$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic head:**

$$
q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 998 \times 8^2 = 499 \times 64 = 31,936 \text{ Pa} = 31.936 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the cavitation number definition for $P_{\infty}$:**

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{q_{\infty}}
$$

$$
P_{\infty} = \sigma_i \cdot q_{\infty} + P_v
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
P_{\infty} = 0.8 \times 31.936 + 2.34
$$

$$
P_{\infty} = 25.549 + 2.34 = 27.889 \text{ kPa (absolute)}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to gauge pressure (vacuum below atmospheric):**

$$
P_{\infty, gauge} = P_{\infty} - P_{atm} = 27.889 - 101.325 = -73.436 \text{ kPa (gauge)}
$$

This is a vacuum of approximately **73.4 kPa** (about 73% of a full vacuum), which is well within the operating range of a research-grade cavitation tunnel.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reynolds Number Scaling

**Problem:** A model propeller of diameter $D_m = 0.25$ m is to be tested in fresh water at 20°C at a free-stream velocity of 10 m/s. The full-scale ship propeller has a diameter $D_s = 4.0$ m and operates in seawater ($\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³, $\mu_s = 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) at an advance speed of 8 m/s. Compute and compare the Reynolds numbers.

**Model (fresh water, 20°C):** $\rho_m = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu_m = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

$$
Re_m = \frac{\rho_m V_m D_m}{\mu_m} = \frac{998 \times 10 \times 0.25}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
Re_m = \frac{2495}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.49 \times 10^6
$$

**Ship (seawater):**

$$
Re_s = \frac{\rho_s V_s D_s}{\mu_s} = \frac{1025 \times 8 \times 4.0}{1.05 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
Re_s = \frac{32,800}{1.05 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.12 \times 10^7
$$

**Interpretation:** The full-scale Reynolds number is about **12.5 times larger** than the model. This is a *Reynolds-number-scaling problem* — at full scale, the boundary layer is much thinner, and the pressure coefficient distribution is different. Cavitation-tunnel results are therefore *cavitation-number scaled* (which is exact for a given body) but only *approximately Reynolds-number scaled* (which is not exact). This is one of the central limitations of cavitation-tunnel testing and is the source of the well-known "scale effect" on $\sigma_i$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Cavitation Tunnel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[De-aeration Reservoir and Vacuum Pump] --> B[Axial Flow Pump with Variable Speed Drive]
    B --> C[Settling Chamber with Honeycomb and Screens]
    C --> D[Contraction Nozzle with Witozinsky Profile]
    D --> E[Test Section with Glass Windows and Model]
    E --> F[Diffuser with 3 to 7 Degree Divergence]
    F --> G[Return Loop and Flowmeter]
    G --> A
    E --> H[Instrumentation Stack]
    H --> H1[Pressure Transducers]
    H --> H2[Sting Force Balance]
    H --> H3[High Speed Camera and Strobe]
    H --> H4[LDA or PIV Optics]
    H --> H5[Temperature and Air Content Probes]
    H1 --> I[Data Acquisition System]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    I --> J[Real Time Sigma Display and Control Console]
    B --> J
    A --> J
```

**How to read the diagram:**
- The top horizontal band (A → B → C → D → E → F → G → A) is the **hydraulic loop** — the closed path of water.
- The branch on the right (E → H → I → J) is the **instrumentation and control loop** — the path of data and command signals.
- Note the *two control arrows* entering the loop from J: one to the pump (sets $V_{\infty}$) and one to the vacuum reservoir (sets $P_{\infty}$). These two controls are the *actuators* that set the cavitation number $\sigma$.

## 4.2 Functional Flow — How the Cavitation Number is Set and Measured

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL[Control Variables]
        Pinf[Set P infinity via vacuum pump]
        Vinf[Set V infinity via pump RPM]
        Temp[Hold T constant via heat exchanger]
    end
    subgraph MEAS[Direct Measurements]
        PT[Test Section Pressure Transducer]
        VT[Flowmeter V infinity]
        TT[Thermocouple T]
    end
    subgraph COMP[Computed Output]
        QH[Dynamic Head q infinity = half rho V squared]
        SIG[Display Cavitation Number sigma]
    end
    subgraph SPECIM[Test Article]
        MODEL[Propeller or Hydrofoil Model]
    end
    subgraph OBS[Observations]
        CAM[Visual Cavity via High Speed Camera]
        FB[Thrust and Torque via Sting Balance]
    end
    Pinf --> PT
    Vinf --> VT
    Temp --> TT
    VT --> QH
    PT --> SIG
    QH --> SIG
    TT --> SIG
    SIG --> CAM
    SIG --> FB
    MODEL --> CAM
    MODEL --> FB
```

**Reading aid:** The control variables on the left are the *knobs* the operator turns. The middle band is the *measurement* of those variables. The right band is the *observation* of the model's response. The cavitation number $\sigma$ is computed in real time from the measured $P_{\infty}$, $V_{\infty}$, and $T$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]
**Define the cavitation number and state its physical significance in marine hydrodynamics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The cavitation number $\sigma$ is a non-dimensional parameter that compares the *available pressure suppression* (numerator, $P_{\infty} - P_v$) to the *free-stream dynamic pressure* (denominator, $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2$):

$$
\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2}
$$

**Physical significance:** $\sigma$ is the single most important parameter governing cavitation in marine flows. It is the ratio of how much the local static pressure would need to be *lowered* (below atmospheric) to reach the vapor pressure, relative to the kinetic energy of the flow. A **low** $\sigma$ means the dynamic pressure is large compared to the available suppression, so cavitation is more likely; a **high** $\sigma$ means the suppression is large and cavitation is suppressed. For a given body, cavitation incepts when $\sigma$ falls to a critical value $\sigma_i$ that equals $-C_p^{min}$ of that body. Hence the cavitation number is, in effect, a *tunable dial* in any cavitation experiment: by varying $P_{\infty}$ (via vacuum) and $V_{\infty}$ (via pump speed), the experimenter can dial the model through all regimes from no cavitation, through incipient, partial, and sheet cavitation, to supercavitation. *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for definition, 1 for the inception-criterion physical meaning.)*

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Sample, 2024 Scheme]
**List the main functional components of a closed-loop cavitation tunnel and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A closed-loop cavitation tunnel consists of:
1. **Settling chamber with honeycomb and screens** — produces uniform, low-turbulence, swirl-free flow. *(1 mark)*
2. **Contraction nozzle** — accelerates the flow smoothly to the test-section velocity with a uniform velocity profile. *(1 mark)*
3. **Test section with glass windows and model support** — houses the model and provides optical access for visualization. *(1 mark for naming these three; the remaining 0.5 mark each if diffuser, pump, vacuum, and de-aeration are also correctly named.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Naming only the test section. A complete answer must include the conditioning components *upstream* of the test section, because without proper conditioning the test is meaningless.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — Model, 2024 Scheme] (14 Marks)

**A model propeller of diameter $D = 0.30$ m is to be tested in a cavitation tunnel at a free-stream velocity of 9 m/s in fresh water at 25°C. The minimum pressure coefficient of the blade section at the design radius is $C_p^{min} = -1.10$.**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the cavitation number and hence find the absolute test-section pressure required for incipient cavitation. Take $\rho = 997$ kg/m³ and $P_v(25°C) = 3.17$ kPa. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the tunnel can be operated down to 18 kPa (absolute), find the maximum free-stream velocity at which cavitation is just incipient, and comment on whether this is achievable with typical tunnel pump systems. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Derive the cavitation number (3 of the 7 marks):**

Apply Bernoulli along a streamline between the free stream and a point on the blade:

$$
P_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2 = P + \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2
$$

Rearrange:

$$
P = P_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2 (1 - k^2)
$$

where $k = V / V_{\infty}$. The pressure coefficient is:

$$
C_p = 1 - k^2
$$

At the minimum-pressure (maximum-velocity) point, $C_p = C_p^{min}$ and $P = P_v$ (cavitation inception):

$$
P_v = P_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2 C_p^{min}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
P_{\infty} - P_v = - C_p^{min} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2
$$

By definition, $\sigma_i = (P_{\infty} - P_v) / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2)$, so:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_i = - C_p^{min}}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerical computation (4 of the 7 marks):**

$$
\sigma_i = -(-1.10) = 1.10
$$

Dynamic head:

$$
q_{\infty} = \frac{1}{2} \times 997 \times 9^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 997 \times 81 = 40,378.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 40.38 \text{ kPa}
$$

Solve for $P_{\infty}$:

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{q_{\infty}} \Rightarrow P_{\infty} = \sigma_i \cdot q_{\infty} + P_v
$$

$$
P_{\infty} = 1.10 \times 40.38 + 3.17 = 44.42 + 3.17 = 47.59 \text{ kPa (absolute)}
$$

In gauge pressure (vacuum below atmospheric):

$$
P_{\infty, gauge} = 47.59 - 101.325 = -53.74 \text{ kPa (gauge)}
$$

**Answer (a):** $P_{\infty} = 47.59$ kPa (absolute), or a vacuum of 53.7 kPa.

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Setup (2 of the 7 marks):**

The inception condition at the new operating point must still hold:

$$
\sigma_i = -C_p^{min} = 1.10
$$

The tunnel can produce a minimum of $P_{\infty,min} = 18$ kPa. The vapor pressure remains $P_v = 3.17$ kPa (at fixed temperature).

**Step 2 — Solve for maximum velocity (3 of the 7 marks):**

$$
q_{\infty, max} = \frac{P_{\infty, min} - P_v}{\sigma_i} = \frac{18 - 3.17}{1.10} = \frac{14.83}{1.10} = 13.48 \text{ kPa}
$$

Since $q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2$:

$$
V_{\infty, max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 q_{\infty, max}}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 13,482}{997}}
$$

$$
V_{\infty, max} = \sqrt{\frac{26,964}{997}} = \sqrt{27.045} = 5.20 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering comment (2 of the 7 marks):**

The maximum velocity at incipient cavitation is only **5.20 m/s** — substantially lower than the original 9 m/s. This reduction in velocity is the trade-off the tunnel operator must accept when lowering the pressure to reach the inception condition. The required pump power scales as $V^3$, so the *pump energy* at 5.20 m/s is only about $(5.20/9)^3 = 0.19$ times the power at 9 m/s — well within the operating envelope of a typical research tunnel. Therefore this operating point is easily achievable. The student should note, however, that to test at *higher* free-stream velocities while maintaining the same $\sigma_i$, the tunnel must be capable of even lower $P_{\infty}$ — and this is the principal engineering limit of the facility.

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice, 2024 Scheme] (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the main components of a closed-loop cavitation tunnel and explain the function of the contraction nozzle and the diffuser. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cavitation tunnel is to be used to investigate a hydrofoil whose design incipient cavitation number is $\sigma_i = 0.65$. If the water temperature is 20°C, the free-stream velocity is 6 m/s, and the test-section absolute pressure is 50 kPa, determine whether cavitation will incept. If not, find the velocity at which cavitation will just begin. Take $\rho = 998$ kg/m³ and $P_v(20°C) = 2.34$ kPa. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

The student should draw the block diagram from Section 4.1 and label the seven primary hydraulic components (reservoir, pump, settling chamber, contraction, test section, diffuser, return loop) and the instrumentation stack. Marks are distributed as follows:

- **Block diagram with all seven components correctly labelled:** 3 marks.
- **Function of contraction nozzle:** 2 marks — accelerates the flow smoothly to test-section velocity with a uniform profile; a fifth-order polynomial or Witozinsky profile gives $CR$ of 6–9:1; minimizes turbulence and separation.
- **Function of diffuser:** 2 marks — recovers static pressure to reduce pumping power; divergence half-angle of 3°–7° to avoid stall; pressure recovery coefficient 0.6–0.8.

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the current cavitation number (3 of 7 marks):**

$$
q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 998 \times 6^2 = 499 \times 36 = 17,964 \text{ Pa} = 17.96 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{current} = \frac{50 - 2.34}{17.96} = \frac{47.66}{17.96} = 2.654
$$

**Step 2 — Compare to $\sigma_i$ (1 of 7 marks):**

$\sigma_{current} = 2.65 > \sigma_i = 0.65$. Since the *current* cavitation number is much larger than the incipient value, **cavitation will not incept** (the pressure suppression is more than enough to prevent it).

**Step 3 — Find the incipient velocity (3 of 7 marks):**

At incipient cavitation, $\sigma = \sigma_i = 0.65$:

$$
0.65 = \frac{50 - 2.34}{\tfrac{1}{2} \times 998 \times V^2} = \frac{47.66}{499 V^2}
$$

$$
V^2 = \frac{47.66}{499 \times 0.65} = \frac{47.66}{324.35} = 0.1469
$$

$$
V = 0.383 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Answer (b):** At the operating point, $\sigma = 2.65$, so cavitation will *not* incept. Cavitation will just begin at $V = 0.38$ m/s, which is impractically low for most practical tests. To incept cavitation at a more useful velocity (say 6 m/s), the operator would need to *lower* $P_{\infty}$ until the new $\sigma$ matches $\sigma_i$. Specifically:

$$
P_{\infty, new} = \sigma_i \cdot q_{\infty} + P_v = 0.65 \times 17.96 + 2.34 = 11.67 + 2.34 = 14.01 \text{ kPa (absolute)}
$$

This is a deep vacuum — about 87 kPa below atmospheric — and is well within the range of a research cavitation tunnel.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **The Five Mark-Loss Traps on Cavitation-Tunnel Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing Pa and kPa. Always convert to a *single* unit system within one equation.
> 2. **Using Gauge Pressure Instead of Absolute:** A tunnel question always wants *absolute* pressure in the cavitation number.
> 3. **Forgetting the Vapor Pressure:** Using $P_v = 0$ when the question specifies a temperature. For low-speed tunnel work, $P_v$ matters.
> 4. **Confusing $\sigma$ with $C_p$:** They are *equal in magnitude* only at inception. For $C_p^{min} = -1.10$, write $\sigma_i = 1.10$, not $\sigma_i = -1.10$.
> 5. **Drawing the Tunnel Without the Conditioning Components:** A block diagram that goes "pump → test section → pump" loses marks because the honeycomb, screens, contraction, and diffuser are not optional.

## 5.4 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cavitation Tunnel and Instrumentation**

- **Definition:** A cavitation tunnel is a **variable-pressure recirculating water tunnel (VPRWT)** that allows the operator to set the test-section pressure, velocity, and temperature independently to study cavitation on submerged models.
- **Cavitation Number:** $\sigma = (P_{\infty} - P_v) / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^2)$ — dimensionless; the single most important parameter.
- **Inception Criterion:** $\sigma_i = -C_p^{min}$ — cavitation begins when the cavitation number equals the *negative* of the body's minimum pressure coefficient.
- **Five Zones of a Tunnel:** Settling chamber (with honeycomb and screens) → Contraction nozzle → Test section (with glass windows) → Diffuser → Pump / vacuum / heat-exchanger / de-aeration loop.
- **Key Tunnel Design Numbers to Memorize:** Contraction ratio 6–9:1; diffuser divergence 3°–7°; turbulence intensity < 0.5%; pressure recovery 0.6–0.8; typical test-section size 0.3 m – 1.5 m.
- **Vapor Pressure is Temperature-Dependent:** Always use the value corresponding to the water temperature given. Common values: 1.70 kPa (15°C), 2.34 kPa (20°C), 3.17 kPa (25°C).
- **Instrumentation Hierarchy:** Primary flow sensors (Pitot, venturi, LDA/PIV) → pressure transducers (test-section static, surface tappings, unsteady) → force balances (sting for thrust/torque, three-component for lift/drag) → visualization (high-speed camera 1000–10000 fps, stroboscope, shadowgraph) → environment (thermocouple, dissolved-air meter).
- **Scale Effect:** Cavitation tunnels match $\sigma$ exactly but not $Re$. The Reynolds-number mismatch is the principal limitation of model testing and motivates a Reynolds-number correction to $\sigma_i$.
- **Three Cavitation Regimes Seen in the Tunnel:** Incipient (small isolated bubbles at $\sigma_i$), partial / sheet (attached cavity covering a region at $\sigma < \sigma_i$), and supercavitation (full cavity extending downstream at $\sigma \ll \sigma_i$).
- **Practical Rule of Thumb:** Lowering $P_{\infty}$ by 1 kPa has the same effect on $\sigma$ as raising $V_{\infty}$ by approximately 0.3% (for typical tunnel conditions), so the vacuum pump is the more *efficient* dial for reaching low $\sigma$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave Makers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Wave Makers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wave Maker (Wave Generator):** A wave maker is a mechanical device used in experimental hydrodynamic laboratories to generate controlled, repeatable surface gravity waves of specified amplitude (height), period (frequency), and direction in a wave basin, wave flume, or towing tank. It is the fundamental boundary-condition actuator for any coastal, ocean, or naval architecture experiment. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST865)**, a wave maker is treated as an **oscillating boundary** that imparts momentum to the water column, thereby radiating energy as progressive surface waves. The mathematical treatment follows the seminal works of **Havelock (1929)** and **Biesel (1951)**, who formulated the source-sink analogy and piston/flap transfer functions respectively. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Bathtub Paddle** > Imagine sliding a flat wooden paddle back-and-forth in a bathtub. The paddle acts as a piston — as it moves forward, it pushes water ahead, generating ripples that travel away from it. The **faster** the paddle moves and the **larger** the stroke, the **bigger** the waves. A wave maker in a laboratory is simply a precisely-engineered, computer-controlled version of that bathtub paddle, capable of producing regular, irregular (random), or focused wave packets with millimetric accuracy. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics The following key metrics are used to characterise a wave maker's performance: - **Stroke (S):** Total peak-to-peak displacement of the wave maker face, measured in **metres (m)**. - **Angular Displacement (θ):** For flap-type wave makers, the maximum rotation about the hinge, in **radians (rad)**. - **Wave Height (H):** Vertical distance between crest and trough, in **metres (m)**. - **Wavelength (λ):** Horizontal distance between successive crests, in **metres (m)**. - **Transfer Function (T):** Dimensionless ratio relating generated wave amplitude to wave maker motion, e.g. **H/λ** versus **S/λ**. - **Energy Reflection Coefficient (Kr):** Ratio of reflected to incident wave energy, dimensionless, range **0 ≤ Kr ≤ 1**. ## 1.4 Classification Overview Wave makers are classified based on their **kinematic motion**: | Classification | Motion Type | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Piston-type** | Pure translational (horizontal) | Long waves, deep water | | **Flap-type** | Rotational about a hinge | Shallow water, tsunami simulation | | **Wedge / Plunger-type** | Vertical translational | Short waves, deep water | | **Segmented / Multi-element** | Combined piston + flap | Broadband spectra, directional seas | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Theory — The "How" and "Why" A wave maker works by **enforcing a non-zero normal velocity boundary condition** on one end of a fluid domain. Mathematically, if the wave maker surface lies at $x = 0$ and oscillates with velocity $U(t)$, then the boundary condition is: $$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0} = U(t) \quad \text{for} \quad -h \le z \le 0$$ where $\phi$ is the velocity potential of the radiated wave. The wave is **generated** because the forced oscillation of the boundary radiates energy into the fluid in the form of surface gravity waves governed by the **dispersion relation**. ### The Havelock Source Method (Why and How) **Why:** A rigid oscillating paddle does not simply translate the water uniformly with depth — the velocity profile over the paddle face is non-uniform. To solve the boundary value problem exactly, **Havelock (1929)** proposed replacing the wave maker with a continuous vertical distribution of **oscillating 2-D line sources** along its face. **How:** 1. Each elementary line source at depth $z'$ radiates a velocity potential decaying with horizontal distance. 2. The total potential is integrated over the wave maker height. 3. By applying the rigid-paddle boundary condition (constant velocity at the paddle, zero normal velocity elsewhere), the source strength distribution $m(z')$ is found. 4. Far from the wave maker ($x \to -\infty$), only the propagating mode survives, yielding the wave amplitude. ### The Biesel Transfer Functions Biesel (1951) used Havelock's method to derive closed-form transfer functions for the two most common geometries. These are the **high-yield results** expected in KTU examinations. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Universal Constants & Variables** > - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ > - Wave number: $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ > - Angular frequency: $\omega = 2\pi / T$ > - Water depth: $h$ | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$ | Linear wave dispersion relation | rad²/s² | | 2 | $c = \omega / k = \sqrt{g \tanh(kh) / k}$ | Wave celerity | m/s | | 3 | $L_0 = gT^2 / (2\pi)$ | Deep-water wavelength | m | | 4 | $\cfrac{H}{\lambda} = \cfrac{4 \sinh(kh)}{2kh + \sinh(2kh)} \cdot \cfrac{S}{\lambda}$ | **Piston wave maker transfer function** | dimensionless | | 5 | $\cfrac{H}{\lambda} = \cfrac{4[\sinh(kh) - kh\cosh(kh)]}{kh[2kh + \sinh(2kh)]} \cdot \cfrac{S}{\lambda}$ | **Flap wave maker (hinged at bottom)** | dimensionless | | 6 | $C_g = \cfrac{c}{2}\left[1 + \cfrac{2kh}{\sinh(2kh)}\right]$ | Group velocity | m/s | | 7 | $P = \cfrac{\rho g^2 H^2 \lambda}{32\pi}\left[1 + \cfrac{2kh}{\sinh(2kh)}\right]$ | Wave power per unit crest length | W/m | | 8 | $K_r = \cfrac{H_r}{H_i}$ | Energy reflection coefficient | dimensionless | | 9 | $K_t = \cfrac{H_t}{H_i}$ | Transmission coefficient | dimensionless | | 10 | $K_r^2 + K_t^2 + K_d = 1$ | Energy balance (d = dissipation) | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Deep-Water Limits ($kh \to \infty$):** > - Piston: $H/\lambda \to (2/\pi)(S/\lambda)$ > - Flap: $H/\lambda \to 0$ (flap cannot radiate waves in deep water) > **Note on Shallow-Water Limits ($kh \to 0$):** > - Piston: $H/\lambda \to 2(S/h)$ > - Flap: $H/\lambda \to 0$ (flap is most effective in shallow water) ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Wave makers are essential for: - **Coastal & Offshore Engineering:** Testing breakwaters, seawalls, offshore wind turbine foundations. - **Naval Architecture:** Resistance and seakeeping tests on ship models. - **Renewable Energy:** Wave Energy Converter (WEC) performance evaluation. - **Tsunami Research:** Solitary wave generation using **piston** or **flap** wave makers. - **Sediment Transport:** Studying bedform migration under wave action. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wave Height from a Piston-Type Wave Maker (Havelock–Biesel Method) This is a **favourite 14-mark derivation question** in KTU exams. ### Step 1 — Velocity Potential of the Radiated Wave For a two-dimensional progressive wave propagating in the **negative x-direction** (i.e. into the basin from the wave maker at $x = 0$), the velocity potential is: $$\phi(x, z, t) = \frac{a g}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+h)}{\cosh kh} \cdot \cos(kx + \omega t)$$ where $a = H/2$ is the wave amplitude. **Logic:** This is the standard linear Airy wave potential, chosen such that the surface boundary condition $\eta = -(1/g)(\partial \phi / \partial t)$ at $z = 0$ is satisfied. ### Step 2 — Horizontal Particle Velocity on the Wave Maker Face Differentiating with respect to $x$: $$u(x, z, t) = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = -\frac{a g k}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+h)}{\cosh kh} \cdot \sin(kx + \omega t)$$ Evaluating at $x = 0$ (the wave maker face): $$u(0, z, t) = -\frac{a g k}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+h)}{\cosh kh} \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$ **Logic:** The horizontal fluid velocity at the wave maker surface must equal the rigid-body velocity of the paddle, since no fluid can pass through the paddle. ### Step 3 — Piston Velocity A piston oscillating with stroke $S$ (peak-to-peak) and angular frequency $\omega$ has displacement: $$X_p(t) = \frac{S}{2} \sin(\omega t)$$ Therefore, the piston velocity is: $$U_p(t) = \frac{dX_p}{dt} = \frac{S \omega}{2} \cos(\omega t)$$ **Logic:** The paddle is treated as a rigid body; its velocity is the time derivative of its displacement. ### Step 4 — Havelock's Source Distribution To match the non-uniform velocity profile (which depends on $z$ via $\cosh k(z+h)$) with the uniform paddle velocity, Havelock postulated an equivalent **oscillating source distribution** $m(z')$ of unit strength per unit height along the wave maker. The far-field wave potential is then: $$\phi = \frac{1}{2} \int_{-h}^{0} m(z') \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+h)}{\cosh kh} \cdot e^{ikx} \, dz' \cdot e^{-i\omega t}$$ **Logic:** Each infinitesimal element $dz'$ at the source location radiates a wave potential decaying as $e^{ikx}$. Integration over the wave maker height sums all contributions. ### Step 5 — Matching the Boundary Condition Equating the wave-maker fluid velocity to the paddle velocity for the propagating mode and integrating vertically (or matching via the orthogonality of $\cosh k(z+h)$), the source strength is found to be: $$m = \frac{2 S \omega^2 \cosh kh}{g k \sinh(kh)} \cdot \frac{1}{2kh + \sinh(2kh)} \cdot \text{(normalisation)}$$ After detailed algebraic manipulation (carried out in standard references such as Dean & Dalrymple, *Water Wave Mechanics for Engineers and Scientists*; the full matching is omitted here only for space, but is required in KTU answer scripts), the result is: ### Step 6 — Final Transfer Function $$\boxed{\frac{H}{\lambda} = \frac{4 \sinh(kh)}{2kh + \sinh(2kh)} \cdot \frac{S}{\lambda}}$$ **Logic & Interpretation:** The factor $\dfrac{4\sinh(kh)}{2kh + \sinh(2kh)}$ is the **transfer function** $T_p(kh)$, bounded between 0 and 1. It represents the **efficiency** with which the paddle's stroke is converted into wave height. As $kh \to \infty$ (deep water), $T_p \to 1/(\pi/2) = 2/\pi \approx 0.637$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Flap-Type Wave Maker (Hinged at Bottom) For a flap wave maker rotating about a hinge at $z = -h$ with maximum angular displacement $\theta_{\max}$ (in radians), the local horizontal velocity at depth $z$ is: $$U_f(z) = \omega \theta_{\max} (z + h) \cos(\omega t)$$ **Logic:** A flap rotating about $z = -h$ has zero velocity at the hinge and maximum velocity at the top ($z = 0$); the velocity is linear in $(z + h)$. The total "effective stroke" is $S_f = h \theta_{\max}$. Following an identical Havelock-source matching, the transfer function becomes: $$\boxed{\frac{H}{\lambda} = \frac{4[\sinh(kh) - kh \cosh(kh)]}{kh[2kh + \sinh(2kh)]} \cdot \frac{S_f}{\lambda}}$$ **Logic & Interpretation:** The factor $[\sinh(kh) - kh\cosh(kh)]$ vanishes in deep water ($kh \to \infty$, since $\cosh > \sinh/kh$), confirming that flap wave makers **cannot generate waves in deep water**. They are ideal for **shallow water** and **tsunami** simulation. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reflection Coefficient for an Active Wave Absorption System When a wave train reflects off a **passive** (non-absorbing) wave maker, the wave in the basin is a superposition of the generated (outgoing) wave and the reflected (incoming) wave. For active absorption, the paddle motion is modulated in real time to suppress reflection. The reflection coefficient for the **passive piston case** (at the natural piston frequency) is given by: $$K_r = \left\vert \frac{1 - i \alpha}{1 + i \alpha} \right\vert$$ where $\alpha = \dfrac{kh \sinh(kh)}{2kh + \sinh(2kh)}$ is a dimensionless parameter. For **ideal active absorption** (force-feedback control), $K_r \to 0$. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Sub-Part) **Problem:** A piston-type wave maker has a stroke of $S = 0.5$ m and operates in water depth $h = 2$ m. The wave period is $T = 1.4$ s. Calculate the wavelength and the generated wave height. **Step 1 — Deep-water wavelength:** $$L_0 = \frac{g T^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 1.4^2}{2\pi} = \frac{19.23}{6.283} \approx 3.06 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Solve dispersion relation iteratively:** $$\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh) \Rightarrow k = \frac{\omega^2}{g \tanh(kh)}$$ With $\omega = 2\pi / 1.4 = 4.488$ rad/s and $h = 2$ m, iterating: - Guess $k = 0.9$: $\tanh(1.8) = 0.9468$, $k_{new} = 4.488^2 / (9.81 \times 0.9468) = 20.14 / 9.29 = 2.168$ - Guess $k = 1.5$: $\tanh(3.0) = 0.9951$, $k_{new} = 20.14 / 9.76 = 2.063$ - Guess $k = 1.8$: $\tanh(3.6) = 0.9985$, $k_{new} = 20.14 / 9.80 = 2.055$ - Converges: $k \approx 2.06$ rad/m So $\lambda = 2\pi / k = 6.283 / 2.06 \approx 3.05$ m. **Step 3 — Calculate transfer function:** $$T_p = \frac{4 \sinh(kh)}{2kh + \sinh(2kh)} = \frac{4 \sinh(4.12)}{2(4.12) + \sinh(8.24)}$$ $$\sinh(4.12) \approx 30.18, \quad \sinh(8.24) \approx 1878$$ $$T_p \approx \frac{4 \times 30.18}{8.24 + 1878} \approx \frac{120.7}{1886.2} \approx 0.0640$$ **Step 4 — Calculate wave height:** $$H = T_p \cdot S \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{1} = T_p \cdot S \cdot \text{(in form)}$$ Using the formula directly: $$H = \lambda \cdot T_p \cdot \frac{S}{\lambda} = T_p \cdot S = 0.0640 \times 0.5 = 0.032 \text{ m} = 3.2 \text{ cm}$$ **Note:** In deep water, the transfer function for the piston is small because the wave maker is in **intermediate-to-deep** water (kh ≈ 4.12 > π, which is effectively deep). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification of Wave Makers (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Wave Makers] --> B[Piston Type] A --> C[Flap Type] A --> D[Wedge or Plunger Type] A --> E[Segmented Type] B --> B1[Translational Horizontal Motion] B --> B2[Long waves, deep water] B --> B3[Transfer Fn Tp] C --> C1[Rotational about bottom hinge] C --> C2[Shallow water, tsunami] C --> C3[Transfer Fn Tf] D --> D1[Vertical plunging motion] D --> D2[Short waves, deep water] D --> D3[Pneumatic or servo driven] E --> E1[Stacked piston and flap] E --> E2[Broadband spectra] E --> E3[Modern research basins] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Wave Generation System ```mermaid flowchart LR sig1[Signal Generator
Sine or JONSWAP] --> ctrl[Controller Unit
PC and DSP] ctrl --> amp[Servo Amplifier] amp --> act[Servo Actuator
Hydraulic or Electric] act --> wm[Wave Maker Paddle or Flap] wm --> bas[Wave Basin or Flume] bas --> probe1[Wave Probe 1
Incident] bas --> probe2[Wave Probe 2
Reflected] probe2 --> proc[Signal Processor
FFT and Reflection] proc --> ctrl probe1 --> mon[Data Acquisition
and Display] proc --> mon ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Comparison: Piston vs Flap Wave Maker ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PISTON P1[Static Frame] --- P2[Piston Plate] P2 -- X = S sin omega t --> P3[Still Water Level SWL] P3 --- P4[Bottom Hinge Fixed] P1 --- P5[Servo Motor Drive] end subgraph FLAP F1[Static Frame] --- F2[Flap Plate] F2 -- theta rotation --> F3[Still Water Level SWL] F3 --- F4[Bottom Hinge Active] F1 --- F5[Servo Motor Drive] end ``` ## 4.4 Wave Maker Calibration Curve (Conceptual Plot) | Regime | Piston Behaviour | Flap Behaviour | |---|---|---| | Deep Water (kh > π) | $T_p \to 2/\pi \approx 0.637$ | $T_f \to 0$ (inoperative) | | Intermediate (0.5 < kh < π) | Decreasing from 0.637 | Maximum efficiency near kh ≈ 1 | | Shallow Water (kh < 0.5) | $T_p \to 2S/h$ (large) | $T_f$ remains significant | > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This explains why **piston wave makers** are preferred for deep water ocean engineering, and **flap wave makers** are preferred for coastal shallow-water and tsunami laboratories. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** Differentiate between a **piston-type** and a **flap-type** wave maker. Mention one specific application of each. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Piston-Type | Flap-Type | |---|---|---| | Motion | Pure horizontal translation | Rotational about a bottom hinge | | Velocity profile over face | Non-uniform (cosh distribution) | Linear, zero at hinge | | Deep-water efficiency | High (T_p → 2/π) | Zero (cannot radiate deep-water waves) | | Shallow-water efficiency | Moderate | High | | Typical application | Ocean engineering, ship model testing | Tsunami simulation, coastal morphology | **(3 marks: 1 for motion type, 1 for efficiency, 1 for application.)** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]** State **Havelock's wave maker theory** and write the resulting transfer function for a piston-type wave maker. **Model Answer:** > **Havelock's Wave Maker Theory (1929):** A rigid oscillating wave maker can be mathematically replaced by a continuous vertical distribution of oscillating 2-D line sources along its submerged face. The source strength distribution is determined by enforcing the rigid-paddle boundary condition. **Piston transfer function (Biesel form):** $$\frac{H}{\lambda} = \frac{4 \sinh(kh)}{2kh + \sinh(2kh)} \cdot \frac{S}{\lambda}$$ where $H$ = wave height, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $S$ = piston stroke, $k$ = wave number, $h$ = water depth. **(3 marks: 1 for theory, 1 for formula, 1 for variable definition.)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the wave height generated by a **piston-type wave maker** using Havelock's source method. State clearly all boundary conditions and assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A piston wave maker operates with a stroke of **0.8 m** in a water depth of **1.5 m**. The wave period is **1.2 s**. Determine the generated wave height and the wavelength. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Assumptions and Boundary Conditions (1 mark):** - Incompressible, inviscid, irrotational flow; linear wave theory valid. - Wave maker at $x = 0$; basin extends to $x = -\infty$. - BC1 (Kinematic, free surface): $\partial \phi / \partial z = \partial \eta / \partial t$ at $z = 0$. - BC2 (Dynamic, free surface): $\partial \phi / \partial t + g \eta = 0$ at $z = 0$. - BC3 (Bottom): $\partial \phi / \partial z = 0$ at $z = -h$. - BC4 (Wave maker face): $\partial \phi / \partial x = U_p(t)$ at $x = 0$, for $-h \le z \le 0$. - BC5 (Radiation): $\phi$ bounded as $x \to -\infty$. **Step 2 — Velocity potential of the radiated wave (1 mark):** $$\phi(x, z, t) = \frac{a g}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+h)}{\cosh kh} \cdot \cos(kx + \omega t)$$ **Step 3 — Horizontal velocity on the wave maker face (1 mark):** $$u(0, z, t) = -\frac{a g k}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+h)}{\cosh kh} \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 4 — Havelock's source distribution (2 marks):** The wave maker is replaced by a continuous source distribution $m(z')$ along its face: $$\phi_{total} = \frac{1}{2} \int_{-h}^{0} m(z') \cdot G(x, z, z') \, dz' \cdot e^{-i\omega t}$$ where $G$ is the Green's function for the 2D Laplace equation in bounded water depth. Matching the boundary condition yields the source strength, and after vertical integration: $$m = \frac{S \omega^2 \cosh kh}{g k \cdot \frac{1}{2}[2kh + \sinh(2kh)]}$$ **Step 5 — Final transfer function (2 marks):** $$\boxed{\frac{H}{\lambda} = \frac{4 \sinh(kh)}{2kh + \sinh(2kh)} \cdot \frac{S}{\lambda}}$$ This is the Biesel (1951) result. **(Total: 7 marks)** --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** $S = 0.8$ m, $h = 1.5$ m, $T = 1.2$ s. **Step 1 — Deep-water wavelength (1 mark):** $$L_0 = \frac{g T^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 1.44}{6.283} = 2.248 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Iterative solution of dispersion (2 marks):** $\omega = 2\pi / 1.2 = 5.236$ rad/s, $\omega^2 = 27.42$ rad²/s². Equation: $k = \omega^2 / [g \tanh(kh)]$ - Iter 1: $k = 1.0$, $\tanh(1.5) = 0.9051$, $k_{new} = 27.42 / (9.81 \times 0.9051) = 3.088$ - Iter 2: $k = 3.0$, $\tanh(4.5) = 0.99975$, $k_{new} = 27.42 / 9.807 = 2.796$ - Iter 3: $k = 2.8$, $\tanh(4.2) \approx 0.99955$, $k_{new} = 27.42 / 9.805 = 2.797$ - **Converged: $k \approx 2.797$ rad/m** (1 mark) **Step 3 — Wavelength (1 mark):** $$\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{k} = \frac{6.283}{2.797} \approx 2.246 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Transfer function (1 mark):** $$T_p = \frac{4 \sinh(kh)}{2kh + \sinh(2kh)} = \frac{4 \sinh(4.196)}{2(4.196) + \sinh(8.392)}$$ $\sinh(4.196) \approx 33.19$, $\sinh(8.392) \approx 2200$ $$T_p \approx \frac{132.76}{8.392 + 2200} = \frac{132.76}{2208.4} \approx 0.0601$$ **Step 5 — Wave height (1 mark):** $$H = T_p \cdot S = 0.0601 \times 0.8 \approx 0.0481 \text{ m} \approx 4.81 \text{ cm}$$ **Final Answer:** $\lambda \approx 2.25$ m, $H \approx 4.8$ cm. **(Total: 7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the working of a **flap-type wave maker**. Discuss its advantages and limitations compared to a piston-type wave maker. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a **flap-type wave maker hinged at the bottom**, derive the expression for the wave height in terms of the angular displacement and water depth. Show that the wave maker is **ineffective in deep water**. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Flap Wave Maker Description (7 marks) **Working Principle (3 marks):** A flap wave maker consists of a rigid plate hinged at the seabed ($z = -h$) and rotated about the hinge by a servo or hydraulic actuator mounted on the overhead gantry. The angular displacement is: $$\theta(t) = \theta_{\max} \sin(\omega t)$$ The horizontal velocity at any depth $z$ on the flap face is: $$U_f(z, t) = \omega \theta_{\max} (z + h) \cos(\omega t)$$ The velocity is **zero at the hinge** and **maximum at the water surface**, producing a sheared (linear) velocity profile. This is the primary physical distinction from a piston, which produces a cosh-distributed velocity. **Schematic (1 mark):** [Show hinge at $z = -h$, plate inclined at angle $\theta$, free surface at $z = 0$, water depth $h$.] **Advantages (1.5 marks):** - Generates large waves in shallow water (high efficiency for $kh < 1$). - Realistic simulation of tsunami run-up and coastal inundation. - Lower peak forces on the actuator (force distributed over the rotation). **Limitations (1.5 marks):** - Ineffective in deep water ($T_f \to 0$ as $kh \to \infty$). - Cannot generate high-frequency (short-period) waves effectively. - Hinge seal and maintenance are engineering challenges. --- #### Part (b) — Flap Wave Maker Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Flap velocity distribution (1 mark):** $$U_f(z, t) = \omega \theta (z + h) \cos(\omega t) = \frac{S \omega}{h}(z + h) \cos(\omega t)$$ where $S = h \theta$ is the linear stroke at the water surface. **Step 2 — Apply Havelock's source method with non-uniform boundary condition (3 marks):** Replacing the flap with a source distribution $m(z')$, the boundary condition is: $$\int_{-h}^{0} m(z') \cdot G(0, z, z') \, dz' = \frac{S \omega}{h}(z + h) \cos(\omega t)$$ Using the orthogonality of $\cosh k(z+h)$ in the depth integration, the source strength is found as: $$m = \frac{2 S \omega^2 \cosh kh}{g k h} \cdot \frac{\cosh(kh) - \cosh(0)}{\frac{1}{2}[2kh + \sinh(2kh)]}$$ After integration and simplification (matching with the linear velocity profile $(z+h)$): **Step 3 — Final transfer function (2 marks):** $$\boxed{\frac{H}{\lambda} = \frac{4[\sinh(kh) - kh \cosh(kh)]}{kh[2kh + \sinh(2kh)]} \cdot \frac{S}{\lambda}}$$ **Step 4 — Deep-water limit (1 mark):** As $kh \to \infty$, using $\sinh(kh) \approx \cosh(kh) \approx \tfrac{1}{2}e^{kh}$: $$\sinh(kh) - kh \cosh(kh) \approx \frac{e^{kh}}{2}(1 - kh) \to -\infty \text{ for } kh \to \infty$$ More precisely, the transfer function: $$T_f = \frac{4[\sinh(kh) - kh \cosh(kh)]}{kh[2kh + \sinh(2kh)]} \to \frac{-4kh \cdot \cosh(kh)}{kh \cdot \sinh(2kh)} = \frac{-2}{\sinh(2kh)/(2\cosh kh) \cdot ...}$$ Evaluating the dominant terms: the numerator behaves as $-kh \cosh(kh)$ and the denominator as $kh \cdot \sinh(2kh)/2 = kh \cosh(kh) \sinh(kh)$. Thus $T_f \to -2 / \sinh(kh) \to 0$ as $kh \to \infty$. **Conclusion:** $|H| \to 0$ in deep water, confirming that the flap wave maker **cannot generate waves in deep water**. **(Total: 7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Wave Maker Questions** > 1. **Sign Error in Transfer Function:** Students often miss the sign in the flap transfer function. The numerator $[\sinh(kh) - kh\cosh(kh)]$ can be **negative** for large $kh$. The wave height $H$ is always positive — take the absolute value. > 2. **Confusing $S$ with $2a$:** The stroke $S$ is **peak-to-peak** displacement. Some textbooks use single-amplitude. Always clarify in the answer. > 3. **Forgetting to Iterate the Dispersion Relation:** Direct substitution of $L_0 = gT^2/2\pi$ as the actual wavelength is only valid in deep water. For intermediate depth, you **must iterate** $k = \omega^2 / [g \tanh(kh)]$. > 4. **Mixing Piston and Flap Formulas:** A frequent error is using the piston formula for a flap problem. Always check whether the question says "piston" or "flap / hinged" first. > 5. **Skipping Boundary Conditions:** KTU examiners award **1 full mark** for stating all five boundary conditions in a derivation. Skipping them costs a mark. > 6. **Ignoring Active Wave Absorption:** Modern wave makers use **force-feedback** or **velocity-feedback** control to absorb reflected waves. Mention this for full credit in design-type questions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - **Wave Maker = Oscillating Boundary Actuator** that enforces a velocity BC on the fluid domain. - **Two Primary Types:** Piston (translational) and Flap (rotational, hinged at bottom). - **Havelock's Source Method (1929):** Replaces wave maker with a continuous distribution of oscillating line sources. - **Biesel Transfer Function (1951):** Closed-form $H/\lambda$ vs. $S/\lambda$ relations for piston and flap. - **Piston Transfer Function:** $T_p = \dfrac{4 \sinh(kh)}{2kh + \sinh(2kh)}$. - **Flap Transfer Function:** $T_f = \dfrac{4[\sinh(kh) - kh\cosh(kh)]}{kh[2kh + \sinh(2kh)]}$. - **Deep Water ($kh \to \infty$):** Piston → $2/\pi$; Flap → $0$. - **Shallow Water ($kh \to 0$):** Piston → $2S/h$ (amplified); Flap → small but non-zero. - **Dispersion Relation (must always be satisfied):** $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$. - **Stroke $S$ = peak-to-peak displacement** of the paddle face. - **Active wave absorption** is essential to prevent re-reflection in long-duration tests. - **Wedge/Plunger wave makers** are used for short waves and air-driven systems. - **Segmented wave makers** combine piston and flap motions for broadband spectra. - **Energy balance:** $K_r^2 + K_t^2 + K_d = 1$ at any reflecting/transmitting structure. - **Most-tested concept in KTU:** Derivation of piston transfer function + numerical calculation of $H$ and $\lambda$. > **End of Module 1 Topic: Wave Makers — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**

topics.title: Planar Motion Mechanism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Planar Motion Mechanism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planar Motion Mechanism (PMM)**: A specialized **mechanical test apparatus** used in experimental marine hydrodynamics to execute prescribed, time-accurate motion trajectories of a ship or submarine model in a single horizontal plane (the *xy*-plane), so that the resulting **hydrodynamic forces and moments** can be measured and used to compute the **hydrodynamic derivatives** (added mass and damping coefficients) governing maneuvering equations.

The PMM is the bridge between **straight-line towing tests** (steady-state) and the need to evaluate **transient, time-dependent hydrodynamic responses** of a vessel undergoing typical maneuvers such as pure sway, pure yaw, or combined sway–yaw oscillation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Classification (ITTC – International Towing Tank Conference):**
> - **PMM in Pure Sway** ($y$-oscillation)
> - **PMM in Pure Yaw** ($\psi$-oscillation)
> - **PMM in Combined Sway–Yaw** (circular / elliptic trajectory)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are pushing a heavy office chair across a tiled floor. If you push it in a **straight, steady line**, you feel a roughly constant resistance — that is the *steady* drag. But if you suddenly **wiggle the chair sideways** (sway) or **spin it** (yaw), you feel an *extra* inertial push from the chair and a swirl of water/air around the wheels. That *extra* push is what the PMM is designed to measure precisely.

- The **chair** = ship model
- The **floor** = towing tank / circulating water channel
- The **wiggling motion** = prescribed oscillatory trajectory
- The **measured force** = hydrodynamic force used to back-calculate the **added mass** and **damping** coefficients

> [!TIP]
> A simpler way to remember it: *PMM = "wiggle the ship on purpose, and measure how the water pushes back."*

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Reduced Frequency** $k = \dfrac{\omega L}{U}$ : **dimensionless**, typically $0.3 \le k \le 1.5$ in PMM tests
- **Standard Model Length** $L$ : usually $3\text{ m}$ to $6\text{ m}$
- **Oscillation Amplitude** $y_0$ / $\psi_0$ : typically $1\text{ m}$ to $2\text{ m}$ (sway) and $15^\circ$ to $35^\circ$ (yaw)
- **Period of Oscillation** $T$ : $5\text{ s}$ to $15\text{ s}$
- **Density of water (fresh)** : $\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity (20 °C)** : $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Kinematic constraint of a PMM:** The mechanism must constrain the model to move in *one* horizontal plane, i.e., no heave ($z$), no roll ($\phi$), no pitch ($\theta$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Model Preparation** — A free-running ship model (typically $1:30$ to $1:50$ scale) is mounted on the PMM carriage with **sting / strut support**, ensuring the model's CG coincides with the PMM center of rotation.
2. **Prescribed Trajectory** — A computer-controlled actuator commands the model to follow a mathematically defined path:
   - Pure sway: $x = 0,\ y = y_0 \sin(\omega t)$
   - Pure yaw: $x = 0,\ \psi = \psi_0 \sin(\omega t)$
   - Combined: $x = R\sin(\omega t + \phi),\ y = R\cos(\omega t)$
3. **Forced Forward Speed (Optional)** — In some PMM designs (e.g., **oscillating carriage + towing carriage**), a steady carriage speed $U$ is superimposed.
4. **Force / Moment Measurement** — A 3- or 4-component dynamometer (strain gauge balance) records surge force $X$, sway force $Y$, and yaw moment $N$ as time histories.
5. **Data Reduction** — The recorded signals are fitted (via Fourier analysis or least-squares) to the linear maneuvering equation to extract hydrodynamic derivatives.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind PMM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard **rotating-arm (RA)** and **circular motion (CM)** tests yield **steady** derivatives only. They **cannot** capture **frequency-dependent** (transient) behavior. PMM closes this gap by performing **forced, controlled oscillation** at known $\omega$, allowing the **added mass** $m'_{ij}$ and **damping** $N'_{ij}$ to be resolved as functions of $\omega$ (or $k$).

## 2.3 Coordinate System and Sign Convention (SNU — Ship-fixed)

Adopting the standard **right-handed ship-fixed frame**:

- $x$ — forward (surge axis)
- $y$ — starboard (sway axis)
- $z$ — downward
- $u, v$ — surge and sway velocities
- $r = \dot{\psi}$ — yaw rate
- $X, Y$ — forces along $x, y$
- $N$ — moment about $z$

> [!WARNING]
> Sign convention errors in $\dot{v}$ and $\dot{r}$ terms cost marks! Always use **positive starboard** for $v$ and **positive turning to starboard** for $r$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers ≥ 60 % of KTU Part B marks on this topic.**

| # | Quantity / Equation | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sway displacement (pure sway PMM) | $y(t) = y_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | $y_0$ in m, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 2 | Sway velocity | $v(t) = \dot{y} = y_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)$ | m/s |
| 3 | Sway acceleration | $\dot{v}(t) = -y_0 \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$ | m/s$^2$ |
| 4 | Yaw displacement (pure yaw PMM) | $\psi(t) = \psi_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | rad |
| 5 | Yaw rate | $r(t) = \psi_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)$ | rad/s |
| 6 | Yaw acceleration | $\dot{r}(t) = -\psi_0 \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$ | rad/s$^2$ |
| 7 | Reduced frequency | $k = \omega L / U$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Circular path radius (combined) | $R$ | m |
| 9 | Surge force (maneuvering eq.) | $X = X_{\dot{u}}\dot{u} + X_{vr}vr + \dots$ | N |
| 10 | Sway force (maneuvering eq.) | $Y = Y_{\dot{v}}\dot{v} + Y_{\dot{r}}\dot{r} + Y_{v}v + Y_{r}r$ | N |
| 11 | Yaw moment (maneuvering eq.) | $N = N_{\dot{v}}\dot{v} + N_{\dot{r}}\dot{r} + N_{v}v + N_{r}r$ | N·m |
| 12 | **Added mass** (sway) | $m'_{22} = -Y_{\dot{v}}$ | kg |
| 13 | **Added mass** (yaw inertia) | $m'_{66} = -N_{\dot{r}}$ | kg·m$^2$ |
| 14 | **Cross-coupled added mass** | $m'_{26} = -Y_{\dot{r}}$, $\ m'_{62} = -N_{\dot{v}}$ | mixed units |
| 15 | **Damping** (sway) | $Y_{v} = -C_{Y_v}$ | N·s/m |
| 16 | **Damping** (yaw) | $N_{r} = -C_{N_r}$ | N·m·s |

> Use $\vert$ in plain text where absolute value is required; never break markdown table rows with raw $\vert$.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

PMM-derived derivatives feed directly into:
- **MMG (Mathematical Modeling Group) standard** for ship maneuvering prediction
- **4-DOF / 6-DOF maneuvering simulations** (e.g., turning circle, zigzag maneuvers)
- **Autonomous surface vessel (USV) controller design**
- **Submarine maneuverability audits** for naval certification
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sway Force in a Pure-Sway PMM Test

**Step 1 — Kinematic prescription.**
For a pure sway oscillation, the model is constrained so that $u = 0$, $r = 0$, and only $v$ varies with time:

$$
y(t) = y_0 \sin(\omega t)
$$

Differentiate once with respect to $t$ to get the sway velocity:

$$
v(t) = \frac{dy}{dt} = y_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

Differentiate again to get the sway acceleration:

$$
\dot{v}(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = -y_0 \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — Reduced linear maneuvering equation in sway.**
For small motions about $u=0$, the sway equation reduces to:

$$
Y = Y_{\dot{v}} \dot{v} + Y_v v
$$

> Reason: With $u=0$ and $r=0$ imposed, every term containing $u$, $r$, or their products vanishes from the linear model. The dominant remaining terms are the *inertial* $Y_{\dot v}$ and *damping* $Y_v$ contributions.

**Step 3 — Substitute kinematics into the force equation.**

$$
Y(t) = Y_{\dot v}\bigl(-y_0 \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)\bigr) + Y_v\bigl(y_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)\bigr)
$$

Group the $\sin$ and $\cos$ terms:

$$
Y(t) = -y_0 \omega^2 Y_{\dot v} \sin(\omega t) + y_0 \omega Y_v \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 4 — Experimental data reduction.**
The measured signal $Y(t)$ is decomposed into its first Fourier component:

$$
Y(t) = A_Y \cos(\omega t) + B_Y \sin(\omega t)
$$

Equating the coefficients of $\cos(\omega t)$ and $\sin(\omega t)$ between the model and the measurement:

$$
A_Y = y_0 \omega\, Y_v \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\,Y_v = \dfrac{A_Y}{y_0 \omega}\,}
$$

$$
B_Y = -y_0 \omega^2\, Y_{\dot v} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\,Y_{\dot v} = -\dfrac{B_Y}{y_0 \omega^2}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Dimensional check.**

- $A_Y$ has units of N. $y_0 \omega$ has units of m · rad/s = m/s. So $A_Y / (y_0 \omega)$ has units of N·s/m, which is the correct unit of $Y_v$ (a damping derivative). ✓
- $B_Y / (y_0 \omega^2)$ has units of N / (m · rad$^2$/s$^2$) = N·s$^2$/m = kg. So $-B_Y / (y_0 \omega^2)$ is in kg, which is the correct unit of $Y_{\dot v}$ (an added-mass derivative). ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Yaw Moment in a Pure-Yaw PMM Test

**Step 1 — Kinematics.**

$$
\psi(t) = \psi_0 \sin(\omega t)
$$

$$
r(t) = \dot{\psi} = \psi_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

$$
\dot{r}(t) = -\psi_0 \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — Reduced yaw equation** (with $u=v=0$):

$$
N = N_{\dot r} \dot{r} + N_r r
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
N(t) = N_{\dot r}\bigl(-\psi_0 \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)\bigr) + N_r\bigl(\psi_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Fourier fit and solve.**

$$
N(t) = A_N \cos(\omega t) + B_N \sin(\omega t)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N_r = \dfrac{A_N}{\psi_0 \omega}\,}\qquad \boxed{\,N_{\dot r} = -\dfrac{B_N}{\psi_0 \omega^2}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Solution Pattern

| Test type | Output signal | Solve for | Solve for |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure sway PMM | $Y(t)$ | $Y_v = A_Y/(y_0 \omega)$ | $Y_{\dot v} = -B_Y/(y_0 \omega^2)$ |
| Pure yaw PMM | $N(t)$ | $N_r = A_N/(\psi_0 \omega)$ | $N_{\dot r} = -B_N/(\psi_0 \omega^2)$ |
| Combined sway–yaw | $Y(t), N(t)$ | $Y_v, Y_r, N_v, N_r$ | $Y_{\dot v}, Y_{\dot r}, N_{\dot v}, N_{\dot r}$ |

> [!TIP]
> The **A-coefficient** (cosine part) always gives the **damping derivative**; the **B-coefficient** (sine part) always gives the **added-mass derivative** (with a sign flip). This single rule covers 80 % of KTU derivation questions.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem.** A ship model of length $L = 4 \text{ m}$ undergoes a pure-sway PMM test. The sway amplitude is $y_0 = 1.2 \text{ m}$ and the oscillation period is $T = 8 \text{ s}$. The measured sway force is:

$$
Y(t) = 850 \cos(\omega t) - 1200 \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{[N]}
$$

Compute $Y_v$ and $Y_{\dot v}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\omega$.**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{8} = 0.7854 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Read Fourier coefficients.**

$$
A_Y = 850 \text{ N}, \qquad B_Y = -1200 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Y_v$.**

$$
Y_v = \frac{A_Y}{y_0 \omega} = \frac{850}{1.2 \times 0.7854} = \frac{850}{0.9425} = 901.9 \text{ N·s/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $Y_{\dot v}$.**

$$
Y_{\dot v} = -\frac{B_Y}{y_0 \omega^2} = -\frac{(-1200)}{1.2 \times (0.7854)^2} = \frac{1200}{1.2 \times 0.6169} = \frac{1200}{0.7402} = 1621.2 \text{ kg}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The added mass in sway is typically **30 % – 80 %** of the model's physical mass. If your answer is wildly outside this range, re-check the sign of $B_Y$ and the units of $\omega^2$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PMM Test Apparatus — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Controller / Waveform Generator] --> B[Servo Actuator]
    B --> C[PMM Carriage / Carousel]
    C --> D[Ship Model on Sting]
    D --> E[Dynamometer / Balance]
    E --> F[Amplifier and DAQ]
    F --> G[Signal Conditioner]
    G --> H[Fourier Analyzer]
    H --> I[Hydrodynamic Derivative Output]
    F --> J[Oscilloscope Monitor]
    A --> K[Trigger and Sync Pulse]
    K --> F
    D --> L[Towing Tank Water Plane]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Controller** sends a sinusoidal command (e.g., $y_0 \sin(\omega t)$) to the **Servo Actuator**.
- The **Actuator** drives the **Carriage**, which forces the **Model** through the prescribed planar trajectory.
- The **Balance** measures the reaction forces and moments.
- The **DAQ** digitises the signals, which are then **Fourier-analysed** to yield the **derivatives**.

## 4.2 PMM Test Sequence — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Mount Model] --> P2[Step 2: Calibrate Balance]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Set Amplitude and Omega]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Run Pure Sway Test]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Run Pure Yaw Test]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Run Combined Sway Yaw Test]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Record X Y N Time Histories]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Fourier Decompose]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Extract Derivatives]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Repeat for Multiple Frequencies]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Coupling Schematic (Sway–Yaw Interaction)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sway_Input
        v[sway velocity v]
        vdot[sway acceleration v dot]
    end
    subgraph Yaw_Input
        r[yaw rate r]
        rdot[yaw acceleration r dot]
    end
    v --> YvTerm[Yv v : damping]
    vdot --> YvdotTerm[Yvdot v dot : added mass]
    r --> YrTerm[Yr r : cross damping]
    rdot --> YrdotTerm[Yrdot r dot : cross added mass]
    YvTerm --> Yforce[Sway Force Y]
    YvdotTerm --> Yforce
    YrTerm --> Yforce
    YrdotTerm --> Yforce
    v --> NvTerm[Nv v : cross coupling]
    vdot --> NvdotTerm[Nvdot v dot : cross added mass]
    r --> NrTerm[Nr r : yaw damping]
    rdot --> NrdotTerm[Nrdot r dot : yaw added mass]
    NvTerm --> Nmoment[Yaw Moment N]
    NvdotTerm --> Nmoment
    NrTerm --> Nmoment
    NrdotTerm --> Nmoment
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice that **every input contributes to both outputs**. This cross-coupling is the reason **combined sway–yaw PMM** is essential — pure-sway or pure-yaw tests alone cannot separate the four derivatives $Y_{\dot v}, Y_{\dot r}, N_{\dot v}, N_{\dot r}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

### Q1. Define a Planar Motion Mechanism. State the two standard types of pure PMM tests.
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Planar Motion Mechanism (PMM)** is a controlled-motion test rig used in marine hydrodynamics to perform prescribed, time-accurate oscillations of a ship model in a single horizontal plane, while a multi-component balance measures the resulting hydrodynamic forces and moments. The two standard pure tests are:

1. **Pure Sway PMM** — model oscillates transversely with $y(t) = y_0 \sin(\omega t)$, $u = r = 0$. Used to obtain $Y_{\dot v}, Y_v$ (and via cross-tests $Y_{\dot r}, Y_r$).
2. **Pure Yaw PMM** — model rotates in heading with $\psi(t) = \psi_0 \sin(\omega t)$, $u = v = 0$. Used to obtain $N_{\dot r}, N_r$ (and via cross-tests $N_{\dot v}, N_v$).

*(End — 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for the two types.)*

### Q2. What is "reduced frequency" $k$ in PMM testing, and why is it important?
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The reduced (or non-dimensional) frequency is defined as:

$$
k = \frac{\omega L}{U}
$$

where $\omega$ is the angular frequency of oscillation, $L$ is the model length, and $U$ is the forward speed (or, in stationary-PMM designs, a reference speed). It is important because:

1. It **collapses the test matrix**: results at any $L$ and $U$ collapse onto a single $k$-curve, enabling extrapolation to full scale.
2. It governs the **regime**: small $k$ ($\lt 0.3$) is quasi-steady; large $k$ ($> 1.0$) is strongly unsteady.
3. It controls the **relative importance of added mass vs. damping** in the response.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three points above.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive, starting from the linear sway-equation, the expressions for the sway damping derivative $Y_v$ and the sway added-mass derivative $Y_{\dot v}$ in a pure-sway PMM test. Clearly state the kinematic input and the Fourier decomposition used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A model of length $L = 5$ m is oscillated in pure sway with amplitude $y_0 = 1.5$ m and period $T = 10$ s. The measured sway force is:

$$
Y(t) = 1450 \cos(\omega t) - 1850 \sin(\omega t) \text{ N}
$$

Compute $Y_v$ and $Y_{\dot v}$. Comment on the physical meaning of the sign of $Y_{\dot v}$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**1. Kinematic input** (1 mark):

$$
y(t) = y_0 \sin(\omega t), \quad v = y_0 \omega \cos(\omega t), \quad \dot v = -y_0 \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)
$$

**2. Reduced linear sway equation** (1 mark):

$$
Y = Y_{\dot v}\, \dot v + Y_v\, v
$$

(All other terms vanish since $u = 0$ and $r = 0$.)

**3. Substitution** (2 marks):

$$
Y(t) = -y_0 \omega^2 Y_{\dot v}\sin(\omega t) + y_0 \omega Y_v \cos(\omega t)
$$

**4. Fourier fit** (1 mark):

$$
Y(t) = A_Y \cos(\omega t) + B_Y \sin(\omega t)
$$

**5. Coefficient matching & final answers** (2 marks):

$$
\boxed{\,Y_v = \dfrac{A_Y}{y_0 \omega}\,}\qquad \boxed{\,Y_{\dot v} = -\dfrac{B_Y}{y_0 \omega^2}\,}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Angular frequency** (1 mark):

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{10} = 0.6283 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Fourier coefficients** (1 mark):

$$
A_Y = 1450 \text{ N}, \quad B_Y = -1850 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Y_v$** (2 marks):

$$
Y_v = \frac{1450}{1.5 \times 0.6283} = \frac{1450}{0.9425} = 1538.5 \text{ N·s/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $Y_{\dot v}$** (2 marks):

$$
Y_{\dot v} = -\frac{(-1850)}{1.5 \times (0.6283)^2} = \frac{1850}{1.5 \times 0.3948} = \frac{1850}{0.5922} = 3124.0 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Sign comment** (1 mark):

> $Y_{\dot v} > 0$ is the *conventionally reported* sway added mass; physically, it means that a positive sway acceleration requires a *positive* external force, i.e., the **fluid resists acceleration** in the same way an added inertial mass would. The magnitude (~3124 kg) is reasonable for a 5 m model.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **pure-yaw Planar Motion Mechanism**. State the linear yaw equation used and derive expressions for $N_r$ and $N_{\dot r}$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A pure-yaw PMM is performed on a model with $L = 4$ m, yaw amplitude $\psi_0 = 20^\circ$ and period $T = 6$ s. The recorded yaw moment is:

$$
N(t) = 920 \cos(\omega t) - 640 \sin(\omega t) \text{ N·m}
$$

Find $N_r$ and $N_{\dot r}$. Hence comment whether the model is **directionally stable**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**1. Description + sketch notes** (2 marks):

In a pure-yaw PMM, the model is mounted on a vertical post whose rotation about the vertical axis is driven by a servomotor through a prescribed $\psi(t) = \psi_0 \sin(\omega t)$. Surge and sway translational velocities are zero; only heading changes. The yaw moment is recorded by a balance beneath the post.

**2. Kinematics** (1 mark):

$$
r = \psi_0 \omega \cos(\omega t), \quad \dot r = -\psi_0 \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)
$$

**3. Linear yaw equation** (1 mark):

$$
N = N_{\dot r}\, \dot r + N_r\, r
$$

**4. Substitution and Fourier fit** (2 marks):

$$
N(t) = -\psi_0 \omega^2 N_{\dot r}\sin(\omega t) + \psi_0 \omega N_r \cos(\omega t)
$$

$$
N(t) = A_N \cos(\omega t) + B_N \sin(\omega t)
$$

**5. Final answers** (1 mark):

$$
\boxed{\,N_r = \dfrac{A_N}{\psi_0 \omega}\,} \qquad \boxed{\,N_{\dot r} = -\dfrac{B_N}{\psi_0 \omega^2}\,}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Step 1 — Convert angle to radians** (1 mark):

$$
\psi_0 = 20^\circ = \frac{20 \pi}{180} = 0.3491 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular frequency** (1 mark):

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi}{6} = 1.0472 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Coefficients** (0.5 mark):

$$
A_N = 920 \text{ N·m}, \quad B_N = -640 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — $N_r$** (1.5 marks):

$$
N_r = \frac{920}{0.3491 \times 1.0472} = \frac{920}{0.3656} = 2516.4 \text{ N·m·s}
$$

**Step 5 — $N_{\dot r}$** (1.5 marks):

$$
N_{\dot r} = -\frac{(-640)}{0.3491 \times (1.0472)^2} = \frac{640}{0.3491 \times 1.0966} = \frac{640}{0.3828} = 1671.9 \text{ kg·m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Directional stability comment** (1.5 marks):

> A ship is **directionally stable** if a small yaw disturbance is **restoring**, i.e., the damping derivative $N_r < 0$ (in the *standard sign convention* with origin at midship). Because the *recorded* moment $N$ and the model's velocity $r$ are in the same direction here, $N_r$ as computed by the formula above is **positive**, indicating a **destabilising** yaw moment for the actual ship; the model is therefore **directionally unstable** at this frequency. (Alternatively, the evaluator may accept the convention used in the syllabus — always state your sign convention explicitly in the answer.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in $\psi_0$ — costs 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Sign error in $Y_{\dot v}$ / $N_{\dot r}$** — the formula has a *minus* sign in front of $B$. Marked strictly.
> 3. **Mixing up damping (cosine) and added-mass (sine) coefficients** — write $A_Y$ for damping, $B_Y$ for added mass explicitly.
> 4. **Missing units in the final answer** — full marks require N·s/m for damping and kg (or kg·m²) for added mass.
> 5. **Not stating the assumed sign convention** — write "positive starboard / positive turning to starboard" at the start of the solution.

---
