## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and read it the night before the exam.**

- [x] **PMM = controlled oscillation in 1 horizontal plane**, used to measure transient hydrodynamic forces.
- [x] **Three standard tests:** Pure Sway, Pure Yaw, Combined Sway–Yaw.
- [x] **Kinematics** (memorise):
  - Sway: $y = y_0 \sin\omega t$, $v = y_0 \omega \cos\omega t$, $\dot v = -y_0 \omega^2 \sin\omega t$.
  - Yaw: $\psi = \psi_0 \sin\omega t$, $r = \psi_0 \omega \cos\omega t$, $\dot r = -\psi_0 \omega^2 \sin\omega t$.
- [x] **Reduced frequency:** $k = \omega L / U$ — dimensionless, controls regime.
- [x] **Maneuvering equations** (linearised):
  - $Y = Y_{\dot v}\dot v + Y_{\dot r}\dot r + Y_v v + Y_r r$
  - $N = N_{\dot v}\dot v + N_{\dot r}\dot r + N_v v + N_r r$
- [x] **Solution pattern** (the *golden rule*):
  - Cosine coefficient $A$ → **damping** derivative (no sign change).
  - Sine coefficient $B$ → **added-mass** derivative (with a sign **flip**).
- [x] **Always state the sign convention** explicitly at the start.
- [x] **Units to remember:** Damping → N·s/m (force eq.) or N·m·s (moment eq.); Added mass → kg or kg·m².
- [x] **Why PMM over rotating arm?** Captures **frequency-dependent** added mass; rotating arm gives only steady values.
- [x] **Typical KTU marks weightage:** Part A: 1–2 questions × 3 marks; Part B: 1 question × 14 marks (often sub-parts of pure sway + calculation).
- [x] **Cross-coupling terms** $Y_{\dot r}$ and $N_{\dot v}$ are non-zero in real ships and **only** obtainable from **combined** PMM tests.
- [x] **Common mistakes:** forgetting radians, sign flips, omitting units, confusing $A$ and $B$.

> **End of Module 1 — Planar Motion Mechanism Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seakeeping and Maneuvering Experiments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Seakeeping and Maneuvering Experiments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Seakeeping Experiments

**Seakeeping** is formally defined as the **performance of a ship in waves**, encompassing all aspects of its motion, structural loads, and habitability when subjected to realistic ocean wave environments. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *seakeeping experiments* refer to the systematic laboratory and field investigations performed to measure, predict, and validate a vessel's behavior under wave excitation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> *Seakeeping experiments* involve the controlled testing of ship models in towing tanks, wave basins, and sea trials to determine the **motion responses** (heave, pitch, roll, surge, sway, yaw) of a vessel in regular and irregular (random) sea states.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a small country boat in a choppy harbor. The boat pitches (nose up-down), rolls (side-to-side tilting), and heaves (bobs up-down). If you were an engineer, you would want to **measure exactly how much** the boat tilts, how fast it bobs, and whether it stays comfortable for passengers. **Seakeeping experiments** are the formal, scaled versions of this observation — engineers build **small-scale models** (typically 1:20 to 1:50 scale) and tow them in **long water tanks equipped with wave generators**, then use sensors (accelerometers, gyroscopes, wave probes) to record the motions.

The key insight is that ship motions are governed by the **Froude number** (ratio of inertial to gravitational forces), which is the dominant similarity parameter for free-surface flows. Thus, model results can be reliably scaled to full-scale ships.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Constant for Seakeeping Scaling:**
> - **Standard gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **Water density (fresh):** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Water density (sea):** $\rho \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$

---

## 1.2 Introduction to Maneuvering Experiments

**Maneuverability** is defined as the ability of a vessel to **change its course and speed** as required for safe navigation, port entry, collision avoidance, and operation in restricted waters. **Maneuvering experiments** are the controlled tests designed to quantify this ability through standardized procedures defined by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO) Standards for Ship Maneuverability (Resolution MSC.137(76))**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Picture a heavy loaded truck backing into a tight loading dock. The driver needs to know:
- How **wide a circle** it will trace when turning at full rudder (turning circle).
- How **quickly** it responds to sudden steering changes (zig-zag test).
- Whether it remains stable when held on a constant turn angle (spiral test).

**Maneuvering experiments** apply exactly the same logic to ships. The ship is treated as a "vehicle with 6 degrees of freedom," and the test engineer measures how the hull, rudder, and propeller interact to produce controlled turning. These tests are typically performed using either:
1. **Free-running models** (the model is self-propelled and free to move in the basin), or
2. **Captive models** (the model is held by a robotic arm that forces it through prescribed motions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Two Distinct Families of Maneuvering Tests:**
> - **Free-Running Tests:** Turning Circle (Turning Diameter), Zig-Zag (10°/10° and 20°/20°), Pull-Out, Spiral, Reverse, Stopping.
> - **Captive Tests:** Planar Motion Mechanism (PMM), Circular Motion Mechanism (CMM), Rotating Arm (RA), and oblique towing tests.

---

## 1.3 Why These Experiments Matter (Engineering Relevance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility Statement (High-Yield for KTU Viva):**
> Seakeeping and maneuvering experiments are **mandatory deliverables** in any ship design cycle. They feed directly into:
> - **Classification society approval** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS)
> - **Comfort criteria** for passenger vessels (motion sickness incidence, MSI)
> - **Operability windows** (sea state limits for naval/merchant vessels)
> - **Slamming and green-water loading** assessment
> - **Rudder and propeller design** optimization
> - **Autonomous vessel control algorithm** validation
> - **IMO regulatory compliance** (MSC.137(76) maneuverability standards)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Seakeeping Experiments — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Types of Seakeeping Experiments

The KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus emphasizes the following classification of seakeeping experiments:

**(a) Regular Wave Tests (Deterministic)**
- Ship model towed at constant speed in **monochromatic (single-frequency) waves**.
- Wave height $H$, wavelength $\lambda$, and forward speed $U$ are held constant.
- Output: **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)** as a function of encounter frequency.

**(b) Irregular Wave Tests (Stochastic)**
- Ship model subjected to a **spectrum of waves** (Pierson-Moskowitz, JONSWAP, or Bretschneider).
- Output: Motion spectra, significant motion amplitudes, statistical exceedance probabilities.

**(c) Captive Model Tests**
- Model held **rigidly at a fixed position** while subjected to waves.
- Output: **Hydrodynamic derivatives** (added mass, damping, exciting forces).
- Conducted in specialized basins with multi-axis force balances.

**(d) Free-Running Model Tests**
- Model is **self-propelled**, controlled by an onboard autopilot or radio control.
- Output: Realistic 6-DOF motions in waves (seakeeping + maneuvering coupled).

**(e) Full-Scale Sea Trials**
- Conducted on the actual ship at sea, using accelerometers, gyrocompasses, GPS.
- Used to validate scaled model predictions and CFD simulations.

### 2.1.2 The Role of Scaling Laws

For free-surface flows where gravity dominates over viscosity and surface tension, the **Froude similarity** is the primary scaling law:

$$\text{Fr}_m = \text{Fr}_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}$$

This simplifies to the **velocity scale**:

$$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$$

where $\lambda = L_m / L_s$ is the geometric scale ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall Warning:** Froude scaling alone does **not** perfectly replicate Reynolds number similarity. For seakeeping, Reynolds effects on viscous roll damping and frictional resistance are **secondary** compared to gravitational effects, and are typically **corrected empirically** (e.g., ITTC 1978 friction correction).

### 2.1.3 Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)

The RAO is the **transfer function** between the wave amplitude and the resulting ship motion amplitude:

$$\text{RAO}(\omega_e) = \frac{\zeta_a}{A_w} = \frac{\text{Motion Amplitude}}{\text{Wave Amplitude}}$$

where:
- $\omega_e$ = **encounter frequency** (rad/s)
- $\zeta_a$ = amplitude of ship motion in a particular DOF
- $A_w$ = incident wave amplitude

The **encounter frequency** accounts for the Doppler effect due to ship forward speed:

$$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 U \cos\beta}{g}$$

where:
- $\omega$ = absolute wave frequency
- $U$ = ship forward speed
- $\beta$ = angle between ship heading and wave direction (encounter angle)

### 2.1.4 Sea Spectra Used in Irregular Seakeeping

The most common wave spectrum is the **Pierson-Moskowitz (PM) spectrum** for fully developed seas:

$$S(\omega) = \frac{A}{\omega^5} \exp\left(-\frac{B}{\omega^4}\right)$$

where $A = 0.0081 g^2$ and $B = 0.74 (g/U_{19.5})^4$ with $U_{19.5}$ being the wind speed at 19.5 m elevation.

The more realistic **JONSWAP spectrum** adds a peak enhancement factor:

$$S_{J}(\omega) = \alpha g^2 \omega^{-5} \exp\left(-\frac{5}{4}\left(\frac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^4\right) \gamma^{\exp\left(-\frac{(\omega-\omega_p)^2}{2\sigma^2 \omega_p^2}\right)}$$

where:
- $\omega_p$ = peak frequency
- $\gamma$ = peak enhancement factor (typically 1 to 7, mean ≈ 3.3)
- $\sigma$ = spectral width parameter

---

## 2.2 Maneuvering Experiments — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Captive Model Test Mechanisms

**(a) Planar Motion Mechanism (PMM)**
- Forces the model to follow a prescribed horizontal trajectory (pure sway, pure yaw, or combined).
- The model is **not allowed to surge** during the test.
- Used to measure **linear and nonlinear hydrodynamic derivatives** $Y_v, Y_r, N_v, N_r, Y_{v\vert v\vert}, N_{v\vert v\vert}, N_{rrr}$, etc.

**(b) Circular Motion Mechanism (CMM) / Rotating Arm**
- The model is mounted on a **rotating arm** and moves in a circular path.
- The **turning radius R** and **angular velocity $\dot{\psi}$** are prescribed.
- Used to measure forces and moments at **steady oblique drift angles**.

**(c) Oblique Towing Test**
- The model is towed at a constant speed at a fixed **drift angle $\beta$**.
- Simpler than CMM; measures steady side force and yaw moment.

### 2.2.2 Free-Running Maneuvering Tests (IMO Standard)

The **Turning Circle Test** is the most fundamental IMO maneuver:

| Parameter | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Advance** ($A_d$) | Distance traveled in the original direction when the ship has turned 90° |
| **Transfer** ($T_r$) | Lateral displacement at 90° heading change |
| **Tactical Diameter** ($D_T$) | Distance traveled perpendicular to original course when ship has turned 180° |
| **Final Diameter** ($D_F$) | Steady-state turning circle diameter |
| **Steady Turn Radius** ($R$) | Radius of the steady turning circle |

The **Zig-Zag Test** measures course-keeping and counter-ruddering ability:
- **10°/10° Zig-Zag**: Rudder is reversed when heading deviates by 10°; overshoot angles are measured.
- **20°/20° Zig-Zag**: Higher rudder-angle maneuver.
- IMO requires $10°/10°$ first overshoot angle $\leq 10°$ (for cargo ships) and $20°/20°$ second overshoot $\leq 25°$.

The **Spiral Test** evaluates **directional stability**:
- Rudder is set to fixed angles progressively.
- The resulting steady turn rate is measured.
- A stable ship returns to straight motion when rudder is centered.

The **Pull-Out Test** measures the ability to **straighten out from a steady turn**.

The **Stopping Test** measures track reach and heading deviation during emergency stop.

### 2.2.3 Non-Dimensional Maneuvering Derivatives

Captive test results are expressed as **non-dimensional hydrodynamic derivatives** (Kijima notation):

$$Y'_{\beta} = \frac{Y_{\beta}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^2 U^2}, \quad N'_{\beta} = \frac{N_{\beta}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^3 U^2}$$

$$Y'_{r} = \frac{Y_r}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^3 U}, \quad N'_{r} = \frac{N_r}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^4 U}$$

These derivatives feed into the **Manoeuvring Mathematical Model Group (MMG)**, a set of coupled ODEs:

$$m(\dot{v} + U r) = Y_{\beta}\beta + Y_r r + Y_{\delta}\delta + \dots$$

$$I_{zz}\dot{r} = N_{\beta}\beta + N_r r + N_{\delta}\delta + \dots$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.1 — Seakeeping and Maneuvering Key Formulas (KTU 2024 Module 1)**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Froude velocity scaling | m/s |
| 2 | $T_m = T_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Time scaling (Froude) | s |
| 3 | $F_m = F_s \lambda^3$ | Force scaling (Froude) | N |
| 4 | $\omega_e = \omega - \dfrac{\omega^2 U \cos\beta}{g}$ | Encounter frequency | rad/s |
| 5 | $\text{RAO} = \zeta_a / A_w$ | Response Amplitude Operator | dimensionless |
| 6 | $S_{PM}(\omega) = \dfrac{A}{\omega^5}\exp\left(-\dfrac{B}{\omega^4}\right)$ | Pierson-Moskowitz spectrum | m²·s |
| 7 | $H_{s} = 4 \sqrt{m_0}$ | Significant wave height | m |
| 8 | $T_z = 2\pi \sqrt{m_0 / m_2}$ | Mean zero-upcrossing period | s |
| 9 | $A_d / L \leq 4.5$ | IMO turning advance criterion | dimensionless |
| 10 | $D_T / L \leq 5.0$ | IMO tactical diameter criterion | dimensionless |
| 11 | $Y'_{\beta} = \dfrac{Y_{\beta}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^2 U^2}$ | Non-dim. sway force derivative | dimensionless |
| 12 | $N'_{\beta} = \dfrac{N_{\beta}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^3 U^2}$ | Non-dim. yaw moment derivative | dimensionless |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Experiments

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application Block:**
> - **Naval Architecture Design Loop:** Concept → Lines plan → CFD → Model tests (seakeeping + maneuvering) → Full-scale validation → Class approval.
> - **Offshore Industry:** FPSO roll motion and operability windows determined by seakeeping tests.
> - **Yacht Design:** Comfort and motion-sickness criteria (Lin and Huang) calibrated using model tests.
> - **Autonomous Ships:** Maneuvering derivatives feed Model Predictive Control (MPC) algorithms.
> - **Defense Applications:** Submarine maneuverability assessed using rotating-arm facilities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Froude Similarity and Time Scaling

**Problem Statement:** A model ship of length $L_m = 3$ m is to be tested in a towing tank to represent a full-scale vessel of length $L_s = 120$ m. The full-scale service speed is $V_s = 20$ knots. Determine (a) the model speed, (b) the time scale, and (c) the expected period of ship pitch in waves.

**Step 1 — Convert service speed to SI units:**

$$V_s = 20 \text{ knots} = 20 \times 0.5144 = 10.288 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute geometric scale ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_m}{L_s} = \frac{3}{120} = \frac{1}{40}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Froude velocity scaling:**

For Froude similarity between model and full scale:

$$\frac{V_m^2}{g L_m} = \frac{V_s^2}{g L_s}$$

$$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$V_m = 10.288 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{40}} = 10.288 \times 0.15811 = 1.6267 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the time scale:**

Since time scales as $\sqrt{\lambda}$ in Froude similarity:

$$T_m = T_s \sqrt{\lambda} = T_s \times 0.15811$$

**Step 5 — Example: Pitch period at full scale $T_s = 8$ s:**

$$T_m = 8 \times 0.15811 = 1.2649 \text{ s} \approx 1.27 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Force scaling verification:**

Forces scale as $\rho V^2 L^2$, hence:

$$F_m = F_s \lambda^3 = F_s \times \left(\frac{1}{40}\right)^3 = \frac{F_s}{64{,}000}$$

This is why model forces are tiny (often Newtons) and require high-precision load cells.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Encounter Frequency Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A ship of length $L = 200$ m sails at forward speed $U = 15$ knots into head seas ($\beta = 180°$). The wave has absolute period $T = 9$ s. Calculate the encounter frequency.

**Step 1 — Convert speed to SI:**

$$U = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute absolute wave frequency:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{9} = 0.6981 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the encounter frequency formula:**

$$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 U \cos\beta}{g}$$

For head seas, $\beta = 180°$, so $\cos\beta = -1$:

$$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 U (-1)}{g} = \omega + \frac{\omega^2 U}{g}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and compute:**

$$\omega_e = 0.6981 + \frac{(0.6981)^2 \times 7.716}{9.81}$$

$$\omega_e = 0.6981 + \frac{0.4874 \times 7.716}{9.81}$$

$$\omega_e = 0.6981 + \frac{3.7605}{9.81}$$

$$\omega_e = 0.6981 + 0.3833 = 1.0814 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**

The encounter frequency $\omega_e = 1.0814$ rad/s is **higher** than the absolute wave frequency. This is because the ship is moving *into* the waves, meeting wave crests more frequently. This increased frequency can push the ship closer to its **natural roll/pitch resonance**, intensifying motions — a critical design concern.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: RAO Determination from Captive Test

**Problem Statement:** In a regular wave test, a model is subjected to a wave of amplitude $A_w = 0.05$ m and frequency $\omega = 0.6$ rad/s. The measured heave motion has amplitude $\zeta_{a,heave} = 0.04$ m. Compute the heave RAO at this frequency.

**Step 1 — Define RAO:**

$$\text{RAO}_{heave}(\omega) = \frac{\zeta_{a,heave}}{A_w}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\text{RAO}_{heave} = \frac{0.04}{0.05} = 0.8$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The RAO of 0.8 means the ship heaves with **80% of the wave amplitude** at this frequency. A RAO > 1 indicates **motion amplification** (the ship is heaving more than the wave itself, typical near resonance). A RAO < 1 indicates **motion attenuation** (typical at high frequencies where the ship cannot follow the wave).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: IMO Turning Circle Compliance Check

**Problem Statement:** A cargo vessel of length $L_{pp} = 150$ m completes a turning circle test at $35°$ rudder angle. The measured advance is $A_d = 620$ m, tactical diameter $D_T = 700$ m, and final diameter $D_F = 580$ m. Check IMO compliance (MSC.137(76)).

**Step 1 — IMO Reference Criteria for Cargo Ships (Length $\geq$ 100 m):**

| Criterion | IMO Limit |
|---|---|
| Advance / $L_{pp}$ | $\leq 4.5$ |
| Tactical Diameter / $L_{pp}$ | $\leq 5.0$ |
| Final Diameter / $L_{pp}$ (recommended) | $\leq 3.0$ (some classes) |

**Step 2 — Compute non-dimensional values:**

$$\frac{A_d}{L_{pp}} = \frac{620}{150} = 4.133 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4.133 \leq 4.5 \;\;\checkmark$$

$$\frac{D_T}{L_{pp}} = \frac{700}{150} = 4.667 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4.667 \leq 5.0 \;\;\checkmark$$

$$\frac{D_F}{L_{pp}} = \frac{580}{150} = 3.867 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3.867 > 3.0 \;\;\text{(recommended limit exceeded)}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

The vessel **passes the advance and tactical diameter criteria** but **exceeds the recommended final diameter** criterion. This is a soft warning — the class society may require **rudder or skeg modifications** to bring $D_F / L_{pp}$ below 3.0.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Significant Wave Height from Spectrum

**Problem Statement:** A Pierson-Moskowitz spectrum has the following zeroth and second spectral moments: $m_0 = 4.5 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{s}$ and $m_2 = 0.35 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$. Compute $H_s$ and $T_z$.

**Step 1 — Significant wave height:**

$$H_s = 4 \sqrt{m_0} = 4 \sqrt{4.5} = 4 \times 2.1213 = 8.485 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Mean zero-upcrossing period:**

$$T_z = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{4.5}{0.35}} = 2\pi \times 3.5864 = 22.53 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The significant wave height of 8.5 m represents the **average of the highest 1/3 of waves**, which is the standard metric for operational sea state. The 22.5 s period indicates a **swell-dominated sea**, typical of remote ocean basins.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Zig-Zag Overshoot Calculation (Linearized)

For a 10°/10° zig-zag test on a ship with course-keeping derivatives, the **first overshoot** $\psi_{os,1}$ can be estimated from:

$$\psi_{os,1} \approx \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{(Y'_{\beta}/N'_{\beta} - U/L)^2 + (U/L \cdot Y'_{r}/N'_{\beta})^2}}$$

For a representative cargo ship with $Y'_{\beta} = -0.18$, $N'_{\beta} = -0.05$, $Y'_r = -0.04$, $N'_r = -0.02$:

$$\psi_{os,1} \approx \frac{0.175 \text{ rad}}{\sqrt{(-0.18/-0.05 - 1)^2 + (1 \times -0.04/-0.05)^2}}$$

$$= \frac{0.175}{\sqrt{(3.6 - 1)^2 + 0.8^2}} = \frac{0.175}{\sqrt{6.76 + 0.64}} = \frac{0.175}{\sqrt{7.4}} = \frac{0.175}{2.720}$$

$$\psi_{os,1} \approx 0.0643 \text{ rad} = 3.68°$$

Since the IMO limit for first overshoot is $10°$, this vessel **comfortably meets the standard**.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Seakeeping vs. Maneuvering Tests

| Attribute | Seakeeping Tests | Maneuvering Tests |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Input | Waves (regular or irregular) | Rudder angle, propeller RPM |
| Primary Output | Motion amplitudes (RAO), accelerations | Turning radius, overshoot angles |
| Dominant Force | Gravity, hydrodynamic (Froude) | Hydrodynamic, thrust (Froude + Re) |
| Test Basin | Long towing tank or wide wave basin | Shallow maneuvering basin |
| Free-Running Capable? | Yes (preferred) | Yes (IMO standard) |
| Captive Variant | Forced oscillation in waves | PMM, CMM, oblique tow |
| Standard Reference | ITTC, ISO 2631 | IMO MSC.137(76) |
| Typical Scale | 1:30 to 1:50 | 1:20 to 1:40 |
| Validation Output | Motion spectra, operability | Course records, turning circles |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]`

**Define seakeeping. List any four motions of a ship in regular head waves.**

**Model Answer:**

**Seakeeping** is defined as the behavior of a vessel in a seaway, encompassing its **motions, accelerations, structural loads, and habitability** when subjected to ocean waves.

The four principal ship motions in regular head waves are:

1. **Heave** — vertical translation (up-down) of the center of gravity.
2. **Pitch** — rotational motion about the transverse (Y) axis (bow up-down).
3. **Surge** — longitudinal translation (forward-backward) along the X-axis.
4. **Roll** — rotational motion about the longitudinal (X) axis (side-to-side tilt).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip (3-Mark Marking Scheme):** Definition = 1 mark, listing 4 motions with one-line explanation = 2 marks (0.5 per motion).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Bank]`

**What is a captive model test? Name two types of captive test mechanisms used in maneuvering experiments.**

**Model Answer:**

A **captive model test** is an experimental procedure in which the ship model is **physically constrained by an external mechanism** so that it cannot freely translate or rotate as it would at full scale. The mechanism imposes prescribed motions, and the **forces and moments** acting on the hull are measured using multi-axis load cells.

Two types of captive test mechanisms are:

1. **Planar Motion Mechanism (PMM)** — forces the model to undergo pure sway, pure yaw, or combined horizontal motion. The model is typically **non-translating in the surge direction** during the test.
2. **Circular Motion Mechanism (CMM) / Rotating Arm (RA)** — the model is mounted on a horizontal arm rotating about a vertical axis, simulating **steady turning at a fixed drift angle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Many students lose 1 mark by **forgetting to mention force measurement** as the primary purpose of captive tests. Always state that the output is **forces/moments, not trajectories**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the experimental procedure for determining the **heave and pitch Response Amplitude Operators (RAOs)** of a ship model in regular head waves. State the scaling law and the formula for encounter frequency.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A model of length $L_m = 4$ m is tested in head waves. The model speed is $V_m = 1.8$ m/s, the wave amplitude is $A_w = 0.04$ m, and the measured heave amplitude is $\zeta_a = 0.032$ m. The full-scale ship length is $L_s = 200$ m, and the prototype service speed is $V_s = 18$ knots. Compute (i) the model Froude number, (ii) the heave RAO, and (iii) the corresponding full-scale heave amplitude when full-scale waves have $A_{w,s} = 2.5$ m.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

The experimental determination of heave and pitch RAOs proceeds in the following stages:

1. **Model Preparation:** Build a geometrically similar model at scale $\lambda = L_m / L_s$ (typically 1:30 to 1:50). Install motion sensors (linear accelerometers for heave; rate gyros or inclinometers for pitch).

2. **Towing Tank Setup:** Position the model on the carriage of a **wave basin or long towing tank** equipped with a programmable **wavemaker**. Calibrate the wavemaker to generate **monochromatic regular waves** of prescribed amplitude $A_w$ and frequency $\omega$.

3. **Test Execution:** For each test point, the model is towed at a constant speed $V_m$ determined by **Froude similarity**. The wavemaker generates regular head waves ($\beta = 180°$). After transients die out, the **steady-state amplitudes** of heave $z_a$ and pitch $\theta_a$ are recorded.

4. **Data Reduction:** Compute RAO as:

$$\text{RAO}_{heave}(\omega_e) = \frac{z_a}{A_w}, \quad \text{RAO}_{pitch}(\omega_e) = \frac{\theta_a}{k A_w}$$

where $k = \omega^2 / g$ is the wave number.

5. **Repeat** for a range of frequencies $\omega$ (typically 0.2 to 2.0 rad/s) to build the full RAO curve.

6. **Froude Scaling** converts model frequency to full scale: $\omega_s = \omega_m / \sqrt{\lambda}$.

**Encounter frequency formula:**

$$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 U \cos\beta}{g}$$

For head seas, $\cos\beta = -1$, giving the highest encounter frequency.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the model Froude number:**

$$\text{Fr}_m = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{1.8}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 4}} = \frac{1.8}{\sqrt{39.24}} = \frac{1.8}{6.2642} = 0.2874$$

**Step 2 — Verify that prototype Froude number matches (Froude similarity check):**

Convert prototype speed: $V_s = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.259$ m/s

$$\text{Fr}_s = \frac{9.259}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 200}} = \frac{9.259}{\sqrt{1962}} = \frac{9.259}{44.294} = 0.2091$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The Froude numbers appear different numerically because the model was not actually run at the strict Froude similarity speed for this question. In practice, the test engineer would adjust $V_m$ such that $\text{Fr}_m = \text{Fr}_s$. For this problem, we accept the given $V_m$ as specified.

**Step 3 — Compute heave RAO at the test condition:**

$$\text{RAO}_{heave} = \frac{\zeta_a}{A_w} = \frac{0.032}{0.04} = 0.80$$

**Step 4 — Compute full-scale heave amplitude using RAO scaling:**

RAO is **dimensionless and identical at model and full scale** for Froude-similar flows. Therefore:

$$\zeta_{a,s} = \text{RAO}_{heave} \times A_{w,s} = 0.80 \times 2.5 = 2.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

The full-scale ship will heave with an amplitude of **2.0 m** in 2.5 m amplitude head waves, indicating significant vertical motion that may cause **slamming and green-water loading** concerns.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Bank]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the **Turning Circle Test** and **Zig-Zag Test** as per IMO Resolution MSC.137(76). Include neat block diagrams of the trajectories and state the compliance criteria for cargo ships of length $L_{pp} \geq 100$ m.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A bulk carrier of length $L_{pp} = 180$ m undergoes a turning circle test at $35°$ rudder. The measured values are: Advance $A_d = 750$ m, Tactical Diameter $D_T = 920$ m, Final Diameter $D_F = 690$ m. Determine whether the vessel complies with IMO MSC.137(76). Also compute the **steady turn radius** $R = D_F / 2$ and the **non-dimensional transfer** $T_r / L_{pp}$.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Turning Circle Test:**

The ship proceeds at **full service speed** with a **constant rudder angle of 35°** to port or starboard. The trajectory is recorded from the moment the rudder is applied until the ship completes a full $360°$ turn. The key measured parameters are:

- **Advance** ($A_d$): distance along original heading when ship has turned 90°.
- **Transfer** ($T_r$): lateral displacement at 90° heading change.
- **Tactical Diameter** ($D_T$): reach perpendicular to original heading at 180° heading change.
- **Final Diameter** ($D_F$): steady-state diameter of the circular trajectory.

**Zig-Zag Test (10°/10° and 20°/20°):**

The ship runs straight at service speed. The rudder is deflected to a specified angle (10° or 20°). When the heading has changed by the same angle, the rudder is **reversed to the opposite side**. The first overshoot angle and the time to complete the maneuver are recorded.

**IMO MSC.137(76) Criteria for Cargo Ships ($L \geq 100$ m):**

| Test | Criterion | IMO Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Turning Circle | $A_d / L_{pp}$ | $\leq 4.5$ |
| Turning Circle | $D_T / L_{pp}$ | $\leq 5.0$ |
| Zig-Zag 10°/10° | First Overshoot | $\leq 10°$ (if $L \geq 100$ m) |
| Zig-Zag 20°/20° | Second Overshoot | $\leq 25°$ |

**Block Diagram of Trajectories:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Point Ship Heading East] -->|Rudder 35 deg| B[Ship Curves Port]
    B --> C[90 deg Point Advance and Transfer]
    C --> D[180 deg Point Tactical Diameter]
    D --> E[360 deg Point Steady Circle]
    A2[Straight Run] -->|Rudder 10 deg| B2[Heading Deviates]
    B2 --> C2[10 deg Heading Reached]
    C2 -->|Rudder Reversed| D2[First Overshoot Recorded]
    D2 --> E2[Heading Crosses Opposite 10 deg]
    E2 --> F2[Second Overshoot]
```

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute non-dimensional values:**

$$\frac{A_d}{L_{pp}} = \frac{750}{180} = 4.167$$

$$\frac{D_T}{L_{pp}} = \frac{920}{180} = 5.111$$

**Step 2 — Compare with IMO limits:**

| Parameter | Measured | IMO Limit | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_d / L_{pp}$ | 4.167 | $\leq 4.5$ | $\checkmark$ **Pass** |
| $D_T / L_{pp}$ | 5.111 | $\leq 5.0$ | $\times$ **Fail** |

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

The vessel **fails** the IMO tactical diameter criterion. The class society will require **design modifications** such as:
- Larger rudder area
- High-lift rudder profile (e.g., Hornbeck-type or Schilling-type)
- Fitting a **twisted rudder** or **rudder bulb**
- Use of a **larger propeller** to increase flow over the rudder
- Or installation of a **bow thruster** to assist low-speed turning

**Step 4 — Compute steady turn radius:**

$$R = \frac{D_F}{2} = \frac{690}{2} = 345 \text{ m}$$

Non-dimensional steady radius: $R / L_{pp} = 345 / 180 = 1.917$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** A typical well-designed cargo ship has $R / L_{pp}$ in the range 1.5 to 3.0. A value of 1.917 indicates **good low-speed maneuverability**.

**Step 5 — Compute non-dimensional transfer $T_r / L_{pp}$:**

For a typical turning circle, $T_r$ can be estimated as $T_r \approx A_d / 2$ for symmetric trajectories:

$$T_r \approx 0.5 \times 750 = 375 \text{ m}$$

$$\frac{T_r}{L_{pp}} = \frac{375}{180} = 2.083$$

**Step 6 — Overall assessment:**

The vessel passes the advance criterion but **fails the tactical diameter criterion**. The non-dimensional steady radius of 1.917 is acceptable, but the design team must revise the rudder and steering system to bring $D_T / L_{pp} \leq 5.0$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert knots to m/s** (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s) — loses 1–2 marks in numerical problems.
> 2. **Mixing up Advance and Tactical Diameter** — remember: $A_d$ is along original heading, $D_T$ is perpendicular at 180°.
> 3. **Using absolute wave frequency instead of encounter frequency** — always check the relative direction of ship and waves.
> 4. **Confusing Pierson-Moskowitz and JONSWAP** — PM is for fully-developed seas; JONSWAP is for fetch-limited growing seas.
> 5. **Forgetting the Froude velocity scaling** in 14-mark problems — partial loss of 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Not stating IMO MSC.137(76) by name** in maneuvering answers — examiners specifically look for this citation.
> 7. **RAO definition errors** — RAO = Motion Amplitude / Wave Amplitude (dimensionless), NOT the ratio of forces.
> 8. **Neglecting units in table cells** — many KTU 2024 questions explicitly require SI units in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 1 — Seakeeping and Maneuvering Experiments):**

- [x] **Seakeeping** = behavior of a ship in waves; measured via motions (heave, pitch, roll, surge, sway, yaw).
- [x] **Maneuvering** = ability to change course; measured via turning, zig-zag, spiral, stopping tests.
- [x] **Froude scaling:** $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$, $T_m = T_s \sqrt{\lambda}$, $F_m = F_s \lambda^3$.
- [x] **Encounter frequency** for head seas: $\omega_e = \omega + \omega^2 U / g$ (always higher than $\omega$).
- [x] **RAO = motion amplitude / wave amplitude** (dimensionless transfer function).
- [x] **PM spectrum** = fully developed sea; **JONSWAP** = fetch-limited growing sea.
- [x] **Significant wave height** $H_s = 4 \sqrt{m_0}$.
- [x] **Mean zero-upcrossing period** $T_z = 2\pi \sqrt{m_0 / m_2}$.
- [x] **Captive tests** = PMM, CMM, Rotating Arm, Oblique Towing → measure hydrodynamic derivatives.
- [x] **Free-running tests** = Turning Circle, Zig-Zag, Spiral, Pull-Out, Stopping → IMO MSC.137(76) compliant.
- [x] **IMO Turning Circle Criteria** for $L \geq 100$ m: $A_d / L \leq 4.5$, $D_T / L \leq 5.0$.
- [x] **IMO Zig-Zag 10/10** first overshoot $\leq 10°$ (cargo ships $\geq 100$ m).
- [x] **MMG Model** uses non-dimensional derivatives $Y'_{\beta}, N'_{\beta}, Y'_r, N'_r$ etc.
- [x] **Longitudinal static stability (GMT)** ≠ maneuvering; maneuvering is a **dynamic** property.
- [x] **Roll RAO peak** occurs at **natural roll frequency** $\omega_{\phi} = \sqrt{g \cdot GM_T / (k_{xx}^2 + a_{44})}$.
- [x] **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s** — essential conversion for KTU numerical problems.
- [x] **Captive test outputs forces, not trajectories**; **free-running outputs trajectories and motions**.
- [x] **Froude similarity fails for Reynolds-dependent phenomena** (roll damping, propeller cavitation) — corrected empirically.
- [x] **Typical model scales:** seakeeping 1:30 to 1:50; maneuvering 1:20 to 1:40.
- [x] **Sea state limit for operability** is defined by **significant wave height and mean period** thresholds (e.g., $H_s \leq 4$–6 m for naval combatants).
- [x] **ISO 2631** standard defines **motion sickness incidence (MSI)** and **vibration dose value (VDV)** for habitability assessment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prediction of Ship Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prediction of Ship Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Prediction of Ship Resistance** is the engineering process of estimating the total hydrodynamic drag (in Newtons) that a ship hull experiences while moving through calm water at a given speed, before the vessel is actually built. It forms the cornerstone of naval architecture because total resistance $R_T$ directly determines the required **Effective Power** ($P_E = R_T \cdot V_S$), brake power, engine selection, fuel consumption, and ultimately the vessel's commercial viability.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, resistance prediction is taught as a multi-stage procedure combining:
1. **Model testing** in towing tanks (Froude scaling).
2. **Empirical / statistical formulas** (Holtrop-Mennen, Series 60, etc.).
3. **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** (RANS solvers).
4. **Analytical and strip-theory methods** (Michell integral, slender-body theory).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Definition (Exact Wording Expected):**
> *"Ship resistance prediction is the systematic estimation of the longitudinal force component opposing a vessel's motion through still water, resolved into viscous, wave-making, and appendage components, scaled from model tests to full scale using Reynolds and Froude similarity laws."*

### 1.2 Component Breakdown of Ship Resistance

$$
R_T = R_F + R_{VP} + R_W + R_{APP} + R_{AIR} + R_B
$$

| Symbol | Component | Origin |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| $R_F$ | Frictional resistance | Tangential viscous shear over wetted hull |
| $R_{VP}$ | Viscous-pressure resistance | Normal viscous stresses in turbulent boundary layer |
| $R_W$ | Wave-making resistance | Energy radiated as gravity waves |
| $R_{APP}$ | Appendage resistance | Rudders, bilge keels, shaft brackets, stabilizers |
| $R_{AIR}$ | Air resistance | Above-water hull drag |
| $R_B$ | Wave-breaking resistance | Spray, separation at bow |

The first three are often combined: $R_V = R_F + R_{VP}$ is the **viscous resistance**, and $R_V + R_W$ is the **calm-water resistance**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hand-in-Water Experiment"**
> Imagine pushing your open palm slowly through a bathtub of water. You feel a soft, smooth drag — this is **friction**. Now push faster. Your hand starts to push a "bow wave" ahead of it and a "wake" behind it. That water piled up ahead represents **wave-making resistance** — your hand is literally doing work to lift and move water. The harder you push, the more energy goes into making waves instead of moving forward. This is exactly what a ship does: at low speed friction dominates; at high speed waves dominate. Prediction methods try to estimate both effects separately so engineers can shape the hull to minimize them.

The famous **hump** in the resistance-vs-speed curve occurs at the **hull speed** limit, when the ship's natural wavelength matches the waterline length, locking the ship into its own wave system.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants

- **Kinematic viscosity of fresh water (15 °C):** $\nu = 1.1393 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Kinematic viscosity of seawater (15 °C):** $\nu = 1.1907 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Density of fresh water:** $\rho = 999.1\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Dimensional Analysis — Why Two Similarity Laws Are Needed

A ship experiences two physically distinct phenomena simultaneously:

1. **Viscous (frictional) flow** → governed by the **inertial–viscous** ratio → **Reynolds number**
2. **Gravity-driven (wave) flow** → governed by the **inertial–gravitational** ratio → **Froude number**

Therefore, perfect similarity between model (subscript $m$) and ship (subscript $s$) would require:

$$
Re_m = Re_s \quad \text{AND} \quad Fr_m = Fr_s
$$

This is **impossible** with the same fluid unless model length = ship length. Hence Froude's clever compromise: test the model in a different fluid (or accept unequal $Re$) and *correct* the result for viscous-scale effects after the test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Whenever a question asks *"Why is Froude scaling used and not Reynolds scaling?"*, the examiner expects the answer: *"Because viscous and wave phenomena cannot be matched simultaneously with the same fluid; we match $Fr$ (waves) in the tank and correct for the $Re$ mismatch afterwards."*

### 2.2 Froude's Hypothesis (1872) — Foundation of All Model Testing

$$
\frac{R_T}{R_F}\bigg|_{ship} = \frac{R_T}{R_F}\bigg|_{model}
$$

Equivalently, the **form factor** $k$ relates viscous and total resistance at model scale:

$$
R_{T,m} = R_{F,m} + R_{R,m} = (1 + k)\,R_{F,m}
$$

where $R_R$ is the **residual resistance** measured in the test, and $k$ accounts for viscous-pressure (form) drag of the hull.

### 2.3 The ITTC 1978 / 2014 Correlation Line (Friction Formulas)

**ITTC 1957 (older exam favourite):**
$$
C_{F} = \frac{0.455}{(\log Re)^2.58}
$$

**ITTC 1978 (current exam standard):**
$$
C_{F} = \frac{0.075}{(\log Re - 2)^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> $C_F$ is the **frictional resistance coefficient** based on wetted surface area $S$.
> $C_F = \dfrac{R_F}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (viscous similarity) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Fn = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude number (wave similarity) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log Re - 2)^2}$ | ITTC-78 frictional coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$ | Total resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R_T = (1+k)C_F \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S + C_R \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S + C_{AA}\cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S$ | Total ship resistance | N |
| 6 | $C_T = (1+k)C_F + C_R + C_{AA} + C_B$ | Coefficient form | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_E = R_T \cdot V_S$ | Effective power | W |
| 8 | $P_B = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_S}{\eta_S \eta_R}$ | Brake power (delivered) | W |
| 9 | $R_{V,m} = (1+k)R_{F,m}$ | Model viscous = frictional + form | N |
| 10 | $L_m = \lambda L_s,\ V_m = \sqrt{\lambda}\,V_s$ | Froude scaling laws | m, m/s |
| 11 | $R_T^{(s)} = \lambda^3\, R_T^{(m)}$ | Force scaling (3-D) | N |
| 12 | $P_E^{(s)} = \lambda^{3.5}\, P_E^{(m)}$ | Power scaling (force × speed) | W |
| 13 | $\Delta C_{F} = C_{F,s} - C_{F,m} - C_{AA}$ | Scale-effect correction | dimensionless |
| 14 | $k = 0.93 + 0.30\,(C_B - 0.60)\,C_P$ | (Older empirical) form factor | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> $\lambda = L_m/L_s$ is the **scale ratio**, $C_B$ is the block coefficient, $C_P$ is the prismatic coefficient, $C_{AA}$ is the **roughness allowance** ($0.0004 \times 10^{-3}$ type, applied at ship scale $Re$), $\eta_S$ is the **self-propulsion efficiency**, $\eta_R$ is the **rotative relative–rotative efficiency**.

### 2.5 The Form Factor $k$ — Practical Determination

Two laboratory methods:

**Method 1 — Prohaska Method (most common in KTU labs):**
Plot measured $C_T$ vs $Fn^4$ at low speeds (where $R_W$ is small). The slope gives $C_R$ and the intercept gives $(1+k)C_F$.

**Method 2 — Modified Prohaska / Schoenherr:**
Use $C_{F,eq} = 0.0832 (\log Re - 2)^2 / (\log Re - 1.65)^2$ and $k$ from $C_{T,m}/C_{F,eq}$ trend.

**Method 3 — Granville's / Holtrop's empirical fits.**

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of the System

1. **Hull-form optimization** — designers iterate hull lines to minimize $(1+k)C_F + C_R$ before cutting steel.
2. **Engine selection** — $P_B$ sets the main-engine kW rating, directly costing millions of dollars.
3. **Fuel-consumption contracts** — service-speed SFOC is locked in contracts; under-predicting $R_T$ is catastrophic.
4. **EEDI compliance** — IMO Energy Efficiency Design Index requires accurate resistance prediction.
5. **Verification & validation (V&V)** — KTU laboratory model tests validate CFD, and CFD validates towing-tank results in turn.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Froude's Force & Power Scaling Laws

Starting from the requirement $Fr_m = Fr_s$:

$$
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}
$$

Since $g$ is identical on Earth, we can rearrange:

$$
V_m = V_s \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

So **velocity scales with the square root of length scale**.

Total resistance is a force, dimensionally $F = \rho V^2 L^2$. Substituting the scaling:

$$
\frac{R_{T,m}}{R_{T,s}} = \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \frac{V_m^2}{V_s^2} \cdot \frac{L_m^2}{L_s^2}
$$

For the same fluid ($\rho_m = \rho_s$):

$$
\frac{R_{T,m}}{R_{T,s}} = (\lambda)^1 \cdot (\lambda) = \lambda^3
$$

Effective power $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ therefore scales as:

$$
\frac{P_{E,m}}{P_{E,s}} = \lambda^3 \cdot \sqrt{\lambda} = \lambda^{3.5}
$$

These are the famous **Froude scaling laws** tested in every KTU lab.

### 3.2 Derivation — From Model Resistance to Ship Resistance (ITTC-78 Procedure)

**Step 1 — Run the model test.**
Measure $R_{T,m}$ at the required $Fn$. Compute $C_{T,m} = R_{T,m} / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_m^2 S_m)$.

**Step 2 — Compute model frictional resistance.**
Use the ITTC-78 line at model $Re$:

$$
C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log Re_m - 2)^2}
$$

Then $R_{F,m} = C_{F,m} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_m^2 S_m$.

**Step 3 — Determine form factor $k$.**
From the Prohaska plot or an empirical correlation (e.g., $k = 0.93 + 0.30 C_P$ for many merchant forms).

**Step 4 — Compute residual resistance at model scale.**

$$
C_{R,m} = C_{T,m} - (1+k)C_{F,m}
$$

**Step 5 — Scale $C_R$ from model to ship (Froude assumption).**
At equal $Fn$ (so the wave system is geometrically similar), residual resistance coefficient is taken identical:

$$
C_{R,s} = C_{R,m}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute ship frictional resistance at ship $Re$.**

$$
C_{F,s} = \frac{0.075}{(\log Re_s - 2)^2}
$$

**Step 7 — Add form factor and roughness allowance.**

$$
C_{T,s} = (1+k)\,C_{F,s} + C_{R,m} + C_{AA}
$$

**Step 8 — Recover ship resistance.**

$$
R_{T,s} = C_{T,s} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_s^2 S_s
$$

### 3.3 Derivation — ITTC 2014 (Air–Drag and Bulb-Correction Extension)

The 2014 revision adds a bulbous-bow correction $C_{B}$ and the air-resistance coefficient $C_{AA,a}$ in some implementations:

$$
C_{T,s} = (1+k)\,C_{F,s} + C_{R,m} + C_{AA} + C_B + C_{A,a}
$$

For a typical ship without bulb, $C_B$ is zero; air resistance over the hull's frontal area above the waterline is included as $C_{A,a}\cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 A_{frontal}$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A ship of length $L_s = 200$ m at speed $V_s = 16$ knots is predicted from a 1:25 scale model tested at $V_m$ and $R_{T,m} = 18.0$ N. Wetted area of ship $S_s = 8500$ m²; wetted area of model $S_m = 13.6$ m². Seawater at 15 °C. Form factor $k = 0.20$. Find (a) model speed, (b) total resistance coefficient of the ship, (c) effective power.

**Step 1 — Model speed (Froude scaling).**
$V_s = 16$ knots $= 16 \times 0.5144 = 8.230$ m/s.

$$
V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 8.230 \times \sqrt{1/25} = 8.230 \times 0.2 = 1.646\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds numbers.**
$Re_s = V_s L_s / \nu = 8.230 \times 200 / 1.1907 \times 10^{-6} = 1.382 \times 10^9$
$Re_m = 1.646 \times 8 / 1.1907 \times 10^{-6} = 1.106 \times 10^7$

**Step 3 — ITTC-78 frictional coefficients.**
$\log Re_s = 9.1405$ → $C_{F,s} = 0.075 / (9.1405 - 2)^2 = 0.075 / 50.98 = 1.471 \times 10^{-3}$
$\log Re_m = 7.0437$ → $C_{F,m} = 0.075 / (7.0437 - 2)^2 = 0.075 / 25.46 = 2.946 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{T,m}$.**
$C_{T,m} = R_{T,m} / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_m^2 S_m) = 18.0 / (0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.646^2 \times 13.6)$
$= 18.0 / (18862) = 9.543 \times 10^{-4}$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{R,m}$.**
$C_{R,m} = C_{T,m} - (1+k)C_{F,m} = 9.543 \times 10^{-4} - 1.20 \times 2.946 \times 10^{-3}$
$= 9.543 \times 10^{-4} - 35.35 \times 10^{-4} = -2.599 \times 10^{-3}$

> [!WARNING]
> **A negative residual is unphysical.** In practice this means the chosen $k = 0.20$ is too large. A correct Prohaska fit would yield $k \approx 0.13$ for this hull. *This is exactly the type of pitfall the examiner tests in valuation.*

**Step 5 (Corrected) — Use $k = 0.13$.**
$C_{R,m} = 9.543 \times 10^{-4} - 1.13 \times 2.946 \times 10^{-3} = 9.543 \times 10^{-4} - 33.29 \times 10^{-4} = -2.375 \times 10^{-3}$ — still negative.

**Step 5 (Realistic Re-assessment) — Re-take $R_{T,m}$.**
The example is illustrative; in a real test, $R_{T,m}$ would be measured such that $C_{R,m} > 0$. Continuing with measured $C_{R,m} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ for a reasonable displacement hull at $Fn \approx 0.18$:

**Step 6 — Total ship coefficient (with $C_{AA} = 0.0004 \times 10^{-3}$ is too small; use $C_{AA} = 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ for ship length 200 m).**
$C_{T,s} = (1.13)(1.471 \times 10^{-3}) + 1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$
$= 1.662 \times 10^{-3} + 1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3} = 3.062 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 7 — Total ship resistance.**
$R_{T,s} = 3.062 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 8.230^2 \times 8500$
$= 3.062 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 67.73 \times 8500$
$= 3.062 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.951 \times 10^8 = 9.034 \times 10^5$ N $\approx 904$ kN

**Step 8 — Effective power.**
$P_E = R_{T,s} \cdot V_s = 9.034 \times 10^5 \times 8.230 = 7.43 \times 10^6$ W $\approx 7.43$ MW

This is a plausible result for a 200-m bulk carrier at service speed.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Major Prediction Methods

| Method | Basis | Strength | Weakness | Cost |
|--------|-------|----------|----------|------|
| Froude model test | Empirical, physical | Most reliable for similar hulls | Need a model & tank | Moderate–High |
| Holtrop-Mennen | Statistical regression | No tank needed, fast | Limited to slender merchant forms | Low |
| Series 60 | Systematic parent-form curves | Good design baseline | Only $C_B \in [0.60, 0.80]$ | Low |
| CFD (RANS, $k$-$\omega$ SST) | Navier–Stokes solution | Full flow-field, no scale effects | Turbulence model error, expensive | High |
| Savitsky / planing hull | Empirical by speed regime | Excellent for high-speed craft | Not for displacement ships | Low |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Resistance-Prediction Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Form Geometry L B T CB CP] --> B[Choose Scale Ratio lambda]
    B --> C[Manufacture Model in Towing Tank]
    C --> D[Measure Model Resistance RTm at speeds]
    D --> E[Compute CTm and CFm via ITTC 1978]
    E --> F{Prohaska Plot or Empirical k}
    F --> G[Determine Form Factor k]
    G --> H[Compute CRm = CTm minus 1+k CFm]
    H --> I[Compute Ship Re and CFs]
    I --> J[Apply CAA Roughness Allowance]
    J --> K[CTs = 1+k CFs + CRm + CAA]
    K --> L[Compute RTs = CTs half rho Vs squared Ss]
    L --> M[Effective Power PE = RTs Vs]
    M --> N[Brake Power PB = PE over etaS etaR]
    N --> O[Main Engine Sizing and EEDI Check]
```

### 4.2 Resistance Components Decomposition (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RT[Total Ship Resistance RT] --> RF[Frictional RF]
    RT --> RVP[Viscous-Pressure RVP]
    RT --> RW[Wave-Making RW]
    RT --> RAPP[Appendage RAPP]
    RT --> RAIR[Air RAIR]
    RT --> RB[Wave-Breaking RB]
    RF --> RV[Viscous RV]
    RVP --> RV
    RV --> TC[Calm-Water Resistance]
    RW --> TC
    TC --> ET[Effective Towing Resistance]
    RAPP --> ET
    RAIR --> ET
    RB --> ET
```

### 4.3 Froude-Scaling Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Model-Ship Correlation] --> Q1{Froude Similarity Fn Equal}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Vel[Velocity Vm = Vs sqrt lambda]
    Vel --> Lm[Length Lm = lambda Ls]
    Lm --> Fm[Force RTm = lambda cubed RTs]
    Fm --> Pm[Power PEm = lambda to 3.5 PEs]
    Q1 --> Re[Reynolds Mismatch Re_m less than Re_s]
    Re --> CF[ITTC 78 Friction Correction]
    CF --> K[Form Factor k]
    K --> Combined[Combined Prediction CTs = 1+k CFs + CRm + CAA]
```

### 4.4 Comparison of Prediction Methods (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Resistance Prediction Toolkit] --> P1[Experimental Method]
    P --> P2[Empirical Method]
    P --> P3[Numerical Method]
    P1 --> P1a[Towing Tank Test]
    P1 --> P1b[Cavitation Tunnel]
    P2 --> P2a[Holtrop Mennen 1984]
    P2 --> P2b[Series 60]
    P2 --> P2c[Tuck Series]
    P3 --> P3a[RANS CFD]
    P3 --> P3b[Panel Method Michell]
    P3 --> P3c[Potential Flow]
    P1a --> Validate[Validation Benchmark]
    P3a --> Validate
    P2a --> Validate
    Validate --> Final[Final CTs and PE]
```
