# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Resistance Curve

A well-drawn resistance curve for a displacement-type hull has the following characteristic regions:

| Speed Region | Froude Number | Dominant Component | Curve Character |
|---|---|---|---|
| Very low | $F_n < 0.10$ | Friction + eddy | Smooth, almost parabolic |
| Low / mid | $0.10 \le F_n \le 0.30$ | Wave + friction | **Hump** appears; curve steepens |
| Cruising | $0.30 \le F_n \le 0.45$ | Wave (divergent + transverse) | Curve steepest; "design point" |
| High | $F_n > 0.45$ | Wave decays; friction rises | **Hollow** — slope flattens slightly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Hump?** As the model accelerates, the transverse and divergent wave systems constructively interfere near $F_n \approx 0.20$–$0.25$, doubling the wave energy locally. The result is a visible "bump" — the dreaded **Hump Resistance**, a key design penalty for slender, low-$C_B$ hulls.

## 2.2 Non-Dimensional Resistance Coefficients

To compare a model ($m$) and a ship ($s$) at dynamically similar speeds, engineers non-dimensionalize the resistance by dynamic pressure and a reference area (wetted surface $S$):

$$C_T = \frac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, S \, V^2}$$

The decomposition of coefficients mirrors the force decomposition:

$$C_T = C_F + C_W + C_{APP} + C_A + \Delta C_F$$

where $\Delta C_F$ is the **roughness allowance** (added for ship-scale propeller and hull roughness).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_n = \frac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | Dimensionless; $L$ = length in m |
| 2 | $F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | Froude number | Dimensionless; $g = 9.81$ |
| 3 | $C_T = \frac{R_T}{\frac{1}{2} \rho S V^2}$ | Total resistance coefficient | — |
| 4 | $C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$ | ITTC 1957 friction coefficient | Towing tank standard |
| 5 | $R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{APP} + R_A$ | Total resistance decomposition | N (Newtons) |
| 6 | $C_T = (1 + k) C_F + C_W + C_{APP} + C_A$ | Form-factor method | $k$ = form factor |
| 7 | $C_{T,s} = (1+k) C_{F,s} + C_W + \Delta C_F + C_A$ | ITTC 1978 ship prediction | — |
| 8 | $EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{1000}$ | Effective horsepower | kW (when $R_T$ in kN, $V$ in m/s) |
| 9 | $S = \nabla^{2/3} \left( 3.43 + 0.65 \frac{L}{B} \right) \cdot L^{1/3}$ | Wetted surface (Holtrop) | For merchant ships |
| 10 | $k = 0.5 C_B - 0.05$ | Form factor (typical tanker) | Empirical; $C_B$ = block coeff. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "plot the resistance curve" or "decompose the resistance," examiners expect you to (1) state Froude's hypothesis, (2) show the friction-line subtraction graphically, and (3) explicitly compute $C_F$ using the ITTC 1957 formula — *not* a "generic" $C_F$ value. Always write the ITTC formula in your answer script.

## 2.4 The Two Curves You Will Be Asked to Draw

### 2.4.1 Resistance–Speed Curve ($R_T$–$V$)
- $x$-axis: Speed $V$ in knots (or m/s)
- $y$-axis (left): Total resistance $R_T$ in kN
- $y$-axis (right): Effective Power $EHP$ in kW
- Curve shape: smooth power-law growth with a **hump** in the mid-speed range.

### 2.4.2 Resistance Coefficient–Froude Curve ($C_T$–$F_n$)
- $x$-axis: Froude number $F_n$
- $y$-axis: $C_T$ (and optionally $C_F$, $C_R$ where $C_R = C_T - C_F$)
- Curve shape: U-shaped at very low $F_n$, rising hump, plateau, then mild decline at high $F_n$.
- The $C_F$ line is a *smoothly decreasing straight-like curve*; the difference $C_R = C_T - C_F$ is the **residuary resistance coefficient**, which carries the wave-making and eddy information.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Industry Application | How Resistance Curve is Used |
|---|---|
| **Ship Design (Concept Stage)** | Iterates hull forms; the lowest $R_T$ at service $F_n$ is selected. |
| **Powering Prediction** | Curve extrapolated from model to ship via ITTC-78 to predict $EHP$. |
| **Propeller Design** | Provides required thrust (= $R_T$ at bollard / service condition). |
| **Naval Architecture Education** | Demonstrates Reynolds-vs-Froude scaling conflict (the "scale effect problem"). |
| **Race Yacht / CFD Validation** | Compares towing-tank measurements with RANS simulations. |
| **Regulatory & Class Society** | Submitted with model test reports to verify contractual speed guarantees. |

## 2.6 The Froude Hypothesis — Foundation Stone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Froude's (1868) Hypothesis of Independent Force Comparison**
> The total resistance of a ship can be split into a part that depends *only* on Reynolds number (frictional) and a part that depends *only* on Froude number (residuary):
>
> $$R_T(R_n, F_n) = R_F(R_n) + R_R(F_n)$$
>
> This is the *conceptual engine* that allows model-to-ship extrapolation despite the impossibility of matching both $R_n$ and $F_n$ simultaneously at the same geometric scale.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The ITTC 1957 Friction Line

The standard friction-line correlation used in **every** towing-tank test report worldwide was derived by the International Towing Tank Conference in 1957, fitting the data of Schoenherr (1932) into a usable closed form.

**Step 1 — Start with the standard plate-friction form** (a power law on Reynolds number):

$$C_F = \frac{A}{R_n^n}$$

**Step 2 — Take the logarithm of both sides to linearize:**

$$\log_{10} C_F = \log_{10} A - n \, \log_{10} R_n$$

**Step 3 — The empirical constants are determined by curve-fitting the turbulent flat-plate data of Schoenherr (over the range $10^6 \le R_n \le 10^9$):**

$$\log_{10} A = -2.996 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = 1.009 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10^{-0.0003} \dots \approx 0.075$$

and $n = 0.139$ (often rounded to 2 in the denominator construction below).

**Step 4 — The closed-form expression finally adopted by ITTC-57:**

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

This is the formula that defines the **friction line** drawn on the $C_T$–$F_n$ plot during resistance analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> The "$-2$" in the denominator is purely a numerical artifact of the ITTC fitting range; it simply anchors the curve at $R_n = 10^7$ where $C_F \approx 0.00275$ for the original Schoenherr data.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Resistance Coefficient Decomposition (Froude)

**Step 1 — Begin with the dimensional total resistance from a model test:**

$$R_{T,m} = R_{F,m} + R_{R,m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the frictional component using the ITTC-57 correlation evaluated at the model's $R_n$:**

$$R_{F,m} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho_m \, S_m \, V_m^2 \cdot C_{F,m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the *residuary* (wave + eddy) component as the leftover:**

$$R_{R,m} = R_{T,m} - R_{F,m}$$

**Step 4 — Express the residual as a *coefficient* using the same denominator:**

$$C_R = \frac{R_{R,m}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_m S_m V_m^2} = C_T - C_F$$

**Step 5 — Form a non-dimensional table of $(F_n, C_T, C_F, C_R)$.** This is the **raw output of a resistance test** and is the input to all subsequent extrapolation methods.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: ITTC 1978 Extrapolation (Model → Ship)

**Step 1 — At the ship's Reynolds number, the ITTC friction line gives $C_{F,s}$:**

$$C_{F,s} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{n,s} - 2)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Allow for the *form factor* $k$ (Hoerner's roughness proxy for the 3D hull vs. 2D plate):**

$$C_{F,\text{hull}} = (1 + k) \, C_{F,s}$$

**Step 3 — Assume the wave-making coefficient $C_W$ is **Froude-similar** between model and ship (per Froude's hypothesis):**

$$C_{W,s} = C_{W,m}$$

**Step 4 — Add a *roughness allowance* $\Delta C_F$ (typically 0.0004 for new ships, 0.001 for old + fouled):**

$$C_{T,s} = (1 + k) C_{F,s} + C_W + C_A + \Delta C_F$$

**Step 5 — Recover the dimensional ship resistance by multiplying back through dynamic pressure:**

$$R_{T,s} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho_s \, S_s \, V_s^2 \cdot C_{T,s}$$

This is the **5-step ITTC-78 ship prediction procedure** that every naval architect applies at the end of a model test.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem:** A 5 m model of a ship is tested in fresh water at $V = 2.0 \ \text{m/s}$. Total resistance measured is $R_{T,m} = 32.4 \ \text{N}$. Wetted surface $S_m = 8.5 \ \text{m}^2$. Compute $C_T$, $C_F$, and $C_R$ at this speed. Use $\nu = 1.1393 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

**Step 1 — Reynolds number:**

$$R_{n,m} = \frac{V_m \, L_m}{\nu} = \frac{2.0 \times 5.0}{1.1393 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.777 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 2 — Friction coefficient (ITTC-57):**

$$C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(8.777 \times 10^{6}) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.9433 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(4.9433)^2} = \frac{0.075}{24.436} = 3.069 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Total resistance coefficient:**

$$C_{T,m} = \frac{R_{T,m}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho S_m V_m^2} = \frac{32.4}{\frac{1}{2} \times 999.1 \times 8.5 \times (2.0)^2} = \frac{32.4}{16985.5} = 1.907 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Residuary resistance coefficient (this is the wave+eddy part):**

$$C_{R,m} = C_{T,m} - C_{F,m} = 1.907 \times 10^{-3} - 3.069 \times 10^{-3} = -1.162 \times 10^{-3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative $C_R$?** At very low speeds this *can* happen because the ITTC-57 line slightly overpredicts friction for some hull forms. A negative $C_R$ is a *flag* to re-check plate roughness assumptions or run at a higher $F_n$. In KTU answers, show this transparently — examiners reward honesty.

**Step 5 — Convert back to dimensional residuary resistance:**

$$R_{R,m} = C_{R,m} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho S_m V_m^2 = (-1.162 \times 10^{-3}) \times 16985.5 = -19.74 \ \text{N}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — What Each Term in $R_T$ Depends On

| Term | Physical Origin | Depends on $R_n$? | Depends on $F_n$? | Scale Effect? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $R_F$ | Viscous shear in boundary layer | **Yes (strongly)** | Negligibly | **Yes** — requires extrapolation |
| $R_W$ | Gravity-induced wave generation | Weakly | **Yes (strongly)** | No (Froude-similar) |
| $R_{APP}$ | Appendage (rudder, bilge keel) drag | **Yes** | Slightly | Yes (small) |
| $R_A$ | Above-water wind drag | $R_n$ of appendage | Slightly | Small |
| $R_{EDDY}$ | Flow separation at stern | **Yes** | Slightly | Yes |
| $R_{WB}$ | Wave-breaking (transom) | Weakly | **Yes** | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The reason the **scale-effect problem** exists at all is that $R_F$ depends on $R_n$ but $R_W$ does not. At model scale, $R_n$ is $10^6$–$10^7$ (laminar-turbulent mixed); at ship scale it is $10^8$–$10^9$ (fully turbulent). Since $C_F \propto R_n^{-0.139}$, the model friction is *larger* than the ship friction, so we *subtract* it and re-add a *larger* $R_n$ ship value.

## 3.6 EHP Curve — The "Useful Output" of Resistance Data

The effective horsepower is just force times speed:

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{1000} \ \ [\text{kW}]$$

When $EHP$ is plotted on the same $x$-axis as $R_T$ (right-side ordinate), the resulting **EHP curve** rises as $V^3$ in the friction-dominated regime and as $V^4$–$V^5$ in the wave-dominated regime. This single plot is the input to **shaft horsepower prediction**:

$$SHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S}$$

where $\eta_D$ = propulsive efficiency and $\eta_S$ = shaft efficiency. This is the **end-of-Module-2** application students must internalize.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Resistance Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Model Hull Geometry] --> B[Towing Tank Test Rig]
    B --> C[Raw Data: V, R_T, sinkage, trim]
    C --> D{Compute Dimensionless Numbers}
    D --> D1[R_n = VL / nu]
    D --> D2[F_n = V / sqrt gL]
    D1 --> E[Plot C_T vs F_n]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Apply ITTC-57 Friction Line]
    F --> G[Compute C_F at model R_n]
    G --> H[Compute C_R = C_T - C_F]
    H --> I[Apply Form Factor Method]
    I --> I1[Plot 1 plus k times C_F vs F_n]
    I1 --> J[Extrapolate C_R to Ship F_n]
    J --> K[Add Roughness Allowance delta C_F]
    K --> L[Compute Ship C_T_s]
    L --> M[Recover Dimensional R_T_s]
    M --> N[Compute EHP_s and SHP_s]
    N --> O[Final Powering Prediction Report]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Component Decomposition Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[R_T Total Resistance] --> B[R_F Frictional]
    A --> C[R_W Wave-Making]
    A --> D[R_EDDY Eddy-Making]
    A --> E[R_APP Appendage]
    A --> F[R_A Air]
    B --> B1[Depends on R_n]
    C --> C1[Depends on F_n]
    D --> D1[Depends on R_n + Form Factor k]
    E --> E1[Subtracted from bare-hull test]
    F --> F1[From wind tunnel or empirical corr]
    B1 --> G[ITTC-57 Line]
    C1 --> H[Hump at F_n approx 0.20-0.25]
    D1 --> I[k approx 0.5 C_B minus 0.05]
    G --> J[C_T Analysis]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    E1 --> J
    F1 --> J
    J --> K[Ship R_T Prediction]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Resistance Curve Interpretation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Resistance Curve R_T vs V] --> B{Inspect Speed Region}
    B --> B1[Low F_n lt 0.10]
    B --> B2[Mid F_n 0.10 to 0.30]
    B --> B3[Cruise F_n 0.30 to 0.45]
    B --> B4[High F_n gt 0.45]
    B1 --> C1[Smooth parabolic, friction-dominated]
    B2 --> C2[Hump zone - critical design penalty]
    B3 --> C3[Steepest slope - service condition]
    B4 --> C4[Hollow - wave decay region]
    C1 --> D[Engineering Action: validate with ITTC-57]
    C2 --> E[Engineering Action: hull-form optimization]
    C3 --> F[Engineering Action: powering prediction baseline]
    C4 --> G[Engineering Action: light-weight or slender form check]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — The ITTC-1978 Extrapolation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Model Test: R_T,m at F_n] --> B[Compute C_T,m]
    B --> C[ITTC-57 gives C_F,m]
    C --> D[Compute C_W,m = C_T,m minus C_F,m]
    D --> E{Choose Method}
    E --> E1[Prohaska Method]
    E --> E2[Hughes Method]
    E --> E3[ITTC-78 Single Parameter k]
    E3 --> F[Estimate k from C_B or Prohaska slope]
    F --> G[Compute C_F,s at ship R_n]
    G --> H[C_T,s = 1+k times C_F,s + C_W,m + delta C_F + C_A]
    H --> I[R_T,s = 0.5 rho_s S_s V_s^2 times C_T,s]
    I --> J[Final Ship Resistance Curve]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Instead of Hand-Drawn Plots?**
> Mermaid cannot render a true 2-axis plot with curves. The block-level architecture maps the **logic flow** of how the resistance curve is *generated, analyzed, and extrapolated* — this is exactly what the KTU board examiner marks when a question asks "Describe the methodology used to obtain the resistance curve of a ship from a model test."

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Define the term "resistance curve" of a ship. What are its principal axes and what is plotted on the secondary axis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A resistance curve is a graphical plot of the total hydrodynamic resistance $R_T$ experienced by a ship or model as a function of its towing speed $V$.
- **Principal axes (1 mark):** The abscissa is ship/model speed $V$ (in knots or m/s) and the ordinate is the total resistance $R_T$ (in kN or N).
- **Secondary axis (1 mark):** The secondary ordinate (usually on the right) plots the corresponding **Effective Horsepower (EHP)** in kW, computed as $EHP = R_T \cdot V / 1000$.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the ITTC 1957 friction line formula and explain the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Formula (2 marks):**
$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$
- **Term explanation (1 mark):** $C_F$ is the frictional resistance coefficient of a turbulent flat plate, $R_n = V L / \nu$ is the Reynolds number based on model/ship speed, length, and kinematic viscosity of water. The factor $0.075$ and the structure $(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$ are empirical best-fit constants from the Schoenherr (1932) data, validated by the International Towing Tank Conference in 1957.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q3(a) [7 marks]** — With a neat sketch, describe the **components of total resistance** of a ship. State Froude's hypothesis of independent force comparison.

**Model Answer:**

**Components of total resistance (5 marks):**
According to Froude's decomposition, the total resistance of a ship moving through calm water is the sum of:
1. **Frictional Resistance $R_F$** — Tangential viscous shear in the turbulent boundary layer over the wetted hull surface. Always present, dominates at low $F_n$.
2. **Wave-Making Resistance $R_W$** — Energy radiated as surface gravity waves from the bow and stern. Dominates at moderate-to-high $F_n$.
3. **Eddy-Making Resistance $R_{EDDY}$** — Form drag from separated flow at the stern; lumped with wave resistance into the *residuary* term in some methods.
4. **Appendage Resistance $R_{APP}$** — Drag of rudder, bilge keels, shaft brackets, etc., measured by running the model with and without appendages.
5. **Air Resistance $R_A$** — Wind drag on the above-water hull; typically 2–4% of $R_T$ at service speed.

$$R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{EDDY} + R_{APP} + R_A$$

**Froude's Hypothesis (2 marks):**
Froude (1868) proposed that the total resistance could be expressed as the sum of two *independent* functions:

$$R_T(R_n, F_n) = R_F(R_n) + R_R(F_n)$$

That is, the frictional part depends *only* on Reynolds number, while the residuary (wave + eddy) part depends *only* on Froude number. This allows model-to-ship extrapolation because model tests match $F_n$ (wave) but not $R_n$ (friction).

---

> **Q3(b) [7 marks]** — A model of length $L_m = 4 \ \text{m}$ is towed in a freshwater tank at $V_m = 1.8 \ \text{m/s}$. The measured total resistance is $R_{T,m} = 22.0 \ \text{N}$ and the wetted surface area is $S_m = 6.8 \ \text{m}^2$. Compute (i) the Reynolds number, (ii) the ITTC-1957 friction coefficient, (iii) the total resistance coefficient $C_T$, and (iv) the residuary resistance coefficient $C_R$. Use $\nu = 1.1393 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ and $\rho = 999.1 \ \text{kg/m}^3$.

**Model Answer (full step-by-step):**

**(i) Reynolds Number (1.5 marks):**
$$R_{n,m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu} = \frac{1.8 \times 4}{1.1393 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{7.2}{1.1393 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.319 \times 10^{6}$$

**(ii) Friction Coefficient (2 marks):**
$$\log_{10} R_{n,m} = \log_{10}(6.319 \times 10^6) = 6.8007$$
$$C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{n,m} - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.8007 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(4.8007)^2} = \frac{0.075}{23.047} = 3.255 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(iii) Total Resistance Coefficient (2 marks):**
$$C_{T,m} = \frac{R_{T,m}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho S_m V_m^2} = \frac{22.0}{0.5 \times 999.1 \times 6.8 \times (1.8)^2} = \frac{22.0}{11000.0} = 2.000 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(iv) Residuary Resistance Coefficient (1.5 marks):**
$$C_{R,m} = C_{T,m} - C_{F,m} = 2.000 \times 10^{-3} - 3.255 \times 10^{-3} = -1.255 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Interpretation:** The negative $C_R$ is a low-speed artifact of the ITTC-57 line over-predicting friction for this hull form. At higher $F_n$ (above 0.18 typically), $C_R$ becomes positive and grows with the hump.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **Q4(a) [7 marks]** — Explain the **ITTC 1978 method of model-to-ship extrapolation** of resistance. State the assumptions, the role of the form factor $k$, and the final expression for ship resistance coefficient.

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Froude's hypothesis is valid: residuary resistance is Froude-similar between model and ship.
2. The form factor $k$ accounts for the 3D pressure drag that the ITTC 2D plate line cannot capture.
3. The roughness allowance $\Delta C_F$ is added at ship scale to account for typical hull roughness, propeller-induced turbulence, and biofouling.

**Role of the Form Factor (2 marks):**
The form factor $k$ is an empirical multiplier applied to the friction coefficient to "convert" the 2D flat-plate ITTC-57 prediction into a 3D hull frictional-plus-form-drag estimate. Typical values:
- $k \approx 0.20$–$0.30$ for slender, fine forms
- $k \approx 0.30$–$0.45$ for full, blunt forms
- For tankers: $k \approx 0.5 \, C_B - 0.05$

It is determined either (a) from the slope of $C_T$–$C_F$ plot (Prohaska method) or (b) from the ITTC-78 standard $k = 0.5 C_B - 0.05$.

**Final Expression (3 marks):**
$$C_{T,s} = (1+k) C_{F,s} + C_W + \Delta C_F + C_A$$

where $C_{F,s}$ is the ITTC-57 line evaluated at the ship's $R_n$, and the dimensional ship resistance follows from:

$$R_{T,s} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_s S_s V_s^2 \, C_{T,s}$$

---

> **Q4(b) [7 marks]** — A container ship of length $L_s = 200 \ \text{m}$ sails at $V_s = 20 \ \text{knots}$ ($10.29 \ \text{m/s}$). The model's $C_W$ at the corresponding $F_n$ was measured as $C_{W,m} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}$. The form factor is $k = 0.25$ and the roughness allowance is $\Delta C_F = 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$. The air resistance coefficient is $C_A = 0.05 \times 10^{-3}$. Compute the ship's $C_{F,s}$, $C_{T,s}$, and the dimensional $R_{T,s}$ (assume $S_s = 6500 \ \text{m}^2$, $\rho_s = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\nu_s = 1.1883 \times 10^{-6}$).

**Model Answer (step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Ship Reynolds number (1.5 marks):**
$$R_{n,s} = \frac{V_s L_s}{\nu_s} = \frac{10.29 \times 200}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2058}{1.1883 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.732 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 2 — Ship friction coefficient (2 marks):**
$$\log_{10} R_{n,s} = \log_{10}(1.732 \times 10^9) = 9.2385$$
$$C_{F,s} = \frac{0.075}{(9.2385 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7.2385)^2} = \frac{0.075}{52.395} = 1.431 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Total ship resistance coefficient (1.5 marks):**
$$C_{T,s} = (1 + 0.25)(1.431 \times 10^{-3}) + 0.8 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3} + 0.05 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$= 1.789 \times 10^{-3} + 0.8 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3} + 0.05 \times 10^{-3} = 3.039 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Dimensional ship resistance (2 marks):**
$$R_{T,s} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_s S_s V_s^2 \cdot C_{T,s} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 6500 \times (10.29)^2 \times 3.039 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 6500 \times 105.884 \times 3.039 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$= 107.3 \times 10^6 \times 3.039 \times 10^{-3} \approx 326.1 \ \text{kN}$$

**Sanity check:** For a 200 m container ship at 20 knots, total resistance of ~326 kN is realistic. Service EHP $\approx 326 \times 10.29 / 1000 \approx 3.36$ MW. ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\log_{10}$ base.** A student who uses $\ln$ (natural log) instead of $\log_{10}$ in the ITTC-57 formula will get $C_F$ wrong by a factor of ~2.3. *Always write "$\log_{10}$" explicitly.*
> 2. **Mixing kinematic viscosities.** Use $\nu = 1.1393 \times 10^{-6}$ for freshwater tank and $\nu \approx 1.1883 \times 10^{-6}$ for seawater. Mixing them gives ~4% error.
> 3. **Skipping the friction line step.** Many students draw $C_T$ vs $F_n$ but forget to *subtract* $C_F$ to get $C_R$. The $C_R$ curve is the entire basis of extrapolation; without it, no marks for the method.
> 4. **Ignoring the form factor $k$.** Dropping the $k C_F$ term under-predicts ship resistance by 25–45%. This is the single most common ITTC-78 mistake.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency in EHP.** EHP must be in kW, not watts. Always divide by 1000.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Resistance Curves (Module 2)**
>
> ✅ Resistance curve is a plot of **$R_T$ vs. $V$** (and secondarily **EHP vs. $V$**).
> ✅ Non-dimensional counterpart is **$C_T$ vs. $F_n$** plot.
> ✅ Total resistance has **5 components**: $R_F, R_W, R_{EDDY}, R_{APP}, R_A$.
> ✅ **Froude's Hypothesis (1868):** $R_T(R_n, F_n) = R_F(R_n) + R_R(F_n)$ — the foundation of model extrapolation.
> ✅ **ITTC-57 Friction Line:** $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$.
> ✅ **Residuary coefficient:** $C_R = C_T - C_F$.
> ✅ **Form factor $k$** is the *3D hull effect multiplier* on 2D plate friction; typical $k = 0.5 C_B - 0.05$.
> ✅ **ITTC-1978 Ship Prediction:** $C_{T,s} = (1+k) C_{F,s} + C_W + \Delta C_F + C_A$.
> ✅ The **Hump** in $R_T$–$V$ curve occurs near $F_n \approx 0.20$–$0.25$ due to constructive wave interference.
> ✅ The **Hollow** at high $F_n$ (> 0.45) is caused by decay of the transverse wave system.
> ✅ **EHP** = $R_T \cdot V / 1000$ (kW); **SHP** = EHP / ($\eta_D \cdot \eta_S$).
> ✅ Reynolds number uses **$L$** (length); Froude number uses **$L$** (length); both are dimensionless.
> ✅ **Scale effect** exists because $R_n$ cannot be matched at geometrically similar model and ship scales simultaneously.
> ✅ Always quote **kinematic viscosity** with temperature and **water density** with salinity.
> ✅ A **negative $C_R$** at very low $F_n$ is acceptable and is a *flag*, not an error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship Models
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ship Models

# Module 2 — Ship Models: Foundations, Scaling Laws & Experimental Correlation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Ship Model** is a precisely scaled geometric replica of a full-scale vessel, constructed and instrumented for hydrodynamic testing in towing tanks, circulating water channels (CWC), or cavitation tunnels, in order to predict the full-scale hydrodynamic behaviour (resistance, propulsion, manoeuvring, seakeeping) of the prototype ship through similarity principles.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, ship model testing is rooted in the **Froude-Krylov Hypothesis** and governed by the **ITTC (International Towing Tank Conference)** standard scaling and correlation procedures, principally involving:

- **Geometric Similarity** — exact proportional correspondence in shape between model and prototype.
- **Kinematic Similarity** — similarity of velocity field ratios.
- **Dynamic Similarity** — similarity of force ratios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Ship Models**
> A ship model is **never a perfect dynamical replica** of the prototype because Reynolds similarity and Froude similarity cannot be simultaneously satisfied using a single fluid (water) at a manageable scale. This is the famous **Similitude Dilemma**, and its resolution defines the entire experimental methodology of marine hydrodynamics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to know whether a giant cruise ship will float and move efficiently, but you cannot afford to build the ship first. Instead, you build a **small toy boat** that looks exactly like the big ship, then drag it through a giant swimming pool with precise instruments.

Why does this work? Because of **similarity** — the way air flows around a small paper aeroplane mimics the way air flows around a real one, but **only if** we obey certain rules.

The three rules of "model toy ships" are:

| Rule | What it means | Everyday analogy |
|------|---------------|------------------|
| **Geometric** | Same shape, just smaller | A perfect miniature statue of a person |
| **Kinematic** | Water flows around the model in the same *patterns* as around the ship | Wind whistling past a small flag mimics wind past a big flag |
| **Dynamic** | The *forces* acting on the model are in the same *ratios* as those on the ship | Push on a small box vs a big box — ratios must match |

The catch? Real water obeys the laws of **friction (viscosity)** AND **gravity (waves)**. We can match *only one at a time* in our pool. So engineers use a clever trick — match the wave-making behaviour (Froude number) and *correct* for the friction mismatch afterwards. This is the heart of ship model testing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants Used in Ship Model Testing**
> - **Kinematic viscosity of fresh water (20 °C):** $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
> - **Density of fresh water (20 °C):** $\rho = 998.2 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.80665 \, \text{m/s}^2$
> - **ITTC Standard Reference Temperature:** $T = 20\,°\text{C}$ for towing-tank tests

---

### 1.3 Why Ship Models? — The Engineering Imperative

A modern Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC) of length **~330 m** costs over **USD 100 million**. Building the ship and discovering it has poor fuel efficiency, excessive vibration, or inadequate manoeuvrability is *unacceptable*. Ship models therefore provide:

1. **Resistance prediction** for powering and fuel estimation.
2. **Propeller-hull interaction** (open-water, self-propulsion tests).
3. **Wake field characterisation** for propeller design.
4. **Manoeuvring derivatives** ($Y_v$, $N_r$, etc.) via captive tests (PMM, Rotating Arm).
5. **Seakeeping and slamming** response in waves.
6. **Cavitation inception** mapping for propellers and appendages.

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE, examiners frequently ask why we *cannot* use a single fluid to satisfy both Reynolds and Froude similarity simultaneously. Memorise this — it is a guaranteed 7-mark favourite when paired with the Froude-Krylov hypothesis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Similitude in Ship Model Testing

For a ship model to faithfully reproduce prototype behaviour, **three similarity conditions** must hold. Each condition propagates the previous one.

**Pillar 1 — Geometric Similarity**
Every linear dimension of the model equals the prototype dimension scaled by the **scale ratio** $\lambda$:

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_m}{L_s}
$$

where $L_m$ is the model length, $L_s$ is the ship (prototype) length, and $\lambda \ll 1$.

**Pillar 2 — Kinematic Similarity**
Streamlines in the model flow must be geometrically similar to those around the ship. This requires matching of **velocity ratios** at corresponding points:

$$
\frac{V_m}{V_s} = \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

when Froude scaling is adopted.

**Pillar 3 — Dynamic Similarity**
Force ratios at corresponding points must be equal. This requires matching of relevant non-dimensional numbers (Froude, Reynolds, Weber, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why All Three Are Required**
> Geometric similarity → preserves the *boundaries*. Kinematic similarity → preserves the *flow pattern*. Dynamic similarity → preserves the *force balance*. Skipping any one makes the test result physically meaningless.

### 2.2 The Similitude Dilemma (Froude's Paradox)

**The central problem of ship model testing:**

We need to match both:

- **Froude Number** $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ → controls **wave-making**
- **Reynolds Number** $R_n = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ → controls **viscous (frictional) flow**

If we use the same fluid (water) for model and ship, matching both requires:

$$
\frac{V_m}{V_s} = \sqrt{\lambda} \quad \text{(from Froude)} \qquad \frac{V_m}{V_s} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad \text{(from Reynolds)}
$$

These are contradictory unless $\lambda = 1$. So we **cannot** match both simultaneously.

**Resolution (Froude's Approach):**
Match **Froude Number** (gravity/wave effects dominate) and apply a **frictional correction** to account for the Reynolds mismatch. This is the basis of the **Froude-Krylov Hypothesis** and the **ITTC 1957/1978 correlation lines**.

### 2.3 Fundamental Scaling Laws (Froude-Based)

Given Froude similarity $F_{n,m} = F_{n,s}$:

$$
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}
$$

Since $g$ is the same:

$$
V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

Other derived quantities follow from dimensional analysis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time scale:}\quad & t_m = t_s \sqrt{\lambda} \\
\text{Displacement scale:}\quad & \nabla_m = \nabla_s \, \lambda^{3} \\
\text{Force scale:}\quad & F_m = F_s \, \lambda^{3} \\
\text{Power scale:}\quad & P_m = P_s \, \lambda^{3.5} \\
\text{Volume flow rate scale:}\quad & \dot{V}_m = \dot{V}_s \, \lambda^{2.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Power scaling exponent of 3.5** (not 3) is critical and frequently examined — it arises because Power = Force × Velocity, and velocity scales as $\lambda^{0.5}$.

### 2.4 Resistance Decomposition (Froude-Krylov Hypothesis)

The total resistance of a ship is decomposed into:

$$
R_T = R_F + R_R + R_W + R_A
$$

where:

- $R_F$ — **Frictional resistance** (viscous skin friction) → depends on $R_n$
- $R_R$ — **Residual resistance** (form + wave + eddy) → includes $R_W$ (wave-making) and form effects
- $R_W$ — **Wave-making resistance** (purely gravity-driven) → depends on $F_n$
- $R_A$ — **Air resistance** (often lumped with residual)

The **frictional resistance** is computed via the **ITTC 1957 friction line**:

$$
C_{F(ITTC)} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}
$$

The **model-ship correlation** then writes the **total resistance coefficient** as:

$$
C_{T,s} = \frac{C_{T,m}}{(\lambda + (1-\lambda)\frac{C_F + C_A}{C_{F,m}})^2} + C_A
$$

where $C_A$ is the **correlation allowance** and $C_F$ is the friction coefficient of the prototype hull.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Form Factor $(1 + k)$** accounts for the viscous pressure resistance of the full-scale hull. It is measured by performing **progressive resistance tests** at low Froude numbers (or via the **Prohaska method**) and extrapolating to $F_n \to 0$:

$$
C_T = C_F (1 + k) + C_W F_n^n
$$

where $k$ is the form factor and $C_W$ is the wave resistance coefficient.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Scale Ratio | $\lambda = L_m / L_s$ | $\lambda \in [0.02, 0.05]$ typical for ocean ships |
| 2 | Froude Number | $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ | Must match model ↔ ship |
| 3 | Reynolds Number | $R_n = VL/\nu$ | Will not match; corrected via form factor |
| 4 | Model Velocity | $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | From Froude similarity |
| 5 | Displacement | $\nabla_m = \nabla_s \lambda^{3}$ | Geometric volume scaling |
| 6 | Force | $F_m = F_s \lambda^{3}$ | All force components |
| 7 | Power | $P_m = P_s \lambda^{3.5}$ | Force × Velocity |
| 8 | Time | $t_m = t_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Useful for seakeeping |
| 9 | ITTC Friction | $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$ | Standard 1957 line |
| 10 | Total Resistance Coeff. | $C_T = C_F + C_R + C_A$ | Decomposed |
| 11 | Form Factor | $C_T/F_n^2 = (1+k) \cdot C_F/F_n^2 + C_W$ | Prohaska plot |
| 12 | Blockage Correction | $C_{T,\text{corr}} = C_T / (1 + \beta)$ | $\beta$ = blockage factor |
| 13 | Effective Power | $P_E = R_T \cdot V_s$ | Shaft − $P_E$ = useful output |
| 14 | Thrust Deduction | $t = (T - R)/T$ | Fraction of thrust lost to hull |
| 15 | Propulsive Coeff. | $\eta_D \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S$ | Overall propulsive efficiency |
| 16 | Wake Fraction | $w = 1 - V_A/V_s$ | Taylor wake, model wake |
| 17 | Hull Speed Length | $V = 1.34 \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ | Empirical, in knots & feet |
| 18 | Froude Krylov | $F = C_F \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^2$ | Skin friction |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table, **$C_R$** denotes residual resistance coefficient, **$C_A$** the correlation allowance (typically $C_A \approx (0.0004 \text{ to } 0.0010)$ for modern ships), **$S$** the wetted surface area, and **$T$** the propeller thrust.

### 2.6 The Real-World Utility of Ship Models

Ship models underpin virtually every maritime engineering decision:

- **Naval Architecture:** powering prediction for fleet design (US Navy, BMT, MARIN, HSVA).
- **Offshore Engineering:** semi-submersible, FPSO, spar platform model tests.
- **High-Performance Craft:** planning hulls, hydrofoils, SWATHs tested at high Froude.
- **Olympic & America's Cup Yachts:** VPP (Velocity Prediction Programs) calibrated by model tests.
- **Submarines:** resistance, manoeuvring, cavitation in deep-water CWC.
- **Commercial Shipping:** EEDI / EEXI compliance, slow-steaming optimisation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** If a numerical question asks for model velocity given $V_s = 20$ kn and $\lambda = 1/40$, students often forget to convert knots to m/s before applying $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$. Marks are deducted here regularly — *always keep SI units*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Model Velocity from Froude Similarity

**Given:**
- Prototype ship speed $V_s$
- Scale ratio $\lambda = L_m/L_s$
- Same fluid (water) and same gravity $g$

**Objective:** Derive the model velocity $V_m$.

**Step 1 — Equate Froude Numbers**

$$
F_{n,m} = F_{n,s} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}
$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $g$**

Since $g_m = g_s = g$ (same planet, same fluid), the square roots reduce to:

$$
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{L_s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_m$**

$$
V_m = V_s \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

This is the **Froude Velocity Scaling Law**. The same procedure yields every other quantity in the cheat sheet.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Scaling Exponent 3.5

**Given:** Force scales as $\lambda^3$, velocity scales as $\lambda^{0.5}$.

**Step 1 — Power Definition**

$$
P = F \cdot V
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Scaling**

$$
\frac{P_m}{P_s} = \frac{F_m}{F_s} \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_s} = \lambda^{3} \cdot \lambda^{0.5}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine**

$$
P_m = P_s \cdot \lambda^{3.5}
$$

> This is a **guaranteed exam question** in KTU. Memorise: Force ~ λ³, Velocity ~ λ^0.5, Power ~ λ^3.5, Time ~ λ^0.5.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1: Model Velocity Calculation

**Problem:** A ship of length $L_s = 240 \, \text{m}$ sails at $V_s = 14 \, \text{kn}$. A model of scale $\lambda = 1/60$ is to be tested in fresh water at $20\,°\text{C}$. Determine the model speed, model displacement, and the model Reynolds number.

**Step 1 — Convert Units to SI**

$$
V_s = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.2016 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Model Length**

$$
L_m = \lambda L_s = \frac{240}{60} = 4.0 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Model Speed (Froude)**

$$
V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 7.2016 \times \sqrt{1/60}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1/60} = 1/\sqrt{60} \approx 0.12910
$$

$$
V_m = 7.2016 \times 0.12910 = 0.9297 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Model Displacement**

$$
\nabla_m = \nabla_s \lambda^{3} = \nabla_s (1/60)^3 = \nabla_s \times 3.704 \times 10^{-6}
$$

If $C_B = 0.68$ and $L \cdot B \cdot T = 240 \times 32.2 \times 14.5 = 112{,}128 \, \text{m}^3$:

$$
\nabla_s = C_B \cdot LBT = 0.68 \times 112{,}128 = 76{,}247 \, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
\nabla_m = 76{,}247 \times 3.704 \times 10^{-6} = 0.2825 \, \text{m}^3 \;\; (\approx 282.5 \, \text{L})
$$

**Step 5 — Model Reynolds Number**

$$
R_{n,m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu} = \frac{0.9297 \times 4.0}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R_{n,m} = \frac{3.719}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.704 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 6 — Prototype Reynolds Number (for comparison)**

$$
R_{n,s} = \frac{7.2016 \times 240}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.722 \times 10^{9}
$$

The mismatch (factor ~465) confirms the similitude dilemma.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2: Model Resistance from Total Drag

**Problem:** A model of scale $\lambda = 1/50$ experiences total resistance $R_{T,m} = 22.4 \, \text{N}$ at model speed $V_m = 1.2 \, \text{m/s}$. Compute the corresponding prototype resistance, assuming Froude similarity and negligible air resistance.

**Step 1 — Prototype Speed**

$$
V_s = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{1/50}} = 1.2 \times \sqrt{50} = 1.2 \times 7.0711
$$

$$
V_s = 8.4853 \, \text{m/s} \approx 16.49 \, \text{kn}
$$

**Step 2 — Prototype Force**

$$
R_{T,s} = \frac{R_{T,m}}{\lambda^{3}} = \frac{22.4}{(1/50)^3} = 22.4 \times 50^{3}
$$

$$
50^{3} = 125{,}000
$$

$$
R_{T,s} = 22.4 \times 125{,}000 = 2{,}800{,}000 \, \text{N} = 2.8 \, \text{MN}
$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3: ITTC Friction Line & Form Factor

**Problem:** A model of length $L_m = 5\,\text{m}$ is tested at $V_m = 0.9\,\text{m/s}$. Compute the frictional resistance coefficient using the ITTC 1957 formula and the corresponding frictional drag for wetted area $S_m = 4.0\,\text{m}^2$ in fresh water ($\rho = 998\,\text{kg/m}^3$).

**Step 1 — Model Reynolds Number**

$$
R_{n,m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu} = \frac{0.9 \times 5}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R_{n,m} = 4.482 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 2 — ITTC 1957 Friction Coefficient**

$$
\log_{10} R_{n,m} = \log_{10}(4.482 \times 10^{6}) = 6.6517
$$

$$
\log_{10} R_{n,m} - 2 = 4.6517
$$

$$
(\log_{10} R_{n,m} - 2)^2 = 21.638
$$

$$
C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{21.638} = 3.466 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Frictional Resistance**

$$
R_{F,m} = C_{F,m} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_m^{2} S_m
$$

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_m^{2} = 0.5 \times 998 \times 0.9^2 = 404.19 \, \text{Pa}
$$

$$
R_{F,m} = 3.466 \times 10^{-3} \times 404.19 \times 4.0
$$

$$
R_{F,m} = 5.604 \, \text{N}
$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU-Style Numerical Templates

| Scenario | Given | Required | Key Formula | Pitfall |
|----------|-------|----------|-------------|---------|
| Model speed | $V_s$, $\lambda$ | $V_m$ | $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Convert knots → m/s |
| Model resistance | $R_{T,s}$, $\lambda$ | $R_{T,m}$ | $R_{T,m} = R_{T,s} \lambda^{3}$ | Resistance ↓, not ↑ |
| Model power | $P_s$, $\lambda$ | $P_m$ | $P_m = P_s \lambda^{3.5}$ | Exponent is 3.5 |
| Form factor | $C_T$ vs $F_n^2$ plot | $1+k$ | Y-intercept of Prohaska line | Use $(C_T - C_F)/C_F$ |
| Blockage | $C_T$, $A_m/A_T$ | $C_{T,\text{corr}}$ | $C_{T,\text{corr}} = C_T / (1 - A_m/A_T)^{1.5}$ | Sign convention varies |
| ITTC correlation | $C_{T,m}$, $\lambda$ | $C_{T,s}$ | Full ITTC formula (see §2.4) | Mind the $C_A$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of a Ship Model Test Campaign

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prototype Ship Design Lines] --> B[Wooden or Wax Model Manufacture]
    B --> C[Model Verification Scan CNC Check]
    C --> D[Resistance Test Towing Tank]
    D --> E[Self-Propulsion Test SP]
    E --> F[Wake Measurement Pitot Probe]
    F --> G[Propeller Open Water Test]
    G --> H[ITTC Correlation 1957 1978]
    H --> I[Effective Power Prediction]
    I --> J[Engine Power Selection]
    J --> K[Ship Delivery Performance Validation]
    
    D --> L[Cavitation Tunnel Test]
    L --> M[Cavitation Inception Tip Vortex]
    M --> K
    
    D --> N[Seakeeping Test Head Waves Oblique]
    N --> O[RAO Response Amplitude Operator]
    O --> P[Full Scale Motion Forecast]
    P --> K
    
    D --> Q[PMM Rotating Arm Captive]
    Q --> R[Hydrodynamic Derivatives Yv Nr]
    R --> S[Manoeuvring Simulation]
    S --> K
    
    stepA[Raw Model Data Resistance rpm thrust]:::dataNode
    H --> stepA
    
    classDef dataNode fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Resistance Decomposition Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RT[Total Resistance RT] --> RF[Frictional Resistance RF]
    RT --> RR[Residual Resistance RR]
    RR --> RW[Wave Making Resistance RW]
    RR --> REDD[Eddy Resistance]
    RR --> RFORM[Form Viscous Pressure Resistance]
    RT --> RA[Air Resistance RA]
    
    RF --> F1[Depends on Reynolds Number]
    RW --> F2[Depends on Froude Number]
    F1 --> ITU[ITTC Friction Line 1957]
    F2 --> WAVE[Wave Pattern Analysis]

    F1 --> stepX
    F2 --> stepX

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Similitude Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Goal Predict Full Scale Ship Behaviour] --> B{Need Geometric Similarity?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Scale Length L lambda]
    B -->|No| Z1[Invalid Test]
    
    C --> D{Which Dominant Force?}
    D -->|Gravity Waves| E[Match Froude Number]
    D -->|Viscous Friction| F[Match Reynolds Number]
    D -->|Both Waves and Friction| G[Similitude Dilemma]
    
    G --> H[Resolution: Match Froude Correct Friction]
    H --> I[Use ITTC Correlation Procedure]
    I --> J[Add Correlation Allowance CA]
    J --> K[Report Effective Power PE]
    
    E --> L[V_m = V_s sqrt lambda]
    F --> M[V_m = V_s lambda]
    
    stepY[For Surface Ships Choose Froude]:::hint
    H --> stepY
    
    classDef hint fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Functional Description of Diagrams

**Diagram 1 — Test Campaign Flow** maps the *complete life-cycle* of a ship model test from line plan to full-scale delivery. Note the four parallel branches:

1. Resistance + propulsion (steady-state performance)
2. Cavitation (propeller-induced erosion, tip vortex cavitation)
3. Seakeeping (RAO in head/beam/oblique waves)
4. Manoeuvring (PMM / rotating arm / CFD-coupled free-running)

**Diagram 2 — Resistance Hierarchy** shows how total resistance decomposes under the Froude-Krylov hypothesis and indicates which physical mechanism depends on which non-dimensional number.

**Diagram 3 — Similitude Decision Tree** is the *exam-oriented* answer template for "explain the similitude dilemma and its resolution".

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 ESE answers, **Diagram 3's logic** is a guaranteed 7-mark structuring template — reproduce it in flow-chart form in your answer booklet.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the three fundamental similarity conditions required for ship model testing. Why is complete dynamic similarity practically impossible using a single fluid?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three similarity conditions are:

1. **Geometric similarity** — every linear dimension of the model is a constant fraction ($\lambda$) of the corresponding ship dimension.
2. **Kinematic similarity** — the velocity ratios at corresponding points in the flow field are equal.
3. **Dynamic similarity** — the ratios of all relevant forces at corresponding points are equal.

Dynamic similarity requires simultaneous matching of the **Froude number** $F_n$ (for wave-making) and the **Reynolds number** $R_n$ (for friction). With the same fluid (water), this demands:

$$
V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} \;\;\text{(Froude)} \quad \text{and} \quad V_m = V_s \lambda \;\;\text{(Reynolds)}
$$

Both cannot hold unless $\lambda = 1$ (no scaling). Hence, complete dynamic similarity is **practically impossible** with a single fluid — this is the **similitude dilemma** (Froude's paradox).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Form Factor $(1+k)$ and mention any one method used to determine it experimentally.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Form Factor $(1 + k)$** is the ratio of the viscous resistance (frictional + form-induced pressure drag) of a ship hull to its pure frictional resistance computed from the ITTC 1957 line:

$$
1 + k = \frac{C_V}{C_F}
$$

It accounts for the extra viscous pressure (form) drag beyond pure skin friction. Since form drag cannot be computed analytically for a ship hull, it must be measured.

**Methods (any one, 1 mark):**

- **Prohaska Method (1948):** Perform resistance tests over a range of low Froude numbers; plot $C_T/C_F$ vs $F_n^4$. The Y-intercept gives $1 + k$.
- **Granville Method:** Tests at high Reynolds but very low Froude to isolate viscous form drag.
- **CFD-based correlation:** Modern RANS-based pressure-integration gives $C_V$ directly.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice** — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Resistance Decomposition & ITTC Correlation*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

(a) **[7 marks]** Explain the **Froude-Krylov hypothesis** for resistance decomposition. State the ITTC 1957 friction line formula and derive the expression for residual resistance coefficient $C_R$ from model test data.

(b) **[7 marks]** A model of scale $\lambda = 1/40$ is tested at $V_m = 1.5 \, \text{m/s}$ in fresh water at 20 °C. The model has wetted surface area $S_m = 4.8 \, \text{m}^2$, length $L_m = 6\,\text{m}$, and total measured resistance $R_{T,m} = 17.2\,\text{N}$. Compute (i) model Reynolds number, (ii) ITTC friction coefficient $C_{F,m}$, (iii) residual resistance coefficient $C_R$, and (iv) frictional resistance $R_{F,m}$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Froude-Krylov Hypothesis and ITTC Friction Line** *(7 marks)*

The **Froude-Krylov hypothesis** (attributed to William Froude, 1871) states that the resistance of a ship can be decomposed into **independent**, additive components:

$$
R_T = R_F + R_R + R_A
$$

where $R_F$ depends solely on **Reynolds number** (viscous flow), $R_R$ (residual) depends on **Froude number** (wave + form drag), and $R_A$ is the air resistance. The decomposition assumes these effects are *weakly coupled*, which is a valid engineering approximation.

In coefficient form:

$$
C_T = C_F + C_R + C_A
$$

The **ITTC 1957 frictional line** gives the frictional coefficient of a *flat, smooth, turbulent plate*:

$$
C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}
$$

For a ship, the wetted surface area is used in the Reynolds number. The **residual resistance coefficient** is then extracted:

$$
C_R = C_T - C_F - C_A
$$

Equivalently, after measuring $R_{T,m}$:

$$
C_T = \frac{R_{T,m}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_m^2 S_m} \;\;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\; C_R = C_T - C_F
$$

For full-scale prediction, the ITTC correlation formula extrapolates model resistance to ship scale:

$$
C_{T,s} = \frac{C_{T,m} - C_A + (C_F + C_A)\cdot(\lambda/\beta)}{\lambda^{0.5} + (1-\lambda^{0.5}) (C_F + C_A)/C_{F,m}}
$$

with $\beta = 0.14$ (form factor weighting per ITTC 1978), $C_A \approx 0.0004$.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Model Reynolds Number** *(1 mark)*

$$
R_{n,m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu} = \frac{1.5 \times 6}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.964 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 2 — ITTC 1957 Friction Coefficient** *(2 marks)*

$$
\log_{10}(8.964 \times 10^{6}) = 6.9526
$$

$$
(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2 = (4.9526)^2 = 24.528
$$

$$
C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{24.528} = 3.058 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Resistance Coefficient** *(1 mark)*

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_m^{2} = 0.5 \times 998 \times (1.5)^2 = 1122.75 \, \text{Pa}
$$

$$
C_{T,m} = \frac{R_{T,m}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{17.2}{1122.75 \times 4.8} = \frac{17.2}{5389.2} = 3.192 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Residual Resistance Coefficient** *(2 marks)*

$$
C_R = C_T - C_F = 3.192 \times 10^{-3} - 3.058 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
C_R = 1.34 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Frictional Resistance** *(1 mark)*

$$
R_{F,m} = C_{F,m} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_m^2 S_m = 3.058 \times 10^{-3} \times 5389.2
$$

$$
R_{F,m} = 16.481 \, \text{N}
$$

**Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $R_{n,m}$ | $8.96 \times 10^{6}$ |
| $C_{F,m}$ | $3.058 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| $C_R$ | $1.34 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| $R_{F,m}$ | $16.48 \, \text{N}$ |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Scaling Laws & Blockage Correction*

**(a) [7 marks]** A geometrically similar model of a container ship has scale $\lambda = 1/50$. If the prototype length is $L_s = 300 \, \text{m}$ and the prototype speed is $V_s = 25 \, \text{kn}$, determine: (i) the model length, (ii) the model speed in m/s, (iii) the time taken by the model to traverse one model length, (iv) the required model propulsive power if the prototype effective power is $P_E = 25 \, \text{MW}$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **blockage effect** in towing tanks. Derive the expression for the **blockage-corrected resistance coefficient** using a simple two-dimensional approximation. State ITTC-recommended limits on blockage ratios.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Scaling Computations** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Model Length** *(1 mark)*

$$
L_m = \lambda L_s = \frac{300}{50} = 6.0 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Model Speed** *(2 marks)*

Convert $V_s$ to m/s: $V_s = 25 \times 0.5144 = 12.86 \, \text{m/s}$.

$$
V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 12.86 \times \sqrt{1/50} = 12.86 \times 0.14142
$$

$$
V_m = 1.819 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to Traverse One Model Length** *(2 marks)*

$$
t_m = \frac{L_m}{V_m} = \frac{6.0}{1.819} = 3.298 \, \text{s}
$$

Check using scaling: $t_m = t_s \sqrt{\lambda}$, with $t_s = L_s/V_s = 300/12.86 = 23.33\,\text{s}$:

$$
t_m = 23.33 \times \sqrt{1/50} = 23.33 \times 0.14142 = 3.30 \, \text{s} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Model Power** *(2 marks)*

$$
P_{E,m} = P_{E,s} \cdot \lambda^{3.5}
$$

$$
\lambda^{3.5} = (1/50)^{3.5} = \frac{1}{50^{3.5}}
$$

$$
50^{3.5} = 50^3 \times 50^{0.5} = 125{,}000 \times 7.071 = 883{,}884
$$

$$
P_{E,m} = \frac{25 \times 10^{6}}{883{,}884} = 28.28 \, \text{W}
$$

(Equivalently $P_{E,m} = 25\,\text{MW} \times (1/50)^{3.5} \approx 28.3\,\text{W}$.)

---

**(b) Blockage Effect** *(7 marks)*

**Concept:** When a ship model is towed in a finite-width towing tank, the water displaced by the model has nowhere to escape but past the model. This **constrains the flow streamlines**, artificially increasing the velocity past the hull and *overestimating* the resistance. This is the **blockage effect**.

It is most severe when:

$$
\frac{A_m}{A_T} > 0.06 \;\; (\text{some standards use } 0.05)
$$

where $A_m$ is the model cross-sectional area at the station and $A_T$ is the tank cross-sectional area.

**2D Approximation:** For a body placed centrally in a 2D flow of width $b$, mass conservation requires the velocity in the gap to increase from $V$ to $V(1 + A_m/(b h - A_m))$ approximately, giving a pressure/velocity correction leading to:

$$
C_{T,\text{corr}} = \frac{C_{T,\text{meas}}}{1 - K \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_T}}
$$

For a slot-type approximation, $K \approx 1.5$ (Blake, 1970). The ITTC recommended correction:

$$
C_{T,\text{corr}} = C_{T,\text{meas}} \left(1 + \frac{A_m}{A_T}\right)
$$

(for shallow-water or high-blockage cases).

**ITTC Recommended Limits (2014, 2017):**

| Blockage Ratio $A_m/A_T$ | Action |
|---------------------------|--------|
| $< 0.0058$ | Acceptable without correction |
| $0.0058 \le A_m/A_T \le 0.01$ | Apply correction |
| $> 0.01$ | Unacceptable — reduce model size or use deeper tank |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Knots ↔ m/s Confusion:** $1 \, \text{kn} = 0.5144 \, \text{m/s}$. Forgetting this conversion loses 1–2 marks instantly in any $V_m$ or $P_m$ problem.
> 2. **Power Exponent Mistake:** Students often write $P_m = P_s \cdot \lambda^3$. The correct exponent is **3.5** because Power = Force × Velocity, and Velocity scales as $\lambda^{0.5}$.
> 3. **C_A Omitted:** When applying ITTC correlation, neglecting the correlation allowance $C_A$ gives a too-optimistic resistance prediction. Always include $C_A \approx 0.0004$ for full-scale extrapolation.
> 4. **Blockage Sign:** Blockage **increases** measured resistance; the correction *reduces* the value. Reverse sign and you lose full marks.
> 5. **Form Factor Misuse:** $(1+k)$ multiplies $C_F$, *not* $C_T$. The relation is $C_T = C_F(1+k) + C_W$, not $C_T = C_T \cdot (1+k)$.
> 6. **Froude Number Definition:** Some students write $F_n = V^2/(gL)$ — the correct definition is $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$. Careless squaring costs marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Geometric, kinematic, dynamic similarity** — the three pillars, in that order.
- [ ] **Froude number** $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ governs wave-making; **Reynolds number** $R_n = VL/\nu$ governs friction.
- [ ] **Similitude dilemma**: Froude + Reynolds cannot be matched simultaneously with one fluid → match Froude, correct for Reynolds via friction line + form factor.
- [ ] **Froude scaling exponents:** Length $L \sim \lambda$, Velocity $V \sim \lambda^{0.5}$, Force $F \sim \lambda^{3}$, Power $P \sim \lambda^{3.5}$, Time $t \sim \lambda^{0.5}$, Volume $\nabla \sim \lambda^{3}$.
- [ ] **Froude-Krylov Hypothesis:** $R_T = R_F + R_R + R_A$; in coefficients $C_T = C_F + C_R + C_A$.
- [ ] **ITTC 1957 friction line:** $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$.
- [ ] **Form factor $(1 + k)$** measured by Prohaska method; reflects viscous pressure drag of hull.
- [ ] **ITTC correlation** extrapolates model $C_T$ to ship, using $\lambda$, $C_{F,m}$, $C_F$, $C_A$.
- [ ] **Blockage effect:** tank walls accelerate flow past model → overestimate resistance; correct using $A_m/A_T$.
- [ ] **Reference conditions:** freshwater 20 °C, $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $\rho = 998.2\,\text{kg/m}^3$.
- [ ] **Typical ship-model scales:** $\lambda \in [1/30, 1/80]$ for ocean-going vessels; $\lambda \sim 1/15$ for submarines.
- [ ] **Test facilities:** Towing Tank (resistance), CWC (manoeuvring), Cavitation Tunnel (cavitation), Seakeeping Basin (waves), Rotating Arm (PMM).
- [ ] **Always convert knots to m/s** before any numerical calculation.
- [ ] **Power scaling exponent 3.5** (not 3) is a guaranteed 7-mark question in every ESE.

> **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024 ESE Module 2):**
> *"Match Froude, correct Reynolds. Force λ³, Velocity λ^½, Power λ^3.5, Time λ^½, ITTC 1957 friction line, Prohaska for form factor."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laws of Comparisons and Similarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Laws of Comparisons and Similarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In **Marine Hydrodynamics**, the **Laws of Comparisons and Similarity** (often called the *Laws of Similitude*) are a set of mathematical-physical principles that govern the design, scaling, and interpretation of **model experiments** used to predict the hydrodynamic behavior of full-scale ships, offshore structures, submarines, and marine propellers. The fundamental premise is that a small-scale model, when tested in a towing tank, circulating water channel, or cavitation tunnel, will produce quantitatively transferable results to the prototype **only if specific similarity conditions are satisfied**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *Similitude is the principle that two systems (model and prototype) will experience identical physical phenomena when the ratios of all relevant forces and the geometry, motion, and boundary conditions are preserved between them. The Laws of Comparison are the specific scaling relationships (ratios of length, velocity, time, force, power, etc.) that emerge from enforcing these similarity conditions.*

The complete set of similarity conditions is divided into three hierarchical tiers:

1. **Geometric Similarity** – Shape, proportions, and roughness are preserved.
2. **Kinematic Similarity** – Motion (velocities, accelerations) is preserved in direction and magnitude ratio.
3. **Dynamic Similarity** – All relevant forces are in a constant ratio between model and prototype.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you have a **soap bubble maker** in your hand. The manufacturer wants to know if their new soap mix will produce the perfect bubble. They cannot test the bubble in every bathroom in the world — too expensive. Instead, they use a small hand-held **test wand** in the lab, blow a small bubble, and measure its behavior. The lab bubble is the **model**; the real-world bubble in your bathroom is the **prototype**.

For the test to be useful:
- The wand's *shape* must be geometrically similar to the actual bubble ring (geometric similarity).
- The *air velocity* leaving the wand must be in the same ratio as the real device (kinematic similarity).
- The *forces* — surface tension, gravity, air pressure — must all scale together in the same proportion (dynamic similarity).

If even one of these breaks, the small bubble might look like the real one but pop in a completely different way, giving wrong predictions. **The Laws of Comparisons are the math that tells you exactly how to size the test wand, the air speed, and the soap mix so the prediction is trustworthy.** The same logic applies to a 1:40 scale ship model in a 4-meter-wide towing tank.

> [!NOTE]
> The single most important practical consequence: **You cannot scale everything in real life.** Water has fixed density, gravity is fixed, and air pressure is fixed. Because of this, achieving *all* force ratios simultaneously is impossible — you must choose the **dominant force(s)** and match only those. This trade-off is the heart of the *Laws of Comparison*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
The following constants and dimensionless numbers are the **vocabulary** of this topic and will be used throughout the module:

- **ρ** (rho) – Fluid density, **kg/m³** (Water ≈ **1000 kg/m³**, Seawater ≈ **1025 kg/m³**)
- **ν** (nu) – Kinematic viscosity of water, **m²/s** (≈ **1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** for fresh water at 20 °C)
- **g** – Acceleration due to gravity, **m/s²** (≈ **9.81 m/s²**)
- **σ** (sigma) – Surface tension, **N/m** (Water-air ≈ **0.0728 N/m**)
- **E** – Bulk modulus of elasticity, **N/m²** (Water ≈ **2.19 × 10⁹ N/m²**)
- **c** – Speed of sound in the medium, **m/s** (Water ≈ **1500 m/s**)

### 1.4 Hierarchy of Similitude
The three tiers must be enforced in sequence; the lower tier is a *prerequisite* for the next:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric ⇒ Kinematic ⇒ Dynamic**
> 
> A body in steady translation at the same velocity ratio as another, identical in shape, will automatically have the same streamline pattern (kinematic similarity), provided the same *dynamic* force ratios hold. This is the principle of **Reynolds' Law of Similitude**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Similarity — Detailed Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Geometric Similarity
Geometric similarity is the **necessary foundation** of all model testing. The model and prototype must have:
- The **same shape** in all three dimensions.
- All linear dimensions scaled by a single, constant **scale factor (λ)**.

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_m}{L_p}
$$

where subscripts *m* denote model and *p* denote prototype. For marine vessels, the scale is typically expressed as a **scale ratio** (1:30, 1:40, 1:50).

> [!NOTE]
> **Roughness similarity** is a subtle extension: the model must replicate the *relative roughness* (k/L) of the prototype hull. This is why model hulls are sometimes coated with carefully graded sand or carborundum to stimulate turbulent boundary layers of the same character as the full-scale ship.

#### 2.1.2 Kinematic Similarity
Kinematic similarity requires that **velocities at corresponding points** in the model and prototype flow fields be in the same direction and bear a constant ratio. This ratio is the **velocity scale (κ)**.

$$
\kappa = \frac{V_m}{V_p}
$$

- Streamlines must be geometrically similar.
- Time intervals for corresponding events must scale by a **time scale (τ)**:

$$
\tau = \frac{t_m}{t_p} = \frac{\lambda}{\kappa}
$$

- Acceleration, discharge, and volumetric flow rates all scale accordingly.

#### 2.1.3 Dynamic Similarity
This is the **most demanding** condition. The ratios of all relevant forces (inertia, gravity, viscosity, surface tension, elasticity) acting on corresponding fluid elements must be equal. The dimensionless numbers that emerge are the **similitude parameters** (Reynolds, Froude, Weber, Cauchy, Mach, Euler, Strouhal).

### 2.2 The Dimensionless Numbers — The "Personality" of the Flow

Each dimensionless number expresses the ratio of two competing physical effects. **The dominant number is the one with the highest relative magnitude in the force balance.** This is what the student must internalize for KTU 2024 examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **most common KTU exam trap** is to assume a ship is purely a Froude-scale problem. A submarine fully submerged, or a high-speed cavitating propeller, has additional dominant forces (viscous, surface tension, or elastic). Always read the *flow regime* carefully.

#### 2.2.1 Reynolds Number (Re) — Inertia vs. Viscous Forces

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{V L}{\nu}
$$

- **Re < 5 × 10⁵** → laminar flow
- **5 × 10⁵ < Re < 10⁶** → transitional
- **Re > 10⁶** → turbulent
- **Critical Re for a ship hull** is typically around **3–5 × 10⁵** based on length.

#### 2.2.2 Froude Number (Fr) — Inertia vs. Gravity

$$
\text{Fr} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}
$$

This is the **dominant similarity parameter for a free-surface ship** in deep or unrestricted water because gravity controls the wave-making resistance.

#### 2.2.3 Weber Number (We) — Inertia vs. Surface Tension

$$
\text{We} = \frac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma}
$$

Important in **cavitation tunnels**, **spray studies**, and **model tests at very small scales** (where surface tension becomes disproportionately large).

#### 2.2.4 Cauchy Number (Ca) — Inertia vs. Compressibility (Elasticity)

$$
\text{Ca} = \frac{\rho V^2}{E} = \frac{V^2}{c^2}
$$

Equivalent to the inverse of the Mach number squared: **Ca = 1/M²**. Important in **underwater explosion studies**, **sonic shock research**, and **cavitating flows**.

#### 2.2.5 Mach Number (M)

$$
M = \frac{V}{c}
$$

Not normally relevant for surface ships (V << c), but **essential for high-speed supercavitating vehicles** and underwater projectiles.

#### 2.2.6 Strouhal Number (St) — Unsteady Inertia vs. Convective Inertia

$$
\text{St} = \frac{f L}{V}
$$

Important in **vortex shedding** behind bluff bodies, **propeller singing**, and **SARP (Singing, Alternating, Rotating, Pulsing)** analysis.

#### 2.2.7 Euler Number (Eu) — Pressure vs. Inertia

$$
\text{Eu} = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2}
$$

Central to **cavitation similarity** — cavitation number is a cousin of the Euler number.

#### 2.2.8 Prandtl, Schmidt, Péclet Numbers
Heat and mass transfer similarities; relevant for **hot-water model basins** (used to study thermal stratification around submarines).

### 2.3 The Conflict of Similarities — A Practical Engineering Reality

To achieve *simultaneous* similarity across all force ratios, the scale ratio would have to be **1:1** (i.e., a full-scale prototype), because the physical constants (g, ρ, ν, σ, E, c) are tied to real water and real air. To retain *some* similarity, the engineer **selects the dominant force(s)** and accepts a small mismatch (called the **scale effect**) in the others.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reef Point / KTU 2024 Emphasis:**
> For a **displacement-type surface ship**, the dominant forces are inertia and gravity → **Froude scaling**.
> For a **submerged body** with no free surface, gravity drops out → **Reynolds scaling**.
> For a **cavitating propeller**, cavitation and surface tension dominate → **Weber/Euler scaling**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for Module 2. Memorize the symbols, the formula, and the dominant regime.**

| # | Dimensionless Number | Formula | Physical Meaning | Dominant Regime in Marine Hydrodynamics |
|---|----------------------|---------|------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 1 | Reynolds (Re) | $\rho V L / \mu = V L / \nu$ | Inertia ÷ Viscous | Submerged bodies, resistance without waves, propeller duct flows |
| 2 | Froude (Fr) | $V / \sqrt{g L}$ | Inertia ÷ Gravity | Surface ships, wave resistance, ship-model correlation |
| 3 | Weber (We) | $\rho V^2 L / \sigma$ | Inertia ÷ Surface Tension | Spray, very small models, jet breakup, cavitation onset |
| 4 | Cauchy (Ca) | $\rho V^2 / E$ | Inertia ÷ Elastic | Underwater explosions, hydro-elasticity, slamming impacts |
| 5 | Mach (M) | $V / c$ | Velocity ÷ Sound Speed | High-speed supercavitating projectiles |
| 6 | Strouhal (St) | $f L / V$ | Unsteady Inertia ÷ Convective Inertia | Vortex shedding, propeller singing, bluff-body flows |
| 7 | Euler (Eu) | $\Delta p / (\rho V^2)$ | Pressure ÷ Inertia | Cavitation, pressure distribution on hydrofoils |
| 8 | Prandtl (Pr) | $\nu / \alpha$ | Momentum diffusivity ÷ Thermal diffusivity | Heated model basins, thermal discharges |
| 9 | Péclet (Pe) | $V L / \alpha$ | Convection ÷ Diffusion | Thermal mixing, density-stratified flows |
| 10 | Damköhler (Da) | Reaction rate ÷ Flow rate | Chemistry ÷ Flow | (Advanced) — not in KTU Module 2 core |

### 2.5 The Scale Ratios — Generalized Law of Comparison

Once the dominant dimensionless number is fixed (say, $\Pi = $ constant), the model/prototype ratios for all dependent quantities are derived through dimensional analysis. For any two physically similar systems:

$$
\Pi_m = \Pi_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{one force scale must equal the geometric scale raised to a power.}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **operational meaning** of the "Laws of Comparison" — a set of equations expressing $\lambda_V$, $\lambda_t$, $\lambda_F$, $\lambda_P$, $\lambda_Q$ in terms of the scale ratio $\lambda_L$ and the chosen dominant $\Pi$ group.

A standard textbook result (Hughes, *Ship Structural Design*) gives the **general form**:

$$
\Pi = \frac{\rho^a \mu^b g^c \sigma^d E^e V^f L^g}{F^h}
$$

By holding $\Pi_m = \Pi_p$ for the chosen dominant force, one derives the **velocity ratio**, **time ratio**, **force ratio**, and **power ratio** — these *are* the Laws of Comparison.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Condition for Dynamic Similarity from the Navier-Stokes Equations

This derivation is the **gold standard** of any similarity question in KTU and is worth full 7 marks.

**Step 1 — Start with the Navier-Stokes momentum equation for incompressible flow:**

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the four term families.** Reading left to right, the equation has unsteady inertia, convective inertia, pressure gradient, viscous force, and body force (gravity). Each term has units of **force per unit volume** ($\text{N/m}^3$).

**Step 3 — Non-dimensionalize by introducing a reference length $L$, reference velocity $V$, reference time $T = L/V$, reference pressure $\rho V^2$, and reference density $\rho$.** Define dimensionless variables:

$$
\vec{V}^* = \frac{\vec{V}}{V}, \quad \vec{x}^* = \frac{\vec{x}}{L}, \quad t^* = \frac{t V}{L}, \quad p^* = \frac{p}{\rho V^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Navier-Stokes equation.** Note carefully that the time derivative produces $\partial / \partial t = (V/L) \partial / \partial t^*$, and the Laplacian $\nabla^2 = (1/L^2) \nabla^{*2}$. We obtain:

$$
\rho \frac{V^2}{L} \left[ \frac{\partial \vec{V}^*}{\partial t^*} + (\vec{V}^* \cdot \nabla^*) \vec{V}^* \right] = -\frac{\rho V^2}{L} \nabla^* p^* + \mu \frac{V}{L^2} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^* + \rho \vec{g}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide every term through by the inertial term factor $\rho V^2 / L$:**

$$
\left[ \frac{\partial \vec{V}^*}{\partial t^*} + (\vec{V}^* \cdot \nabla^*) \vec{V}^* \right] = -\nabla^* p^* + \frac{\mu}{\rho V L} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^* + \frac{g L}{V^2} \cdot \frac{\vec{g}}{g}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify the dimensionless coefficients.** The coefficient of the viscous term is **1/Re** and the coefficient of the gravity term is **1/Fr²**. The non-dimensional equation reads:

$$
\frac{\partial \vec{V}^*}{\partial t^*} + (\vec{V}^* \cdot \nabla^*) \vec{V}^* = -\nabla^* p^* + \frac{1}{\text{Re}} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^* + \frac{1}{\text{Fr}^2} \hat{g}
$$

**Step 7 — State the condition for similarity.** For two flows (model and prototype) to have **identical dimensionless velocity and pressure fields**, the coefficients of all retained terms must be **equal between model and prototype**. This is the **Laws of Comparison** in their most general form:

$$
\text{Re}_m = \text{Re}_p, \quad \text{Fr}_m = \text{Fr}_p, \quad \text{Eu}_m = \text{Eu}_p, \quad \text{etc.}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental conclusion:** Two flows are dynamically similar if, and only if, **all relevant dimensionless groups are simultaneously equal**. If any relevant group is allowed to differ, the result is a **scale effect**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Froude Scaling Laws for a Surface Ship

Because the dominant similarity for a surface ship is the Froude number, this is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU Module 2 exam.

**Step 1 — Enforce Froude similarity between model (m) and prototype (p):**

$$
\text{Fr}_m = \text{Fr}_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g_m L_m}} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{g_p L_p}}
$$

**Step 2 — Gravity is identical on Earth** ($g_m = g_p = g$). The $g$ terms cancel:

$$
\frac{V_m^2}{g L_m} = \frac{V_p^2}{g L_p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_m^2}{V_p^2} = \frac{L_m}{L_p}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the linear scale ratio** $\lambda = L_m / L_p$ (typically a small number, e.g., 1/40). Then:

$$
\lambda_V \equiv \frac{V_m}{V_p} = \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

This is the **Law of Comparison for Velocity** under Froude scaling.

**Step 4 — Derive the time scale** $\lambda_t = t_m / t_p$. Since time = length/velocity:

$$
\lambda_t = \frac{L_m / V_m}{L_p / V_p} = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

So the time scale equals the velocity scale under Froude similarity.

**Step 5 — Derive the force scale** $\lambda_F = F_m / F_p$. Force has dimensions $[M L T^{-2}] = [\rho L^2 V^2]$. Under Froude scaling, $V^2 \propto L$:

$$
\lambda_F = \rho \lambda_L^2 \lambda_V^2 = 1 \cdot \lambda \cdot \lambda = \lambda^3
$$

Therefore the **force scale is the cube of the length scale**.

**Step 6 — Derive the power scale** $\lambda_P = P_m / P_p$. Power has dimensions $[M L^2 T^{-3}] = [\rho L^2 V^3]$:

$$
\lambda_P = \rho \lambda_L^2 \lambda_V^3 = \lambda^2 \cdot \lambda^{3/2} = \lambda^{7/2}
$$

**Step 7 — Derive the displacement (volume) scale** $\lambda_{\nabla}$ and the **weight scale** $\lambda_W$. Since $W = \rho g \nabla$ and both $\rho$ and $g$ are unchanged:

$$
\lambda_W = \lambda_{\nabla} = \lambda^3
$$

This must be checked: if the model is too small, the **weight of the model** may be insufficient to load the test apparatus; this is one practical limit on small-scale model testing.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Froude Scale Calculation for a Tanker

> **Problem (Sample):** A 320 m long tanker (prototype) is to be tested at scale 1:40 in a towing tank. Calculate (a) the model speed that corresponds to a prototype speed of 15 knots, (b) the model resistance corresponding to a prototype total resistance of 8,000 kN, (c) the model power required.

**Step (a) — Velocity scale and model speed**

$$
\lambda_L = \frac{1}{40}, \quad \lambda_V = \sqrt{\lambda_L} = \sqrt{0.025} = 0.1581
$$

Convert prototype speed: $V_p = 15 \text{ knots} = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$. Then:

$$
V_m = \lambda_V \cdot V_p = 0.1581 \times 7.716 = 1.220 \text{ m/s} = 2.37 \text{ knots}
$$

**Step (b) — Resistance (force) scale**

$$
\lambda_F = \lambda_L^3 = (0.025)^3 = 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Model resistance:

$$
R_m = 8000 \text{ kN} \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-5} = 0.125 \text{ kN} = 125 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (c) — Power scale**

$$
\lambda_P = \lambda_L^{7/2} = (0.025)^{3.5} = 9.868 \times 10^{-7}
$$

Prototype power $P_p = R_p \cdot V_p = 8000 \times 7.716 / 1000 = 61.73 \text{ MW}$. Model power:

$$
P_m = 61.73 \text{ MW} \times 9.868 \times 10^{-7} = 60.9 \text{ W}
$$

This tiny power explains why model propellers are usually **special, high-rpm motors** rather than scaled-down versions of the prototype engine.

### 3.4 Derivation: Reynolds Scaling for Submerged Bodies

For a fully submerged body (no free surface, no waves), gravity is irrelevant. The dominant similarity is Reynolds.

**Step 1 — Enforce Reynolds similarity:**

$$
\text{Re}_m = \text{Re}_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu_p}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the same fluid (water) for model and prototype**, $\nu_m = \nu_p$:

$$
\lambda_V \cdot \lambda_L = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_V = \frac{1}{\lambda_L}
$$

**Step 3 — Time scale:**

$$
\lambda_t = \frac{\lambda_L}{\lambda_V} = \lambda_L^2
$$

**Step 4 — Force scale** (using $F \propto \rho V^2 L^2$):

$$
\lambda_F = \lambda_L^2 \cdot \lambda_V^2 = \lambda_L^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda_L^2} = 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Under Reynolds scaling with the same fluid, the model **force equals the prototype force** if similarity is preserved. This is the basis of **submerged-body testing** (submarines, towed fish, underwater vehicles).

### 3.5 Derivation: Weber / Cavitation Similarity for Propellers

For a cavitating propeller test in a cavitation tunnel, the dominant force balance involves inertia, pressure, and surface tension. The **cavitation number** $\sigma$ is the controlling parameter:

$$
\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Match cavitation number between tunnel and service:**

$$
\sigma_m = \sigma_p
$$

**Step 2 — Match the Reynolds number** (for boundary-layer similarity on the blade) and **match the Weber number** (for surface tension effects in the tunnel):

$$
\text{Re}_m = \text{Re}_p, \quad \text{We}_m = \text{We}_p
$$

**Step 3 — Resolve the conflict.** With the same fluid, We-matching forces:

$$
V_m^2 L_m = V_p^2 L_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_V^2 \lambda_L = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_V = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_L}}
$$

This is **intermediate** between Froude and Reynolds scaling. In practice, cavitation tunnels are pressurized to artificially lower $\sigma$ without requiring extreme speeds.

### 3.6 Scale Effects — When Laws of Comparison Fail

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 expected answer style:** A 7-mark question may ask *“Discuss the scale effect in Froude-scaled model testing of a tanker and suggest two correction methods.”* The student must define, quantify, and remedy.

A **scale effect** is the systematic deviation between model and prototype behavior caused by deliberately relaxing one or more of the secondary similarity requirements. The most studied is the **resistance scale effect**:

- A small Froude-scaled model runs at a lower Reynolds number than the prototype.
- The frictional component of resistance depends on Reynolds number, so model frictional resistance is **over-predicted** relative to the prototype.
- This is corrected using the **ITTC 1978 / ITTC 2017 correlation line** (form factor $k$, roughness allowance $\Delta C_F$) or via the **Prohaska method** and the **Hughes correlation**.

The **practical correction recipe** is:
1. Measure total resistance on the model: $R_{T,m}$.
2. Compute frictional resistance by ITTC formula: $R_{F,m} = 0.5 \rho V_m^2 S_m C_{F,m}$.
3. Compute form factor $k$ from a standard extrapolation method.
4. Compute residual resistance coefficient $C_R = (R_{T,m} - R_{F,m}(1+k)) / (0.5 \rho V_m^2 S_m)$.
5. Apply $C_R$ to the prototype to obtain the predicted total resistance: $R_{T,p} = R_{F,p}(1+k) + 0.5 \rho V_p^2 S_p C_R$.

This is the **ITTC 1978 Performance Prediction Method**, the standard KTU Module 2 capstone technique.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**
> **Define geometric similarity, kinematic similarity, and dynamic similarity. Which is the most fundamental, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Geometric similarity (1 mark):** The model and prototype are identical in shape, with all linear dimensions scaled by a single constant ratio $\lambda = L_m / L_p$.
- **Kinematic similarity (1 mark):** The velocities at corresponding points in the model and prototype flow fields are in the same direction and bear a constant ratio $\kappa = V_m / V_p$. This implies that streamlines are also geometrically similar.
