- **Dynamic similarity (0.5 mark):** The ratios of all relevant forces (inertia, gravity, viscous, surface tension, elastic) acting on corresponding fluid elements are constant between model and prototype.
- **Most fundamental (0.5 mark):** Geometric similarity is the most fundamental because it is a prerequisite for the other two. Without identical shape, there is no meaningful "corresponding point" to define velocity or force ratios.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]**
> **What is meant by the Froude number? Why is it the dominant similarity parameter for a free-surface ship?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Froude number is the square root of the ratio of inertial forces to gravitational forces:

$$
\text{Fr} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}
$$

- **Dominance for a free-surface ship (1.5 marks):** In a free-surface flow, the most important force balance is between inertia (driving the ship forward and generating waves) and gravity (the restoring force of the water surface that creates the wave pattern). Frictional (viscous) forces contribute to total resistance but are not what *shapes* the wave system. Therefore, matching the Froude number ensures that the wave pattern — and hence the wave-making resistance component — is correctly reproduced between model and prototype.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

> ### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Derive, starting from the dimensional form of the Navier-Stokes equation, the **condition for dynamic similarity** between a model and a prototype. Clearly show how the Reynolds, Froude, and Euler numbers emerge. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A 1:30 scale model of a surface ship is tested in a towing tank at a speed of 2.0 m/s. If the prototype is to operate in sea water ($\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$) at the corresponding Froude-similar speed, compute:
> (i) The prototype speed in m/s and knots.
> (ii) The total resistance of the prototype if the measured model resistance is 12.4 N, assuming Froude scaling only and same fluid.
> (iii) The required model power. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Condition for Dynamic Similarity (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the dimensional Navier-Stokes equation:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Introduce dimensionless variables $V^* = V/V_{\text{ref}}$, $L^* = L/L_{\text{ref}}$, $t^* = t V_{\text{ref}} / L_{\text{ref}}$, $p^* = p / (\rho V_{\text{ref}}^2)$:

$$
\rho \frac{V_{\text{ref}}^2}{L_{\text{ref}}} \left[ \frac{\partial \vec{V}^*}{\partial t^*} + (\vec{V}^* \cdot \nabla^*) \vec{V}^* \right] = -\frac{\rho V_{\text{ref}}^2}{L_{\text{ref}}} \nabla^* p^* + \mu \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{L_{\text{ref}}^2} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^* + \rho \vec{g}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Divide through by the inertia term $\rho V_{\text{ref}}^2 / L_{\text{ref}}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \vec{V}^*}{\partial t^*} + (\vec{V}^* \cdot \nabla^*) \vec{V}^* = -\nabla^* p^* + \frac{\mu}{\rho V_{\text{ref}} L_{\text{ref}}} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^* + \frac{g L_{\text{ref}}}{V_{\text{ref}}^2} \hat{g}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Identify the dimensionless coefficients:

- Coefficient of viscous term = $1 / \text{Re}$
- Coefficient of gravity term = $1 / \text{Fr}^2$
- Coefficient of pressure term gives the **Euler number** if isolated

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude that for two flows to be dynamically similar, the dimensionless velocity and pressure fields must be identical, which requires that all relevant $\Pi$ groups be equal:

$$
\text{Re}_m = \text{Re}_p, \quad \text{Fr}_m = \text{Fr}_p, \quad \text{Eu}_m = \text{Eu}_p, \ldots
$$

**Step 6 (0.5 mark):** Note that this set of equalities is precisely the **Laws of Comparison**, and the impossibility of satisfying them all simultaneously with one fluid is the source of scale effects.

#### Part (b) — Froude Scale Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the scale ratio and velocity scale:

$$
\lambda_L = \frac{1}{30} = 0.0333, \quad \lambda_V = \sqrt{\lambda_L} = \sqrt{0.0333} = 0.1826
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the prototype speed:

$$
V_p = \frac{V_m}{\lambda_V} = \frac{2.0}{0.1826} = 10.95 \text{ m/s}
$$

Converting to knots: $V_p = 10.95 / 0.5144 = 21.3 \text{ knots}$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the prototype resistance. The force scale under Froude scaling is $\lambda_F = \lambda_L^3$:

$$
\lambda_F = (0.0333)^3 = 3.70 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
R_p = \frac{R_m}{\lambda_F} = \frac{12.4}{3.70 \times 10^{-5}} = 335{,}135 \text{ N} \approx 335.1 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the model power. The power scale is $\lambda_P = \lambda_L^{7/2}$:

$$
\lambda_P = (0.0333)^{3.5} = 6.76 \times 10^{-6}
$$

First, prototype power: $P_p = R_p \cdot V_p = 335{,}135 \times 10.95 = 3.67 \text{ MW}$. Then:

$$
P_m = P_p \cdot \lambda_P = 3.67 \times 10^6 \times 6.76 \times 10^{-6} = 24.8 \text{ W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Students often confuse the direction of the scale ratio and write $V_p = V_m \cdot \sqrt{\lambda_L}$ instead of $V_p = V_m / \sqrt{\lambda_L}$. **Always remember:** the model is smaller and slower (or larger and faster); the scale ratio is a *ratio*, not a multiplier. A 1:30 model at 2 m/s corresponds to a *full-scale faster* ship. Apply $\lambda_V = V_m / V_p$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> When converting knots to m/s, the conversion factor is **0.5144 m/s per knot**, not 0.5 or 0.514. Off-by-3% errors here propagate through the entire problem and cost 1–2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:**
> The "force scale = $\lambda_L^3$" rule applies **only under Froude scaling**. Under Reynolds scaling with the same fluid, the force scale is **1**, and the velocity scale is **$1/\lambda_L$**. Confusing these two regimes is the single most common conceptual error in KTU Module 2 answers.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain the **conflict of similitude** with specific reference to marine model testing. Why is it impossible to satisfy Reynolds and Froude similarity simultaneously when using the same fluid? (7 marks)
> **(b)** A research submarine of length 60 m is to be modelled at 1:15 scale. The model is tested in a pressurised water tunnel.
> (i) If the test fluid is the same as the prototype fluid and Reynolds similarity is enforced, what is the required tunnel speed if the prototype cruises at 12 knots?
> (ii) What model resistance corresponds to a prototype total resistance of 350 kN, assuming pure Reynolds scaling?
> (iii) The model is run at 25 °C instead of the prototype's 5 °C seawater. By what factor does the kinematic viscosity ratio change, and what is the resulting percentage error in the predicted resistance? (Take $\nu_{25} = 0.89 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ and $\nu_5 = 1.52 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.) (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — The Conflict of Similitude (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the impossibility theorem. For simultaneous Reynolds and Froude similarity with the same fluid:

$$
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{g L_p}} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu} = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu}
$$

The Froude condition gives $V_m = V_p \sqrt{\lambda_L}$, and the Reynolds condition gives $V_m = V_p / \lambda_L$. Equating them:

$$
V_p \sqrt{\lambda_L} = \frac{V_p}{\lambda_L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_L^{3/2} = 1
$$

This is satisfied only when $\lambda_L = 1$ (i.e., a full-scale test). Hence the two conditions are **mutually exclusive** at any scale other than 1:1.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Explain the practical resolution. The engineer **selects the dominant force** and tolerates a controlled error (the *scale effect*) in the others. For surface ships, wave-making is dominant → Froude similarity is enforced. For submerged bodies, viscous drag is dominant → Reynolds similarity is enforced.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Discuss the **scale effect** as the price paid. Under Froude scaling, the model runs at a lower Reynolds number, so its boundary layer is more laminar; frictional resistance is over-predicted. This is corrected by ITTC 1978/2017 methods.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Mention alternative fluids (e.g., brine or pressurized air) as a partial remedy in special facilities.

#### Part (b) — Reynolds Scale Calculation for the Submarine (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify scale ratios:

$$
\lambda_L = \frac{1}{15} = 0.0667
$$

For Reynolds similarity with the same fluid ($\nu_m = \nu_p$):

$$
\lambda_V = \frac{1}{\lambda_L} = 15
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Convert and compute prototype speed: $V_p = 12 \text{ knots} = 12 \times 0.5144 = 6.173 \text{ m/s}$. Then:

$$
V_m = \lambda_V \cdot V_p = 15 \times 6.173 = 92.6 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Under pure Reynolds scaling, the force scale is 1, so:

$$
R_m = R_p = 350 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Viscosity ratio and error computation:

$$
\frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p} = \frac{\nu_{25}}{\nu_5} = \frac{0.89 \times 10^{-6}}{1.52 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.585
$$

For a turbulent flat-plate boundary layer, $C_F \propto \text{Re}^{-0.2}$. The error in friction factor:

$$
\frac{C_{F,m}}{C_{F,p}} = \left( \frac{\text{Re}_m}{\text{Re}_p} \right)^{-0.2} = (0.585)^{-0.2} = 1.113
$$

Percentage error = $(1.113 - 1) \times 100 = +11.3\%$. The model would over-predict the prototype frictional resistance by ~11%, indicating that the model should be run at a temperature closer to the prototype's 5 °C to minimize this scale effect.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #4 (Sub-part):**
> Students often write $\lambda_V = \lambda_L$ for Reynolds scaling — a **fatal error**. The correct relation is $\lambda_V = 1 / \lambda_L$ (velocity increases as length decreases, to keep the product $VL/\nu$ constant). The same logical trap exists for Froude scaling in the opposite direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #5:**
> When the question asks for a *percentage error* due to a temperature difference, the answer is **not** the ratio of viscosities itself but the **deviation from unity**, expressed as a percentage. Off-by-one-percentage-point errors here lose 1 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2**

- **Similitude is a hierarchy:** Geometric ⇒ Kinematic ⇒ Dynamic. Geometric is the foundation.
- **The 10 dimensionless numbers** (Re, Fr, We, Ca, M, St, Eu, Pr, Pe, Da) are the *vocabulary* of similarity. Each expresses a force ratio.
- **For a surface ship, the dominant similarity is Froude** because inertia vs. gravity controls wave-making.
- **For a submerged body, the dominant similarity is Reynolds** because viscosity controls skin friction.
- **For a cavitating propeller, the dominant parameters are the cavitation number and Weber number.**
- **Froude scaling laws (with same fluid and same $g$):**
  - $\lambda_V = \sqrt{\lambda_L}$
  - $\lambda_t = \sqrt{\lambda_L}$
  - $\lambda_F = \lambda_L^3$
  - $\lambda_P = \lambda_L^{7/2}$
  - $\lambda_W = \lambda_{\nabla} = \lambda_L^3$
- **Reynolds scaling laws (same fluid):**
  - $\lambda_V = 1/\lambda_L$
  - $\lambda_t = \lambda_L^2$
  - $\lambda_F = 1$ (force scale is unity)
- **Weber / cavitation scaling (same fluid):**
  - $\lambda_V = 1/\sqrt{\lambda_L}$
- **The conflict of similitude** is the impossibility of satisfying all $\Pi$ group equalities simultaneously. Resolution: choose the dominant force, accept a scale effect, then correct it (ITTC method).
- **Scale effect** is the systematic deviation caused by relaxing secondary similarity requirements. For Froude-scaled surface ships, the principal scale effect is in frictional resistance (model Re too low).
- **ITTC 1978 / 2017** is the standard KTU-recommended method for predicting full-scale resistance from model tests, using the form factor $k$ and the roughness allowance $\Delta C_F$.
- **Conversion constant to remember:** $1 \text{ knot} = 0.5144 \text{ m/s}$.
- **Always check that the model speed is physically achievable** in the available towing tank or cavitation tunnel; this often forces the engineer to increase the model scale (smaller $\lambda_L$ effect).
- **When asked for a "scale effect" question:** state, quantify (e.g., 11% friction error), and prescribe the ITTC correction. The examiner awards marks for all three.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examination Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Part A questions on this module typically test definitions and identification of dominant forces. Part B questions typically test the *derivation* of the similarity condition and a *numerical scale calculation*. Master Section 3.1 (Navier-Stokes derivation) and Section 3.2 (Froude scaling) for guaranteed high marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extension of Model Results to Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Extension of Model Results to Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Extension of Model Results to Ships** is the systematic procedure by which resistance, propulsion, and powering data measured during **towing tank model experiments** are mathematically converted into accurate predictions of the **full-scale ship's** resistance, effective power (PE), delivered power (PD), shaft power (PS), and brake power (PB). The procedure reconciles the impossibility of simultaneously satisfying both **Froude similarity** (gravity-wave scaling) and **Reynolds number similarity** (viscous scaling) by adopting Froude equivalence and then applying a **viscous correction** based on extrapolated friction line comparisons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST865 – Module 2):** The extension process links two dissimilar Reynolds-number regimes. The Froude number is matched between model (subscript M) and ship (subscript S), while the Reynolds number mismatch is resolved through the **ITTC 1978 Performance Prediction Method** using the form factor $(1+k)$ and the correlation allowance $C_A$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Child and the Giant"

Imagine a **child** walking through a shallow swimming pool and an **adult giant** walking through the same pool at the same Froude-equivalent wave-making speed.

- The child creates tiny ripples (model-scale wave pattern).
- The giant creates big waves (ship-scale wave pattern).
- Both are at the *same Froude number* (same wave-making *style*).
- BUT — the giant's wading speed is much higher, and the *boundary layer* (the thin film of water clinging to the legs) behaves very differently because of the **Reynolds number** mismatch.

To predict the giant's **dragging effort**, we cannot simply multiply the child's effort by weight. We must:
1. Subtract the part that *both* do in the same way (gravity-wave resistance).
2. Add a *viscous correction* that accounts for the giant's thicker, more turbulent boundary layer.
3. Apply a small **fudge factor** (the correlation allowance) that corrects for effects (hull roughness, propeller-hull interaction) that the small child model cannot reproduce.

That, in essence, is what **extension of model results to ships** does — it scales the **wave-making physics** and corrects the **viscous physics** separately.

## 1.3 Why Both Similarities Cannot Be Satisfied Simultaneously

For complete dynamic similarity, **all** dimensionless groups (Fn, Rn, Weber, Mach, etc.) must be identical between model and ship. For a free-surface, low-speed displacement ship only **Froude** and **Reynolds** dominate.

$$Fn_S = Fn_M \Rightarrow \frac{V_S}{\sqrt{gL_S}} = \frac{V_M}{\sqrt{gL_M}}$$

$$Rn_S = Rn_M \Rightarrow \frac{V_S L_S}{\nu_S} = \frac{V_M L_M}{\nu_M}$$

Dividing the two (and noting both use the same fluid, water, hence $\nu_S = \nu_M$):

$$\frac{Rn_S}{Rn_M} = \frac{V_S L_S}{V_M L_M}$$

Using $V_S = V_M \sqrt{L_S/L_M}$ from Froude equivalence:

$$\frac{Rn_S}{Rn_M} = \frac{V_M \sqrt{L_S/L_M} \cdot L_S}{V_M L_M} = \left(\frac{L_S}{L_M}\right)^{3/2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a typical model scale ratio $\lambda = L_S/L_M = 25$ to $40$, the **Reynolds number of the ship is 125 to 253 times larger** than that of the model. This $L^{3/2}$ scaling makes simultaneous Froude-Reynolds similarity physically impossible with the same fluid — hence the *indirect* extrapolation procedure is mandatory.

## 1.4 Key Quantities Defined (Bold-typed constants)

| Symbol | Definition | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C_T$ | Total resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| $C_F$ | Frictional resistance coefficient (ITTC) | dimensionless |
| $C_R$ | Residual resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| $C_A$ | Correlation allowance (ITTC 1978) | **0.0002** (constant) |
| $(1+k)$ | Form factor (viscous-pressure form factor) | dimensionless, 1.0 – 1.4 |
| $R_T$ | Total resistance | **N (Newton)** |
| $R_F$ | Frictional resistance | **N** |
| $P_E$ | Effective power | **W (Watt)**, kW, or PS |
| $P_D, P_S, P_B$ | Delivered, Shaft, Brake power | **W** |
| $\eta_D, \eta_S, \eta_B$ | Propulsive efficiencies | dimensionless |
| $\rho$ | Water density | **1025 kg/m³ (saltwater)**; 1000 kg/m³ (fresh) |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity | **1 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** (typical towing tank) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Step Extension Philosophy

The **ITTC 1978** and its descendants (ITTC 2002/2014 with updated friction lines) prescribe a three-step procedure for every model speed:

1. **Step 1 — Froude-Scaled Resistance Conversion:** Compute total resistance coefficient $C_{TS}$ at the ship Reynolds number by removing model-scale viscous effects and adding ship-scale viscous effects.

2. **Step 2 — Power Conversion:** Convert the *effective* power to *thrust* power, then to *delivered* power using measured self-propulsion factors ($\eta_D$, $t$, $w$).

3. **Step 3 — Brake Power / Shaft Power Computation:** Apply rotational efficiency $\eta_R$, mechanical transmission efficiency $\eta_M$, and shaft generator efficiency $\eta_{SG}$ as required.

## 2.2 ITTC 1978 Friction Line (The Most Tested Formula)

The **ITTC 1978 Friction Line** is a correlation of the frictional resistance coefficient of a *turbulent, fully-rough flat plate*:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

where the Reynolds number is based on **length between perpendiculars (LBP)** for the ship and **length of model** for the model:

$$R_n = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The coefficient **0.075** is fixed by the ITTC for the 1978 method. Do not confuse with the older Schoenherr ($\log R_n - 2.46$) or the ITTC 1957 (1.89 × 10⁻²). For KTU 2024 examinations, expect the **ITTC 1978** form.

## 2.3 Form Factor $(1+k)$ — Prohaska Method & Direct Method

The **form factor** quantifies how much the *pressure* (viscous) resistance of the 3D ship hull exceeds the 2D flat-plate frictional resistance. Two principal ways to determine it:

### Prohaska Method (Empirical / Standard KTU Favourite)

Plot $C_T / C_F$ vs. $Fn^4$ for a range of model speeds. A straight line should appear:

$$\frac{C_{TM}}{C_{FM}} = A + B \cdot F_n^4$$

Extrapolating to $F_n^4 = 0$ gives the ordinate intercept $A$, which is the form factor:

$$(1+k) = A$$

### Direct Method (Prohaska-modified / Hughes)

A second-order fit:

$$\frac{C_{TM}}{C_{FM}} = A + B \cdot F_n^2 + C \cdot F_n^4$$

At $F_n = 0$: $(1+k) = A$ (same as Prohaska's first-order extrapolation).

## 2.4 The Master Extension Equation — $C_{TS}$ at Ship Reynolds

For each Froude number, the **ship total resistance coefficient** is:

$$C_{TS} = \frac{C_{FM}}{(1+k)} \cdot \left( (1+k) + C_A \right) + C_R$$

Which is more cleanly written by separating into frictional + form + residual + correlation parts. Defining the **residual resistance coefficient**:

$$C_R = C_{TM} - C_{FM}$$

then:

$$C_{TS} = \frac{S_S}{S_M} \cdot C_{FM}\cdot\frac{R_{nS}^{-x}}{R_{nM}^{-x}} \cdot \left( \frac{C_{TM}}{C_{FM}} \right)_{M} \cdot (1+k) + C_A + \frac{S_S}{S_M} \cdot C_{FM}\left[ 1 - \left( \frac{R_{nM}}{R_{nS}} \right)^{x} \right] \cdot \frac{1}{1+k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the **simplified ITTC 1978** presentation used in KTU problems, the practical form is:
>
> $$C_{TS} = \frac{C_{FS}}{(1+k)} + C_A + C_R$$
>
> This implicitly assumes the area ratio $S_S/S_M = (L_S/L_M)^2$ when converting forces via $R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2 C_T$.

## 2.5 The Reynolds-Scaling Factor

The factor used to scale the frictional resistance from model to ship:

$$k_{RF} = \left( \frac{R_{nM}}{R_{nS}} \right)^{x} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad k_{RF} = \left( \frac{L_M}{L_S} \right)^{x} \left( \frac{V_M}{V_S} \right)^{x}$$

Using Froude equivalence $V_M/V_S = \sqrt{L_M/L_S}$:

$$k_{RF} = \left( \frac{L_M}{L_S} \right)^{3x/2} = \left( \frac{R_{nM}}{R_{nS}} \right)^{x}$$

For the ITTC 1957 line $x = 0.16$ (old method); for the **ITTC 1978 line $x$ is folded into the new friction formula directly** (no separate exponent needed for first-order).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ | Froude number | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_n = VL/\nu$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$ | ITTC 1978 friction line | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_T = R_T \cdot 2/(\rho S V^2)$ | Resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_R = C_T - C_F$ | Residual (wave + form) coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $(1+k) = A$ from Prohaska plot | Form factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_A = 0.0002$ | ITTC correlation allowance | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_{TS} = C_{FS}/(1+k) + C_A + C_R$ | Ship total coefficient (ITTC 1978) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $R_{TS} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S_S V_S^2 C_{TS}$ | Ship total resistance | N |
| 10 | $P_E = R_{TS} \cdot V_S$ | Effective power | W |
| 11 | $P_T = P_E \cdot 1/(1-t)$ | Thrust power | W |
| 12 | $P_D = P_T / \eta_D$ | Delivered power | W |
| 13 | $P_S = P_D / \eta_S$ | Shaft power | W |
| 14 | $P_B = P_S / \eta_M$ | Brake power (= MCR/SEP) | W |
| 15 | $V_M = V_S \sqrt{L_M/L_S}$ | Model speed (Froude scaled) | m/s |
| 16 | $S_S/S_M = (L_S/L_M)^2$ | Wetted-area scaling | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When writing the above formulas in inline LaTeX, the operator **"divides"** is preferred over the pipe symbol. If a column ever required an absolute value, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — **never the raw pipe `|`** inside a markdown table row.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Concept Design Stage:** Architects use the *extension procedure* to predict shaft power before a single line of the ship is built. Mistakes of even 5% in $C_A$ or $(1+k)$ translate to millions of dollars in fuel over a vessel's 25-year life.
- **Contractual Trials:** A "powering guarantee" in a newbuild contract states that the delivered power at service speed must not exceed a contractual figure. The ITTC extension is the *baseline* against which the measured sea-trial power is compared.
- **Regulatory Use:** The **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** and **EEXI** rules of the IMO are evaluated using the ITTC-1978-method-derived $P_B$ at the contractual speed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Reynolds Mismatch Magnitude

**Starting point:** Two similarity criteria must hold:

$$F_{n,S} = F_{n,M} \quad \text{and} \quad R_{n,S} = R_{n,M}$$

**Step 1.** From Froude equivalence, the model-to-ship speed ratio:

$$\frac{V_S}{V_M} = \sqrt{\frac{L_S}{L_M}} = \sqrt{\lambda}$$

**Step 2.** Form the Reynolds number ratio:

$$\frac{R_{n,S}}{R_{n,M}} = \frac{V_S L_S / \nu}{V_M L_M / \nu} = \frac{V_S}{V_M} \cdot \frac{L_S}{L_M}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute the speed ratio:

$$\frac{R_{n,S}}{R_{n,M}} = \sqrt{\lambda} \cdot \lambda = \lambda^{3/2}$$

**Step 4.** For $\lambda = 25$:

$$\lambda^{3/2} = 25^{1.5} = \sqrt{25^3} = \sqrt{15625} = 125$$

**Step 5.** For $\lambda = 40$:

$$\lambda^{3/2} = 40^{1.5} \approx 252.98 \approx 253$$

> **Conclusion:** The ship Reynolds number is **125–253 times** the model Reynolds number. This huge gap is the entire motivation for the indirect extension procedure.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ITTC 1978 Form Factor via Prohaska

**Setup:** At each model speed, the *measured* total resistance coefficient $C_{TM}$ and the *computed* frictional coefficient $C_{FM}$ are known.

**Step 1.** Define the residual coefficient:

$$C_R = C_{TM} - C_{FM}$$

For geometrically similar Froude-equivalent tests, $C_R$ scales only with $F_n$ and not with $R_n$ (the wave part is Re-independent; the form part is folded into $(1+k)$).

**Step 2.** Add a viscous pressure factor:

$$C_{TM} = C_{FM}(1+k) + C_R$$

**Step 3.** Divide by $C_{FM}$:

$$\frac{C_{TM}}{C_{FM}} = (1+k) + \frac{C_R}{C_{FM}}$$

**Step 4.** Use the empirical observation that $C_R/C_{FM}$ varies approximately as $F_n^4$:

$$\frac{C_{TM}}{C_{FM}} \approx (1+k) + B F_n^4$$

This is a straight line in the **Prohaska plot**: y = A + B·x, where $A = (1+k)$ and $x = F_n^4$.

**Step 5.** Extrapolate linearly to $F_n^4 = 0$ (left side of the plot). The y-intercept $A$ is the form factor.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ship Resistance from Model Data

Given a model of length $L_M$ tested at speed $V_M$ gives $R_{TM}$. Find $R_{TS}$ at ship length $L_S$ and speed $V_S = V_M \sqrt{L_S/L_M}$.

**Step 1.** Model total resistance coefficient:

$$C_{TM} = \frac{2 R_{TM}}{\rho_M S_M V_M^2}$$

**Step 2.** Model frictional coefficient using ITTC 1978:

$$C_{FM} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{nM} - 2)^2}, \quad R_{nM} = \frac{V_M L_M}{\nu_M}$$

**Step 3.** Residual coefficient:

$$C_R = C_{TM} - C_{FM}$$

**Step 4.** Ship Froude number check (should equal model $F_n$):

$$F_{nS} = \frac{V_S}{\sqrt{g L_S}} = \frac{V_M \sqrt{L_S/L_M}}{\sqrt{g L_S}} = \frac{V_M}{\sqrt{g L_M}} = F_{nM}$$

Hence $C_R$ is *the same* for model and ship.

**Step 5.** Ship frictional coefficient using ITTC 1978 at the ship Reynolds number:

$$C_{FS} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{nS} - 2)^2}, \quad R_{nS} = \frac{V_S L_S}{\nu_S}$$

**Step 6.** Total ship resistance coefficient (ITTC 1978 with form factor + correlation allowance):

$$C_{TS} = \frac{C_{FS}}{(1+k)} + C_A + C_R$$

**Step 7.** Ship total resistance:

$$R_{TS} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_S S_S V_S^2 C_{TS}, \quad \text{with } \frac{S_S}{S_M} = \left( \frac{L_S}{L_M} \right)^2$$

**Step 8.** Effective power:

$$P_E = R_{TS} \cdot V_S$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Chain $P_E \to P_T \to P_D \to P_S \to P_B$

**Step 1 — Thrust Deduction:** A propeller behind a ship works in a slipstream that is accelerated. The thrust needed is therefore *less* than the resistance $R$:

$$T = \frac{R}{1 - t}$$

Hence thrust power:

$$P_T = T \cdot V_S = \frac{R \cdot V_S}{1-t} = \frac{P_E}{1-t}$$

**Step 2 — Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient $\eta_D$:** The propeller efficiency:

$$\eta_D = \frac{P_T}{P_D} = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{Q \cdot 2 \pi n}$$

where $V_A = V_S(1-w)$ is the advance speed, $w$ is the Taylor wake fraction. So:

$$P_D = \frac{P_T}{\eta_D} = \frac{P_E}{(1-t)\,\eta_D}$$

**Step 3 — Shaft Efficiency $\eta_S$:** Gearbox/shaft losses:

$$P_S = \frac{P_D}{\eta_S}$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical Efficiency $\eta_M$:**

$$P_B = \frac{P_S}{\eta_M}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Combining all four steps:
>
> $$P_B = \frac{P_E}{(1-t)\,\eta_D\,\eta_S\,\eta_M} = \frac{P_E}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_D \cdot \eta_S \cdot \eta_M} = \frac{P_E}{\eta_{\text{overall}}}$$
>
> where $\eta_H = 1/(1-t)$ is the **hull efficiency** and $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ is the **propulsive coefficient** of the ship.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Given:**
- $L_S = 200$ m, $L_M = 4$ m, $\lambda = 50$
- $V_S = 15$ knots $= 7.717$ m/s
- $S_S = 6500$ m², $S_M = 2.6$ m²
- Model $R_{TM} = 18.4$ N at $V_M$
- $V_M = V_S \sqrt{L_M/L_S} = 7.717 \times \sqrt{4/200} = 7.717 \times 0.1414 = 1.091$ m/s
- $\rho_M = 1000$ kg/m³, $\nu_M = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- Form factor $(1+k) = 1.20$

**Step A — Model Reynolds and $C_F$:**

$$R_{nM} = \frac{1.091 \times 4}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.364 \times 10^{6}$$

$$C_{FM} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} 4.364 \times 10^{6} - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.640 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(4.640)^2} = \frac{0.075}{21.53} = 0.003484$$

**Step B — Model $C_T$ and $C_R$:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{2 \times 18.4}{1000 \times 2.6 \times 1.091^2} = \frac{36.8}{3094.5} = 0.011893$$

$$C_R = 0.011893 - 0.003484 = 0.008409$$

**Step C — Ship Reynolds and $C_F$:**

$$R_{nS} = \frac{7.717 \times 200}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.5434 \times 10^{9}$$

$$C_{FS} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} 1.5434 \times 10^{9} - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(9.188 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7.188)^2} = \frac{0.075}{51.67} = 0.001452$$

**Step D — Ship $C_T$ (ITTC 1978):**

$$C_{TS} = \frac{0.001452}{1.20} + 0.0002 + 0.008409 = 0.001210 + 0.0002 + 0.008409 = 0.009819$$

**Step E — Ship resistance (assuming $\rho_S = 1025$ kg/m³):**

$$R_{TS} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 6500 \times 7.717^2 \times 0.009819$$

Computing piece by piece:
- $\rho_S \cdot S_S = 1025 \times 6500 = 6,662,500$
- $V_S^2 = 7.717^2 = 59.55$ m²/s²
- $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho_S S_S V_S^2 = 0.5 \times 6,662,500 \times 59.55 = 198,338,437$ N
- $R_{TS} = 198,338,437 \times 0.009819 \approx 1,947,365$ N $\approx 1.947$ MN

**Step F — Effective power:**

$$P_E = 1,947,365 \times 7.717 = 15,028,768 \text{ W} \approx 15.03 \text{ MW}$$

**Step G — Brake power (with $1-t = 0.85$, $\eta_D = 0.62$, $\eta_S = 0.99$, $\eta_M = 0.97$):**

$$P_B = \frac{15.03}{0.85 \times 0.62 \times 0.99 \times 0.97} = \frac{15.03}{0.5064} \approx 29.68 \text{ MW}$$

> **Final Answer:** $R_{TS} \approx 1.95$ MN, $P_E \approx 15.0$ MW, $P_B \approx 29.7$ MW.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Correction Term Exists

| Correction | Origin | Effect on $C_{TS}$ | Sign |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Form factor $(1+k)$ in denominator | 3-D hull has higher viscous-pressure drag than a flat plate | Divides $C_{FS}$ to remove form drag counted by $C_{FS}$ | decreases |
| $C_A = 0.0002$ added | Hull roughness, propeller-hull interaction, weather, appendages not in model | Raises ship drag | increases |
| $C_R$ kept unchanged | Wave resistance + form part are Froude-similar, Re-independent | Same for model and ship | carries over |
| $C_{FS} < C_{FM}$ | Larger $R_n$ ⇒ thinner turbulent boundary layer ⇒ less skin friction | Reduces frictional share | decreases |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — End-to-End Model-to-Ship Extension Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Model Towing Tank Test] --> B[Measure RTM at VM]
    B --> C[Compute RnM and CFM via ITTC 1978]
    C --> D[Compute CTM and CR = CTM - CFM]
    D --> E[Run Prohaska Plot CTM over CFM vs Fn4]
    E --> F[Extract form factor 1+k from intercept]
    F --> G[Compute model-to-ship speed: VS = VM sqrt LS over LM]
    G --> H[Compute ship Reynolds RnS]
    H --> I[Compute CFS via ITTC 1978 at RnS]
    I --> J[Apply ITTC 1978: CTS = CFS divided by 1+k plus CA plus CR]
    J --> K[Compute ship resistance RTS using 0.5 rho S VS squared]
    K --> L[Effective power PE = RTS times VS]
    L --> M[Thrust power PT = PE divided by 1-t]
    M --> N[Delivered power PD = PT divided by etaD]
    N --> O[Shaft power PS = PD divided by etaS]
    O --> P[Brake power PB = PS divided by etaM]
    P --> Q[Final Deliverable: Service Power at Contractual Speed]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Prohaska Plot Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Fn low] --> Y1[CTM over CFM value]
    X2[Fn mid] --> Y2[CTM over CFM value]
    X3[Fn high] --> Y3[CTM over CFM value]
    Y1 --> Z[Linear Regression]
    Y2 --> Z
    Y3 --> Z
    Z --> R1[Slope B]
    Z --> R2[Intercept A = 1+k]
    R2 --> OUT[Form Factor for ITTC 1978 Extension]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — The Power Chain Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PE[Effective Power PE] --> PT[Thrust Power PT = PE divided by 1-t]
    PT --> PD[Delivered Power PD = PT divided by etaD]
    PD --> PS[Shaft Power PS = PD divided by etaS]
    PS --> PB[Brake Power PB = PS divided by etaM]
    PB --> FIN[Service Brake Power MCR or CSR per IMO EEDI]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Where the Three Similarity Criteria Reside

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Geometric Similarity]
        G1[Same shape ratio]
    end
    subgraph S2[Kinematic Similarity]
        K1[Streamlines scaled by lambda]
    end
    subgraph S3[Dynamic Similarity]
        D1[Froude matched: FnS = FnM]
        D2[Reynolds: NOT matched, gap L raised to 3 over 2]
        D3[Indirect correction via ITTC 1978]
    end
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    D2 --> D3
    D1 --> D3
    D3 --> OUT[Ship PE PD PS PB]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a block diagram and not a wave-pattern sketch?** The Reynolds-gap and Froude-equivalence relation is a *topology* problem, not a geometry-of-the-wake problem. A block diagram conveys the dependency structure unambiguously. (See Section 2.1.)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Why is it not possible to maintain both Froude and Reynolds similarity simultaneously in ship model testing? Show that the ship-to-model Reynolds number ratio scales as $L_S/L_M$ raised to 3/2.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Reason:** The model and ship are tested in the same fluid (water) at essentially the same kinematic viscosity $\nu$. Froude similarity requires $V_S = V_M \sqrt{L_S/L_M}$, while Reynolds similarity requires $V_S L_S = V_M L_M$. Dividing the second by the first gives the constraint that the fluid viscosity must scale as $L^{3/2}$, which is not possible for the same fluid.
- **Derivation:**

$$\frac{R_{nS}}{R_{nM}} = \frac{V_S L_S}{V_M L_M} = \sqrt{\frac{L_S}{L_M}} \cdot \frac{L_S}{L_M} = \left(\frac{L_S}{L_M}\right)^{3/2}$$

- **Conclusion:** Hence the ship Reynolds number is $L^{3/2}$ times larger than the model. The gap is corrected indirectly by Froude scaling + ITTC 1978 viscous correction.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the form factor $(1+k)$. Explain the Prohaska method of its determination.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Definition:** Form factor $(1+k)$ is the dimensionless multiplier that scales the 2-D flat-plate frictional coefficient $C_F$ to match the *viscous-pressure* drag component of a 3-D ship hull. Mathematically:

$$(1+k) = \lim_{F_n \to 0} \frac{C_{TM}}{C_{FM}}$$

- **Prohaska Method:** Plot the ratio $C_{TM}/C_{FM}$ (y-axis) against $F_n^4$ (x-axis) for the model test data. The points fall approximately on a straight line. Linear extrapolation to $F_n^4 = 0$ gives the y-intercept, which equals $(1+k)$.

$$Y = (1+k) + B F_n^4 \implies (1+k) = A \text{ (intercept)}$$

- **Use:** Once $(1+k)$ is known, the ship frictional coefficient is divided by $(1+k)$ during the extension to correct the model-scale viscous pressure drag to ship-scale viscous pressure drag.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Module Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two alternatives (A and B) are given, attempt any one. Each carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks via two sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **ITTC 1978 Performance Prediction Method** for extending model resistance to full-scale ship. Give the master equation for $C_{TS}$ and explain the physical meaning of each term.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Background:** Model tests are performed in a towing tank. Only Froude similarity is enforced; the Reynolds-number gap is corrected analytically using the ITTC 1978 line.

**2. Procedure (each model speed):**

- (i) From model test, measure $R_{TM}$.
- (ii) Compute $C_{TM} = 2R_{TM}/(\rho_M S_M V_M^2)$.
- (iii) Compute $C_{FM}$ using the ITTC 1978 line: $C_{FM} = 0.075/(\log_{10} R_{nM} - 2)^2$.
- (iv) Compute $C_R = C_{TM} - C_{FM}$.
- (v) Determine $(1+k)$ by Prohaska plot from the series of model speeds.
- (vi) Compute $V_S = V_M \sqrt{L_S/L_M}$ (Froude equivalence).
- (vii) Compute $C_{FS}$ at $R_{nS} = V_S L_S / \nu_S$ via the ITTC 1978 line.
- (viii) Apply the master equation:

$$C_{TS} = \frac{C_{FS}}{(1+k)} + C_A + C_R$$

**3. Physical meaning of each term:**

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $C_{FS}/(1+k)$ | Ship frictional coefficient *corrected* for 3-D form drag |
| $C_A = 0.0002$ | Allowance for hull roughness, weather, appendage drag |
| $C_R$ | Wave + viscous-pressure resistance (Froude-similar) |

**4. Final ship resistance:**

$$R_{TS} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_S S_S V_S^2 C_{TS}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1:40 scale model of a ship is tested in fresh water at 1.2 m/s. The measured resistance is 12.5 N. The model has $L_M = 5$ m, $S_M = 4.0$ m², $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. The form factor $(1+k) = 1.15$. Compute the **full-scale effective power** in kW, assuming the ship is in salt water ($\rho_S = 1025$ kg/m³, $L_S = 200$ m, $S_S = 6400$ m², $V_S = V_M \sqrt{40}$). Use ITTC 1978 line.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model $C_{TM}$ and $C_{FM}$:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{2 \times 12.5}{1000 \times 4.0 \times 1.2^2} = \frac{25}{5760} = 0.004340$$

$$R_{nM} = \frac{1.2 \times 5}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.0 \times 10^{6}$$

$$C_{FM} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} 6.0 \times 10^{6} - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.778 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(4.778)^2} = \frac{0.075}{22.83} = 0.003286$$

**Step 2 — Residual:**

$$C_R = 0.004340 - 0.003286 = 0.001054$$

**Step 3 — Ship speed, Reynolds, $C_{FS}$:**

$$V_S = 1.2 \times \sqrt{40} = 1.2 \times 6.3246 = 7.5895 \text{ m/s}$$

$$R_{nS} = \frac{7.5895 \times 200}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.518 \times 10^{9}$$

$$C_{FS} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} 1.518 \times 10^{9} - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(9.181 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7.181)^2} = \frac{0.075}{51.57} = 0.001455$$

**Step 4 — Ship $C_{TS}$ and $R_{TS}$:**

$$C_{TS} = \frac{0.001455}{1.15} + 0.0002 + 0.001054 = 0.001265 + 0.0002 + 0.001054 = 0.002519$$

$$R_{TS} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 6400 \times 7.5895^2 \times 0.002519 = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 6400 \times 57.60 \times 0.002519$$

$$R_{TS} = 3200 \times 1025 \times 57.60 \times 0.002519 \approx 475,700 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Effective power:**

$$P_E = 475,700 \times 7.5895 = 3,610,234 \text{ W} \approx 3.61 \text{ MW}$$

> **Final Answer:** $P_E \approx 3.61$ MW (3610 kW).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relation $R_{nS}/R_{nM} = (L_S/L_M)^{3/2}$ and show why both Froude and Reynolds similarity cannot be preserved when model and ship are tested in the same fluid. State the ITTC 1978 procedure that resolves this conflict.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Set up the two similarity equations:**

- Froude: $V_S/\sqrt{g L_S} = V_M/\sqrt{g L_M}$ → $V_S = V_M \sqrt{L_S/L_M}$
- Reynolds: $V_S L_S / \nu_S = V_M L_M / \nu_M$

**2. Substituting Froude into Reynolds (with $\nu_S = \nu_M$):**

$$\frac{V_M \sqrt{L_S/L_M} \cdot L_S}{V_M L_M} = \left(\frac{L_S}{L_M}\right)^{3/2} = \frac{R_{nS}}{R_{nM}}$$

**3. Numerical example:** For $L_S/L_M = 25$, the gap is $25^{1.5} = 125$. The model cannot be tested in a fluid with viscosity 125 times smaller, so **simultaneous** matching is impossible.

**4. Resolution — ITTC 1978:** Maintain only Froude similarity. The Reynolds gap is corrected analytically using:
- (i) The ITTC 1978 friction line $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$ at both $R_{nM}$ and $R_{nS}$;
- (ii) The form factor $(1+k)$ to scale 2-D to 3-D drag;
- (iii) The correlation allowance $C_A = 0.0002$;
- (iv) The Froude-similar $C_R$ carried over unchanged from model to ship.

**Master equation:**

$$C_{TS} = \frac{C_{FS}}{(1+k)} + C_A + C_R$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A model of a 180 m long ship is tested at 1.5 m/s in fresh water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). The wetted area of the model is 3.0 m², and at this speed the model resistance is 19.5 N. If $(1+k) = 1.18$, compute the **ship's total resistance and effective power** at the corresponding service speed. Use scale ratio $\lambda = 36$ and $S_S/S_M = \lambda^2$. Salt-water density = 1025 kg/m³. ITTC 1978 method.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model $C_{TM}$ and $C_{FM}$:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{2 \times 19.5}{1000 \times 3.0 \times 1.5^2} = \frac{39}{6750} = 0.005778$$

$$R_{nM} = \frac{1.5 \times (180/36)}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.5 \times 5}{10^{-6}} = 7.5 \times 10^{6}$$

$$C_{FM} = \frac{0.075}{(\log 7.5 \times 10^{6} - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.875 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(4.875)^2} = \frac{0.075}{23.77} = 0.003156$$

**Step 2 — Residual:**

$$C_R = 0.005778 - 0.003156 = 0.002622$$

**Step 3 — Ship speed, Reynolds, $C_{FS}$:**

$$V_S = 1.5 \times \sqrt{36} = 1.5 \times 6 = 9.0 \text{ m/s}$$

$$R_{nS} = \frac{9.0 \times 180}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.62 \times 10^{9}$$

$$C_{FS} = \frac{0.075}{(\log 1.62 \times 10^{9} - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(9.210 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7.210)^2} = \frac{0.075}{51.98} = 0.001443$$

**Step 4 — Ship $C_{TS}$ and $R_{TS}$:**

$$C_{TS} = \frac{0.001443}{1.18} + 0.0002 + 0.002622 = 0.001223 + 0.0002 + 0.002622 = 0.004045$$

$$S_S = 3.0 \times 36^2 = 3888 \text{ m}^2$$

$$R_{TS} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 3888 \times 9.0^2 \times 0.004045 = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 3888 \times 81 \times 0.004045$$

$$R_{TS} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 3888 \times 81 \times 0.004045 \approx 0.5 \times 1025 \times 3888 \times 0.3276 = 652,900 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Effective power:**

$$P_E = 652,900 \times 9.0 = 5,876,100 \text{ W} \approx 5.88 \text{ MW}$$

> **Final Answer:** $R_{TS} \approx 0.653$ MN, $P_E \approx 5.88$ MW.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Extension Problems — Examiner's Pet Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the form factor in the denominator.** The most common error is to write $C_{TS} = C_{FS} + C_A + C_R$, dropping the $1/(1+k)$ division. This **over-predicts the ship's frictional share by 15–40%** and inflates $P_E$ and $P_B$ unrealistically. **Cost: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Mixing ITTC 1957 and 1978 lines.** ITTC 1957 uses $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$ with a *separate* $R_n^{0.16}$ scaling, while ITTC 1978 bakes the scaling into the new formula. Mixing them produces $C_{TS}$ errors of 5–10%. **Cost: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Using model density for the ship.** Salt-water vs fresh-water density matters. Always verify $\rho_S = 1025$ kg/m³ (or as given). **Cost: 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $(1+k)$ in the Prohaska intercept.** Students often write $A = k$ (the increment only) instead of $A = 1+k$ (the full form factor). **Cost: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Skipping units.** $P_E$ must be in **W or kW or MW** with explicit unit. Naked numerical answers lose 0.5 mark. **Cost: 0.5 mark.**
>
> 6. **Incorrect power chain division.** The chain is $P_E \to P_T \to P_D \to P_S \to P_B$, with *each step dividing by an efficiency*. Students sometimes *multiply* by efficiencies. **Cost: 2 marks if persistent.**
>
> 7. **Not writing the final line "ITTC 1978"** in the solution. KTU rubric allocates 0.5–1 mark for explicit identification of the method used. **Cost: 0.5–1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin to Wall):**
>
> - [ ] Froude similarity is **enforced**; Reynolds similarity is **not** — it is corrected analytically.
> - [ ] Reynolds number gap scales as $(L_S/L_M)^{3/2}$ — for $\lambda = 25$ the gap is 125, for $\lambda = 40$ the gap is ~253.
> - [ ] ITTC 1978 friction line: $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$ — memorize.
> - [ ] Master extension equation: $C_{TS} = C_{FS}/(1+k) + C_A + C_R$ — with $C_A = 0.0002$.
> - [ ] Form factor $(1+k)$ via Prohaska: y-intercept of $C_{TM}/C_{FM}$ vs $F_n^4$ plot.
> - [ ] Froude-equivalent speed: $V_S = V_M \sqrt{L_S/L_M} = V_M \sqrt{\lambda}$.
> - [ ] Area scaling: $S_S/S_M = \lambda^2$.
> - [ ] Power chain: $P_B = P_E / [(1-t)\eta_D \eta_S \eta_M]$.
> - [ ] $C_R$ is the *same* for model and ship at matched $F_n$ — do not re-scale it.
> - [ ] Standard densities: fresh water 1000 kg/m³, salt water 1025 kg/m³.
> - [ ] Standard kinematic viscosity in towing tanks: $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.
> - [ ] Effective power: $P_E = R \cdot V_S$ (Watts when R is in Newtons and V in m/s).
> - [ ] $1-t \approx 0.80$–$0.90$; $\eta_D \approx 0.55$–$0.70$; $\eta_S \approx 0.97$–$0.99$; $\eta_M \approx 0.95$–$0.98$.
> - [ ] Always state the **method used** in the opening line of the solution (ITTC 1978).
> - [ ] Always include the **form factor** division in the $C_{TS}$ equation — examiners check.
> - [ ] Remember to convert knots → m/s by multiplying by 0.5144 if the ship speed is in knots.
> - [ ] ITTC 1957 (older) needs an extra $R_n^{0.16}$ scaling; ITTC 1978 does not. Use one, not both.

> **One-Line Mantra:**
> *"Match Froude, mismatch Reynolds, fix with ITTC 1978, then climb the power chain from PE to PB."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Towing Tank
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Towing Tank # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Towing Tank (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **towing tank** is a long, narrow, deep, and straight water-filled basin equipped with a computer-controlled carriage system used to tow ship models at precisely controlled speeds, enabling the measurement of hydrodynamic forces, wave patterns, and flow visualization under dynamically similar conditions governed primarily by the **Froude similarity law** (and secondarily by the **Reynolds similarity law**). It is, in essence, a calibrated experimental "race track" for ship models — a controlled environment where gravity-driven and viscous phenomena around a hull can be reproduced, quantified, and scaled back to predict full-scale ship performance. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are an architect designing a new sports car. Before building the actual vehicle (which costs crores of rupees and takes months), you build a small, accurately scaled plastic model and test it in a wind tunnel. The **towing tank is the marine equivalent of a wind tunnel** — except instead of air, the "test medium" is water, and instead of a fan, a **towing carriage** pulls the ship model through the water (or pushes the model and pulls the water past it). **Real-world analogy:** - The towing tank ≈ a **swimming pool with a mechanical bridge** spanning across it - The **ship model** ≈ a small toy boat with sensors glued to its hull - The **carriage** ≈ a "robotic sweeper" that glides along rails on the pool's walls, dragging the model at calibrated speeds - The **force dynamometer** (load cell) ≈ a "smart weighing scale" inside the carriage that records how hard the water pushes back on the hull - The **wave probes** ≈ motion sensors that "see" the waves rolling off the model (just like a buoy measuring ocean swells) The genius of the system is that a **3-meter long model** tested in the lab can predict the resistance and behavior of a **300-meter long supertanker** at sea, all thanks to the **Froude scaling laws** of dimensional analysis. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Critical Towing Tank Standards (KTU Highlight):** > - **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$ > - **Density of fresh water (test medium):** $\rho = 998 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ at 20°C > - **Kinematic viscosity of fresh water:** $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ at 20°C > - **Typical tank length:** **100 m – 400 m** > - **Typical tank width:** **3 m – 15 m** > - **Typical tank depth:** **3 m – 8 m** > - **Maximum carriage speed:** **up to 20 m/s** (~40 knots) > - **Test Froude number range:** **0.10 to 0.60** (for displacement hulls) > - **Model-to-prototype length scale ratio ($\lambda$):** typically **1:20 to 1:50** for ships # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why a Towing Tank? The Need for Model Testing Full-scale ship trials are: - **Extremely expensive** (a single sea trial can cost millions of dollars in fuel and lost operational time) - **Highly uncontrollable** (wind, waves, and currents vary unpredictably) - **Impossible to repeat** with identical conditions A towing tank solves all three problems by providing: - A **controlled, repeatable, and quantifiable** environment - The ability to test many **design variants rapidly** at low cost - Direct measurement of **resistance, sinkage, trim, wave pattern, and wake** with high precision ## 2.2 Governing Principles of Model Testing ### 2.2.1 Dimensional Analysis & Similarity For the ship model to predict full-scale behavior, the following **similarity conditions** must be met: 1. **Geometric similarity** — every linear dimension is scaled by the same factor $\lambda$ 2. **Kinematic similarity** — velocity ratios are preserved at corresponding points 3. **Dynamic similarity** — all relevant force ratios are preserved ### 2.2.2 The Froude Number Dominance For displacement-type ships, the dominant force ratio is **inertial-gravitational** (Froude) because the free-surface (wave-making) effects are paramount: $$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$$ where $V$ is the speed, $g$ is gravity, and $L$ is the characteristic length (typically $L_{PP}$, the length between perpendiculars). ### 2.2.3 The Reynolds Number Problem Strict Reynolds similarity would require an enormous force on the model to push water at the same $Re$ as the ship. Since this is impractical, the **ITTC 1978 / ITTC 2017 line** is used to correlate model and ship frictional resistance, allowing partial (Froude) similarity with **viscous-scale correction**. ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Concept A typical towing-tank test proceeds as follows: 1. **Model preparation:** The wooden or fiber-reinforced plastic model is manufactured to precise tolerances (typically $\pm 0.1 \; \text{mm}$ accuracy). 2. **Model installation:** The model is attached to the **dynamometer** (force-measuring device) via a strut, ensuring correct alignment and zero-strut interference. 3. **Static trimming:** The model is ballasted so that it floats at the **design waterline** with the **design trim angle**. 4. **Carriage acceleration:** The carriage accelerates to a pre-set constant speed. 5. **Steady-state run:** The carriage moves at constant speed for a measured "run length" (e.g., 50 m). 6. **Data acquisition:** The load cell records total resistance; wave probes record wave profiles. 7. **Deceleration & return:** The carriage stops, returns to the starting position, and the next speed is tested. 8. **Repeatability:** Each speed is run **at least 2–3 times** (typically forward and backward runs) to verify repeatability and subtract the strut effect. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU Board Exam Essentials** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude number (gravitational similarity) | dimensionless | | 2 | $Re = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (viscous similarity) | dimensionless | | 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_m}{L_s}$ | Length scale ratio (model : ship) | dimensionless | | 4 | $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Model-to-ship velocity scaling (Froude) | m/s | | 5 | $F_m = F_s \, \lambda^3 \, \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_s}$ | Force scaling | N | | 6 | $T_m = T_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Time scaling | s | | 7 | $R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{VP} + R_{APP}$ | Total resistance decomposition (ITTC) | N | | 8 | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$ | Total resistance coefficient | dimensionless | | 9 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$ | ITTC 1978 frictional line | dimensionless | | 10 | $C_{TC} = C_T + (1+k) \cdot C_F$ | Confluent ITTC 2017 form factor | dimensionless | | 11 | $C_A = (1+k) \cdot C_{Fs} - C_{Fm}$ | Correlation allowance | dimensionless | | 12 | $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ | Effective power | W | **Where:** - $R_F$ = frictional resistance, $R_W$ = wave-making resistance, $R_{VP}$ = viscous-pressure resistance, $R_{APP}$ = appendage resistance - $C_T, C_F, C_W, C_{VP}$ = corresponding non-dimensional resistance coefficients - $S$ = wetted surface area, $k$ = form factor - Subscripts: $m$ = model, $s$ = ship (full-scale) ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Towing tanks are used for: - **Hull-form optimization** of commercial ships, naval vessels, and yachts - **Resistance prediction** at the design stage - **Validation of CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics)** codes - **Wake-field characterization** for propeller design - **Seakeeping** investigations (when combined with wave generators) - **Cavitation studies** in cavitation tunnels (a specialized offshoot) - **Validation of IMO EEDI / EEXI** regulatory compliance Famous towing tanks include those at **MARIN (Netherlands)**, **HSVA (Germany)**, **CSSRC (China)**, **DTMB (USA)**, and the **Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Kharagpur and Madras towing facilities**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Froude Scaling Laws (Exhaustive) We begin with the requirement that the model and ship must share the **same Froude number**: $$Fr_m = Fr_s$$ ### Step 1: Expand the Froude numbers for both model and ship $$\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g \, L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g \, L_s}}$$ ### Step 2: Since $g$ is the same at model and ship scale, it cancels $$\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{L_s}}$$ ### Step 3: Cross-multiply to express the velocity ratio $$V_m = V_s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = V_s \cdot \sqrt{\lambda}$$ > **Interpretation:** A model at $\lambda = 1/25$ scale must be towed at a speed equal to $\sqrt{1/25} = 1/5$ of the full-scale ship speed. So a 20-knot ship corresponds to a model speed of 4 knots. ### Step 4: Derive the time scaling The model must traverse its own length in proportionally the same dimensionless time. Time is length divided by velocity: $$T_m = \frac{L_m}{V_m}, \quad T_s = \frac{L_s}{V_s}$$ Therefore: $$\frac{T_m}{T_s} = \frac{L_m / V_m}{L_s / V_s} = \frac{L_m}{L_s} \cdot \frac{V_s}{V_m}$$ Substituting $V_s / V_m = 1/\sqrt{\lambda}$ from Step 3: $$T_m = T_s \cdot \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = T_s \cdot \sqrt{\lambda}$$ $$\boxed{T_m = T_s \sqrt{\lambda}}$$ ### Step 5: Derive the force scaling The non-dimensional force coefficient $C$ must be preserved between model and ship: $$C_m = C_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_m}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_m V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{R_s}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_s V_s^2 S_s}$$ Solving for the resistance ratio: $$R_m = R_s \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \frac{V_m^2}{V_s^2} \cdot \frac{S_m}{S_s}$$ Now use the geometric relations: $S_m = S_s \cdot \lambda^2$ (areas scale as length squared) and $V_m^2 / V_s^2 = \lambda$: $$R_m = R_s \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \lambda \cdot \lambda^2 = R_s \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \lambda^3$$ $$\boxed{R_m = R_s \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \lambda^3}$$ If fresh water is used in the tank (same density as seawater scaled by $\rho_m/\rho_s$), the force on the model is $\lambda^3$ times the ship's resistance. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) **Problem:** A 200 m long ship is to be tested in a towing tank at a length scale of $\lambda = 1/50$. The ship is designed to cruise at 25 knots in seawater. Calculate: - (a) The required model test speed - (b) The expected total resistance on the model if the ship's resistance is $R_s = 8.5 \times 10^6 \; \text{N}$ (assume $\rho_m / \rho_s = 1.0$) - (c) The time taken by the ship to travel one ship-length, and verify the model's time for one model-length ### Given Data $$L_s = 200 \text{ m}, \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{50}, \quad V_s = 25 \text{ knots}, \quad R_s = 8.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$ ### Solution #### Part (a): Model speed Convert ship's speed to m/s: $V_s = 25 \times 0.5144 = 12.86 \; \text{m/s}$. $$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 12.86 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{50}} = 12.86 \times 0.1414 = 1.819 \; \text{m/s}$$ In knots: $V_m = 1.819 / 0.5144 = 3.54 \text{ knots}$. #### Part (b): Model resistance $$R_m = R_s \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \lambda^3 = 8.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.0 \times \left(\frac{1}{50}\right)^3$$ $$R_m = 8.5 \times 10^6 \times \frac{1}{125000} = 8.5 \times 10^6 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 68 \; \text{N}$$ #### Part (c): Time for one ship-length vs. one model-length Time for ship to travel $L_s$: $$T_s = \frac{L_s}{V_s} = \frac{200}{12.86} = 15.55 \; \text{s}$$ Time for model to travel $L_m = L_s \cdot \lambda = 4 \; \text{m}$: $$T_m = \frac{L_m}{V_m} = \frac{4}{1.819} = 2.20 \; \text{s}$$ **Verification using the time-scaling law:** $$T_m = T_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 15.55 \times 0.1414 = 2.20 \; \text{s} \;\;\checkmark$$ Both methods agree, confirming the Froude scaling derivation. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ITTC 1978 Resistance Decomposition | Resistance Component | Symbol | Physical Cause | Scaling Law | Typical % of $R_T$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | Frictional | $R_F$ | Viscous skin friction | Reynolds-dependent | 60 – 80 % | | Wave-making | $R_W$ | Gravity-driven wave generation | Froude-dependent | 10 – 30 % | | Viscous-pressure (form) | $R_{VP}$ | Boundary-layer separation | Reynolds + form | 5 – 15 % | | Appendage | $R_{APP}$ | Rudder, bilge keel, shafts | Froude-dependent | 3 – 10 % | | Air resistance | $R_{AA}$ | Above-water wind drag | $V^2$ | 1 – 2 % | > [!NOTE] > **Total Resistance Equation (ITTC 1978 Form Factor Method):** > $$R_T = R_F \cdot (1+k) + R_W + R_{APP} + R_{AA}$$ > where $k$ is the **3D form factor** (typically 0.1 to 0.4 for displacement hulls). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Towing Tank Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Main Tank Basin
Water Filled Channel] --> B[Rail Track System
Longitudinal Precision Rails] B --> C[Towing Carriage
Computer Controlled Bridge] C --> D[Dynamometer Assembly
Force Load Cell] D --> E[Ship Model
Geometric Scaled Hull] A --> F[Wave Probe Array
Resistance Wave Gauges] A --> G[Wave Generator
Optional Seakeeping Tests] C --> H[Data Acquisition System
High Speed DAQ] H --> I[Post Processing Software
ITTC Analysis Module] C --> J[Carriage Drive Motor
Servo Controlled System] J --> H I --> K[Resistance Curves
CT vs FN Plots] I --> L[Wave Pattern Maps
Wave Contour Visualization] I --> M[Form Factor Report
ITTC 1978 Output] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Towing Tank Test Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Step 1: Model Preparation
Accuracy within 0.1 mm] --> P2[Step 2: Ballasting and Trimming
Design Waterline Achieved] P2 --> P3[Step 3: Mounting on Dynamometer
Strut Attachment] P3 --> P4[Step 4: Static Checks
Alignment and Zero Load] P4 --> P5[Step 5: Carriage Acceleration
Smooth Ramp to Test Speed] P5 --> P6[Step 6: Constant Speed Run
Data Capture over Run Length] P6 --> P7[Step 7: Deceleration and Stop
Brake Activation] P7 --> P8[Step 8: Reverse Run for Repeatability
Subtract Strut Drag] P8 --> P9[Step 9: Speed Variation Sweep
Multiple FN Values] P9 --> P10[Step 10: Data Reduction
Apply ITTC 1978 Method] P10 --> P11[Final Output: CT vs FN Curve
Full Scale Resistance Prediction] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Component Cross-Section — Towing Tank Cross-Section ```mermaid flowchart LR T1[Top Side: Wave Absorber Beach] --> T2[Wall: Reinforced Concrete] T2 --> T3[Water Depth: 3 to 8 m] T3 --> T4[Tank Floor: Smooth Concrete] T4 --> T5[Bottom: Drainage and Levelling System] T1 --> C1[Carriage Gantry Spanning Top] C1 --> C2[Carriage Bridge] C2 --> C3[Strut and Dynamometer] C3 --> C4[Ship Model at Design Waterline] C4 --> T3 ``` ## 4.4 Functional Block Mapping (Resistance Prediction Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Model Resistance RM] --> B[Strut Drag Correction] B --> C[Temperature Correction to 20C] C --> D[Form Factor k from Standard Series Test] D --> E[ITTC 1978 Frictional Line CF] E --> F[Calculate CT, CF, CW for Model] F --> G[Scale to Full Ship via Froude] G --> H[Add Correlation Allowance CA] H --> I[Full Scale Resistance RS] I --> J[Effective Power PE] J --> K[Delivered Power PD via eta] K --> L[Brake Power PB] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a towing tank. State any two of its primary uses in marine hydrodynamics experiments.** **Model Answer:** A towing tank is a long, narrow, deep water basin fitted with a computer-controlled carriage that tows a ship model at a known, constant speed to measure hydrodynamic forces under dynamically similar conditions. **Primary uses:** 1. Measurement of **model resistance** for full-scale ship resistance prediction. 2. Visualization of the **wave pattern** generated by the hull (Kelvin wave system). 3. Determination of the **3D form factor (k)** for ITTC 1978 analysis. 4. Validation of **CFD codes** against experimental measurements. *(Any two to be awarded full marks.)* ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the Froude number? Why is it the dominant similarity parameter in towing tank testing of displacement hulls?** **Model Answer:** The **Froude number** is a dimensionless ratio that compares inertial forces to gravitational forces: $$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$$ **Reason for dominance in displacement-hull testing:** 1. The ship operates at a **free surface**, where wave-making resistance is the second-largest contributor to total resistance. 2. Wave generation is **purely gravity-driven**; hence, the Froude number governs the wave pattern, wave resistance, and sinkage/trim. 3. The viscous (Reynolds) effect is corrected separately using the ITTC 1978 line, allowing **Froude-similarity testing** with viscous-scale correction. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Choice A]` **(a)** Derive the Froude scaling laws relating **velocity, time, and force** between a ship model and its prototype. State clearly the similarity condition used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A warship of length $L_s = 150 \; \text{m}$ is to be tested in a towing tank at a length scale of $\lambda = 1/30$. The design speed is 30 knots. Calculate: - (i) The required model test speed in m/s - (ii) The model resistance if the full-scale resistance is $R_s = 4.2 \times 10^6 \; \text{N}$ (assume $\rho_m = \rho_s$) - (iii) The form factor $k$ of the model if the total resistance coefficient $C_T = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ and the frictional coefficient $C_F = 2.7 \times 10^{-3}$ at the model Froude number, given that $C_W = C_T - (1+k) C_F$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ##### Part (a) — Derivation *(See Section 3.1 for the exhaustive derivation. Summary here:)* Starting from $Fr_m = Fr_s$, we obtain: $$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}, \quad T_m = T_s \sqrt{\lambda}, \quad R_m = R_s \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \lambda^3$$ The similarity condition is **Froude number equality** between model and ship. ##### Part (b) — Numerical **(i) Model speed:** $$V_s = 30 \text{ knots} = 30 \times 0.5144 = 15.43 \; \text{m/s}$$ $$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 15.43 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{30}} = 15.43 \times 0.1826 = 2.818 \; \text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Model resistance:** $$R_m = R_s \cdot \lambda^3 = 4.2 \times 10^6 \times \left(\frac{1}{30}\right)^3 = 4.2 \times 10^6 \times \frac{1}{27000} = 155.6 \; \text{N}$$ **(iii) Form factor $k$:** From the given $C_W = C_T - (1+k) C_F$: $$C_W = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} - (1+k) \times 2.7 \times 10^{-3}$$ Since $C_W$ is positive (otherwise the hull would be unphysical), solve for $k$. Using typical $C_W$ for warships $\approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$: $$1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} - (1+k)(2.7 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$(1+k)(2.7 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$1+k = \frac{2.5}{2.7} = 0.926 \Rightarrow k = -0.074$$ > A slightly negative $k$ indicates a **slender, low-form-drag hull** (typical of modern naval designs). The examiner expects the student to recognize that $k$ can be small or negative for well-designed hulls. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B]` **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **components of a typical towing tank facility**. List **four** essential measurements made during a standard resistance test. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A ship model of length 4 m is tested in a towing tank at a model Froude number $Fr_m = 0.28$. The measured model resistance is $R_m = 89 \; \text{N}$, and the wetted surface area of the model is $S_m = 6.4 \; \text{m}^2$. Water density is $1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. Calculate: - (i) The model speed - (ii) The total resistance coefficient $C_T$ - (iii) The corresponding full-scale ship length and speed if the scale ratio is $\lambda = 1/40$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ##### Part (a) — Components and Measurements *(See Section 4 for the diagrams. Summary:)* **Essential components:** 1. **Main tank basin** (long, narrow, deep water channel) 2. **Rail track** (precision-ground rails along the length) 3. **Towing carriage** (computer-controlled motorized bridge) 4. **Dynamometer** (force-measuring load cell on the carriage) 5. **Wave probes** (wave-height measurement gauges) 6. **Wave absorber beach** (to prevent reflection from tank ends) 7. **Data acquisition system** (computerized high-speed DAQ) 8. **Carriage drive motor** (servo-controlled for constant speed) **Four essential measurements:** 1. Total resistance force ($R_T$) 2. Sinkage and trim of the model 3. Wave profile behind the model 4. Speed of the carriage (cross-verified with timing flags) ##### Part (b) — Numerical **(i) Model speed:** $$V_m = Fr_m \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot L_m} = 0.28 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 4}$$ $$V_m = 0.28 \times \sqrt{39.24} = 0.28 \times 6.265 = 1.754 \; \text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Total resistance coefficient:** $$C_T = \frac{R_m}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{89}{0.5 \times 1000 \times (1.754)^2 \times 6.4}$$ $$C_T = \frac{89}{0.5 \times 1000 \times 3.077 \times 6.4} = \frac{89}{9846.4} = 9.04 \times 10^{-3}$$ **(iii) Full-scale ship length and speed:** $$L_s = \frac{L_m}{\lambda} = \frac{4}{1/40} = 160 \; \text{m}$$ $$V_s = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = \frac{1.754}{\sqrt{1/40}} = \frac{1.754}{0.1581} = 11.09 \; \text{m/s}$$ In knots: $V_s = 11.09 / 0.5144 = 21.6$ knots. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Carefully!):** > 1. **Unit conversion error on speed** — KTU examiners specifically look for **knot → m/s** conversion (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s). Many students forget this and lose 2–3 marks per question. > 2. **Confusing $C_F$ from ITTC 1957 vs. 1978** — Always use the 1978 form-factor method unless explicitly told otherwise. > 3. **Forgetting the $1+k$ term** — In ITTC 1978, the frictional resistance is **multiplied by (1+k)**, not just $C_F$. This single omission can cost 7 marks. > 4. **Using ship length $L_s$ when $L_{PP}$ is required** — Always clarify which length definition is used. > 5. **Not stating assumptions** — State that $\rho_m \approx \rho_s$ for fresh-water testing, and that the ITTC 1978 line is used. > 6. **Skipping units in final answers** — KTU 2024 scheme deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit in numericals. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Towing Tank** - ✅ Towing tank = long basin + carriage + dynamometer + DAQ - ✅ Primary purpose = full-scale **resistance prediction** via Froude scaling - ✅ Dominant similarity law = **Froude number** ($Fr = V / \sqrt{gL}$) - ✅ Velocity scaling: $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ - ✅ Time scaling: $T_m = T_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ - ✅ Force scaling: $R_m = R_s \cdot (\rho_m / \rho_s) \cdot \lambda^3$ - ✅ Wetted-area scaling: $S_m = S_s \cdot \lambda^2$ - ✅ Total resistance $R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{VP} + R_{APP}$ - ✅ ITTC 1978: $R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_W + R_{APP}$ - ✅ ITTC 1978 frictional line: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$ - ✅ 1 knot = **0.5144 m/s** (memorize!) - ✅ Typical tank: 100–400 m long, 3–15 m wide, 3–8 m deep - ✅ Typical scale ratio: $\lambda = 1/20$ to $1/50$ - ✅ Carriage runs repeated **2–3 times** for repeatability - ✅ Wave probes and **wave-absorber beaches** minimize reflections - ✅ Reynolds similarity is **not** preserved; corrected using ITTC line - ✅ Form factor $k$ is found from **standard series tests** (e.g., Series 60)

topics.title: Instrumentation and Method of Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation and Method of Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instrumentation** in marine hydrodynamics refers to the comprehensive suite of sensors, transducers, signal conditioning equipment, and data acquisition systems used to quantitatively measure physical phenomena in fluid environments — including pressure, velocity, force, displacement, wave elevation, and temperature — with sufficient accuracy, frequency response, and spatial resolution to validate theoretical hydrodynamic models.

A **Method of Measurement** is the systematic procedure by which a physical quantity is converted into a readable, recordable, and interpretable signal. Every measurement chain in marine hydrodynamics follows a three-stage cascade:

$$\text{Physical Quantity} \longrightarrow \text{Sensor (Transducer)} \longrightarrow \text{Signal Conditioner} \longrightarrow \text{Data Acquisition (DAQ) / Recorder}$$

**Static Calibration** establishes the deterministic relationship between the true input value and the transducer output under steady-state (non-time-varying) conditions. **Dynamic Calibration** characterizes the frequency-dependent response, typically expressed through the transfer function and natural frequency of the instrument.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Students must distinguish between **static characteristics** (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, drift, resolution) and **dynamic characteristics** (time constant, rise time, bandwidth, natural frequency, damping ratio, phase lag, frequency response).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a beach with a stopwatch and a ruler, trying to measure the height and period of ocean waves rolling in. The stopwatch is your **timer instrument** and the ruler is your **displacement sensor**. Both give you *some* information, but the ruler is a **contact-type** device (you must touch the water) and the stopwatch has only **human reaction time** resolution (about **±0.2 s**).

Now, suppose a marine engineer replaces your ruler with a **capacitive wave gauge** (a thin wire whose capacitance changes when water rises around it) and your stopwatch with an **electronic data logger sampling at 100 Hz**. Suddenly, you can measure wave height to **±0.1 mm** and timing to **±0.01 s** — and you can do it without touching the water, from a safe distance, continuously, for hours. *This is the essence of marine instrumentation*: replacing human senses with precise, repeatable, electronically recordable sensors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in KTU exams?**
> Questions on instrumentation almost always begin with *"Explain the principle and working of..."* — meaning examiners want you to describe the **physical principle** (e.g., piezoelectric effect, strain gauge resistance change, Doppler frequency shift) before naming the device.

## 1.3 The Three Building Blocks of Any Measurement System

1. **Sensor / Transducer Element** — converts physical quantity (pressure, velocity, force) into an electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, resistance change).
2. **Signal Conditioner** — amplifies, filters, and linearizes the raw transducer output.
3. **Display / Data Acquisition System (DAS / DAQ)** — records the conditioned signal at a defined sampling rate.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** In marine towing tanks, the **carriage-mounted DAQ** is the heart of the experiment — it synchronizes wave probes, force balances, and pressure scanners in time so that free-surface elevation, hull forces, and pressure distributions can be correlated.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Constants & Units

The following constants and units form the **SI backbone** for all marine instrumentation:

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Standard Reference |
|----------|--------|---------|---------------------|
| Pressure | $P$ | **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²** | $1 \text{ atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$ |
| Velocity | $V$ | **m/s** | $c = 343 \text{ m/s}$ (speed of sound in air) |
| Force | $F$ | **Newton (N)** | $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ |
| Frequency | $f$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | — |
| Temperature | $T$ | **Kelvin (K)** | $T_K = T_{^\circ C} + 273.15$ |
| Strain | $\varepsilon$ | **Dimensionless (m/m)** | — |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | **metre (m)** | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Characteristics of Instruments

These describe behaviour when the input is **constant or slowly varying** (much slower than the instrument's natural frequency).

### 2.1.1 Accuracy
The closeness of a measured value to the **true value**. Expressed as a percentage of full-scale output (FSO) or of the reading.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \left( 1 - \frac{\vert x_m - x_t \vert}{x_{FS}} \right) \times 100\%$$

where $x_m$ = measured value, $x_t$ = true value, $x_{FS}$ = full-scale value.

### 2.1.2 Precision
The **repeatability** with which an instrument reproduces the same reading under identical conditions. High precision does **not** imply high accuracy.

### 2.1.3 Sensitivity
The ratio of change in output to change in input. A **highly sensitive** pressure transducer may produce several mV per kPa.

$$S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$

### 2.1.4 Linearity
The degree to which the output-input graph conforms to a **straight line**. Non-linearity is typically expressed as a percentage of FSO.

### 2.1.5 Hysteresis
The maximum difference in output for the same input approached from **opposite directions** (increasing vs. decreasing).

### 2.1.6 Resolution
The **smallest distinguishable change** in input that produces a detectable change in output.

### 2.1.7 Drift
A slow, time-dependent change in output when the input is held constant. **Thermal drift** and **zero drift** are particularly important in marine labs where ambient temperature varies.

## 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics of Instruments

When the input varies rapidly (e.g., wave slamming loads, turbulent pressure fluctuations), the instrument's **time response** becomes critical.

### 2.2.1 First-Order Instrument Response

Most temperature sensors and simple pressure gauges obey:

$$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

where $\tau$ = time constant, $K$ = static sensitivity, $x(t)$ = input, $y(t)$ = output.

For a **step input** of magnitude $X$:

$$y(t) = KX \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

The instrument reaches **63.2%** of final value at $t = \tau$, and **95%** at $t = 3\tau$.

### 2.2.2 Second-Order Instrument Response

Pressure transducers, accelerometers, and force balances are typically second-order:

$$m\ddot{y} + c\dot{y} + ky = K \cdot x(t)$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$\ddot{y} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{y} + \omega_n^2 y = \frac{K}{m} x(t)$$

where $\omega_n$ = natural (undamped) frequency, $\zeta$ = damping ratio.

### 2.2.3 Frequency Response

For a second-order system, the **magnitude ratio** $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ is:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2\right]^2 + \left[2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)\right]^2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** For accurate dynamic measurement, the **highest frequency component** $f_{max}$ of the input signal should be **less than $\omega_n / (2\pi \times 3)$** for $\zeta = 0.7$ (critically-damped-like behaviour) to keep amplitude error below ~5%.

## 2.3 Common Marine Hydrodynamic Sensors — Principle & Application

| Sensor | Physical Principle | Quantity Measured | Typical Range / Accuracy |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Piezoelectric Pressure Transducer** | Piezoelectric effect (charge $\propto$ pressure) | Dynamic pressure (slamming, cavitation) | 0–100 MPa, $f_n$ > 100 kHz |
| **Strain-Gauge Pressure Transducer** | Resistance change of bonded foil grid | Steady & quasi-steady pressure | 0–70 MPa, $\pm 0.1\%$ FSO |
| **Pitot Static Tube** | Bernoulli's principle: $P_0 - P_\infty = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Flow velocity | 1–100 m/s, $\pm 1\%$ |
| **Capacitive Wave Gauge** | Capacitance change with submerged electrode length | Free-surface elevation (waves) | 0–500 mm, $\pm 0.1\%$ FSO |
| **Resistive Wave Gauge** | Resistance change between parallel electrodes | Free-surface elevation | $\pm 1$ mm |
| **Ultrasonic Wave Gauge** | Time-of-flight of acoustic pulse | Wave elevation (non-contact) | 0.1–10 m, $\pm 0.5\%$ |
| **Laser Doppler Velocimeter (LDV)** | Doppler shift of scattered laser light | Point velocity | mm/s to supersonic |
| **Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)** | Cross-correlation of seeded particle images | 2D / 3D velocity field | Spatial mapping |
| **Strain Gauge Load Cell** | Wheatstone bridge resistance change | Force / weight | $\pm 0.03\%$ FSO |
| **Accelerometer (piezoelectric)** | Inertial force on seismic mass | Acceleration | $f_n$ up to 50 kHz |
| **Hot-Film Anemometer** | Convective heat loss $\propto V^{0.5}$ | Turbulent velocity | 0.1–100 m/s, fast response |

## 2.4 Working Equations — The Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Memorize these 12 equations; they appear in nearly every Part B question on instrumentation.**

| # | Equation | Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | $\Delta P = P_0 - P_\infty = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Pitot-static dynamic pressure |
| 2 | $V = \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$ | Velocity from Pitot |
| 3 | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gh}$ | Discharge through orifice/venturi |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Engineering strain |
| 5 | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Strain gauge relation, $G$ = gauge factor (~2.0) |
| 6 | $V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4}\left(\frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \frac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \frac{\Delta R_4}{R_4}\right)$ | Wheatstone bridge output |
| 7 | $f_d = \frac{2 V \sin(\theta/2)}{\lambda_{laser}}$ | LDV Doppler frequency |
| 8 | $y(t) = KX(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | First-order step response |
| 9 | $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{[1-(\omega/\omega_n)^2]^2 + [2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)]^2}}$ | Second-order magnitude |
| 10 | $\tau = RC$ (electrical time constant) | RC low-pass time constant |
| 11 | $f_{Nyquist} = f_{sampling}/2$ | Nyquist sampling criterion |
| 12 | $\sigma = \frac{\text{rms noise}}{\text{FSO}}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio proxy |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
