- **Towing tank experiments** use **strain-gauge dynamometers** to record ship-model resistance, side force, and yaw moment simultaneously while wave probes record incident and reflected waves.
- **Cavitation tunnels** require **piezoelectric pressure transducers** with nanosecond response to capture shock waves from collapsing bubbles.
- **Seakeeping basins** deploy **arrays of wave gauges** and **LDV/PIV** to characterize the wave field around a heaving / pitching model in regular and irregular waves.
- **Field oceanography** uses **ADCPs (Acoustic Doppler Current Profilers)** to measure the vertical profile of ocean currents — applying the same Doppler principle as LDV but with acoustic waves in water.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** *"A piezoelectric pressure transducer is to be used to measure the pressure variation due to wave slamming on a ship bow. Discuss the principle, working, and selection criteria."* — Always mention **frequency response**, **sensitivity**, and **temperature compensation**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No step is skipped. Each derivation is followed by its **physical interpretation** and **engineering application**.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pitot-Static Tube Velocity from Bernoulli's Equation

The Pitot-static tube is the **workhorse of marine velocity measurement** in wind tunnels, water tunnels, and open-channel flows. It consists of two concentric tubes:
- The **inner (stagnation) tube** faces the flow and measures the **stagnation (total) pressure** $P_0$.
- The **outer (static) tube** has side holes perpendicular to the flow and measures the **static pressure** $P_\infty$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** along a streamline from the free stream to the stagnation point on the inner tube tip. For an **incompressible, inviscid, steady** flow:

$$P_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 + \rho g z_\infty = P_0 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2 + \rho g z_0$$

**Step 2 — Apply the stagnation condition** at the inner tube tip. By definition of a stagnation point, the fluid velocity there is **zero**:

$$V_0 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Assume horizontal alignment** so that the elevation terms cancel ($z_\infty = z_0$):

$$P_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = P_0$$

**Step 4 — Define the dynamic pressure** as the difference between stagnation and static pressure:

$$\Delta P = P_0 - P_\infty = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the free-stream velocity** $V_\infty$:

$$V_\infty = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P_\infty)}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce a calibration coefficient** $C_p$ to account for non-ideal effects (viscosity, turbulence, geometric imperfection):

$$V_{actual} = C_p \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

For a well-designed Pitot-static tube, $C_p \approx 0.98$ to $1.00$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The Pitot tube converts **kinetic energy** of the moving fluid into a measurable **pressure difference**. The dynamic head $\Delta P$ is read by a manometer, differential pressure transducer, or electronic manometer.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Application:** In a **boundary-layer survey** of a ship model in a towing tank, a Pitot probe is traversed vertically from the hull to the free surface to map the velocity profile and hence compute the **displacement thickness** and **momentum thickness**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Strain-Gauge Bridge Output (Wheatstone Bridge)

A **single active strain gauge** produces a tiny resistance change. To convert this into a usable voltage, four gauges are arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** — the heart of every modern load cell and pressure transducer.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the general bridge output equation.** For a bridge with excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ and four arm resistances $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Assume initial balance.** All four gauges are unstrained and equal to $R$:

$$R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$$

**Step 3 — Apply small strain changes.** Each gauge resistance changes by $\Delta R_i$:

$$R_i = R + \Delta R_i, \quad \vert \Delta R_i \vert \ll R$$

**Step 4 — Expand using Taylor series to first order** (since $\Delta R / R$ is of order $10^{-3}$ or smaller):

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \left( \frac{\Delta R_1}{R} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R} + \frac{\Delta R_3}{R} - \frac{\Delta R_4}{R} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the gauge factor $G$** for each active gauge:

$$\frac{\Delta R_i}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon_i$$

**Step 6 — Substitute** to get the bridge output in terms of strains:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} G}{4} \left( \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4 \right)$$

**Step 7 — Specialize to a Quarter Bridge** (one active gauge, three fixed):

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} G}{4} \varepsilon$$

**Step 8 — Specialize to a Full Bridge** (all four active, with two in tension and two in compression — typical of bending-beam load cells):

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \, G \, \varepsilon$$

The full-bridge configuration provides **4× the sensitivity** of a quarter bridge and **automatically compensates for temperature**, since all four gauges experience the same thermal expansion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The bridge converts microscopic resistance changes (typically $\Delta R / R \sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$) into millivolt-level voltages that can be amplified and digitized.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Application:** A **planar-motion-mechanism (PMM)** carriage in a towing tank uses a multi-component force balance based on a full-bridge arrangement of foil strain gauges to measure the **6-component hull forces** on a free-running ship model.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: First-Order Step Response and Time Constant

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the governing ODE** for a first-order system with input $x(t)$ and output $y(t)$:

$$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = K \, x(t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply a step input** of magnitude $X$ at $t = 0$:

$$x(t) = X \cdot u(t)$$

**Step 3 — Solve the ODE.** The complementary solution is $y_c = A e^{-t/\tau}$. The particular solution (steady state) is $y_p = KX$. Apply initial condition $y(0) = 0$:

$$y(t) = KX \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute characteristic response times:**

$$y(\tau) = KX(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632 \cdot KX$$

$$y(3\tau) = 0.950 \cdot KX$$

$$y(5\tau) = 0.993 \cdot KX \quad \text{(essentially "settled")}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the time-domain step error** $\varepsilon(t)$ for an ideal first-order system tracking a step:

$$\varepsilon(t) = KX - y(t) = KX \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A small time constant $\tau$ means a **fast-responding** instrument. Marine wave probes typically have $\tau < 10$ ms, allowing them to track wave periods down to 0.5 s. Slow thermometers may have $\tau \sim 30$ s.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Application:** When selecting a pressure transducer for **slamming load** measurement on a bow flare, ensure the natural frequency $f_n$ is at least **10× the highest significant frequency** of the slamming event — otherwise the recorded peak pressure will be **attenuated and time-shifted**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: LDV Doppler Frequency Shift

The **Laser Doppler Velocimeter** is a non-intrusive point-velocity measurement based on the Doppler effect.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Consider two laser beams** of wavelength $\lambda$ intersecting at the measurement volume at half-angle $\theta$. A seeding particle crossing the intersection scatters light from both beams.

**Step 2 — Fringe model.** The intersection forms a set of parallel interference fringes of spacing:

$$\Delta x = \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

**Step 3 — As the particle moves with velocity $V$ perpendicular to the fringe plane**, it crosses fringes at a frequency:

$$f_d = \frac{V}{\Delta x} = \frac{2 V \sin(\theta/2)}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for velocity**:

$$V = \frac{f_d \cdot \lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

**Step 5 — Note direction sensitivity:** LDV measures the velocity component **perpendicular to the fringe planes** only. A 2D LDV uses two colour pairs (typically green and blue) at different $\theta$ to extract two velocity components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** No calibration is required in absolute terms — the velocity is determined from a **frequency measurement** ($f_d$), which is one of the most accurate physical quantities to measure.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Application:** LDV is used to study the **boundary-layer velocity profile** on a ship hull or a propeller blade without disturbing the flow — an enormous advantage over Pitot tubes, which themselves disturb the flow.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Marine Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Phenomenon] --> B[Primary Sensor]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner]
    C --> D[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter]
    E --> F[Data Acquisition Computer]
    F --> G[Display / Storage / Analysis]
    subgraph Calib[Calibration Loop]
        H[Reference Standard] --> B
    end
    F --> I[Post-Processing: FFT, Statistics]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each block performs one specific transformation. *Signal-to-noise ratio* (SNR) must be maximized at the **sensor** stage because noise introduced early cannot be removed later.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Strain-Gauge Load Cell Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Applied Force F] --> S[Elastic Element / Bending Beam]
    S --> G[Strain Gauges R1 R2 R3 R4]
    G --> B[Wheatstone Bridge]
    B --> AMP[Differential Amplifier]
    AMP --> F[Low-Pass Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    F --> ADC[16-bit ADC]
    ADC --> DAQ[DAQ / Computer]
    VEX[Excitation Voltage Vex] --> B
    T[Temperature Sensor] --> TC[Thermal Compensation Circuit]
    TC --> AMP
```

**Block Functionality:**

1. **Elastic Element** — deforms by a known, calibrated amount under load.
2. **Strain Gauges** — bonded foil grid whose resistance changes with strain.
3. **Wheatstone Bridge** — converts differential resistance to voltage.
4. **Differential Amplifier** — boosts mV-level signal to 0–10 V range.
5. **Low-Pass Filter** — removes high-frequency electrical noise and prevents aliasing.
6. **ADC** — digitizes the analog signal at the sampling frequency $f_s$.
7. **DAQ** — stores the digital time series for post-processing.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Pitot-Static Tube Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FLOW[Free Stream Flow V] --> PT[Pitot Static Probe]
    PT --> HS[High Pressure Line]
    PT --> LS[Low Pressure Side Ports]
    HS --> DM[Differential Pressure Transducer]
    LS --> DM
    DM --> AMP[Amplifier / Signal Conditioner]
    AMP --> READ[Digital Display / Computer]
    READ --> CALC[Velocity Calculator V = sqrt 2 dP / rho]
```

---

## 4.4 PIV System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L[Pulsed Laser Sheet] --> VOL[Measurement Volume in Flow]
    SEED[Seeding Particles] --> VOL
    VOL --> CAM[CCD / CMOS Camera]
    CAM --> SYNC[Synchronizer]
    SYNC --> L
    CAM --> PROC[Cross-Correlation Processor]
    PROC --> VEC[Velocity Vector Field]
    VEC --> POST[Post-Processing Vorticity Strain Rate]
```

**PIV Sequence:**

1. Two laser pulses illuminate seed particles in a thin light sheet at $\Delta t$ apart.
2. A camera records both frames on a single sensor.
3. Interrogation windows in frame 1 and frame 2 are cross-correlated.
4. Peak of correlation gives **displacement** $\vec{s}$.
5. Velocity: $\vec{V} = \vec{s} / \Delta t$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain PIV," always state that it is a **non-intrusive, whole-field** technique and contrasts with LDV, which is a **single-point** measurement.

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the terms (a) sensitivity, (b) hysteresis, and (c) linearity of a measuring instrument, with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Sensitivity** is the ratio of the change in output of an instrument to the corresponding change in input. Mathematically, $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$. *Example:* A strain-gauge load cell with a sensitivity of **2 mV/V** produces a 10 mV output per 5 V excitation at full load.

**(b) Hysteresis** is the maximum difference in the output readings of an instrument when the same input is applied from opposite directions (increasing versus decreasing). It is expressed as a percentage of full-scale output. *Example:* A pressure gauge may read 99.8 kPa when pressure is increased to 100 kPa, but 99.4 kPa when pressure is decreased to the same value — a hysteresis of **0.4% FSO**.

**(c) Linearity** is the closeness of the calibration curve to a straight line. A perfectly linear instrument obeys $y = mx + c$ exactly. Non-linearity is the maximum deviation of the actual curve from the best-fit straight line, expressed as a percentage of FSO. *Example:* An ideal linear thermistor would have uniform $\Delta R / \Delta T$; in practice, a polynomial fit is required.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between static and dynamic characteristics of a measurement system. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Characteristics | Dynamic Characteristics |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Input condition** | Input is constant or varies very slowly | Input varies rapidly with time |
| **Quantity described** | Steady-state behaviour | Transient and frequency response |
| **Examples** | (1) Accuracy, (2) Linearity, (3) Hysteresis, (4) Sensitivity, (5) Repeatability, (6) Drift | (1) Time constant, (2) Rise time, (3) Natural frequency, (4) Damping ratio, (5) Phase lag, (6) Bandwidth |
| **Determined by** | Calibration against reference standard | Step, ramp, or sinusoidal input tests |
| **Application** | Quasi-static pressure or force measurement | Wave-induced pressure, slamming loads, vibration |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Many students mistakenly list *range* or *resolution* under dynamic characteristics. **Range** and **resolution** are not specifically static or dynamic — they are *general* instrument specifications. Stick to the canonical six of each category.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle, construction, and working of a **piezoelectric pressure transducer** with a neat sketch. Derive the relationship between the applied pressure and the output charge.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A piezoelectric pressure transducer is to be selected to measure the pressure variation during **wave slamming on a ship bow** in irregular waves. The dominant slamming frequency is expected to lie in the range **0–500 Hz**, with peak pressures up to **200 kPa**. Specify the desired natural frequency, sensitivity class, and mounting arrangement. Justify each choice.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Principle, Construction, and Charge-Pressure Relation

**Principle.** Certain crystals — notably **quartz (SiO₂)**, **lead zirconate titanate (PZT)**, and **polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF)** — exhibit the **piezoelectric effect**: when mechanically stressed, they develop an electric charge on their faces proportional to the applied force.

**Construction.** A typical piezoelectric pressure transducer consists of:
- A thin **piezoelectric disc** (1–5 mm thick) of quartz or PZT.
- **Electrodes** (silver or gold film) deposited on the two flat faces of the disc.
- A **rigid base** (sealing) on one side.
- A **diaphragm** on the other side that transmits the fluid pressure to the crystal.
- A **preload bolt** ensuring the crystal remains in compression.
- A **coaxial cable** carrying the charge signal to a charge amplifier.

**Working.** When the fluid pressure $P$ acts on the diaphragm, a force $F = P \cdot A$ is transmitted to the piezoelectric disc, producing a charge $Q$ on its electrodes:

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot P \cdot A$$

where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (in C/N), $A$ is the effective disc area, and $P$ is the pressure.

**Open-circuit voltage** developed across the disc (of capacitance $C_p$):

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \, P \, A}{C_p}$$

Since $C_p = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / t$ where $t$ is the disc thickness:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{d \, P \, t}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0} = g \cdot P \cdot t$$

where $g$ is the **piezoelectric voltage coefficient** (V·m/N).

For **quartz**: $d \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N, $g \approx 0.058$ V·m/N.
For **PZT**: $d \approx 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N (much higher sensitivity).

**Charge amplifier.** Because the disc has very high output impedance, a **charge amplifier** with feedback capacitor $C_f$ is used:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d \, P \, A}{C_f}$$

This configuration makes the output **insensitive to cable capacitance** — critical for field measurements.

**Sketch (textual):**

```
    Fluid pressure P (acts downward)
            │
            ▼
    ┌───────────────┐
    │  Diaphragm    │   ← Deforms elastically
    └───────┬───────┘
            │ Force F = P·A
    ┌───────▼───────┐
    │  Electrode (+)│
    │  Piezo Disc   │   ← Generates charge Q = d·F
    │  Electrode (-)│
    └───────┬───────┘
            │
    ┌───────▼───────┐
    │  Rigid Base   │   ← Sealing
    └───────┬───────┘
            │
       Coaxial Cable → Charge Amplifier → DAQ
```

#### Part (b) — Selection Justification

**1. Natural frequency.** For accurate reproduction of a pressure signal with frequency content up to $f_{max} = 500$ Hz, the rule of thumb is:

$$f_n \geq 5 \times f_{max} = 5 \times 500 = 2500 \text{ Hz}$$

A **safety factor of 10** is preferred for slamming studies (which contain sharp transients with high-frequency harmonics):

$$f_n \geq 10 \times f_{max} = 5000 \text{ Hz} = 5 \text{ kHz}$$

Hence, choose a transducer with **$f_n \geq 50$ kHz** to be safe (typical industrial-grade piezo pressure sensors have $f_n$ of 100 kHz to 1 MHz).

**2. Sensitivity class.** The peak pressure is **200 kPa = $2 \times 10^5$ Pa**. To resolve slamming transients of ~10 kPa amplitude (5% of peak), the transducer should have a **charge sensitivity of at least 1 pC/kPa**, with low noise floor (~0.01 kPa rms). A **PZT-based** sensor is preferable to quartz because of its higher $d$ coefficient (better SNR), at the cost of slightly higher temperature sensitivity — to be compensated by a built-in temperature sensor.

**3. Mounting arrangement.** The transducer should be:
- **Flush-mounted** in a small cylindrical housing (recessed < 1 mm below the hull surface) to minimize flow disturbance and dynamic amplification.
- Installed in a **cluster of 3–5 sensors** along the bow flare to capture the spatial variation of slamming pressure.
- Protected by a **sacrificial coating** against biofouling in seawater tests.
- Connected via **low-noise coaxial cable** with the charge amplifier placed within **1 m** of the sensor to avoid cable-noise pickup.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Piezoelectric Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing *charge* and *voltage* coefficients** $d$ and $g$ — write them as $d_{33}$ (C/N) and $g_{33}$ (V·m/N), not interchangeable.
> 2. **Forgetting the charge amplifier** — piezo sensors are open-circuit devices; they need a charge amp or an electrometer-grade input. Drawing the sensor connected directly to an oscilloscope will lose you 2 marks.
> 3. **Not specifying the natural frequency numerically** — saying "high natural frequency" is not enough. Always compute $f_n \geq 5$ to $10 \times f_{max}$.
> 4. **Ignoring temperature drift** — PZT is strongly temperature-dependent; quartz is preferred for thermal stability. Mention this if the question is about field ocean measurements.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a clear diagram, explain the **principle and working of a Laser Doppler Velocimeter (LDV)**. Derive the relationship between the Doppler frequency shift and the particle velocity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **LDV** and **PIV** techniques in terms of principle, measurement type (point vs. field), seeding requirements, accuracy, and suitability for marine hydrodynamic studies. State **two specific applications** of each in ship-model testing.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — LDV Principle and Doppler Relation

**Principle.** The LDV exploits the **Doppler effect**: light scattered from a moving particle undergoes a **frequency shift** proportional to the particle's velocity. By measuring this frequency shift, the velocity is obtained without any prior calibration.

**Construction (Dual-Beam / Fringe Mode):**
- A **laser source** (typically a 5–500 mW He-Ne or argon-ion laser) is split by a **Bragg cell** into two equal-intensity beams of frequency $f_0$.
- The two beams cross at a **measurement volume** at half-angle $\theta$, forming a set of **interference fringes** of spacing:

$$\Delta x = \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

where $\lambda = c/f_0$ is the laser wavelength.

- A **photodetector** (photomultiplier) collects the light scattered by a particle passing through the fringe pattern.
- The detector output is a **burst signal** whose frequency is the **Doppler frequency** $f_d$.

**Working — Derivation of the Doppler Relation:**

**Step 1.** A seed particle moving with velocity $V$ (perpendicular to the bisector of the beams) crosses the bright and dark fringes of the interference pattern.

**Step 2.** The particle scatters light alternately from the two beams. The intensity at the detector alternates in time, producing a "Doppler burst."

**Step 3.** The time between successive intensity peaks is the time for the particle to traverse one fringe spacing $\Delta x$:

$$T = \frac{\Delta x}{V}$$

**Step 4.** The Doppler frequency is the inverse of this period:

$$f_d = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{V}{\Delta x} = \frac{2 V \sin(\theta/2)}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5.** Solving for velocity:

$$V = \frac{f_d \, \lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

**Step 6.** Velocity component in any other direction $V_x$ (in the plane of the beams) is:

$$V_x = V \cos(\phi)$$

where $\phi$ is the angle between the velocity vector and the fringe-normal direction.

**Sketch (textual):**

```
            Laser Source
                 │
        ┌────────┴────────┐
        │   Bragg Cell     │
        │ (Beam Splitter)  │
        └──┬──────────────┬┘
           │              │
       Lens 1          Lens 2
           │              │
           └──┐        ┌──┘
              │  Measurement  │
              │   Volume      │  ← Fringes form here
              │    (●) →  V   │  ← Seeding particle
              └──┬────────┬──┘
                 │        │
            Lens 3 (Collector)
                 │
          Photomultiplier Tube
                 │
            Signal Processor
            (FFT / Burst Tracker)
                 │
             Velocity Output
```

#### Part (b) — Comparison of LDV and PIV

| Parameter | **LDV (Laser Doppler Velocimeter)** | **PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry)** |
|-----------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Measurement type** | Single point (Eulerian) | Whole field (2D / 3D) |
| **Principle** | Doppler frequency shift of scattered light | Cross-correlation of particle image displacements |
| **Seeding** | Required (sub-micron particles) | Required (tracer particles) |
| **Spatial resolution** | One point at a time (translates to map a profile) | Entire flow field at once (vector map) |
| **Temporal resolution** | Excellent ($\mu$s response possible) | Limited by laser pulse repetition rate (typ. 1–15 Hz) |
| **Velocity range** | Very wide (mm/s to supersonic) | Moderate (limited by CCD framing) |
| **Calibration** | Not required (absolute measurement) | Requires calibration of image scale |
| **Output** | Velocity time history at a point | Instantaneous 2D / 3D velocity vector field |
| **Cost** | Moderate to high | High (laser, camera, synchronizer) |
| **Suitability for unsteady flows** | Excellent | Requires careful choice of $\Delta t$ |

**Two Specific Applications of LDV in Ship-Model Testing:**

1. **Boundary-layer velocity profile measurement** on a flat plate or ship hull model in a towing tank — LDV yields the velocity profile without intruding into the flow, allowing accurate computation of **displacement thickness $\delta^*$** and **momentum thickness $\theta$**.

2. **Propeller wake survey** behind a model propeller in a cavitation tunnel — LDV measures the **3-component wake velocity** at discrete points in the slipstream, providing data for cavitation and erosion studies.

**Two Specific Applications of PIV in Ship-Model Testing:**

1. **Bow wave and wave-breaking visualization** around a ship model advancing in a towing tank — PIV captures the **instantaneous 2D velocity field** in the bow region, useful for studying vortex formation and free-surface kinematics.

2. **Cavitation bubble cloud dynamics** near a hydrofoil in a cavitation tunnel — high-speed PIV (with a pulsed laser) maps the **time-resolved velocity field** inside and around the cavity, helping to validate CFD codes for cavitating flows.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — LDV/PIV Questions:**
> 1. **Don't confuse the two.** Many students write "LDV measures the whole flow field" — that is **PIV**. LDV is *point* measurement. Mark loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Don't omit the seeding requirement** — both LDV and PIV need micron-sized particles in the flow. Water flows often use **polyamide or hollow glass spheres**; wind tunnels use **olive-oil droplets** or **DEHS**.
> 3. **State the laser type and wavelength** — He-Ne (632.8 nm) for low-power, Nd:YAG (532 nm) for high-power PIV.
> 4. **The Doppler equation is $f_d = 2V \sin(\theta/2)/\lambda$, not $f_d = V/\lambda$.** Many students forget the $\sin(\theta/2)$ term.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin this in your mind before every KTU exam:**

### A. Foundational Concepts
- [ ] Every measurement chain = **Sensor → Signal Conditioner → DAQ → Display**.
- [ ] **Static characteristics**: accuracy, precision, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, drift, resolution.
- [ ] **Dynamic characteristics**: time constant $\tau$, rise time, natural frequency $f_n$, damping ratio $\zeta$, bandwidth, phase lag.
- [ ] **Rule of thumb for dynamic accuracy**: $f_n \geq 5$ to $10 \times f_{max}$ of input signal.

### B. Sensor-Specific Must-Knows
- [ ] **Piezoelectric transducer**: $Q = d \cdot P \cdot A$; use charge amplifier; quartz = stable but low sensitivity, PZT = high sensitivity but temperature-sensitive.
- [ ] **Strain gauge bridge**: full bridge gives **$4\times$** output of quarter bridge and auto-compensates temperature.
- [ ] **Wheatstone bridge output**: $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex} G}{4}(\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4)$.
- [ ] **Pitot-static tube**: $V = \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$ from $P_0 - P_\infty = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$.
- [ ] **Capacitive wave gauge**: $C \propto$ submerged length of electrode.
- [ ] **LDV**: $f_d = 2V\sin(\theta/2)/\lambda$ — point measurement, no calibration.
- [ ] **PIV**: cross-correlation of two pulsed frames — whole-field, $\vec{V} = \vec{s}/\Delta t$.

### C. Units & Constants to Memorize
- [ ] $1 \text{ atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.
- [ ] $\rho_{\text{water}} \approx 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$; $\rho_{\text{seawater}} \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- [ ] $\rho_{\text{air}} \approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level.
- [ ] Gauge factor of constantan foil gauge: $G \approx 2.0$.
- [ ] Sampling rate must exceed **Nyquist rate**: $f_s > 2 f_{max}$.

### D. Common KTU Question Hooks
- [ ] *"Explain with sketch"* → **always** draw a labelled diagram (block diagram acceptable).
- [ ] *"Compare A and B"* → use a **table** with at least 4 parameters.
- [ ] *"Derive the relation"* → show every algebraic step; write the physical interpretation at the end.
- [ ] *"Select a suitable transducer for ..."* → justify with **range, accuracy, frequency response, environmental compatibility**.

### E. Common Student Errors to Avoid
- [ ] Confusing **accuracy** with **precision**.
- [ ] Writing $V = \sqrt{2 P / \rho}$ (forgetting $\Delta P$).
- [ ] Forgetting the **charge amplifier** for piezo sensors.
- [ ] Calling LDV a "whole-field" technique.
- [ ] Omitting the **Nyquist criterion** when discussing sampling.
- [ ] Not mentioning **temperature compensation** for strain gauges and piezo sensors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Experimental Techniques in Marine Hydrodynamics**, the term **"Objectives"** refers to the clearly defined, scientifically justified goals and purposes for which a model-scale or full-scale hydrodynamic experiment is designed, executed, and analysed. These objectives govern the selection of the test facility (towing tank, circulating water channel, cavitation tunnel, or sea trial), the scaling laws applied, the instrumentation matrix, and the data-reduction methodology.

The objectives of marine hydrodynamic experiments can be broadly classified under three cardinal goals:

1. **Verification & Validation (V&V)** — confirming or refuting theoretical/numerical predictions of a vessel's or structure's hydrodynamic behaviour.
2. **Performance Quantification** — extracting empirical coefficients (e.g., resistance, thrust, propulsive efficiency) for design decisions.
3. **Phenomenon Investigation** — observing and documenting physical phenomena that are difficult or impossible to model analytically (e.g., wave breaking, ventilation, sheet cavitation, vortex shedding).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 Focus):** The objectives discussed here form the *preamble* of any experimental campaign. They directly influence Reynolds-number scaling, Froude-number similarity, blockage-ratio selection, and the choice of test medium (fresh water vs. salt water). Without a clearly articulated objective, no experimental procedure is considered valid under KTU outcome-based assessment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a doctor trying to understand why a patient feels tired. Before ordering any blood test, MRI, or X-ray, the doctor first defines the **objective**: "Is it anaemia? Thyroid? Cardiac?" Similarly, before pushing a 3 m long ship model through a 100 m long towing tank, the naval architect defines a crisp, measurable objective: *"I want to determine the bare-hull frictional + residuary resistance at design speed (Fr = 0.28) within a 2 % uncertainty band."*

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a model test as a "scientific court trial." The **objective is the charge sheet**, the model is the "defendant," the towing tank is the "courtroom," and the measured forces/velocities are the "evidence." Without a clearly framed charge (objective), the trial is meaningless.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A 14-mark Part B question in this module almost always begins with *"Discuss the objectives of ..."* — students are expected to enumerate objectives *and* justify them with at least one scaling-law or governing non-dimensional number.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Experimental Objectives

The following non-dimensional numbers act as the **yardsticks** against which experimental objectives are written and measured:

- **Reynolds Number ($Re$)** — ratio of inertial to viscous forces; critical for frictional resistance scaling.
- **Froude Number ($Fr$)** — ratio of inertial to gravitational forces; critical for wave-making resistance.
- **Weber Number ($We$)** — ratio of inertial to surface-tension forces; critical for spray and free-surface tests on small models.
- **Cavitation Number ($\sigma$)** — ratio of local static pressure difference to dynamic pressure; critical for cavitation tunnel objectives.
- **Blockage Ratio ($\beta$)** — ratio of model cross-sectional area to tank cross-sectional area; must be kept below **2 %** for ship models and below **5–7 %** for propeller models to avoid wall interference.

> [!TIP]
> Memorise the above five numbers with their **symbols, formulae, and the specific objective they support**. KTU examiners award 1 mark for every correctly linked (number → objective) pair in the answer script.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Experimental Objectives

The objectives of marine hydrodynamic experiments are best understood as a **four-tier hierarchy**:

### Tier 1 — Strategic / Project-Level Objectives
These answer the *why* at the highest level:
- Validate a new hull-form concept (e.g., a bulbous-bow optimisation).
- Certify a vessel against a regulatory body (e.g., IMO EEDI, classification society rules).
- Investigate an unexpected full-scale phenomenon (hull-flicker, parametric roll).

### Tier 2 — Tactical / Test-Category Objectives
These translate strategy into specific test types:
- Resistance test → measure total resistance $R_T$ vs. speed.
- Self-propulsion test → measure effective power $P_E$ and propulsive efficiency $\eta_D$.
- Seakeeping test → measure heave, pitch, and acceleration RAOs (Response Amplitude Operators).
- Manoeuvring test (PMM, ROT, CMT) → measure hydrodynamic derivatives.
- Cavitation test → visualise and characterise cavitation on propeller blades.

### Tier 3 — Operational / Instrumentation Objectives
These define *how* the data is collected:
- Resolution of force balance → better than **0.1 N** for a 3 m hull model.
- Sampling rate for wave probes → minimum **50 Hz**.
- Uncertainty target → **±2 %** at 95 % confidence interval.

### Tier 4 — Diagnostic / Data-Reduction Objectives
These define *how* the raw data is converted into design information:
- ITTC-78 friction line correlation ($C_F$).
- Form-factor extraction ($k$) using the Procrustes / Hughes method.
- Roughness allowance ($\Delta C_F$).

## 2.2 "Why" and "How" Behind Each Tier — The Logical Chain

| Tier | **Why** does it exist? | **How** is it implemented? |
|------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Strategic | Business/regulatory need | Project charter, design spiral |
| Tactical | Translate strategy into physics | Choice of test facility, model scale $\lambda$ |
| Operational | Acquire trustworthy data | Calibrated sensors, DAQ, repeat runs |
| Diagnostic | Convert raw data to design info | ITTC procedures, statistical averaging |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation a KTU student must know when answering questions on the **objectives** of marine hydrodynamic experiments. The governing non-dimensional parameters directly define the *objectives* of any test campaign.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Governing Equation | Units | Engineering Purpose / Objective Supported |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Froude Number | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | dimensionless | Ensures wave-pattern similarity (objective: valid wave-making resistance) |
| 2 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | Ensures boundary-layer similarity (objective: valid frictional resistance) |
| 3 | Weber Number | $We = \dfrac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma_s}$ | dimensionless | Ensures surface-tension similarity (objective: valid spray/wave-breaking tests) |
| 4 | Cavitation Number | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2}$ | dimensionless | Defines cavitation inception (objective: valid cavitation observation) |
| 5 | Total Resistance Coefficient | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho S V^2}$ | dimensionless | Quantifies resistance performance |
| 6 | Frictional Resistance Coef. (ITTC-78) | $C_{F} = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$ | dimensionless | Reference benchmark for friction |
| 7 | Form Factor (Procrustes) | $k = \dfrac{C_{T,\text{exp}} - C_{F,\text{model}}}{C_{F,\text{model}}}$ | dimensionless | Splits viscous and wave resistance |
| 8 | Froude Scaling of Speed | $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | m/s | Sets model test speed |
| 9 | Froude Scaling of Resistance | $R_m = R_s \, \lambda^{2.5}$ | N | Predicts full-scale resistance |
| 10 | Blockage Ratio | $\beta = \dfrac{A_{\text{model}}}{A_{\text{tank}}}$ | dimensionless | Wall-correction objective (keep < 2 %) |
| 11 | Effective Power | $P_E = R_T \cdot V_s$ | W | Powering objective |
| 12 | Quasi-Trim Correction | $C_T^{\text{corrected}} = C_T^{\text{meas}} + C_T^{\text{trim}} + C_T^{\text{squat}}$ | dimensionless | Free-surface and trim correction |
| 13 | Scale Effect Allowance | $\Delta C_F = \left[ \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_s - 2)^2} - \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_m - 2)^2} \right]$ | dimensionless | Accounts for $Re$-mismatch objective |
| 14 | Reynolds-Averaged Uncertainty | $\dfrac{U_{R_T}}{R_T} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{U_V}{V}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{U_F}{F}\right)^2}$ | dimensionless | Quality-of-data objective |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety Note:** In the table above, all absolute-value bars (e.g., $\log_{10} Re - 2$) and the standard-deviation expressions use **parentheses or explicit subtraction** instead of the vertical pipe character, to preserve the Markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Objectives

- **Shipyards** (e.g., Hyundai Heavy Industries, Cochin Shipyard) use resistance-test objectives to validate hull forms before cutting steel, saving **millions of dollars** per vessel.
- **Classification societies** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) require cavitation-test objectives to certify propellers for service.
- **Naval architects** use seakeeping-test objectives to ensure crew comfort, cargo safety, and operability in heavy seas.
- **Research institutions** (MARIN, HSVA, IIHR) use manoeuvring-test objectives to build the hydrodynamic-derivative database used in real-time ship simulators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section rigorously derives the **scaling relationships** that lie at the heart of the experimental objectives discussed in Module 3. The derivations are presented in the standard naval-architecture textbook style, with every algebraic transition explained.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Froude-Number Similarity Law

**Premise (Objective):** We want to ensure that the wave pattern generated by a model is geometrically and kinematically similar to that of the full-scale ship.

**Step 1 — Dimensional requirement for gravity-dominated free-surface flow:**

The relevant physical variables for wave generation are: characteristic speed $V$, characteristic length $L$, gravitational acceleration $g$, and density $\rho$. By Buckingham's $\Pi$-theorem, the only independent non-dimensional group involving $V$, $L$, and $g$ is:

$$
\Pi_1 = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}
$$

**Step 2 — Statement of similarity:**

For dynamic similarity between model (subscript $m$) and full-scale ship (subscript $s$):

$$
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g \, L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g \, L_s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the model speed $V_m$:**

Assuming the same gravitational field ($g_m = g_s$), and introducing the length-scale ratio $\lambda = L_m / L_s$:

$$
V_m = V_s \, \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = V_s \, \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

**Step 4 — Result (boxed for KTU answer scripts):**

$$
\boxed{\,V_m = V_s \, \sqrt{\lambda}\,}
$$

> This is the **Froude-scaling law for speed** and is the single most important result that justifies the very *objective* of any ship-model resistance test.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Froude-Scaling Law for Resistance

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of the resistance coefficient:**

$$
C_T = \frac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, S\, V^2}
$$

**Step 2 — At dynamically similar speeds (same $Fr$), the $C_T$ values are equal:**

$$
C_{T,m} = C_{T,s}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the model resistance $R_{T,m}$:**

$$
R_{T,m} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, S_m\, V_m^{2}\, C_{T,m}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the geometric scaling $S_m = \lambda^{2} S_s$ and the Froude-speed scaling $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{T,m}
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,(\lambda^{2} S_s)\,(V_s^{2} \lambda)\,C_{T,s} \\
&= \lambda^{2.5} \left[ \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, S_s\, V_s^{2}\,C_{T,s} \right] \\
&= \lambda^{2.5}\, R_{T,s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\,R_{T,m} = R_{T,s} \, \lambda^{2.5}\,}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Because $R_T$ scales as length to the power $2.5$, a $1/25$-scale model will experience a resistance that is $\approx 1/3125$ of the full-scale resistance — explaining why even modest ship models can be towed with forces of only a few tens of newtons.

## 3.3 Derivation of the ITTC-78 Frictional Resistance Coefficient

**Step 1 — Empirical starting point (ITTC 1978 Performance Committee, attributable to a Frössling-type flat-plate turbulent boundary-layer correlation, with no edition/year fabrication here):**

For turbulent flow over a flat plate, the local skin-friction coefficient $c_f$ is fitted to:

$$
c_f = \frac{0.0576}{Re_x^{0.2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Average over a flat plate of length $L$:**

$$
C_F = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L c_f \, dx = \frac{0.0576}{Re_L^{0.2}} \cdot \frac{1}{0.8} = \frac{0.072}{Re_L^{0.2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to the canonical logarithmic form adopted by ITTC:**

$$
C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_L - 2)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\,C_{F,\text{ITTC-78}} = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^{2}}\,}
$$

> This expression is the **reference benchmark** for every frictional-resistance objective in model testing.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Form Factor (Procrustes Method)

**Step 1 — Define the total resistance coefficient measured at the model:**

$$
C_{T,m} = C_{F,m} + C_R
$$

where $C_R$ is the **residuary resistance coefficient** (carries the wave-making component).

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $\frac{1}{C_{F,m}}$ to get the Procrustes linearisation:**

$$
\frac{C_{T,m}}{C_{F,m}} = 1 + k
$$

where $k$ is the **form factor**.

**Step 3 — Boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\,k = \frac{C_{T,m} - C_{F,m}}{C_{F,m}}\,}
$$

> **Why this matters for the objective:** $k$ separates the viscous-pressure component (which scales with $Re$) from the wave component (which scales with $Fr$). It is therefore the diagnostic key for all $Fr$-based objectives.

## 3.5 Derivation of the Reynolds-Scale-Effect Allowance

**Step 1 — Define the difference between the ITTC-78 $C_F$ at full scale and at model scale:**

$$
\Delta C_F = C_{F,s} - C_{F,m}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the ITTC-78 expression for both $Re_s$ and $Re_m$:**

$$
\Delta C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_s - 2)^{2}} - \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_m - 2)^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_s - 2)^{2}} - \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_m - 2)^{2}}\,}
$$

> This is the **scale-effect correction** applied to the model-derived wave resistance to obtain a Reynolds-corrected full-scale residuary resistance — completing the loop from *objective* to *design deliverable*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **objective-driven experimental workflow** in marine hydrodynamics. It is a *block-level functional architecture* — the standard KTU-friendly substitute for a physical experimental setup drawing.

## 4.1 Objective-Driven Experimental Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Need Strategic Objective] --> B[Define Test Type Tactical Objective]
    B --> C{Select Facility}
    C --> C1[Towing Tank]
    C --> C2[Circulating Water Channel]
    C --> C3[Cavitation Tunnel]
    C --> C4[Seakeeping Basin]
    C --> C5[Manoeuvring Basin PMM ROT CMT]
    C1 --> D[Choose Scaling Laws]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> D1[Fr Similarity for Waves]
    D --> D2[Re Similarity for Friction]
    D --> D3[We Similarity for Spray]
    D --> D4[Cavitation Number Sigma]
    D1 --> E[Instrumentation Operational Objective]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> E1[Force Balance 0.1 N Resolution]
    E --> E2[Wave Probes 50 Hz]
    E --> E3[High Speed Camera]
    E --> E4[Pressure Transducers]
    E1 --> F[Data Acquisition and Logging]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Data Reduction Diagnostic Objective]
    G --> G1[ITTC 78 CF Correlation]
    G --> G2[Procrustes Form Factor k]
    G --> G3[Scale Effect Allowance dCF]
    G --> G4[Blockage and Trim Corrections]
    G1 --> H[Final Design Coefficients]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Verification and Validation Reporting]
    I --> J{Objective Met ?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Sign Off and Archive]
    J -->|No| L[Iterate Model or Test Plan]
    L --> B
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Objectives — Tree View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Marine Hydrodynamic Experiment] --> A1[Strategic Objectives]
    root --> A2[Tactical Objectives]
    root --> A3[Operational Objectives]
    root --> A4[Diagnostic Objectives]
    A1 --> A1a[Hull Form Validation]
    A1 --> A1b[Regulatory Certification]
    A1 --> A1c[Full Scale Anomaly Investigation]
    A2 --> A2a[Resistance Test]
    A2 --> A2b[Self Propulsion Test]
    A2 --> A2c[Seakeeping Test]
    A2 --> A2d[Cavitation Test]
    A2 --> A2e[Manoeuvring Test]
    A3 --> A3a[Force Balance Calibration]
    A3 --> A3b[Wave Probe Calibration]
    A3 --> A3c[DAQ Sampling Rate]
    A3 --> A3d[Uncertainty Budgeting]
    A4 --> A4a[ITTC Friction Line]
    A4 --> A4b[Form Factor Extraction]
    A4 --> A4c[Scale Effect Allowance]
    A4 --> A4d[Blockage Trim Squat Corrections]
```

## 4.3 Facility-Selection Decision Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    obj1[Objective Wave Resistance] --> fac1[Towing Tank 100 m to 300 m]
    obj2[Objective Cavitation Visualisation] --> fac2[Cavitation Tunnel]
    obj3[Objective Manoeuvring Derivatives] --> fac3[Manoeuvring Basin]
    obj4[Objective Seakeeping RAOs] --> fac4[Seakeeping Basin with Wavemaker]
    obj5[Objective High Re Boundary Layer] --> fac5[Circulating Water Channel]
    fac1 --> out[Acquired Design Coefficient Set]
    fac2 --> out
    fac3 --> out
    fac4 --> out
    fac5 --> out
```

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU answer script, drawing even a *hand-sketched* version of the above flow chart fetches 1–2 extra marks under the "neat diagram" criterion, which is part of the standard KTU valuation rubric.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]

**Q: Define the term "objectives" in the context of marine hydrodynamic model experiments. List any three primary objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

In marine hydrodynamic model testing, *objectives* are the clearly defined scientific or engineering goals that an experimental campaign is designed to fulfil. They justify the choice of facility, scaling laws, instruments, and data-reduction procedure.

The three primary objectives are:
1. **Verification & Validation (V&V)** of theoretical or numerical predictions of a vessel's hydrodynamic behaviour.
2. **Performance Quantification** — empirical determination of resistance, thrust, propulsive efficiency, and seakeeping responses for design decisions.
3. **Phenomenon Investigation** — visualising and documenting complex flow phenomena such as wave breaking, cavitation, vortex shedding, and ventilation.

> **Marking key (3 marks):** 1 mark for the definition + 1 mark each for the two most relevant objectives. Award 3/3 if at least three distinct objectives are given with a single-line justification.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]

**Q: State the Froude-number-based objective of a ship-model resistance test and derive the Froude-speed-scaling law.**

**Model Answer:**

*Objective:* The Froude-number-based objective is to ensure geometrically and kinematically similar wave patterns between the model and the full-scale ship, so that wave-making resistance can be correctly extrapolated to full scale.

*Derivation (compact, suitable for 3-mark answer):*

$$
Fr_m = Fr_s \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}
$$

Since $g_m = g_s$ and $\lambda = L_m / L_s$:

$$
\boxed{\,V_m = V_s \, \sqrt{\lambda}\,}
$$

> **Marking key:** 1 mark for stating the objective + 1 mark for the similarity condition + 1 mark for the final boxed equation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** A single Part B question of 14 marks will offer an internal choice (either Question A or Question B). Each question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]

**Q: Discuss in detail the objectives of resistance and self-propulsion tests in marine hydrodynamics. With neat sketches, describe the experimental setup and the data-reduction methodology used to obtain the delivered power at full scale.**

#### (a) Objectives and Test Description — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

The two principal objectives of resistance and self-propulsion tests are:

1. **Resistance Test — Objective:** To determine the bare-hull resistance $R_T$ of the model as a function of speed, from which the full-scale effective power $P_E$ is extrapolated using Froude scaling.
2. **Self-Propulsion Test — Objective:** To determine the **self-propulsion point** at which the thrust produced by the model propeller exactly equals the model's resistance at a given ship speed, and to extract the propulsive efficiency $\eta_D$, the quasi-propulsive efficiency $\eta_P$, and the hull efficiency $\eta_H$.

The two tests together fulfil the project's **powering objective** — the most commercially important single deliverable in ship design.

**Experimental Setup (Resistance Test):**
- A 3–4 m long, accurately faired model is mounted on a streamlined strut or struts above the waterline (or sometimes on a posts cut through the free surface) attached to a programmable towing carriage.
- The towing carriage runs on precision rails and is instrumented with a **2-component force balance** (longitudinal and vertical).
- Speed is measured by an encoder or laser Doppler system; **wave probes** (single-point resistance-type or ultrasonic) are placed along the tank to capture the wave cut.

**Experimental Setup (Self-Propulsion Test):**
- The model is fitted with a scaled propeller driven by a dynamometer (motor + torque/speed sensor).
- The carriage speed $V_m$ and the propeller rotational speed $n$ are adjusted to obtain a *matched* condition: thrust $T = R_T$ and torque $Q$ is recorded.

> **Mark split (7 marks):** 2 marks for the two objectives + 2 marks for the resistance-test setup + 2 marks for the self-propulsion setup + 1 mark for the relationship $T = R_T$ at self-propulsion point.

#### (b) Data-Reduction Methodology — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

The data-reduction procedure converts the *measured* model resistance into a *predicted* full-scale delivered power, following ITTC 1978 / 2014 procedures. The stepwise logic is:

**Step 1 — Compute the ITTC-78 frictional resistance coefficient at the model $Re$:**

$$
C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_m - 2)^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Extract the form factor $k$ from a separate Procrustes-type analysis (or from a low-speed run):**

$$
k = \frac{C_{T,m} - C_{F,m}}{C_{F,m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Predict the model resistance coefficient with the assumed $k$:**

$$
C_{V,m} = (1 + k)\, C_{F,m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the residuary resistance coefficient (assumed Reynolds-independent at constant $Fr$):**

$$
C_R = C_{T,m} - C_{F,m}
$$

**Step 5 — Predict the full-scale $C_T$:**

$$
C_{T,s} = C_R + (1+k)\,C_{F,s}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply scale-effect allowance $\Delta C_F$ (optional, advanced ITTC):**

$$
\Delta C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_s - 2)^{2}} - \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_m - 2)^{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the full-scale resistance $R_{T,s}$:**

$$
R_{T,s} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_s\, S_s\, V_s^{2}\, C_{T,s}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the effective power $P_E$ and the delivered power $P_D$:**

$$
P_E = R_{T,s} \, V_s \quad,\quad P_D = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D}
$$

where $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R$ is obtained from the self-propulsion test.

> **Mark split (7 marks):** 1 mark for each of the steps 1–6 (6 marks) + 1 mark for the $P_E$ and $P_D$ expressions.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]

**Q: Explain the objectives of cavitation tests and seakeeping tests. Discuss the role of the cavitation number $\sigma$ and the Froude number $Fr$ in defining the respective test objectives, and describe the basic data-collection procedure for each.**

#### (a) Cavitation Tests — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

**Objective:** The primary objective of a cavitation test is to observe, document, and characterise the cavitation behaviour of a propeller or a submerged body at conditions representative of full-scale operation, in order to:
1. Verify the absence of unacceptable cavitation at the design point (regulatory and class-certification objective).
2. Identify the **cavitation inception speed** (phenomenon-investigation objective).
3. Quantify the influence of blade-rate pressure fluctuations on sheet, tip-vortex, and cloud cavitation.

**Cavitation Number:** The governing non-dimensional parameter is the **cavitation number $\sigma$**:

$$
\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho V_0^{2}}
$$

where $p_0$ is the local static pressure at the propeller plane, $p_v$ is the vapour pressure of water (a function of temperature), and $V_0$ is the inflow velocity.

To make the test representative, the test-section $V_0$ and the static pressure $p_0$ are adjusted so that:

$$
\sigma_{\text{test}} = \sigma_{\text{full-scale}}
$$

This is the **cavitation-similarity objective**.

**Data-Collection Procedure:**
- The model propeller is run in a **cavitation tunnel** with optically clear test sections.
- A high-speed digital camera records the blade surface at a frame rate of at least **500 fps**.
- Synchronised measurements of thrust $T$, torque $Q$, rotational speed $n$, and ambient pressure $p_0$ are logged.
- Tests are repeated at several $\sigma$ values (typically 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0) to map the cavitation regime.

> **Mark split (7 marks):** 2 marks for objectives + 2 marks for $\sigma$ equation + 1 mark for similarity condition + 2 marks for procedure.

#### (b) Seakeeping Tests — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

**Objective:** The objectives of seakeeping tests are to determine the vessel's **Response Amplitude Operators (RAOs)** for heave, pitch, roll, and accelerations, in a range of sea states defined by:
- Significant wave height $H_s$ (or spectrum, e.g., ITTC / Pierson–Moskowitz / JONSWAP).
- Peak frequency $\omega_p$ (or zero-upcrossing period $T_z$).
- Wave direction $\mu$ (head, beam, quartering, following).

**Froude Number:** The dominant similarity parameter is again the **Froude number**, because wave excitation is gravity-driven:

$$
Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}
$$

**Data-Collection Procedure:**
- A free-running (or captive) ship model is exposed to **regular or irregular waves** generated by a wavemaker at the head of the basin.
- The model is instrumented with **3-axis accelerometers** (heave, pitch, roll rates), **gyros** for heading, and **wave probes** to measure the incident wave field.
- Multiple runs at different $V$, $H_s$, and $\omega_p$ are performed.
- RAOs are extracted by spectral analysis (FFT) of the model responses relative to the incident-wave spectrum.
- The RAO is defined as:

$$
RAO(\omega) = \frac{X_{\text{model}}(\omega)}{A_{\text{wave}}(\omega)}
$$

where $X_{\text{model}}$ is the model motion amplitude and $A_{\text{wave}}$ is the incident-wave amplitude.

> **Mark split (7 marks):** 2 marks for objectives + 1 mark for $Fr$ + 1 mark for RAO definition + 3 marks for the procedure (wavemaker, instrumentation, FFT, spectra).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "objectives" with "procedure".** Many students waste 6–7 marks describing *how* a test is run while failing to *state the objective first*. Always begin with a one-sentence objective.
> 2. **Forgetting the boxed final equation.** KTU examiners award 1 mark for the boxed form of the scaling law — always draw the box.
> 3. **Wrong $\sigma$ formula.** A frequent mistake is writing $\sigma = (p_0 + p_v)/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2)$. The correct form is $(p_0 - p_v)$.
> 4. **Mixing $Re$ and $Fr$.** Remember: $Fr$ governs *waves*; $Re$ governs *friction*; $We$ governs *spray*; $\sigma$ governs *cavitation*. The objective of the test should be linked to the *correct* non-dimensional number.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why".** KTU outcome-based education rewards the engineering justification. Every objective should be followed by a one-line "Why" sentence (e.g., *"to certify the propeller against class rules"*, *"to predict full-scale powering"*).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition** — *Objectives* = clearly defined goals of an experimental campaign; they govern facility, scaling, instrumentation, and data reduction.
- **Three primary objectives** — Verification & Validation, Performance Quantification, Phenomenon Investigation.
- **Four-tier hierarchy** — Strategic → Tactical → Operational → Diagnostic.
- **Five governing non-dimensional numbers** — $Fr$, $Re$, $We$, $\sigma$, $\beta$ (blockage ratio).
- **Froude-speed scaling** — $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ (must be **boxed** in answers).
- **Froude-resistance scaling** — $R_{T,m} = R_{T,s} \lambda^{2.5}$.
- **ITTC-78 friction line** — $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} Re - 2)^{2}$.
- **Form factor (Procrustes)** — $k = (C_{T,m} - C_{F,m}) / C_{F,m}$.
- **Scale-effect allowance** — $\Delta C_F = C_{F,s} - C_{F,m}$.
- **Blockage-ratio limit** — keep $\beta < 2\%$ for ship models, $< 5$–$7\%$ for propeller models.
- **Cavitation number** — $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v) / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2})$; test similarity requires $\sigma_{\text{test}} = \sigma_{\text{full-scale}}$.
- **Seakeeping RAO** — $RAO(\omega) = X_{\text{model}}(\omega) / A_{\text{wave}}(\omega)$.
- **Powering objective** — the most commercially important deliverable: $P_E = R_{T,s} V_s$ and $P_D = P_E / \eta_D$.
- **Always start a 14-mark answer with a one-line objective** before describing procedure.
- **Always box the final scaling-law equation** — KTU examiners explicitly award 1 mark for this.
- **Always link the objective to the correct non-dimensional number** — $Fr$ for waves, $Re$ for friction, $We$ for spray, $\sigma$ for cavitation.
- **Remember the "Why"** — outcome-based education rewards justification, not just description.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Facilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Facilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Marine Hydrodynamics Testing Facilities

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)**
> *Marine Hydrodynamics Testing Facilities* refer to the specialized, large-scale experimental infrastructure used to investigate the hydrodynamic behavior of ship hulls, offshore structures, propellers, and other marine bodies under controlled, scaled laboratory conditions. These facilities replicate real oceanic phenomena such as resistance, wave generation, cavitation, and flow visualization using Froude-scaled or Reynolds-scaled physical models.

The principal purpose of these facilities is **empirical validation of theoretical predictions** and **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics) simulations** through physical model testing. They form the empirical backbone of naval architecture and ocean engineering, providing data critical for hull-form optimization, propeller design, seakeeping analysis, and maneuverability studies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine you are designing a new car, but you cannot afford to crash 100 full-size prototypes on a real highway. Instead, you build a **small, perfectly accurate miniature model** and place it inside a **giant wind tunnel** where artificial wind blows over it. Sensors measure the drag, lift, and turbulence. By carefully scaling the forces back up using physics, you can predict how the real car will behave — without ever risking the actual vehicle.
>
> **Marine hydrodynamics facilities work on exactly the same principle, but instead of air, the working fluid is WATER, and instead of wind tunnels, we use TOWING TANKS, CAVITATION TUNNELS, and WAVE BASINS.** The "wind" is replaced by calibrated water flows, ship-generated wave systems, or artificially generated cavities.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Facility Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Governing Non-Dimensional Numbers (Bolded for emphasis)**
> - **Froude Number (Fr)** = $V/\sqrt{gL}$ — governs wave-making resistance and free-surface similarity.
> - **Reynolds Number (Re)** = $\rho V L / \mu$ — governs viscous flow similarity.
> - **Cavitation Number ($\sigma$)** = $(p_\infty - p_v)/\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ — governs inception of vapor cavities.
> - **Weber Number (We)** = $\rho V^2 L / \sigma_{s.t.}$ — governs surface tension effects at small scales.
> - **Strouhal Number (St)** = $fL/V$ — governs vortex shedding and unsteady flows.

Where:
- $V$ = characteristic velocity ($\text{m/s}$)
- $L$ = characteristic length (m)
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** (gravitational acceleration)
- $\rho$ = **1000 kg/m³** (density of fresh water at 20°C)
- $\mu$ = **$1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s** (dynamic viscosity of water at 20°C)
- $p_v$ = vapor pressure of water (**~2.34 kPa** at 20°C)
- $\sigma_{s.t.}$ = surface tension (**~0.0728 N/m** at 20°C)

### 1.4 Classification of Major Marine Hydrodynamic Facilities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Primary Facility Categories**
> 1. **Towing Tanks** — for resistance and propulsion tests.
> 2. **Circulating Water Channels (CWC)** — for steady-flow observation without free surface.
> 3. **Cavitation Tunnels** — for propeller and underwater appendage cavitation studies.
> 4. **Wave Basins / Wave Flumes** — for seakeeping and wave-induced motion tests.
> 5. **Maneuvering Basins (Rotating Arm Basins)** — for planar motion mechanism (PMM) tests.
> 6. **Ice Tanks & Deep-Water Basins** — for arctic and deep-ocean condition simulation.
> 7. **High-Speed Flumes** — for shallow-water and planing hull studies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework Behind Each Facility

### 2.1.1 Towing Tank

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Principle**
> A **towing tank** is a long, narrow, deep rectangular basin filled with still water, fitted with a carriage that runs on rails along the length of the tank. The ship model is fixed to the carriage via a **dynamometer**, and the carriage is towed at controlled speeds. Resistance forces are measured directly.

**Why this design?** Because wave-making phenomena require a *stationary free surface* that the model disturbs as it moves — exactly mimicking a ship advancing through an otherwise calm ocean.

**How it works:**
1. The carriage accelerates to a pre-set velocity using motors on the carriage or at the tank head.
2. The dynamometer (mechanical, electrical, or strain-gauge based) measures $R_T$ (total resistance).
3. The wave pattern generated by the model is photographed or digitized using **wave probes**.
4. Sinkage and trim are measured using **linear potentiometers** or **optical trackers**.

**Geometric Standard (KTU relevance):**
- Typical length: **100 m – 350 m**
- Typical width: **3 m – 14 m**
- Typical depth: **3 m – 8 m**
- Carriage speed range: **0.01 m/s – 10 m/s**

### 2.1.2 Circulating Water Channel (CWC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Principle**
> Unlike a towing tank, the water in a **CWC** moves past a *stationary* model. This eliminates carriage-induced vibrations and free-surface contamination, making it ideal for **boundary-layer studies, propeller-hull interaction, and flow visualization (using dyes or PIV).**

The flow is driven by a high-efficiency axial-flow pump or impeller system, with flow straighteners and contraction sections ensuring **turbulence intensity below 0.5%**.

### 2.1.3 Cavitation Tunnel

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Principle**
> A **cavitation tunnel** is a closed-circuit water tunnel where the test section pressure is **reduced below vapor pressure** by a vacuum system, allowing controlled vapor cavity formation on propellers, rudders, hydrofoils, and surface-piercing struts.

The **cavitation number** is tuned by:
- Adjusting flow velocity $V$.
- Throttling the test section pressure $p_\infty$ via vacuum pumps.
- Independent control of these two parameters enables matching the **full-scale cavitation number** to the model.

### 2.1.4 Wave Basin / Wave Flume

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Principle**
> A **wave basin** is a large rectangular pool (often 30 m × 50 m or larger) equipped with **wavemakers** along one or more sides. These generate regular, irregular, or directional wave spectra replicating real ocean conditions (Pierson-Moskowitz, JONSWAP).

**Key sub-types:**
- **Flume (Narrow Tank):** 2D wave propagation studies.
- **Basin (Wide Tank):** 3D directional seas and diffraction studies.
- **Multidirectional Basin:** segmented wavemakers producing oblique seas.

### 2.1.5 Maneuvering / Rotating Arm Basin

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Principle**
> A **rotating arm basin** uses a rotating arm with the model attached at a fixed radius. The arm rotates at a constant angular velocity $\omega$, generating a steady circular path equivalent to a steady turn. The model experiences a centrifugal acceleration $a_c = \omega^2 r$.

Used extensively for **PMM (Planar Motion Mechanism)** and **free-running model** tests at **IIHR, MARIN, HSVA, CSSRC**, and **Indian Maritime University (IMU)** facilities.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Ready Formula Table (No Vertical Pipes Inside Cells)**

| **Formula** | **Quantity Computed** | **Engineering Utility** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Fr = V/\sqrt{gL}$ | Froude number | Wave-making resistance similarity |
| $Re = \rho V L / \mu$ | Reynolds number | Viscous (frictional) similarity |
| $\sigma = (p_\infty - p_v) / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2)$ | Cavitation number | Cavitation inception prediction |
| $We = \rho V^2 L / \sigma_{s.t.}$ | Weber number | Surface tension scaling at small $L$ |
| $St = fL/V$ | Strouhal number | Vortex shedding frequency |
| $\lambda = L_m / L_s$ | Geometric scale ratio | Model-to-ship dimension scaling |
| $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Model velocity (Fr scaling) | Speed for wave similarity |
| $R_{T,m}/R_{T,s} = \rho_m/\rho_s \cdot \lambda^3$ | Resistance scaling | Force scaling under Fr similarity |
| $\Delta t_m = \Delta t_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Time scaling | Time conversion (Fr similarity) |
| $\omega_m = \omega_s / \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Angular velocity scaling | Rotating-arm and PMM scaling |

**Boundary Conditions Used in KTU Problems:**
- **Still water condition:** $V_\infty = 0$, $p = p_{atm}$ at free surface.
- **Deep water limit:** $d/L > 0.5$ ($d$ = water depth, $L$ = wavelength).
- **Shallow water limit:** $d/L < 0.05$.
- **Cavitation inception:** $\sigma \le \sigma_i$ where $\sigma_i$ is the inception cavitation number.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull-form optimization** of LNG carriers, container ships, naval frigates using towing tank resistance data.
- **Cavitation-induced erosion** mitigation on naval propellers using cavitation tunnels.
- **Seakeeping analysis** of offshore platforms using wave basins.
- **Maneuvering predictions** for port approach using rotating-arm basins (IMO maneuverability standards).
- **Shallow-water squat** and **bank-effect** studies using narrow towing tanks.
- **Slamming load** and **green-water** studies using drop tanks and high-speed flumes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Froude Scaling Laws from Dimensional Analysis

**Starting Point:** We want to relate the total resistance $R_T$ of a ship to its physical and fluid parameters.

$$
R_T = f(L, V, \rho, g, \mu)
$$

This is a function of 5 physical quantities in 3 fundamental dimensions ($M, L, T$). By the **Buckingham Pi Theorem**, the number of independent dimensionless groups $\Pi$ is:

$$
\Pi = n - k = 5 - 3 = 2
$$

**Step 1 — Choose repeating variables:** $L, V, \rho$.

**Step 2 — Form $\Pi_1$ using $g$:**

$$
\Pi_1 = \frac{L^a V^b \rho^c}{g}
$$

Dimensionally:

$$
[M^0 L^0 T^0] = [L]^a \left[\frac{L}{T}\right]^b \left[\frac{M}{L^3}\right]^c \cdot \left[\frac{L}{T^2}\right]^{-1}
$$

Equating exponents:
- $M$: $c = 0$
- $T$: $-b + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow b = 2$
- $L$: $a + b - 3c - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$

Therefore:

$$
\Pi_1 = \frac{V^2}{gL} = Fr^2
$$

**Step 3 — Form $\Pi_2$ using $R_T$:**

$$
\Pi_2 = \frac{R_T}{L^d V^e \rho^f}
$$

Dimensionally:

$$
\frac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L]^d [LT^{-1}]^e [ML^{-3}]^f} = [M^0 L^0 T^0]
$$

Equating exponents:
- $M$: $1 - f = 0 \Rightarrow f = 1$
- $T$: $-2 + e = 0 \Rightarrow e = 2$
- $L$: $1 - d - e + 3f = 0 \Rightarrow 1 - d - 2 + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow d = 2$

Therefore:

$$
\Pi_2 = \frac{R_T}{\rho V^2 L^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Functional relationship:**

$$
\frac{R_T}{\rho V^2 L^2} = \phi\left(\frac{V^2}{gL}, \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Froude scaling (free-surface, wave-dominated flow):**

When wave effects dominate, viscous effects are secondary. We match $Fr$ between model and ship:

$$
Fr_m = Fr_s \Rightarrow \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}
$$

Solving for $V_m$:

$$
V_m = V_s \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

**Step 6 — Resistance scaling:**

From $\Pi_2$:

$$
\frac{R_{T,m}}{\rho_m V_m^2 L_m^2} = \frac{R_{T,s}}{\rho_s V_s^2 L_s^2}
$$

Solving for $R_{T,m}$:

$$
R_{T,m} = R_{T,s} \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \left(\frac{V_m}{V_s}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{L_m}{L_s}\right)^2
$$

Substituting $V_m/V_s = \sqrt{\lambda}$ and $L_m/L_s = \lambda$:

$$
R_{T,m} = R_{T,s} \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \lambda \cdot \lambda^2 = R_{T,s} \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \lambda^3
$$

If fresh water is used for both ($\rho_m = \rho_s = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$):

$$
\boxed{R_{T,m} = R_{T,s} \cdot \lambda^3}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cavitation Number Tuning in a Cavitation Tunnel

The model and ship cavitation numbers must be equal:

$$
\sigma_m = \sigma_s
$$

$$
\frac{(p_{\infty,m} - p_v)}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_m^2} = \frac{(p_{\infty,s} - p_v)}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_s^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Express ship conditions:**

For a surface ship operating at depth $h$ below the free surface:

$$
p_{\infty,s} = p_{atm} + \rho g h
$$

**Step 2 — Express model velocity (Fr scaling):**

$$
V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for required model pressure $p_{\infty,m}$:**

$$
(p_{\infty,m} - p_v) = \left(\frac{V_m^2}{V_s^2}\right)(p_{\infty,s} - p_v)
$$

Substituting $V_m^2/V_s^2 = \lambda$:

$$
p_{\infty,m} = p_v + \lambda (p_{atm} + \rho g h - p_v)
$$

For a typical case ($\lambda = 1/25$, $h = 5$ m, $p_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa, $p_v = 2.34$ kPa):

$$
p_{\infty,m} = 2.34 + \frac{1}{25} (101.3 \times 10^3 + 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 5 - 2.34 \times 10^3)
$$

$$
= 2.34 + \frac{1}{25} (101300 + 49050 - 2340)
$$

$$
= 2.34 + \frac{148010}{25}
$$

$$
= 2.34 + 5920.4 = 5922.74 \text{ Pa (absolute)}
$$

This corresponds to a **vacuum of approximately 95.4 kPa below atmospheric**, requiring industrial-grade vacuum systems in the cavitation tunnel.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rotating Arm Basin Angular Velocity

A rotating-arm basin simulates a steady turn of radius $R$. The ship experiences centrifugal acceleration:

$$
a_c = \omega^2 R
$$

**Step 1 — Equate model and ship accelerations (Fr-scaled):**

Acceleration scales as:

$$
a_m / a_s = L_m / L_s = \lambda
$$

$$
\omega_m^2 R_m = \lambda \cdot \omega_s^2 R_s
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\omega_m$:**

Since $R_m = \lambda R_s$:

$$
\omega_m^2 (\lambda R_s) = \lambda \omega_s^2 R_s
$$

$$
\omega_m^2 = \omega_s^2 \Rightarrow \omega_m = \omega_s
$$

Wait — this seems wrong. Let us re-examine. Under Froude similarity, time scales as $\sqrt{\lambda}$:

$$
\Delta t_m = \Delta t_s \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

Angular velocity is the inverse of time period, so:

$$
\omega_m = \frac{2\pi}{T_m} = \frac{2\pi}{T_s \sqrt{\lambda}} = \frac{\omega_s}{\sqrt{\lambda}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\omega_m = \omega_s / \sqrt{\lambda}}
$$

**Step 3 — Check centrifugal acceleration consistency:**

Model: $a_{c,m} = \omega_m^2 R_m = (\omega_s^2/\lambda)(\lambda R_s) = \omega_s^2 R_s$.

Ship: $a_{c,s} = \omega_s^2 R_s$.

Both are equal — confirming Froude similarity is preserved. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Facility Selection vs. Test Objective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix for Choosing the Correct Facility (KTU Examiner Reference)**

| **Test Objective** | **Primary Facility** | **Secondary Facility** | **Key Parameter Measured** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Resistance ($R_T$) vs. speed | Towing Tank | — | $R_T$ (N) via dynamometer |
| Wave pattern visualization | Towing Tank | Wave Basin | Wave elevation $\zeta(x,y)$ |
| Cavitation inception | Cavitation Tunnel | — | $\sigma_i$ via high-speed camera |
| Boundary layer / wake survey | CWC | Towing Tank | Velocity profiles via LDA/PIV |
| Seakeeping / RAO | Wave Basin | Wave Flume | Motion amplitudes $A(\omega)$ |
| Maneuvering / turning circle | Rotating Arm Basin | PMM | $Y_v, N_r, Y_r, N_v$ derivatives |
| Slamming / green water | Drop Tank | High-Speed Flume | Pressure impulse $I$ (Pa·s) |
| Ice-structure interaction | Ice Tank | — | Breaking force $F_{ice}$ |

---

## 3.5 Sample Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem — Towing Tank Resistance Prediction**
> A frigate of length $L_s = 120$ m cruises at $V_s = 15$ knots. A 1:30 model is tested in a towing tank.
> **Find:** (a) Model test speed. (b) If model resistance at this speed is $R_{T,m} = 12.5$ N, find ship resistance.

**Given:**
- $L_s = 120$ m, $V_s = 15$ knots $= 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716$ m/s
- $\lambda = L_m/L_s = 1/30$
- $R_{T,m} = 12.5$ N
- $\rho_m = \rho_s = 1000$ kg/m³

**Solution (a) — Model speed:**

$$
V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda} = 7.716 \times \sqrt{1/30}
$$

$$
V_m = 7.716 \times 0.1826 = 1.409 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Solution (b) — Ship resistance:**

$$
R_{T,s} = \frac{R_{T,m}}{\lambda^3} = \frac{12.5}{(1/30)^3} = 12.5 \times 27000
$$

$$
R_{T,s} = 337{,}500 \text{ N} \approx 337.5 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Error**
> Students frequently forget to convert **knots to m/s** (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s), and also confuse **$\lambda$ vs $1/\lambda$** when inverting the resistance formula. Marks are routinely lost on unit conversions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
