# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**List any three major marine hydrodynamic test facilities and state the primary measurement quantity associated with each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Towing Tank** — Primary measurement: **Total resistance ($R_T$)** of a ship model as a function of speed, measured using a dynamometer fixed to the towing carriage.
2. **Cavitation Tunnel** — Primary measurement: **Cavitation number ($\sigma$)** at inception on propeller blades, observed via high-speed photography through optical windows.
3. **Wave Basin** — Primary measurement: **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)** — the ratio of ship motion amplitude to incident wave amplitude, obtained from optical motion-tracking systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (KTU Key):** 1 mark for each facility + 1 mark each for the corresponding measurement = 3 marks.

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Explain the Froude scaling law for a ship model tested in a towing tank. Why is it preferred over Reynolds scaling for free-surface flows?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Froude scaling law** is based on matching the **Froude number** between model and ship:

$$
Fr_m = Fr_s \Rightarrow V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

It is **preferred over Reynolds scaling** for free-surface flows because:
- Wave-making phenomena are **gravity-dominated**, so the relevant ratio is inertia to gravity (Froude), not inertia to viscosity (Reynolds).
- It is **physically impossible** to match both Fr and Re simultaneously with the same fluid (water) unless extreme pressures or different fluids are used.
- Reynolds similarity would require model speeds so high that **cavitation and surface tension effects** would distort the flow.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a **towing tank** used for resistance tests on ship models. Mention typical dimensions and the role of the dynamometer. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A model of scale 1:25 is tested in a towing tank. The model resistance at $V_m = 2.5$ m/s is 18 N. Estimate the corresponding ship resistance, given that $L_s = 150$ m. State any assumptions made. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Solution to Question A(a) — Construction & Working of a Towing Tank**

A **towing tank** is a long, rectangular, water-filled basin with the following construction features:

**Structural Components:**
1. **Tank Shell** — Reinforced concrete or steel, with inner lining to minimize leakage. Typical dimensions: **length 100–350 m, width 3–14 m, depth 3–8 m**.
2. **Rails** — Precisely levelled steel rails along the tank walls, supporting the towing carriage.
3. **Towing Carriage** — Motor-driven bridge that travels on the rails, capable of speeds from **0.01 m/s to 10 m/s**.
4. **Dynamometer** — A force-measuring instrument (mechanical, hydraulic, or strain-gauge based) rigidly fixed between the carriage strut and the ship model.
5. **Wave Probe Array** — Capacitance or resistance-type probes distributed along the tank to record wave elevations.
6. **Beach (Wave Absorber)** — Sloped or perforated structure at the far end of the tank to absorb the model-generated waves and prevent reflection.
7. **Overtake Carriage (optional)** — A second carriage running ahead of the main carriage to absorb return-flow effects.
8. **Carriage Control System** — Closed-loop servo with tachogenerator feedback for accurate speed control (±0.1%).

**Working Principle:**
- The model is rigidly attached to the carriage via the dynamometer.
- The carriage accelerates to a preset speed and travels a measured distance.
- The dynamometer records the **horizontal towing force $R_T$**.
- Wave probes simultaneously record the **wave pattern** at fixed locations.
- **Sinkage and trim** are measured using linear potentiometers or non-contact optical sensors.
- The procedure is repeated at multiple speeds to obtain the **$R_T$–$V$ curve**, used to predict ship resistance.

**Mark Distribution (KTU Key):**
- Schematic + components: 3 marks
- Working principle: 2 marks
- Typical dimensions and dynamometer role: 2 marks

#### **Solution to Question A(b) — Resistance Estimation by Froude Scaling**

**Given:**
- Scale ratio $\lambda = L_m/L_s = 1/25$
- Model speed $V_m = 2.5$ m/s
- Model resistance $R_{T,m} = 18$ N
- Ship length $L_s = 150$ m

**Step 1 — Compute ship speed using Froude scaling:**

$$
V_s = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{1/25}} = \frac{2.5}{0.2} = 12.5 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to knots (optional but recommended):**

$$
V_s = 12.5 / 0.5144 = 24.30 \text{ knots}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ship resistance using $R_T$ scaling:**

$$
R_{T,s} = \frac{R_{T,m}}{\lambda^3} = \frac{18}{(1/25)^3} = 18 \times 15625
$$

$$
R_{T,s} = 281{,}250 \text{ N} = 281.25 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — State assumptions:**
1. Froude similarity is preserved (wave-making regime dominates).
2. Reynolds number effects are accounted for via ITTC-1978 friction correction.
3. Fluid is fresh water at 20°C for both model and ship ($\rho_m = \rho_s$).
4. No scale effects due to surface tension, since $L_m = 6$ m $\gg$ capillary length.
5. Tank walls and bottom have negligible boundary-layer blockage.

**Mark Distribution (KTU Key):**
- Correct formula application: 4 marks
- Numerical evaluation: 2 marks
- Assumptions: 1 mark

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Describe with a neat diagram the working of a **cavitation tunnel**. Derive the relation between model and ship test-section pressures required for cavitation similarity. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A ship propeller of diameter $D_s = 6$ m operates at $n_s = 1.2$ Hz in seawater at depth $h = 4$ m. A 1:6 model is tested in a cavitation tunnel. Compute (i) the model rotational speed for Froude similarity, and (ii) the required test-section pressure for cavitation similarity. Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $p_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa, $p_v = 2.34$ kPa. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Solution to Question B(a) — Cavitation Tunnel Working & Pressure Derivation**

**Working Description:**
A **cavitation tunnel** is a closed-loop water tunnel comprising:
1. **Test Section** — Transparent acrylic windows for optical observation.
2. **Contraction Nozzle** — Accelerates flow and reduces turbulence.
3. **Diffuser** — Decelerates flow and recovers pressure.
4. **Axial-flow Pump** — Drives the circulation.
5. **Flow Straighteners and Honeycomb** — Reduce turbulence intensity.
6. **Vacuum System** — Reduces test-section pressure below $p_v$.
7. **Throttle Valve** — Adjusts downstream pressure for fine control.
8. **Instrumentation** — Pressure transducers, high-speed cameras, LDA/PIV.

**Derivation of Pressure Relation (already shown in Section 3.2):**

Starting from $\sigma_m = \sigma_s$:

$$
(p_{\infty,m} - p_v) = \lambda (p_{\infty,s} - p_v)
$$

For a surface ship:

$$
p_{\infty,s} = p_{atm} + \rho g h
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{p_{\infty,m} = p_v + \lambda \left(p_{atm} + \rho g h - p_v\right)}
$$

#### **Solution to Question B(b) — Numerical Cavitation Similarity**

**Given:**
- $D_s = 6$ m, $n_s = 1.2$ Hz, $h = 4$ m, $\lambda = 1/6$
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $p_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa, $p_v = 2.34$ kPa

**Part (i) — Model rotational speed:**

For Froude similarity, time scales as $\sqrt{\lambda}$:

$$
\Delta t_m = \Delta t_s \sqrt{\lambda} \Rightarrow n_m = n_s / \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

$$
n_m = 1.2 / \sqrt{1/6} = 1.2 \times \sqrt{6} = 1.2 \times 2.449
$$

$$
n_m = 2.939 \text{ Hz} \approx 176.4 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Part (ii) — Required test-section pressure:**

$$
p_{\infty,s} = p_{atm} + \rho g h = 101300 + 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 4
$$

$$
p_{\infty,s} = 101300 + 40221 = 141{,}521 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
p_{\infty,m} = p_v + \lambda (p_{\infty,s} - p_v)
$$

$$
p_{\infty,m} = 2340 + \frac{1}{6} (141521 - 2340)
$$

$$
p_{\infty,m} = 2340 + \frac{139181}{6} = 2340 + 23197
$$

$$
p_{\infty,m} = 25{,}537 \text{ Pa (absolute)}
$$

This corresponds to a **vacuum of $101300 - 25537 = 75{,}763$ Pa below atmospheric**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not use $p_{atm}$ instead of $p_{\infty,s}$ in cavitation calculations.** Students commonly forget the hydrostatic term $\rho g h$, leading to wrong model pressures.
> 2. **The model rotational speed is HIGHER than ship speed** (since $n_m = n_s/\sqrt{\lambda}$ and $\lambda < 1$), not lower. Confusion here costs 2 marks instantly.
> 3. **Vacuum systems cannot achieve absolute zero pressure** — practical cavitation tunnels operate down to ~5–10 kPa absolute. If the computed $p_{\infty,m}$ is below this limit, the test is not feasible at that scale, and a larger model must be used.
> 4. **Always state assumptions explicitly** (fresh water vs seawater, isothermal vs adiabatic, atmospheric pressure variation).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Facility Classification:** Towing Tanks, CWC, Cavitation Tunnels, Wave Basins/Flumes, Rotating Arm Basins, Ice Tanks, High-Speed Flumes.
- **Primary Non-Dimensional Numbers:** $Fr = V/\sqrt{gL}$, $Re = \rho VL/\mu$, $\sigma = (p_\infty - p_v)/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2)$, $We = \rho V^2 L / \sigma_{s.t.}$, $St = fL/V$.
- **Froude Scaling Rules:** $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$, $\Delta t_m = \Delta t_s \sqrt{\lambda}$, $R_{T,m}/R_{T,s} = \rho_m/\rho_s \cdot \lambda^3$, $\omega_m = \omega_s / \sqrt{\lambda}$.
- **Towing Tank Dimensions:** Length 100–350 m, width 3–14 m, depth 3–8 m; carriage speed 0.01–10 m/s.
- **Cavitation Tunnel Requirement:** $p_{\infty,m} = p_v + \lambda(p_{atm} + \rho g h - p_v)$.
- **Wave Basin Components:** Wavemaker, basin, beach, moored model, wave probes, motion capture, data acquisition.
- **Rotating Arm Basin:** $\omega_m = \omega_s / \sqrt{\lambda}$; radius $R_m = \lambda R_s$; preserves $a_c$ automatically.
- **Boundary Conditions:** Deep water if $d/L > 0.5$; shallow water if $d/L < 0.05$.
- **Blockage Effect:** When model cross-section $> 5\%$ of tank cross-section, blockage corrections are mandatory.
- **ITTC 1978 Friction Line:** $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$ — used to extract residual resistance from total resistance.
- **Constant Conversion:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s; $g = 9.81$ m/s²; $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ (fresh), 1025 kg/m³ (sea).
- **Facility Selection Rule:** Resistance → Towing Tank; Cavitation → Cavitation Tunnel; Seakeeping → Wave Basin; Maneuvering → Rotating Arm Basin / PMM; Boundary Layer → CWC; Slamming → Drop Tank.
- **Scale Effect Caveat:** Froude similarity does NOT preserve Re — viscous scale effects must be corrected using ITTC formulations.
- **Cavitation Inception Criterion:** $\sigma \le \sigma_i$ where $\sigma_i$ depends on body geometry, surface finish, and nuclei content.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test Setup
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Test Setup

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Marine Hydrodynamics** and the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Experimental Techniques*, a **Test Setup** refers to the comprehensive, integrated arrangement of physical infrastructure, scaled geometric models, instrumentation, boundary control systems, and data acquisition hardware required to experimentally simulate, measure, and analyze the hydrodynamic behaviour of marine bodies (ship hulls, offshore platforms, propellers, hydrofoils, etc.) under controlled laboratory conditions.

A test setup must satisfy **dynamical similarity** with the prototype by preserving the relevant non-dimensional parameters (Reynolds, Froude, Weber, Cavitation numbers), and must provide **repeatable, instrumented, and well-bounded** flow conditions for valid data acquisition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> A test setup is NOT just the model. It is a *system* comprising:
> 1. Test facility (towing tank / circulating water channel / wave basin / cavitation tunnel)
> 2. Model (scaled, dynamically similar)
> 3. Mounting system (struts, sting, suspension)
> 4. Instrumentation (force balances, pressure transducers, LDA/PIV, wave probes)
> 5. Drive / Carriage system
> 6. Data Acquisition System (DAQ)
> 7. Boundary and environmental control

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to test how a new ship hull design performs in the ocean. You obviously cannot build a full-size ship and tow it through the Bay of Bengal every time you tweak the hull shape — that would cost crores of rupees and months of time. Instead, you build a small replica (a **scaled model**, say 1:30 scale) and test it inside a long, narrow swimming-pool-like channel filled with water — the **towing tank**. A motorised **carriage** runs along rails above the tank, pulling the model through the water (or the model stays still while water flows past it). Sensors attached to the model act like a "stethoscope", measuring forces, pressures, and motion.

This entire orchestrated arrangement — the tank + rails + carriage + model + sensors + cables + computer — is the **Test Setup**.

- The **tank** is the ocean's substitute.
- The **carriage** is the ship's engine and steering wheel.
- The **sensors** are the ship's instruments.
- The **rules of similarity** are the "translation dictionary" that converts small-scale results back to full-scale reality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metrics Used in Test Setup Design:**
> - Model scale ratio, $\lambda$ (typically **1:10 to 1:50** for ship models)
> - Maximum carriage speed, $V_{max}$ (commonly **3–8 m/s**)
> - Reynolds number, $Re$ (typically **$10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$**)
> - Froude number, $Fr$ (typically **0.1 to 0.6**)
> - Blockage ratio, $\varepsilon$ (must remain **< 5%** for reliable data)

## 1.3 Classification of Test Setups

| **Facility Type** | **Primary Use** | **Typical Dimensions** |
|---|---|---|
| Towing Tank (Planar) | Resistance, propulsion, maneuvering | 250–300 m long, 6–10 m wide |
| Circulating Water Channel | Cavitation, boundary layer studies | 1.5–3 m test section |
| Wave Basin | Wave-structure interaction, seakeeping | 30–60 m wide, 4–6 m deep |
| Cavitation Tunnel | Propeller, hydrofoil cavitation | 0.6–1.2 m throat diameter |
| Rotating Arm Basin | Free-running maneuvering tests | 30–40 m radius |
| Deep Water Towing Tank | Added resistance, slamming | 10–12 m depth |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy of a Test Setup

A well-designed test setup must obey three cardinal principles:

1. **Geometric Similarity** — every linear dimension scaled by the same ratio $\lambda$.
2. **Kinematic Similarity** — velocity ratios at corresponding points match the prototype.
3. **Dynamic Similarity** — ratios of all relevant forces (inertia, gravity, viscous, surface tension) match between model and prototype.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Achieving perfect dynamic similarity is *practically impossible* when more than one non-dimensional parameter governs the flow. Therefore, test setups are designed for **partial similarity**, preserving the dominant parameters and tolerating distortions in secondary ones.

## 2.2 Functional Subsystems of a Test Setup

### A. The Facility (Test Section)
- Long, narrow rectangular basin with smooth walls.
- Rails on top for the carriage to ride.
- Quiet settling region at the upstream end to dampen disturbances.
- Wave absorber beaches at the downstream end to prevent reflected waves contaminating the test.

### B. The Carriage / Drive System
- A steel bridge structure riding on precision rails.
- Towed by an electric motor (often a servo-controlled AC motor).
- Capable of constant velocity, acceleration, and deceleration profiles.
- Carries the dynamometer and the model-attachment hardware.

### C. The Model & Mounting System
- Model constructed from wood, fiberglass, or wax (paraffin) — must be **hydrodynamically smooth** and accurately faired.
- Attached to a **sting** (rear support) or **struts** (side supports).
- The sting houses the **force balance** that resolves the total force into components: $F_X$ (drag), $F_Y$ (side force), $F_Z$ (lift), and moments $M_X$, $M_Y$, $M_Z$.

### D. The Force Balance (Dynamometer)
- A precision-machined instrument that measures the **6 components of force and moment** transmitted from the model.
- Common types: **Strain-gauge balance**, **piezoelectric balance**, **mechanical beam balance**.
- Calibration performed by known dead-weight loading and moment application.

### E. The Instrumentation Chain
- **Pressure transducers** → hull surface pressures.
- **Wave probes** (capacitance / resistance type) → wave amplitude measurement.
- **LDA / PIV** → non-intrusive velocity field measurement.
- **Flow visualization** (dye injection, hydrogen bubbles, particle tracking).

### F. The Data Acquisition System (DAQ)
- High-speed analog-to-digital converters.
- Sampling rate typically **100 Hz – 10 kHz** depending on phenomenon.
- Real-time display, filtering, FFT analysis.

### G. The Environmental Control System
- Water temperature regulation (affects viscosity $\nu$ and thus $Re$).
- De-aeration system to prevent micro-bubbles disturbing boundary layer.
- Anti-algae UV treatment.

## 2.3 Scaling Laws Governing the Test Setup

### 2.3.1 Geometric Scaling
For a linear scale ratio $\lambda = L_m / L_p$ (model/prototype):

$$A_m = \lambda^2 A_p \quad \text{(area scales as } \lambda^2)$$

$$\nabla_m = \lambda^3 \nabla_p \quad \text{(volume scales as } \lambda^3)$$

### 2.3.2 Kinematic Scaling
Velocity scales as:

$$V_m = V_p \sqrt{\lambda} \quad \text{(Froude similarity)}$$

### 2.3.3 Dynamic Scaling
Force scales as:

$$F_m = \lambda^3 F_p \quad \text{(inertia-dominated Froude scaling)}$$

### 2.3.4 Time Scaling
$$t_m = t_p \sqrt{\lambda}$$

### 2.3.5 Blockage Effect Correction
When the model occupies a non-negligible fraction of the tank cross-section, the flow accelerates around it, increasing the apparent velocity. The blockage ratio is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{A_M}{A_T}$$

where $A_M$ is the model's frontal (mid-section) area and $A_T$ is the tank cross-sectional area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $\varepsilon > 5\%$, blockage correction **must** be applied. Otherwise the data is invalid for ship resistance prediction.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Meaning / Use** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number — viscous / inertia ratio | dimensionless |
| $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | Froude number — inertia / gravity ratio | dimensionless |
| $C_F = \dfrac{F}{0.5 \,\rho\, V^2 \, A}$ | Force coefficient (drag / lift) | dimensionless |
| $\varepsilon = \dfrac{A_M}{A_T}$ | Blockage ratio (must be $< 0.05$) | dimensionless |
| $C_T = \dfrac{R}{0.5 \,\rho\, V^2 \, S}$ | Total resistance coefficient on wetted surface | dimensionless |
| $\sigma = \dfrac{p_\infty - p_v}{0.5 \,\rho\, V^2}$ | Cavitation number | dimensionless |
| $St = \dfrac{f L}{V}$ | Strouhal number — vortex shedding | dimensionless |
| $V_m = V_p \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Froude-scaled model velocity | m/s |
| $R_m = R_p \, \lambda^3 \, (\rho_m / \rho_p)$ | Resistance scaling (equal fluid density) | N |
| $P = F \, V$ | Towing power required | W |
| $\Delta V_{corr} = V_m \left(1 + C \cdot \varepsilon\right)$ | Blockage-corrected velocity | m/s |
| $t_m = t_p \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Time scaling under Froude | s |
| $n_m = n_p \, \lambda^{-3/2}$ | Propeller RPM scaling (Froude) | rev/s |
| $C_P = \dfrac{P}{0.5 \,\rho\, n^3 D^5}$ | Propeller power coefficient | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exams, remember the **"$\lambda$ power rules"**:
> - Length: $\lambda^1$
> - Area: $\lambda^2$
> - Volume / Mass: $\lambda^3$
> - Velocity (Froude): $\lambda^{1/2}$
> - Time: $\lambda^{1/2}$
> - Force (Froude): $\lambda^3$
> - RPM: $\lambda^{-3/2}$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

A properly designed test setup is the **backbone of marine design**. Without it:
- CFD validation data would be unavailable.
- New hull forms cannot be optimized for fuel efficiency.
- Propeller cavitation and erosion cannot be predicted.
- Offshore platform responses to waves cannot be certified.
- IMO regulations on **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** and **EEXI** cannot be verified experimentally.

Test setups are used by ITTC member organizations (MARIN, HSVA, CSSRC, NMRI, etc.) and shipyards (Hyundai, Daewoo, Mazagon Dock) for full-form resistance and self-propulsion tests.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Froude Velocity Scaling Law

**Starting point:** Froude similarity between model (subscript m) and prototype (subscript p):

$$Fr_m = Fr_p$$

**Expanding both sides:**

$$\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g \, L_m}} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{g \, L_p}}$$

**Squaring both sides:**

$$\frac{V_m^{\,2}}{g \, L_m} = \frac{V_p^{\,2}}{g \, L_p}$$

**Cancelling $g$ (same on Earth):**

$$\frac{V_m^{\,2}}{L_m} = \frac{V_p^{\,2}}{L_p}$$

**Solving for $V_m$:**

$$V_m^{\,2} = V_p^{\,2} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_p}$$

$$V_m^{\,2} = V_p^{\,2} \cdot \lambda$$

**Taking square root:**

$$\boxed{\,V_m = V_p \, \sqrt{\lambda}\,}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Since the model is smaller ($\lambda < 1$), $V_m < V_p$ — we must tow the model *slower* than the ship's real speed to maintain wave-making similarity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force Scaling Under Froude Similarity

Force has dimensions $[F] = M \cdot L \cdot T^{-2}$.

**Step 1 — Express force via force coefficient:**

$$F = C_F \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A$$

For dynamic similarity, $C_{F,m} = C_{F,p}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute scaling relations:**

- $\rho_m = \rho_p$ (same water)
- $V_m = V_p \sqrt{\lambda}$
- $A_m = A_p \, \lambda^2$

**Step 3 — Build the ratio:**

$$\frac{F_m}{F_p} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_m V_m^{\,2} A_m}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_p V_p^{\,2} A_p} = \frac{V_m^{\,2} A_m}{V_p^{\,2} A_p}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_m$ and $A_m$:**

$$\frac{F_m}{F_p} = \frac{(V_p \sqrt{\lambda})^2 \cdot (\lambda^2 A_p)}{V_p^{\,2} \cdot A_p} = \frac{V_p^{\,2} \, \lambda \, \lambda^2 A_p}{V_p^{\,2} A_p}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\frac{F_m}{F_p} = \lambda^3$$

$$\boxed{\,F_m = \lambda^3 \, F_p\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Blockage Correction (Two-Dimensional Approximation)

When a body of frontal width $b$ sits in a channel of width $B$, the flow accelerates in the constricted region. The measured drag $R_{meas}$ is higher than the unbounded-flow drag $R_\infty$.

**Step 1 — Conservation of mass (per unit depth):**

$$V_\infty \cdot B = V_{throat} \cdot (B - b)$$

$$V_{throat} = \frac{V_\infty}{1 - \varepsilon} \quad \text{where} \quad \varepsilon = \frac{b}{B}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity at the model becomes:**

$$V_M = V_\infty \left(1 + \varepsilon + \varepsilon^2 + \cdots\right) \approx V_\infty (1 + \varepsilon)$$

**Step 3 — Drag in unbounded flow:**

$$R_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{\,2} \, A \cdot C_D$$

**Step 4 — Measured drag in confined flow:**

$$R_{meas} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_M^{\,2} \, A \cdot C_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{\,2}(1+\varepsilon)^2 A \cdot C_D$$

**Step 5 — Ratio:**

$$\frac{R_{meas}}{R_\infty} = (1 + \varepsilon)^2$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $R_\infty$:**

$$\boxed{\,R_\infty = \frac{R_{meas}}{(1 + \varepsilon)^2}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For three-dimensional blockage, a more accurate **Maskell correction** is preferred. The above derivation is the KTU-exam-style first-order approximation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Required by the Towing Carriage

The towing carriage must overcome the model's resistance at the test speed.

**Step 1 — Define mechanical power:**

$$P = F \cdot V$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the resistance force:**

$$P = R \cdot V_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Froude-scaled quantities:**

$$R = R_p \, \lambda^3 \quad ; \quad V_m = V_p \sqrt{\lambda}$$

**Step 4:**

$$P = R_p \, \lambda^3 \cdot V_p \sqrt{\lambda} = R_p V_p \, \lambda^{7/2}$$

**Step 5 — Since $P_p = R_p V_p$, the prototype shaft power:**

$$\boxed{\,P_m = P_p \, \lambda^{7/2}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** Froude scaling exponent on power = (length exponent) + (velocity exponent) = $3 + 1/2 = 7/2$. Verify by dimensional analysis: $[P] = M L^2 T^{-3} \Rightarrow \lambda^3 \cdot \lambda = \lambda^4$? No — the trick is using $R_p V_p$ which already carries units, so the residual $\lambda$ comes purely from the velocity scaling.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A ship of length $L_p = 200$ m, cruising at $V_p = 25$ knots, is to be tested at $\lambda = 1/40$. Find:
(a) Model speed
(b) Model length
(c) If prototype resistance $R_p = 2 \times 10^6$ N, find model resistance.
(d) Time to traverse 50 m at model scale, given prototype covers the equivalent in 30 s.

**Given:**
- $L_p = 200$ m, $V_p = 25$ knots, $\lambda = 1/40$

**(a) Model speed:**
$$V_m = V_p \sqrt{\lambda} = 25 \sqrt{1/40} = 25 \times 0.1581 = 3.953 \text{ knots}$$

**(b) Model length:**
$$L_m = L_p \cdot \lambda = 200 \times (1/40) = 5 \text{ m}$$

**(c) Model resistance:**
$$R_m = R_p \, \lambda^3 = (2 \times 10^6) \times (1/40)^3 = 2 \times 10^6 / 64000 = 31.25 \text{ N}$$

**(d) Time scaling:**
$$t_m = t_p \sqrt{\lambda} = 30 \times \sqrt{1/40} = 30 \times 0.1581 = 4.74 \text{ s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This problem pattern is a **favourite KTU exam question** — typically worth 7 marks when broken into sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a "test setup" in marine hydrodynamics. List any four essential components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A test setup is the integrated experimental arrangement used to study the hydrodynamic behaviour of marine bodies under controlled laboratory conditions while preserving dynamic similarity with the prototype.

Four essential components:
1. **Test facility** — e.g., towing tank, wave basin, or cavitation tunnel.
2. **Scaled geometric model** — dynamically similar to the prototype.
3. **Force measurement system** — strain-gauge balance / dynamometer.
4. **Data acquisition system** — transducers, amplifiers, ADC, computer.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for components = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is blockage ratio? Why must it be kept below 5% in towing tank tests?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The blockage ratio is defined as:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{A_M}{A_T}$$

where $A_M$ is the model's maximum frontal area and $A_T$ is the tank's cross-sectional area.

It must be kept below **5%** because higher blockage causes:
- Excessive flow acceleration around the model (choked flow effect).
- Increased wave reflection from tank walls.
- Distorted pressure field, leading to inaccurate drag and lift measurements.

Without correction, the measured resistance can be **20–40% higher** than the unbounded value, making the test data invalid for prototype prediction.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a towing tank test setup** for ship resistance experiments. Label all major subsystems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 m long ship is to be tested at 1:50 scale. If the prototype cruises at 20 knots, determine: **(7 marks)**
   (i) Model test speed
   (ii) Model length
   (iii) If prototype total resistance is $1.5 \times 10^6$ N, calculate the expected model resistance.
   (iv) If the prototype covers a distance equivalent to 100 m in 13 seconds, find the corresponding model time.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Block diagram description (7 marks):**

A towing tank test setup consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Towing tank facility** — long rectangular basin (typically 250–300 m × 6–10 m × 4–5 m deep) with smooth walls, glass viewing windows, wave absorber beach at the downstream end, and settling chamber at the upstream end. (1 mark)

2. **Carriage and drive system** — a steel bridge running on precision-ground rails at the top of the tank, driven by a servo-controlled electric motor capable of programmable constant-velocity, acceleration, and deceleration profiles. (1 mark)

3. **Force balance / dynamometer** — mounted on the carriage, holding the model via a sting; resolves total hydrodynamic force into drag, side force, lift, and three moments. (1 mark)

4. **Model and mounting** — geometrically scaled (1:50), faired hull made of wood or fiberglass, attached via rear sting or twin side struts. (1 mark)

5. **Instrumentation** — pressure transducers on hull, wave probes for wave amplitude, flow visualization cameras, LDA/PIV for velocity field. (1 mark)

6. **Data acquisition system (DAQ)** — signal conditioners, low-pass anti-alias filters, 16-bit ADC, computer with real-time display and storage. (1 mark)

7. **Environmental control** — water temperature regulation (typically 15–25 °C ± 0.5 °C), de-aeration to keep dissolved oxygen below saturation, anti-algae treatment. (1 mark)

*(Refer to diagrams in Section 4.1 and 4.2.)*

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L_p = 220$ m, $\lambda = 1/50$, $V_p = 20$ knots, $R_p = 1.5 \times 10^6$ N, $t_p = 13$ s

**(i) Model test speed (2 marks):**

$$V_m = V_p \sqrt{\lambda} = 20 \sqrt{1/50}$$

$$V_m = 20 \times 0.1414 = 2.828 \text{ knots}$$

Converting to m/s:

$$V_m = 2.828 \times 0.5144 = 1.455 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Model length (1 mark):**

$$L_m = L_p \cdot \lambda = 220 \times (1/50) = 4.4 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Model resistance (2 marks):**

$$R_m = R_p \cdot \lambda^3 = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times (1/50)^3$$

$$R_m = 1.5 \times 10^6 / 125000 = 12 \text{ N}$$

**(iv) Model time (2 marks):**

$$t_m = t_p \sqrt{\lambda} = 13 \times \sqrt{1/50} = 13 \times 0.1414 = 1.838 \text{ s}$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the **principle of dynamic similarity** as applied to the design of a marine hydrodynamics test setup. Explain why Froude scaling is preferred for surface ships while Reynolds scaling is preferred for fully submerged bodies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrofoil of chord $c = 0.6$ m is tested in a water channel at a speed such that $Re_c = 4 \times 10^6$. If a geometrically similar model at 1:5 scale is to be tested in air, find the required air-speed and the corresponding drag ratio, given: $\nu_{water} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\nu_{air} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Dynamic similarity (7 marks):**

Dynamic similarity requires that the ratios of all relevant forces acting on the model and prototype are equal. This is expressed through non-dimensional parameters being matched between model and prototype.

For a ship moving on the free surface, two dominant forces are **gravity** (creating waves) and **inertia**. The relevant non-dimensional number is the **Froude number**:

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$$

For a surface ship, wave-making resistance dominates at high Froude numbers, so Froude similarity must be preserved. However, Reynolds similarity cannot be simultaneously preserved because:

$$\frac{V_m}{V_p} = \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p} \quad \text{(Reynolds similarity)}$$
$$\frac{V_m}{V_p} = \sqrt{\lambda} \quad \text{(Froude similarity)}$$

Setting them equal: $\nu_m / \nu_p = \sqrt{\lambda}$, which requires a fluid whose viscosity scales as $\sqrt{\lambda}$. With water on both sides, this is impossible. Therefore, **Froude similarity is preferred for surface ships**, and viscous (frictional) resistance is estimated using the **ITTC 1978 friction line** and form-factor approach (1.5 marks).

For **fully submerged bodies** (submarines, hydrofoils, torpedoes), there is no free surface, so gravity and wave-making are absent. The dominant forces are inertia and viscosity, so the **Reynolds number**:

$$Re = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

must be matched. This is achieved by using air as the test fluid for small models, exploiting the higher kinematic viscosity of air to scale $V$ appropriately. (1.5 marks)

Additional discussion points (4 marks):
- Weber number similarity (for free-surface effects in cavitation)
- Cavitation number similarity (for cavitating flows)
- ITTC tolerances on $Fr$ and $Re$ mismatch (typically ±2% and ±10% respectively)

---

**(b) Hydrofoil numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $c_p = 0.6$ m, $Re_{c,p} = 4 \times 10^6$, $\lambda = 1/5$ (model = 1/5 of prototype)
- $\nu_{water} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\nu_{air} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Find prototype speed in water (1 mark):**

$$V_p = \frac{Re_{c,p} \cdot \nu_{water}}{c_p} = \frac{4 \times 10^6 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.6} = 6.667 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Model chord (0.5 marks):**

$$c_m = c_p \cdot \lambda = 0.6 / 5 = 0.12 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — For Reynolds similarity, $Re_{c,m} = Re_{c,p}$ (1 mark):**

$$V_m = \frac{Re_{c,m} \cdot \nu_{air}}{c_m} = \frac{4 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.12}$$

$$V_m = \frac{60}{0.12} = 500 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Drag ratio using $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A C_D$, and $C_D$ preserved under Re similarity (2 marks):**

$$\frac{F_m}{F_p} = \frac{\rho_m V_m^{\,2} A_m}{\rho_p V_p^{\,2} A_p}$$

$$= \frac{1.225 \times (500)^2 \times (0.12)^2}{1000 \times (6.667)^2 \times (0.6)^2}$$

$$= \frac{1.225 \times 250000 \times 0.0144}{1000 \times 44.44 \times 0.36}$$

$$= \frac{4410}{16000} = 0.2756$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (2.5 marks):**

The model drag is approximately **27.6%** of the prototype drag. This sub-linear scaling reflects the use of air (lower $\rho$) to achieve the same Reynolds number at a much higher velocity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often confuse $V_m = V_p \sqrt{\lambda}$ (Froude) with Reynolds similarity. Remember — for submerged bodies use $Re$ matching, NOT Froude.
> 2. Many forget to use **air properties** when the test fluid changes — always re-evaluate $\nu$ and $\rho$.
> 3. The drag ratio formula must include BOTH the $\rho$ AND $V^2$ ratios; partial credit is awarded if at least one is correct.
> 4. Unit conversion: **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s** — losing 1 mark for wrong unit conversion is the #1 killer on these problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Test Setup** = Facility + Model + Mounting + Instrumentation + DAQ + Control. Memorize the **7 subsystems**.
- [ ] **Geometric similarity** uses $\lambda$; **Froude** uses $\sqrt{\lambda}$; **Reynolds** uses $\nu$ matching.
- [ ] Force scales as $\lambda^3$, Velocity as $\sqrt{\lambda}$ (Froude), Time as $\sqrt{\lambda}$ (Froude).
- [ ] Power scales as $\lambda^{7/2}$ under Froude.
- [ ] **Blockage ratio $\varepsilon$** must be $< 5\%$, otherwise apply $(1+\varepsilon)^2$ correction.
- [ ] For surface ships → **Froude similarity**; for submerged bodies → **Reynolds similarity**.
- [ ] Reynolds number is preserved by changing test fluid (e.g., water → air for hydrofoils).
- [ ] **ITTC tolerances:** $Fr$ match within ±2%, $Re$ match within ±10%.
- [ ] Force balance measures 6 components: $F_X, F_Y, F_Z, M_X, M_Y, M_Z$.
- [ ] Wave probes → free surface; pressure transducers → local pressure; LDA/PIV → velocity field.
- [ ] Standard ship model scale = **1:30 to 1:50**.
- [ ] **DAUs sampling rate:** 100 Hz (slow manoeuvres) to 10 kHz (cavitation / slamming).
- [ ] **Blockage corrected drag:** $R_\infty = R_{meas} / (1+\varepsilon)^2$.
- [ ] **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s** — write this on your cheat sheet.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis and Conclusions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis and Conclusions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Experimental Analysis** in marine hydrodynamics refers to the systematic post-processing, statistical evaluation, and physical interpretation of raw data acquired from model-scale tests (towing tank, circulating water tunnel, wave basin, or cavitation tunnel). It encompasses the transformation of measured quantities — forces, velocities, pressures, wave heights, and moments — into **dimensionless coefficients** (e.g., $C_T$, $C_F$, $C_L$, $C_M$, $C_P$) suitable for full-scale prediction, while rigorously quantifying the associated **uncertainty bands**.

**Conclusions** constitute the validated engineering statements derived from this analysis, which establish the relationship between independent variables (e.g., $Re$, $Fr$, $\sigma$, $K_T$) and dependent response variables, including the documentation of **scale effects**, **blockage corrections**, and the **limits of validity** of the experimental campaign.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) guidelines, the **Analysis and Conclusions** stage directly maps to **CO3 (Apply experimental techniques to interpret hydrodynamic data)** and **CO4 (Develop valid engineering conclusions from model tests)**. This module is the highest-weightage cognitive area in Module 3.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a medical laboratory technician running a patient's blood test. The raw data is a long list of numbers on a printout — meaningless to a patient. The **analysis** stage is when the technician (i) plots the numbers against normal reference ranges, (ii) calculates averages, standard deviations, and confidence intervals, (iii) checks for outliers (systematic errors), and (iv) applies instrument-specific correction factors (calibration curves).

The **conclusion** is the doctor's final diagnosis: *"Based on this patient's cholesterol level being $X$ mg/dL with a 95% confidence interval of $[X_1, X_2]$, and after applying age and instrument corrections, the patient is at moderate cardiovascular risk."*

In **marine hydrodynamics**, the "patient" is the ship or marine structure, the "blood test" is the **resistance test** or **propulsion test** in the towing tank, and the "diagnosis" is the predicted full-scale resistance/power — with an **uncertainty band** clearly stated.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard quantities are foundational to all analysis work in this module:

- **Density of fresh water:** $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of sea water:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity of fresh water at 20°C:** $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Standard ITTC 1978 correlation line constant:** $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$
- **Standard uncertainty reporting level:** **95% confidence interval** ($k = 2$ for normal distribution)

> [!NOTE]
> Every dimensionless coefficient reported in KTU lab reports and examinations MUST be accompanied by its defining non-dimensional group. Marks are routinely deducted for "naked" numerical values without proper non-dimensionalization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Analytical Workflow

The transition from raw towing-tank data to a publishable ship hydrodynamic conclusion follows a strict, sequential protocol:

### Stage 1 — Data Validation and Screening
- Reject transient startup/shutdown portions of the time-history signal.
- Verify calibration constants (load cell zeros, pressure transducer spans) at the start and end of each run.
- Check for **repeatability** between successive runs at identical test conditions (deviation should be $< 2\%$ for forces, $< 5\%$ for free-surface measurements).

### Stage 2 — Coordinate Transformation and Reference Frame Alignment
- Correct force/moment readings for **tare** (zero-offset drift).
- Apply **alignment corrections** so that the model centerline coincides with the tank centerline.
- Convert to the standard **ship-fixed coordinate system**: $X$ forward, $Y$ to port, $Z$ downward.

### Stage 3 — Non-Dimensionalization
- Convert measured forces $R$ to resistance coefficient $C_T = \dfrac{R}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2}$.
- Convert measured thrust $T$ to thrust coefficient $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$.
- Convert torque $Q$ to torque coefficient $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$.

### Stage 4 — Statistical Treatment
- Compute **mean** $\bar{x}$, **standard deviation** $s$, and **standard error of the mean** $SE = s/\sqrt{N}$.
- Apply **outlier rejection** using Chauvenet's criterion or Grubbs' test.
- Report expanded uncertainty $U = k \cdot u_c$ at **95% confidence**.

### Stage 5 — Physical Interpretation and Extrapolation
- Compare $C_T$ with ITTC-78 correlation line.
- Compute **form factor** $k$ from Prohaska method or Schoenherr method.
- Apply **roughness allowance** $\Delta C_F$ and **scale-effect correction** $(1+k)$.
- Predict full-scale power via the **IATP-1978 procedure**.

## 2.2 Why Each Stage Matters — Engineering Justification

| Stage | Engineering "Why" |
|---|---|
| 1 — Screening | Removes **non-stationary bias** from acceleration/deceleration phases. |
| 2 — Transformation | Ensures **vector consistency** between model and prototype. |
| 3 — Non-dimensionalization | Enables **Reynolds-number similarity** to be checked explicitly. |
| 4 — Statistics | Quantifies the **engineering confidence** of the result. |
| 5 — Extrapolation | Provides the **design-relevant** full-scale prediction. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the KTU Module 3 examination. Memorize all entries and the associated units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_T = \dfrac{R}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2}$ | Total resistance coefficient | $S$ = wetted surface area $[\text{m}^2]$ |
| 2 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$ | ITTC-1978 frictional line | $Re = VL/\nu$ |
| 3 | $C_R = C_T - C_F$ | Residual resistance coefficient | — |
| 4 | $k = \dfrac{C_T}{C_F} - 1$ at low $Fr$ | Form factor (Prohaska plot) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Re = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | $L$ = length $[\text{m}]$ |
| 6 | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude number | gravity-based |
| 7 | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | Thrust coefficient | $D$ = propeller dia. $[\text{m}]$ |
| 8 | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ | Torque coefficient | $Q$ in N·m |
| 9 | $J = \dfrac{V_A}{nD}$ | Advance coefficient | — |
| 10 | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}$ | Propulsive efficiency (open water) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\sigma = \dfrac{n^2 D^4}{A_E^{1.5} \cdot g}$ | Cavitation number (Ekpoudja) | — |
| 12 | $C_P = \dfrac{p - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2}$ | Pressure coefficient | $p_v$ = vapor pressure |
| 13 | $U = k_c \cdot u_c$ | Expanded uncertainty | $k_c = 2$ (95% conf.) |
| 14 | $u_c = \sqrt{\sum_i \left(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} u_i\right)^2}$ | Combined standard uncertainty (GUM) | root-sum-of-squares |
| 15 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta C_F^{\text{rough}}}{C_F^{\text{smooth}}}$ | Roughness allowance (ITTC-78) | from $\Delta C_F$ table |
| 16 | $C_{A} = 0.006 (\nabla / L^3)^{-3} \cdot 10^{-3}$ | Correlation allowance | $\nabla$ = displacement $[\text{m}^3]$ |
| 17 | $C_{\text{ship}} = (1+k) C_{F,s} + C_A + C_R$ | Ship resistance coefficient | full-scale ITTC-78 |
| 18 | $\tau = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = Re$ | Skin-friction Reynolds (alt.) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions for valid use of the ITTC-78 line:** $Re \geq 10^6$, smooth model surface, fully turbulent boundary layer, no significant laminar-turbulent transition effects in the test $Re$ range. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can identify **when the ITTC correlation is not applicable** (e.g., for very small models, $Re < 10^6$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The analytical framework above is the backbone of:

- **Ship design contracts:** Predicting EHP/SHP for newbuild tankers, bulk carriers, and naval vessels.
- **Propeller design:** Determining open-water characteristics ($K_T$, $K_Q$ vs. $J$) for B-Series, Kaplan, or custom propellers.
- **Seakeeping assessment:** RAO (Response Amplitude Operator) extraction from wave-basin tests.
- **Cavitation warranty:** Comparing measured $C_P_{\min}$ with contractual cavitation limits.
- **Offshore engineering:** Mooring line tension analysis, VIV (vortex-induced vibration) risk assessment.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Form Factor $k$ from Prohaska's Method

Prohaska's method is the **canonical KTU examination question** for the "Analysis" stage. It extracts the form factor $k$ from resistance data taken at multiple speeds, exploiting the linear relationship that holds at low $Fr$:

$$C_T = C_F + C_R$$

Using the ITTC-78 line for $C_F$, the residual resistance $C_R$ is plotted against $Fr^4$ (or $Fr^5$ for slender forms). The $y$-intercept of the best-fit line (extrapolated to $Fr \to 0$) gives $C_F^{\text{ship}} + k \cdot C_F^{\text{model}}$, from which $k$ is solved.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Begin with the ITTC-78 decomposition** of total resistance coefficient:

$$C_T = C_F^{\text{model}} \cdot (1+k) + C_R$$

**Step 2 — For low $Fr$**, residual resistance scales empirically as:

$$C_R = a \cdot Fr^n$$

where the exponent $n$ is typically $4$ for slender merchant forms and $5$ for finer forms. For this derivation, we adopt $n = 4$ (most common KTU assumption for a Series-60 or similar form):

$$C_R = a \cdot Fr^4$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:**

$$C_T = C_F^{\text{model}} \cdot (1+k) + a \cdot Fr^4$$

**Step 4 — Define the "Prohaska y-axis"** by subtracting the frictional component. Let:

$$Y \equiv C_T - C_F^{\text{model}}$$

Then:

$$Y = k \cdot C_F^{\text{model}} + a \cdot Fr^4$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the linear structure** in $Fr^4$:

$$Y = k \cdot C_F^{\text{model}} + a \cdot Fr^4$$

Since $C_F^{\text{model}}$ is a function of $Re$ and hence of $V$ (and thus of $Fr$), the abscissa is taken as $C_F^{\text{model}}$ and the parameter is $Fr^4$ — meaning we perform a **two-variable linear regression** of the form:

$$Y = \alpha \cdot C_F^{\text{model}} + \beta \cdot Fr^4$$

**Step 6 — Identification of coefficients** by least-squares fitting:

$$\alpha = k, \quad \beta = a$$

**Step 7 — Solve for form factor:**

$$k = \alpha = \text{slope of best-fit plane along the } C_F^{\text{model}} \text{ axis}$$

For a single speed where $Fr \to 0$ (low-speed extrapolation), the equation collapses to:

$$k = \frac{C_T - C_F^{\text{model}}}{C_F^{\text{model}}}$$

**Step 8 — Validate dimensional consistency** — both $C_T$ and $C_F$ are dimensionless, hence $k$ is dimensionless. $\checkmark$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for the KTU exam:** The Prohaska method is a **14-mark question** in virtually every ESE cycle. The examiner expects you to (i) state the assumption that $C_R = a Fr^n$, (ii) derive the working equation, (iii) describe the graphical/extrapolation procedure, and (iv) explicitly write the form factor formula. Skipping the assumption statement loses 2 marks.

## 3.2 Derivation: Expanded Uncertainty per ISO/IEC Guide (GUM)

The **Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement (GUM)** framework is the KTU-mandated standard for reporting experimental confidence. Let us derive the combined standard uncertainty for a derived quantity.

**Step 1 — Define the measurand** $Y$ as a function of independent inputs $X_i$:

$$Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_N)$$

For example, for resistance coefficient:

$$C_T = f(\rho, S, V, R) = \frac{2R}{\rho S V^2}$$

**Step 2 — Identify each input uncertainty** $u(x_i)$ as either Type A (statistical, from repeated observations) or Type B (judgmental, from calibration certificates, etc.).

**Step 3 — Apply the linearized propagation law** (first-order Taylor expansion, valid when uncertainties are small):

$$u_c^2(y) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right)^2 u^2(x_i)$$

**Step 4 — Compute partial derivatives for the $C_T$ example:**

$$\frac{\partial C_T}{\partial R} = \frac{2}{\rho S V^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial C_T}{\partial \rho} = -\frac{2R}{\rho^2 S V^2} = -\frac{C_T}{\rho}$$

$$\frac{\partial C_T}{\partial S} = -\frac{2R}{\rho S^2 V^2} = -\frac{C_T}{S}$$

$$\frac{\partial C_T}{\partial V} = -\frac{4R}{\rho S V^3} = -\frac{2 C_T}{V}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the combined standard uncertainty:**

$$u_c^2(C_T) = \left(\frac{2}{\rho S V^2}\right)^2 u^2(R) + \left(\frac{C_T}{\rho}\right)^2 u^2(\rho) + \left(\frac{C_T}{S}\right)^2 u^2(S) + \left(\frac{2 C_T}{V}\right)^2 u^2(V)$$

**Step 6 — Compute the expanded uncertainty** at 95% confidence (coverage factor $k = 2$):

$$U_{95}(C_T) = 2 \cdot u_c(C_T)$$

**Step 7 — Report the result** in the standard KTU/ITTC format:

$$C_T = c \pm U_{95}(C_T)$$

where $c$ is the best estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common student error:** Using **relative uncertainties** $u(x_i)/x_i$ without squaring the partial derivative. The correct propagation uses the **absolute** form shown above. Mixing relative and absolute terms in the same RSS is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction in 14-mark problems.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Full Analysis Chain

A Series-60 model ($C_B = 0.60$, $L = 6.0$ m, $S = 4.85$ m²) is tested in fresh water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s) at $V = 1.85$ m/s, giving a measured resistance $R = 14.2$ N. Compute the total resistance coefficient and identify the residual component.

**Step 1 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{VL}{\nu} = \frac{1.85 \times 6.0}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.106 \times 10^7$$

**Step 2 — ITTC-78 frictional coefficient:**

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.106 \times 10^7) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7.0436 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{25.466} = 2.945 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Total resistance coefficient:**

$$C_T = \frac{2R}{\rho S V^2} = \frac{2 \times 14.2}{1000 \times 4.85 \times (1.85)^2} = \frac{28.4}{16601.13} = 1.711 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Residual resistance coefficient (assuming $k = 0.18$ from Prohaska plot):**

$$C_R = C_T - (1+k) C_F = 1.711 \times 10^{-3} - 1.18 \times 2.945 \times 10^{-3} = 1.711 \times 10^{-3} - 3.475 \times 10^{-3}$$

Note the **negative value**: this is a physically nonsensical result that signals the assumed $k$ is too high, OR the ITTC-78 line does not apply at this $Re$. In practice, $C_R$ is always non-negative.

**Step 5 — Correct interpretation:**

$$C_R = 1.711 \times 10^{-3} - 3.475 \times 10^{-3} = -1.764 \times 10^{-3}$$

The conclusion is that **$k$ must be re-estimated from the full data set, not assumed**. A correct $k$ for Series-60 ($C_B = 0.60$) typically lies in $[0.12, 0.20]$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Method

| Question being asked | Recommended method | KTU marks weightage |
|---|---|---|
| What is $k$? | Prohaska plot, Schoenherr method | 7 |
| How repeatable is my data? | Standard deviation + Grubbs' test | 3 |
| Is there a blockage effect? | Comparison with ITtTC blockage correction | 3 |
| What is the full-scale $C_T$? | ITTC-78 with $(1+k)$ and $C_A$ | 7 |
| What is propeller $\eta_0$? | $K_T$-$K_Q$-$J$ regression | 3 |
| Did cavitation occur? | $C_P_{\min}$ vs. cavitation criterion | 3 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Analysis-and-Conclusion Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end flow** of data from a model test through to a validated engineering conclusion. This is the KTU-mandated mental model for the "Analysis and Conclusions" stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Towing Tank Data: R, V, t, n] --> B[Stage 1: Data Validation]
    B --> B1{Repeatability Check}
    B1 -->|Deviation within 2 percent| C[Stage 2: Tare and Alignment Correction]
    B1 -->|Deviation exceeds 2 percent| B2[Re-run Test]
    B2 --> A
    C --> D[Stage 3: Non-Dimensionalization]
    D --> D1[Compute C_T from R]
    D --> D2[Compute C_F via ITTC-78]
    D --> D3[Compute C_R = C_T minus C_F]
    D1 --> E[Stage 4: Form Factor Extraction]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Prohaska Plot]
    E --> E2[Schoenherr Plot]
    E1 --> F[Stage 5: Statistical Treatment]
    E2 --> F
    F --> F1[Compute Mean, Std Dev]
    F --> F2[Apply GUM Uncertainty Propagation]
    F --> F3[95 percent Confidence Interval]
    F1 --> G[Stage 6: Full-Scale Extrapolation]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Apply 1+k and C_A Correction]
    G --> G2[Compute Effective Horsepower]
    G1 --> H[Stage 7: Conclusions]
    G2 --> H
    H --> H1[Statement of Design Prediction]
    H --> H2[Statement of Uncertainty Band]
    H --> H3[Statement of Validity Limits]
    H1 --> I[Final KTU Report Section]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Uncertainty Propagation Hierarchy

This diagram shows how **Type A** (statistical) and **Type B** (calibration) uncertainties feed into the final expanded uncertainty for a typical resistance-coefficient measurement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TypeA[Type A Uncertainties - Statistical]
        A1[Run-to-run repeatability of R]
        A2[Wave reflection effects]
        A3[Temperature drift of nu]
    end
    subgraph TypeB[Type B Uncertainties - Calibration]
        B1[Load cell calibration certificate]
        B2[Speed encoder resolution]
        B3[Model length and scale error]
    end
    TypeA --> C[Standard Uncertainties u_x_i]
    TypeB --> C
    C --> D[Linearized Partial Derivatives df by dx_i]
    D --> E[Root Sum of Squares RSS]
    E --> F[Combined Standard Uncertainty u_c]
    F --> G[Expanded Uncertainty U at k=2]
    G --> H[Final Report: C_T = value plus minus U at 95 percent]
```

## 4.3 Process Diagram: Prohaska Method Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Collect Resistance Data at Minimum 5 Speeds] --> P2[Compute C_T at Each Speed]
    P2 --> P3[Compute C_F via ITTC-78 at Each Speed]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Y = C_T minus C_F]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Fr at Each Speed]
    P5 --> P6[Two-Variable Regression Y = alpha C_F + beta Fr^4]
    P6 --> P7[Identify alpha as Form Factor k]
    P6 --> P8[Identify beta as Residual Coeff a]
    P7 --> P9[Validate: k lies in expected Range 0.10 to 0.25]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10{Validation Pass}
    P10 -->|Yes| P11[Use k for Full-Scale Extrapolation]
    P10 -->|No| P12[Investigate: Boundary Layer Trip, Surface Roughness, Blockage]
    P12 --> P1
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: ITTC-78 vs. Schoenherr

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Schoenherr Method] --> S2[Iterative Solve: 0.242 over sqrt C_F = log_10 Re C_F]
    S2 --> S3[Plot C_F vs Re: Implicit Form]
    S3 --> S4[Extract C_F at Model Re]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Form Factor k Same as Prohaska]
    I1[ITTC-78 Method] --> I2[Explicit Formula C_F = 0.075 over log Re minus 2 squared]
    I2 --> I3[Direct Substitution of Re]
    I3 --> I4[Compute Form Factor k Same as Prohaska]
    S5 --> C[Common End: Form Factor k]
    I4 --> C
    C --> D[Apply to Full-Scale C_T]
```
