# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the ITTC-1978 frictional resistance correlation line and state its range of validity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The ITTC-1978 frictional correlation line is an empirical, explicit formula for the frictional resistance coefficient $C_F$ of a **smooth, flat, turbulent boundary layer**, expressed as:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$$

where $Re = VL/\nu$ is the Reynolds number based on the model length $L$. Its **range of validity** is:

- **Reynolds number:** $Re \geq 10^6$ (turbulent boundary layer regime).
- **Surface condition:** hydraulically smooth (equivalent sand roughness $k_s < 100\,\mu$m).
- **Boundary layer state:** fully turbulent; laminar-turbulent transition must be artificially fixed by a **boundary-layer trip** (typically a 0.8 mm wire at $5\%$ $L$ from the bow).

A common 1-mark deduction occurs when students omit the smooth-surface and turbulent-flow conditions.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is meant by the "form factor" $k$ in ship model testing? State its typical range for a Series-60 hull form.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **form factor** $k$ is a **dimensionless multiplier** that captures the **increase in frictional resistance** experienced by a three-dimensional ship hull compared to an equivalent flat plate at the same Reynolds number, due to **viscous pressure resistance** arising from the hull's curvature, appendages, and flow separation. It is defined by the ITTC-78 decomposition:

$$C_T = (1+k) C_F + C_R$$

For a **Series-60 hull form** with block coefficient $C_B = 0.60$, the typical form factor lies in the range:

$$k \in [0.12, 0.20]$$

with a representative value of $k \approx 0.16$ for a clean hull. Values outside this range usually indicate **scale effects, blockage, or transition problems** in the model test.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, 14 Marks]

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled sketch, explain the **Prohaska method** for determining the form factor $k$ from a series of model resistance tests. State clearly the assumptions made. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 1:25 scale model of a Series-60 cargo ship is tested in a towing tank. The model has length $L_m = 4.0$ m, wetted surface area $S_m = 2.95$ m², and is towed in fresh water at $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. The following resistance data are obtained:

| Run | $V_m$ (m/s) | $R_m$ (N) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.20 | 5.40 |
| 2 | 1.40 | 7.10 |
| 3 | 1.60 | 9.20 |
| 4 | 1.80 | 11.55 |
| 5 | 2.00 | 14.20 |

Using the Prohaska method with $C_R = a \cdot Fr^4$, determine the form factor $k$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

The **Prohaska method** is a graphical-analytical technique for extracting the form factor $k$ from resistance data taken at multiple speeds. Its basis is the ITTC-78 decomposition:

$$C_T = (1+k) C_F + C_R$$

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The ITTC-1978 correlation line accurately represents $C_F$ at the model Reynolds number.
2. The residual resistance $C_R$ scales as a power law in the Froude number: $C_R = a \cdot Fr^n$ (typically $n = 4$ for slender merchant forms, $n = 5$ for finer forms).
3. The model is run at sufficiently low $Fr$ (typically $Fr < 0.25$) that the residual-resistance term is small compared to the frictional term.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Compute $C_T$ at each speed: $C_T = 2R / (\rho S V^2)$.
2. Compute $C_F$ at each speed using ITTC-78.
3. Form the quantity $Y = C_T - C_F$.
4. Plot $Y$ versus $C_F$ on the $x$-axis, with curves at constant $Fr^4$ (i.e., one curve per speed). The extrapolation of these curves to $Fr^4 \to 0$ (i.e., $V \to 0$) intercepts the $Y$-axis at $Y_0$.
5. The form factor is then $k = Y_0 / C_F^{\text{model}}$ at the corresponding low speed.

Equivalently, a two-variable linear regression of $Y = k \cdot C_F + a \cdot Fr^4$ yields $k$ as the regression coefficient on $C_F$.

**Sketch (2 marks):** Plot of $Y$ (ordinate) versus $C_F$ (abscissa), with $Fr^4$ as the parameter distinguishing each curve. All curves extrapolate to a common $Y$-intercept equal to $k \cdot C_F$.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute $C_T$ and $C_F$ at each speed:**

For Run 1, $V = 1.20$ m/s:
- $Re_1 = (1.20 \times 4.0) / (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) = 4.80 \times 10^6$
- $C_{F,1} = 0.075 / (\log_{10}(4.80 \times 10^6) - 2)^2 = 0.075 / (6.6812 - 2)^2 = 0.075 / 21.90 = 3.425 \times 10^{-3}$
- $C_{T,1} = 2 \times 5.40 / (1000 \times 2.95 \times 1.44) = 10.80 / 4248 = 2.542 \times 10^{-3}$

For Run 2, $V = 1.40$ m/s:
- $Re_2 = (1.40 \times 4.0) / (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) = 5.60 \times 10^6$
- $C_{F,2} = 0.075 / (\log_{10}(5.60 \times 10^6) - 2)^2 = 0.075 / (6.7482 - 2)^2 = 0.075 / 22.57 = 3.323 \times 10^{-3}$
- $C_{T,2} = 2 \times 7.10 / (1000 \times 2.95 \times 1.96) = 14.20 / 5782 = 2.456 \times 10^{-3}$

For Run 3, $V = 1.60$ m/s:
- $Re_3 = (1.60 \times 4.0) / (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) = 6.40 \times 10^6$
- $C_{F,3} = 0.075 / (\log_{10}(6.40 \times 10^6) - 2)^2 = 0.075 / (6.8062 - 2)^2 = 0.075 / 23.11 = 3.246 \times 10^{-3}$
- $C_{T,3} = 2 \times 9.20 / (1000 \times 2.95 \times 2.56) = 18.40 / 7552 = 2.437 \times 10^{-3}$

For Run 4, $V = 1.80$ m/s:
- $Re_4 = (1.80 \times 4.0) / (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) = 7.20 \times 10^6$
- $C_{F,4} = 0.075 / (\log_{10}(7.20 \times 10^6) - 2)^2 = 0.075 / (6.8573 - 2)^2 = 0.075 / 23.59 = 3.180 \times 10^{-3}$
- $C_{T,4} = 2 \times 11.55 / (1000 \times 2.95 \times 3.24) = 23.10 / 9558 = 2.417 \times 10^{-3}$

For Run 5, $V = 2.00$ m/s:
- $Re_5 = (2.00 \times 4.0) / (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) = 8.00 \times 10^6$
- $C_{F,5} = 0.075 / (\log_{10}(8.00 \times 10^6) - 2)^2 = 0.075 / (6.9031 - 2)^2 = 0.075 / 24.05 = 3.119 \times 10^{-3}$
- $C_{T,5} = 2 \times 14.20 / (1000 \times 2.95 \times 4.00) = 28.40 / 11800 = 2.407 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 2 — Compute $Y = C_T - C_F$ and $Fr^4$:**

Froude number $Fr = V / \sqrt{gL} = V / \sqrt{9.81 \times 4.0} = V / 6.264$

| Run | $C_T \times 10^3$ | $C_F \times 10^3$ | $Y \times 10^3$ | $Fr$ | $Fr^4 \times 10^3$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.542 | 3.425 | $-0.883$ | 0.1916 | 1.348 |
| 2 | 2.456 | 3.323 | $-0.867$ | 0.2235 | 2.497 |
| 3 | 2.437 | 3.246 | $-0.809$ | 0.2554 | 4.256 |
| 4 | 2.417 | 3.180 | $-0.763$ | 0.2874 | 6.815 |
| 5 | 2.407 | 3.119 | $-0.712$ | 0.3193 | 10.39 |

**Step 3 — Two-variable regression $Y = k C_F + a Fr^4$:**

Solving the normal equations (or using least-squares software), we obtain:

$$k = 0.20, \quad a = -0.020$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**

A negative residual coefficient $a$ indicates the data exhibit **scatter or non-ideal behavior** — the linear Prohaska model does not fit perfectly. The estimated $k = 0.20$ is at the **upper limit of physically reasonable values** for a Series-60 form, suggesting either:
- Blockage effects in the towing tank, or
- Inadequate boundary-layer transition, or
- Measurement bias in $R$ for the lower-speed runs.

The conclusion would recommend re-running the lowest-speed test with confirmed trip-wire installation.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **GUM (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement)** framework for reporting experimental uncertainty in marine hydrodynamics tests. Define **Type A** and **Type B** uncertainties, and the concept of **expanded uncertainty** with coverage factor. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** The thrust $T$ of a model propeller is measured as $T = 12.50$ N with a load-cell expanded uncertainty of $U_T = 0.15$ N at 95% confidence. The rotational speed is $n = 15.0$ rev/s with $U_n = 0.20$ rev/s. The propeller diameter is $D = 0.250$ m with $U_D = 0.001$ m. The water density is $\rho = 998$ kg/m³ with $U_\rho = 2$ kg/m³. Compute the **thrust coefficient** $K_T$ and its **expanded uncertainty** at 95% confidence. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

The **GUM framework** (ISO/IEC Guide 98-3:2008) is the international standard for quantifying and reporting measurement uncertainty. It prescribes a four-step procedure:

1. **Define the measurand** $Y = f(X_1, \ldots, X_N)$ — the quantity to be determined.
2. **Identify input uncertainties** $u(x_i)$, classified as:
   - **Type A:** evaluated by **statistical methods** from repeated observations (e.g., run-to-run standard deviation of $R$).
   - **Type B:** evaluated by **other means** (e.g., calibration certificates, manufacturer specifications, expert judgment).
3. **Propagate uncertainties** via the linearized law of propagation of uncertainty (root-sum-of-squares of sensitivity-weighted input standard uncertainties):

$$u_c(y) = \sqrt{\sum_i \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right)^2 u^2(x_i)}$$

4. **Compute expanded uncertainty** $U = k_c \cdot u_c(y)$, where $k_c$ is the **coverage factor** (typically $k_c = 2$ for ~95% confidence in a normal distribution). The final report takes the form $Y = y \pm U$.

**Coverage factor explanation (2 marks):** The coverage factor $k_c$ multiplies the standard uncertainty to give a confidence level. For a normal distribution:
- $k_c = 1$ gives 68.27% confidence (1$\sigma$)
- $k_c = 2$ gives 95.45% confidence (2$\sigma$)
- $k_c = 2.58$ gives 99% confidence

The ITTC and most KTU 2024 Scheme reports mandate $k_c = 2$ as the standard.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute $K_T$:**

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} = \frac{12.50}{998 \times (15.0)^2 \times (0.250)^4}$$

Compute denominator:
- $n^2 = 225$ (rev/s)²
- $D^4 = (0.250)^4 = 0.00390625$ m⁴
- $\rho n^2 D^4 = 998 \times 225 \times 0.00390625 = 877.10$ N

$$K_T = \frac{12.50}{877.10} = 0.01425$$

**Step 2 — Compute standard uncertainties from expanded ones (divide by $k = 2$):**

- $u_T = 0.15 / 2 = 0.075$ N
- $u_n = 0.20 / 2 = 0.10$ rev/s
- $u_D = 0.001 / 2 = 0.0005$ m
- $u_\rho = 2 / 2 = 1$ kg/m³

**Step 3 — Compute sensitivity coefficients (partial derivatives of $K_T$ with respect to each input):**

$$\frac{\partial K_T}{\partial T} = \frac{1}{\rho n^2 D^4} = \frac{1}{877.10} = 1.140 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{\partial K_T}{\partial n} = -\frac{2T}{\rho n^3 D^4} = -\frac{2K_T}{n} = -\frac{2 \times 0.01425}{15.0} = -1.900 \times 10^{-3} \text{ (rev/s)}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{\partial K_T}{\partial D} = -\frac{4T}{\rho n^2 D^5} = -\frac{4K_T}{D} = -\frac{4 \times 0.01425}{0.250} = -0.2280 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{\partial K_T}{\partial \rho} = -\frac{T}{\rho^2 n^2 D^4} = -\frac{K_T}{\rho} = -\frac{0.01425}{998} = -1.428 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the GUM propagation law:**

$$u_c^2(K_T) = \left( \frac{\partial K_T}{\partial T} u_T \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\partial K_T}{\partial n} u_n \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\partial K_T}{\partial D} u_D \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\partial K_T}{\partial \rho} u_\rho \right)^2$$

Compute each term:
- Term 1: $(1.140 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.075)^2 = (8.55 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 7.31 \times 10^{-9}$
- Term 2: $(-1.900 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.10)^2 = (-1.90 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 3.61 \times 10^{-8}$
- Term 3: $(-0.2280 \times 0.0005)^2 = (-1.14 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 1.30 \times 10^{-8}$
- Term 4: $(-1.428 \times 10^{-5} \times 1)^2 = (1.428 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 2.04 \times 10^{-10}$

Sum: $u_c^2(K_T) = 7.31 \times 10^{-9} + 36.1 \times 10^{-9} + 13.0 \times 10^{-9} + 0.204 \times 10^{-9} = 5.66 \times 10^{-8}$

$$u_c(K_T) = \sqrt{5.66 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.379 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — Expanded uncertainty at 95% confidence:**

$$U(K_T) = 2 \times u_c(K_T) = 2 \times 2.379 \times 10^{-4} = 4.76 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Final reported value:**

$$\boxed{K_T = 0.01425 \pm 0.00048 \text{ at 95\% confidence}}$$

Or in percentage form, the relative expanded uncertainty is:

$$\frac{U(K_T)}{K_T} \times 100\% = \frac{0.00048}{0.01425} \times 100\% = 3.34\%$$

This is within the typical ITTC-acceptable range of $\pm 5\%$ for propulsion tests.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — 14-Mark Questions**
> 1. **Sign errors in partial derivatives:** The negative signs in $\partial K_T / \partial n$, $\partial K_T / \partial D$, and $\partial K_T / \partial \rho$ are **squared away** in the propagation law. A common student error is to retain the negative sign and subtract contributions — this gives a **negative variance**, which is mathematically impossible and loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing expanded and standard uncertainties:** If $U_T$ is given at 95% confidence, you **must divide by $k=2$** to get $u_T$ before propagating. Forgetting this step yields an answer roughly twice too large — 3-mark deduction.
> 3. **Unit consistency in $K_T$:** The denominator $\rho n^2 D^4$ must have units of Newtons, not mixed SI/base units. Always carry the unit check.
> 4. **Prohaska regression trap:** Do NOT force the regression through the origin. The intercept IS the form-factor term.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Analysis and Conclusions"**

- [ ] **ITTC-78 formula** must be memorized exactly: $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$. Valid for $Re \geq 10^6$ and hydraulically smooth surfaces.
- [ ] **Form factor $k$** is extracted via **Prohaska** (graphical, $Fr^4$ power law) or **Schoenherr** (iterative implicit) methods, not assumed.
- [ ] **Typical $k$ range for merchant hulls:** $0.10$–$0.25$; Series-60: $0.15$–$0.20$.
- [ ] **Full-scale resistance (ITTC-78):** $C_{\text{ship}} = (1+k) C_{F,s} + C_A + C_R$. The $(1+k)$ term corrects for viscous pressure; $C_A$ is the correlation allowance.
- [ ] **GUM uncertainty propagation** is the **mandatory** reporting standard; use RSS of sensitivity-weighted standard uncertainties, with $k_c = 2$ for 95% confidence.
- [ ] **Type A vs. Type B uncertainties:** Type A = statistical (from data spread); Type B = judgmental (from calibration, manufacturer specs).
- [ ] **Propeller coefficients** use $\rho n^2 D^4$ and $\rho n^2 D^5$ as denominators, NOT $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S$. Mixing these is a frequent 2-mark loss.
- [ ] **Conclusions must include:** (i) the design prediction, (ii) the uncertainty band, (iii) the limits of validity of the test, and (iv) recommended further work.
- [ ] **Blockage correction** applies when model cross-section area $> 5\%$ of tank cross-section area. KTU examiners often test whether you know when to apply it.
- [ ] **Reynolds number similarity** cannot be fully achieved between model and prototype; the ITTC-78 procedure **accepts this** and provides a correlation allowance $C_A$ to bridge the gap.
- [ ] **Always quote $C_P_{\min}$ vs. cavitation number $\sigma$** when interpreting pressure measurements; a negative $C_P_{\min}$ indicates flow separation or measurement error.
- [ ] **Final KTU report conclusion** should be **quantitative, dimensionless, and uncertainty-quantified** — avoid qualitative statements like "the model performed well."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Self Propulsion Experiments - Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Self Propulsion Experiments - Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Self-propulsion experiments** are towing tank / model basin tests in which a ship model, fitted with a scale model of its propeller, is towed at the design (or contractual) speed while the propeller is driven by an external dynamometer (motor) at a controlled rotational speed and torque. The test's central aim is to determine the **self-propulsion point** — the operating condition where the thrust developed by the propeller exactly equals the *effective resistance* of the model hull (i.e., hull resistance minus the *thrust-deduction* effect, plus the *rotational* resistance increment).

Mathematically, the self-propulsion point is defined by the equilibrium:

$$T_E = R_T = R_F + R_R + R_{APP}$$

where $T_E$ is the effective thrust, $R_T$ is the total resistance, $R_F$ is the frictional component, $R_R$ is the residual (wave + form) component, and $R_{APP}$ is the apparent resistance increment due to propeller action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The self-propulsion test is the *only* model experiment that simultaneously couples **hull hydrodynamics**, **propeller open-water characteristics**, and **hull–propeller interaction** in a single measurement. It is therefore the cornerstone of power-prediction methodology used by ITTC (International Towing Tank Conference).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pedalling a bicycle up a slight slope. Your legs (the *propeller*) must push hard enough to overcome the slope and wind resistance (the *hull resistance*). At a steady cruising speed, the pedalling force exactly balances the total drag. Now, if you stop pedalling, you decelerate. The exact pedalling force at which you neither accelerate nor decelerate is the **"self-propulsion point."**

In a model basin:
- The **towing carriage** drags the model at constant speed (simulating the forward motion).
- The **propeller**, driven by a small electric motor, must produce thrust equal to the model's *effective* drag.
- The **dynamometer** measures the rotational speed (n) and torque (Q) needed to maintain this balance.

By comparing this "loaded" condition with the "bare hull" resistance (without propeller), we can extract the **wake fraction (w)**, **thrust deduction (t)**, and the overall **propulsive efficiency (ηD)** — three of the most economically important numbers in ship design, because they directly determine the brake power required from the main engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard physical constants used in self-propulsion analysis:**
> - Density of fresh water: $\rho = \mathbf{998 \ kg/m^3}$ at 20 °C
> - Density of sea water: $\rho = \mathbf{1025 \ kg/m^3}$ at 15 °C
> - Kinematic viscosity (fresh): $\nu = \mathbf{1.004 \times 10^{-6} \ m^2/s}$
> - Standard reference temperature: **20 °C (ITTC 1978)**
> - Standard reference density: **1025 kg/m³ (ITTC 1978)**

## 1.3 The Strategic Role of Self-Propulsion Tests

A resistance test alone tells you *how much* force opposes the ship, but not *how much power* the engine must deliver. A propeller open-water test alone tells you *how efficient* a propeller is in a uniform inflow, but not how the hull's wake distorts its performance. The self-propulsion test **bridges these two worlds**, allowing the naval architect to:

1. Quantify the hull–propeller interaction (w, t).
2. Predict the **Effective Power (EHP)**, **Thrust Power (TP)**, and **Delivered Power (DHP)** at full scale.
3. Validate the propulsive coefficients against sea-trial data via extrapolation methods (e.g., ITTC 1978 / 2017 procedures).
4. Investigate **cavitation**, **vibration**, and **pressure-pulse** phenomena at the design point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Point Test Philosophy (ITTC Standard)

A complete self-propulsion investigation, as prescribed by the **ITTC 7.5-04-01-01.1** procedure, consists of **three coordinated model tests** performed on the same hull and propeller models:

| Test | Purpose | Key Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bare Hull Resistance** | Towing without propeller; measures total drag $R_T$ at various speeds | $R_T(V)$, $C_T$, $C_F$, $C_R$ |
| **Propeller Open Water (B-Series / Wageningen)** | Propeller in uniform inflow behind a stand; no hull | $K_T(J)$, $K_Q(J)$, $\eta_0(J)$ |
| **Self-Propulsion** | Model + propeller + dynamometer, balance condition | $n$, $Q$, $T$, $w$, $t$, $\eta_D$ |

The self-propulsion test is performed at **one (or sometimes two) design speeds**, and the data from the other two tests are used to back-calculate $w$ and $t$ by matching the propeller's thrust-loading at the self-propulsion point to its open-water curve.

## 2.2 The Wake Fraction (w) and Thrust Deduction (t) — Conceptual Breakdown

- **Wake Fraction (w):** The propeller does not see the ship's forward speed $V_S$. It sees a slower inflow $V_A$ because the hull "drags" water along with it. The wake fraction is the fractional reduction in velocity at the propeller disc:
$$w = \frac{V_S - V_A}{V_S} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad V_A = V_S(1 - w)$$

- **Thrust Deduction (t):** The propeller's suction effect lowers the pressure at the stern, which slightly *increases* the hull resistance. The thrust the propeller must produce ($T$) is therefore slightly *more* than the bare-hull resistance ($R$):
$$T = \frac{R}{1 - t} \quad \text{or} \quad R = T(1 - t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" and "How":** The propeller accelerates the flow at the stern (creating thrust), but this low-pressure region also "pulls" the hull backwards, increasing the wave-making and form drag. This penalty is the **thrust deduction**. Both $w$ and $t$ are *positive* for single-screw ships; for twin-screw or unusual configurations they can sometimes be *negative* (i.e., the propeller "helps" by filling in the wake).

## 2.3 The Propulsive Efficiency Chain

The **quasi-propulsive coefficient** $\eta_D$ (or "total propulsive efficiency") is decomposed into three multiplicative factors:

$$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R$$

| Symbol | Name | Definition | Typical Range |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| $\eta_H$ | Hull Efficiency | $(1 - t) \, / \, (1 - w)$ | 1.05 – 1.30 |
| $\eta_O$ | Propeller Open-Water Efficiency | $T V_A \, / \, (2 \pi n Q)$ | 0.55 – 0.75 |
| $\eta_R$ | Rotative Efficiency | Effective work / Shaft work (model only) | 0.95 – 1.05 |
| $\eta_D$ | Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient | $R_T V_S \, / \, (2 \pi n Q)$ | 0.55 – 0.75 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** In ship design, $\eta_D$ is the **single most important conversion factor** between *Effective Power* (the useful power to push the ship) and *Brake Power* (the power the engine must actually deliver, including all losses). A 1% improvement in $\eta_D$ on a 300,000 DWT tanker translates to **several thousand tonnes of fuel saved per year** at sea.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
| :-: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_T = R_F + R_R + R_{APP}$ | Total resistance breakdown at self-propulsion point |
| 2 | $T = R \, / \, (1 - t)$ | Propeller thrust at self-propulsion point |
| 3 | $V_A = V_S(1 - w)$ | Effective advance velocity at the propeller disc |
| 4 | $J = V_A \, / \, (n D)$ | Propeller advance coefficient (used to read $K_T, K_Q$) |
| 5 | $T = K_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$ | Propeller thrust from open-water coefficient |
| 6 | $Q = K_Q \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^5$ | Propeller torque from open-water coefficient |
| 7 | $K_Q \, / \, K_T = \eta_0 \, / \, J$ | Standard open-water relationship (used for curve fitting) |
| 8 | $\eta_H = (1 - t) \, / \, (1 - w)$ | Hull efficiency |
| 9 | $\eta_O = T V_A \, / \, (2 \pi n Q)$ | Open-water propeller efficiency |
| 10 | $\eta_R = (\text{used in model–ship correlation})$ | Rotative efficiency (model value, scaled up) |
| 11 | $\eta_D = R_T V_S \, / \, (2 \pi n Q)$ | Quasi-propulsive coefficient |
| 12 | $EHP = R_T \cdot V_S$ | Effective horsepower (useful power pushing the ship) |
| 13 | $DHP = 2 \pi n Q$ | Delivered horsepower (power reaching the propeller) |
| 14 | $BHP = DHP \, / \, \eta_S \eta_B$ | Brake horsepower (power the engine must supply) |
| 15 | $C_T = R_T \, / \, (0.5 \rho V_S^2 S)$ | Total resistance coefficient (Froude-based) |
| 16 | $C_P = (P / 0.5 \rho V^3 L^2)$ | Powering coefficient (used in extrapolation) |

> All lengths are in **metres**, forces in **newtons**, powers in **watts**, and rotational speeds in **rev/s** (or convert $n$ from RPM by $n_{rev/s} = n_{RPM} / 60$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hull Efficiency $\eta_H$

The hull efficiency is defined as the ratio of the *useful thrust work* to the *thrust work that the propeller must actually produce*, both evaluated in the propeller's frame of reference.

**Step 1 — Write the definition of useful work (per unit time).**
The useful thrust work done per second is the resistance force times the ship speed:
$$P_{\text{useful}} = R \cdot V_S$$

**Step 2 — Write the work the propeller actually does per second.**
The propeller pushes against the inflow velocity $V_A$ with a thrust $T$:
$$P_{\text{prop}} = T \cdot V_A$$

**Step 3 — Express R and T in terms of their definitions at the self-propulsion point.**
At the self-propulsion point, the thrust $T$ overcomes the resistance, and the relationship between $R$ and $T$ involves the thrust-deduction factor:
$$R = T(1 - t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{R}{1 - t}$$

Also, the wake relates $V_S$ to $V_A$:
$$V_A = V_S(1 - w)$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio $\eta_H$.**
$$\eta_H = \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{prop}}} = \frac{R \cdot V_S}{T \cdot V_A} = \frac{R \cdot V_S}{\left(\dfrac{R}{1 - t}\right) \cdot V_S (1 - w)}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the common terms $R$ and $V_S$.**
After cancellation, we obtain the canonical ITTC form:
$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Because $t$ is typically *smaller* than $w$ for a single-screw ship, $(1 - t) > (1 - w)$, so $\eta_H > 1$. This is *not* a violation of energy conservation — $\eta_H > 1$ simply means the propeller's slipstream recovers more energy than is lost to the suction effect, *in the reference frame of the hull*. The total $\eta_D$ is still well below 1.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Open-Water Propeller Efficiency $\eta_O$

**Step 1 — Define thrust power and torque power.**
Thrust power is the useful work the propeller does on the fluid:
$$P_T = T \cdot V_A$$

Torque power is the mechanical power delivered to the propeller shaft:
$$P_Q = 2 \pi n Q$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio.**
$$\eta_O = \frac{P_T}{P_Q} = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2 \pi n Q}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the advance coefficient.**
By definition, $J = V_A / (nD)$, so $V_A = J n D$. Substituting:
$$\eta_O = \frac{T \cdot J n D}{2 \pi n Q} = \frac{T}{Q} \cdot \frac{J D}{2 \pi}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the thrust and torque coefficients.**
$T = K_T \rho n^2 D^4$ and $Q = K_Q \rho n^2 D^5$. Dividing:
$$\frac{T}{Q} = \frac{K_T}{K_Q} \cdot \frac{1}{D}$$

**Step 5 — Combine.**
$$\eta_O = \frac{K_T}{K_Q} \cdot \frac{1}{D} \cdot \frac{J D}{2 \pi} = \frac{J}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}$$

This is the **famous ITTC identity** that is used to plot the open-water efficiency curve:
$$\boxed{\eta_O = \frac{J}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient $\eta_D$

**Step 1 — Definition.**
$\eta_D$ is the ratio of *effective power* (what is needed to push the hull) to *delivered power* (what reaches the propeller shaft):
$$\eta_D = \frac{EHP}{DHP} = \frac{R_T \cdot V_S}{2 \pi n Q}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose using the self-propulsion relations.**
At the self-propulsion point: $R_T = T(1 - t)$ and $V_S = V_A / (1 - w)$. Substituting:
$$\eta_D = \frac{T(1 - t) \cdot \dfrac{V_A}{1 - w}}{2 \pi n Q}$$

**Step 3 — Group terms.**
$$\eta_D = \underbrace{\frac{1 - t}{1 - w}}_{\eta_H} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{T V_A}{2 \pi n Q}}_{\eta_O} \cdot \eta_R$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**
$$\boxed{\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Scale)

A tanker model of length $L = 6.0 \ m$ and a propeller of diameter $D = 0.20 \ m$ is tested at $V_S = 2.0 \ m/s$. The following are observed at the self-propulsion point:

- Bare-hull resistance (corrected): $R = 22.0 \ N$
- Propeller rotational speed: $n = 12.0 \ rev/s$
- Measured torque: $Q = 0.85 \ N \cdot m$
- Water density: $\rho = 998 \ kg/m^3$
- Wake fraction (Taylor): $w = 0.25$
- Thrust deduction (assumed): $t = 0.15$

**Compute the propulsive coefficients.**

**Step 1 — Effective advance velocity.**
$$V_A = V_S (1 - w) = 2.0 \times 0.75 = 1.50 \ m/s$$

**Step 2 — Thrust required.**
$$T = \frac{R}{1 - t} = \frac{22.0}{0.85} = 25.882 \ N$$

**Step 3 — Hull efficiency.**
$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \frac{0.85}{0.75} = 1.133$$

**Step 4 — Open-water efficiency.**
$$\eta_O = \frac{T V_A}{2 \pi n Q} = \frac{25.882 \times 1.50}{2 \pi \times 12.0 \times 0.85} = \frac{38.824}{64.088} = 0.606$$

**Step 5 — Quasi-propulsive coefficient (assuming $\eta_R = 1.0$ for a model).**
$$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R = 1.133 \times 0.606 \times 1.0 = 0.687$$

**Step 6 — Effective and Delivered Power.**
$$EHP = R \cdot V_S = 22.0 \times 2.0 = 44.0 \ W$$
$$DHP = 2 \pi n Q = 2 \pi \times 12.0 \times 0.85 = 64.09 \ W$$

**Step 7 — Cross-check.**
$$\eta_D = EHP / DHP = 44.0 / 64.09 = 0.687 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that $\eta_D \approx 0.69$ is a typical value for a single-screw merchant ship model. At full scale, after Reynolds-number correction, the *delivered* power is computed and then the **shaft efficiency** $\eta_S$ and **bearing / gearbox efficiency** $\eta_B$ are applied to get the brake power that the main engine must develop.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Procedure to Determine (w, t, ηD) from Test Data

| Step | Data Needed | Operation | Output |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_T(V)$ from resistance test | Polynomial fit $R_T = a V^b$ | Smooth $R_T$ curve |
| 2 | $K_T(J), K_Q(J)$ from open-water test | Polynomial fit | Smooth $K_T, K_Q$ |
| 3 | $V_S, n, Q$ from self-propulsion test | Read directly | Operating point |
| 4 | Compute $V_A = V_S(1 - w)$ with assumed $w$ | Iteration 1 | Tentative $J = V_A/(nD)$ |
| 5 | Read $K_T$ at $J$ from open-water curve | $T = K_T \rho n^2 D^4$ | Propeller thrust $T$ |
| 6 | Compare $T$ to $R$ — adjust $w$ until $T(1-t) = R$ | Iteration 2 | Converged $w, t$ |
| 7 | Compute $\eta_H, \eta_O, \eta_R$ | Standard formulas | $\eta_D$ |
| 8 | Use ITTC 1978 / 2017 method | Extrapolate to full scale | Ship $EHP, DHP, BHP$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Self-Propulsion Test

The diagram below maps the **instrumentation chain**, the **measurement flow**, and the **analytical reduction** that together constitute a complete self-propulsion experiment. (Adapted as a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow because the physical layout of a towing tank cannot be meaningfully rendered in Mermaid syntax.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Towing Carriage] --> nodeB[Model Hull with Propeller]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Resistance Force Transducer]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Data Acquisition Unit]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Propeller Dynamometer]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Torque and RPM Sensor]
    nodeF --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeG[Raw Time Histories]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Signal Conditioning and Filtering]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Self Propulsion Point Identification]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{Iterative Solver}
    nodeK[Bare Hull Resistance Test] --> nodeJ
    nodeL[Propeller Open Water Test] --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Wake Fraction w]
    nodeJ --> nodeN[Thrust Deduction t]
    nodeJ --> nodeO[Hull Efficiency eta H]
    nodeJ --> nodeP[Open Water Efficiency eta O]
    nodeJ --> nodeQ[Quasi Propulsive Coefficient eta D]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[ITTC 1978 Extrapolation]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Full Scale EHP DHP BHP]
    nodeR --> nodeT[Cavitation and Pressure Pulse Maps]

    style nodeA fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366
    style nodeB fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600
    style nodeD fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style nodeJ fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style nodeR fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#330099
    style nodeS fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#666600
```

## 4.2 Causal Loop of Hull–Propeller Interaction

This diagram illustrates the **feedback mechanism** between hull drag, propeller suction, and the resulting performance metrics. Reading it clockwise, you can see why the self-propulsion point is a *self-consistent* solution of the coupled equations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Ship Speed V S] --> nodeB[Wake at Stern]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Wake Fraction w]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Advance Velocity V A]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Propeller Loading J]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Thrust T and Torque Q]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Thrust Deduction t]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Effective Resistance R]
    nodeH --> nodeI{T equals R ?}
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ[Self Propulsion Point Converged]
    nodeI -- No --> nodeB
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Output eta H eta O eta D]

    style nodeA fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366
    style nodeJ fill:#ccff99,stroke:#006600
    style nodeK fill:#ffff99,stroke:#666600
```

## 4.3 Logical Flowchart — Procedure to Identify (w, t) from a Self-Propulsion Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input: V S, n, Q, R bare hull] --> nodeB[Assume w approx 0.20 to 0.30]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute V A = V S 1 minus w]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute J = V A divided by n D]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Look up K T and K Q from open water curve]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute T = K T rho n squared D to the 4]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Compare T to R}
    nodeG -- T greater than R --> nodeH[Increase w and re iterate]
    nodeG -- T less than R --> nodeI[Decrease w and re iterate]
    nodeH --> nodeC
    nodeI --> nodeC
    nodeG -- T equals R --> nodeJ[Converged w and t]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Compute eta H, eta O, eta D]
    nodeK --> nodeL[ITTC extrapolation to full scale]

    style nodeA fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366
    style nodeJ fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600
    style nodeL fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the self-propulsion point of a ship model. Why is it not equivalent to the bare-hull resistance condition?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **self-propulsion point** is the operating condition (V, n, Q) at which the thrust developed by the model propeller **exactly equals the effective resistance** experienced by the model hull. At this point, the model neither accelerates nor decelerates, and the measured torque and RPM are the basis for power prediction.
- It is **not equivalent to the bare-hull resistance condition** because, in the self-propulsion test, the propeller's suction effect **increases** the hull resistance (thrust-deduction effect) and the hull's wake **reduces** the inflow velocity at the propeller disc. Therefore, the propeller must produce a thrust $T = R / (1 - t)$, which is **greater** than the bare-hull resistance $R$. The self-propulsion point captures these **hull–propeller interaction** effects, whereas a bare-hull test does not.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*List any **three** primary objectives of conducting a self-propulsion experiment in a model basin.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Determine the self-propulsion factors** — wake fraction (w) and thrust deduction (t) — that govern the hull–propeller interaction.
2. **Measure the propulsive efficiency chain** — hull efficiency ($\eta_H$), open-water efficiency ($\eta_O$), and quasi-propulsive coefficient ($\eta_D$) — at the design speed.
3. **Predict the full-scale Effective Power (EHP), Delivered Power (DHP), and Brake Power (BHP)** via ITTC 1978/2017 correlation procedures, including Reynolds-number correction and form-factor application.
4. *(Optional fourth point for partial credit)* Investigate **cavitation inception**, **vibration**, and **pressure-pulse** characteristics of the propeller in the wake field.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete procedure** of a self-propulsion test conducted in a towing tank. List the instruments used and the measurements recorded. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A model of length $L = 5.0 \ m$ is tested at $V_S = 2.4 \ m/s$ in fresh water ($\rho = 998 \ kg/m^3$, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \ m^2/s$). The measured bare-hull resistance is $R = 30.0 \ N$. At the self-propulsion point, the propeller (diameter $D = 0.18 \ m$, $n = 14 \ rev/s$) develops a thrust of $T = 35.5 \ N$. Compute the **wake fraction**, **thrust deduction**, **hull efficiency**, and **quasi-propulsive coefficient** (assume $\eta_R = 1.0$ and that the measured torque is $Q = 1.0 \ N \cdot m$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Procedure (7 marks):**
1. **Model preparation:** The ship model (typically scale $\lambda = 1/20$ to $1/40$) is fitted with a dynamically similar propeller model. The propeller is driven by a **torsion-bar or in-line dynamometer** mounted inside the hull.
2. **Carriage setup:** The model is attached to the towing carriage via a **resistance force transducer** (load cell). The dynamometer is connected to a **variable-speed electric motor** (or hydraulic motor) through a flexible coupling.
3. **Instrumentation:**
   - Resistance force transducer (measures $R$)
   - Propeller shaft **torque sensor** (measures $Q$)
   - **Optical / magnetic RPM pickup** (measures $n$)
   - **Pressure transducers** on the hull and stern (for cavitation / wake surveys)
   - **Data acquisition system** (sampling rate ≥ 100 Hz)
4. **Test execution:** The carriage is accelerated to the design speed. The motor RPM is adjusted until the **towing force** on the load cell becomes *zero* (or matches a calibrated offset). This is the **self-propulsion point**: $T = R_{\text{eff}}$.
5. **Data recording:** Time-histories of $V, R, n, Q$ are recorded. Steady-state values (mean over a flat segment of the run) are extracted.
6. **Post-processing:** The open-water propeller curves ($K_T, K_Q$ vs. $J$) and the bare-hull resistance curve are used in an iterative solver to back-calculate $w, t$ and compute $\eta_H, \eta_O, \eta_D$.

*(A full-marks answer should include a labelled block diagram; refer to SECTION 4.1.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the advance velocity from the measured thrust and torque.**
$$V_A = \frac{2 \pi n Q \eta_O}{T} \Rightarrow \text{first estimate using } \eta_O = T V_A / (2 \pi n Q)$$

Using the open-water identity directly: from $T = 35.5 \ N$, $n = 14 \ rev/s$, $Q = 1.0 \ N \cdot m$:
$$\eta_O = \frac{T V_A}{2 \pi n Q} \Rightarrow \text{solve iteratively for } V_A$$

Alternatively, use the wake and thrust-deduction relations directly. From the given data:
$$\text{Advance coefficient: } J = \frac{V_A}{n D}$$

**Step 2 — Compute thrust deduction.**
$$R = T(1 - t) \Rightarrow t = 1 - \frac{R}{T} = 1 - \frac{30.0}{35.5} = 0.1549$$

**Step 3 — Compute wake fraction from propeller kinematics.**
For a Wageningen B-series propeller of typical pitch ratio $P/D \approx 0.7$ at the design $J \approx 0.55$ to $0.65$, and given the self-propulsion operating point, the wake can be solved iteratively. Using the explicit value consistent with the data:
$$V_A = V_S(1 - w) \Rightarrow w = 1 - \frac{V_A}{V_S}$$

With $V_S = 2.4 \ m/s$ and the standard self-propulsion operating point solution $V_A \approx 1.8 \ m/s$:
$$w = 1 - \frac{1.8}{2.4} = 0.25$$

**Step 4 — Hull efficiency.**
$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \frac{1 - 0.1549}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{0.8451}{0.75} = 1.127$$

**Step 5 — Open-water efficiency.**
$$\eta_O = \frac{T V_A}{2 \pi n Q} = \frac{35.5 \times 1.8}{2 \pi \times 14 \times 1.0} = \frac{63.9}{87.96} = 0.726$$

**Step 6 — Quasi-propulsive coefficient.**
$$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R = 1.127 \times 0.726 \times 1.0 = 0.818$$

**Step 7 — Sanity check.**
$$EHP = R V_S = 30.0 \times 2.4 = 72.0 \ W$$
$$DHP = 2 \pi n Q = 87.96 \ W$$
$$\eta_D = EHP / DHP = 72.0 / 87.96 = 0.818 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students confuse the **hull efficiency** with the **quasi-propulsive coefficient**. Remember: $\eta_H$ can be **greater than 1** because it is a ratio of *work in two different reference frames*. Always cross-check by computing $EHP / DHP$ at the end — this ratio must equal $\eta_D$ and *cannot* exceed 1.0. Marks are commonly lost for writing $\eta_H$ as if it were a "real" efficiency in the energy-conservation sense.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **quasi-propulsive coefficient** $\eta_D$ in terms of the hull, open-water, and rotative efficiencies. Clearly state the meaning of each term and the physical reason why $\eta_H$ can exceed unity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data are obtained from the open-water test of a Wageningen B-series propeller in fresh water ($\rho = 998 \ kg/m^3$):

| J | $K_T$ | $K_Q$ |
| :-: | :-: | :-: |
| 0.50 | 0.225 | 0.0340 |
| 0.60 | 0.180 | 0.0285 |
| 0.70 | 0.140 | 0.0235 |

For a self-propulsion test with $V_S = 2.5 \ m/s$, $n = 11.5 \ rev/s$, $D = 0.22 \ m$, bare-hull resistance $R = 28.0 \ N$, and an assumed thrust deduction $t = 0.16$, determine the **wake fraction** by iteration, the **hull efficiency**, the **open-water efficiency**, and the **required torque** $Q$ at the self-propulsion point. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition.**
$$\eta_D = \frac{\text{Effective Power}}{\text{Delivered Power}} = \frac{R V_S}{2 \pi n Q}$$

**Step 2 — Use self-propulsion relations.**
At the self-propulsion point: $R = T(1 - t)$ and $V_S = V_A / (1 - w)$.

$$\eta_D = \frac{T(1 - t) \cdot \dfrac{V_A}{1 - w}}{2 \pi n Q} = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w} \cdot \frac{T V_A}{2 \pi n Q} = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O$$

**Step 3 — Introduce rotative efficiency** (which accounts for model-scale rotational losses):
$$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R$$

**Step 4 — Why $\eta_H > 1$ is physical.**
$\eta_H$ is *not* a thermodynamic efficiency. It compares the *useful thrust work* $R V_S$ (work done on the ship) with the *work the propeller delivers to the fluid* $T V_A$ (work done on the slipstream). Because the propeller disc sees a **slower** flow than the ship speed ($V_A < V_S$, so $1 - w < 1$), the denominator $T V_A$ is *smaller* than the numerator $R V_S$ when $t < w$ (typical for single-screw ships). Hence $\eta_H > 1$. This "gain" is paid for in the **rotational augmentation** of the wake and in **propeller–hull interaction losses** captured by $\eta_R$. The total $\eta_D$ is always less than 1.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Initial guess for wake fraction.**
Assume $w_0 = 0.25$. Then:
$$V_A = V_S(1 - w_0) = 2.5 \times 0.75 = 1.875 \ m/s$$
$$J_0 = \frac{V_A}{n D} = \frac{1.875}{11.5 \times 0.22} = \frac{1.875}{2.53} = 0.741$$

**Step 2 — Read $K_T, K_Q$ at $J_0 = 0.741$ by interpolation** (between $J = 0.70$ and $J = 0.80$, extrapolating slightly; using the trend of B-series data):
$$K_T \approx 0.140 - 0.040 \times \frac{0.741 - 0.70}{0.10} = 0.140 - 0.0164 = 0.1236$$
$$K_Q \approx 0.0235 - 0.0050 \times \frac{0.041}{0.10} = 0.0235 - 0.00205 = 0.02145$$

**Step 3 — Compute the thrust the propeller would develop at $n = 11.5 \ rev/s$.**
$$T_0 = K_T \rho n^2 D^4 = 0.1236 \times 998 \times (11.5)^2 \times (0.22)^4$$
$$T_0 = 0.1236 \times 998 \times 132.25 \times 0.002342 = 38.13 \ N$$

**Step 4 — Required thrust for self-propulsion.**
$$T_{\text{req}} = \frac{R}{1 - t} = \frac{28.0}{1 - 0.16} = \frac{28.0}{0.84} = 33.33 \ N$$

**Step 5 — Compare and iterate.**
Since $T_0 = 38.13 \ N > T_{\text{req}} = 33.33 \ N$, the assumed wake is **too small** (the propeller is over-producing thrust). Increase $w$:

Try $w_1 = 0.30$:
$$V_A = 2.5 \times 0.70 = 1.75 \ m/s$$
$$J_1 = \frac{1.75}{2.53} = 0.692$$
$$K_T \approx 0.140 + 0.040 \times \frac{0.70 - 0.692}{0.10} = 0.140 + 0.0032 = 0.1432$$
$$T_1 = 0.1432 \times 998 \times 132.25 \times 0.002342 = 44.18 \ N$$

Now $T_1 = 44.18 \ N$ is *higher* than $T_0 = 38.13 \ N$ — this is the wrong direction. The issue is that for a typical B-series propeller, **$K_T$ decreases with increasing $J$**, so increasing $w$ (decreasing $J$) actually *increases* $K_T$ and hence $T$. The correct root lies at a *lower* $w$ than initially assumed.

**Re-iterate with $w_2 = 0.22$:**
$$V_A = 2.5 \times 0.78 = 1.95 \ m/s$$
$$J_2 = \frac{1.95}{2.53} = 0.771$$
$$K_T \approx 0.140 - 0.040 \times \frac{0.771 - 0.70}{0.10} = 0.140 - 0.0284 = 0.1116$$
$$T_2 = 0.1116 \times 998 \times 132.25 \times 0.002342 = 34.43 \ N$$

This is very close to $T_{\text{req}} = 33.33 \ N$. **Linear interpolation between $w_1 = 0.25$ (T=38.13) and $w_2 = 0.22$ (T=34.43):**
$$w^* = 0.22 + 0.03 \times \frac{34.43 - 33.33}{34.43 - 38.13} = 0.22 + 0.03 \times \frac{1.10}{-3.70} = 0.22 - 0.00892 = 0.211$$

Round to the conventional two-decimal precision: $w \approx 0.21$.

**Step 6 — Hull efficiency.**
$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \frac{0.84}{0.79} = 1.063$$

**Step 7 — Open-water efficiency** (using $J^* = 0.795$ and $K_Q \approx 0.0200$ from interpolation):
$$\eta_O = \frac{J^*}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.795}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{0.1078}{0.0200} = 0.1265 \times 5.39 = 0.682$$

**Step 8 — Required torque at self-propulsion point.**
$$Q = \frac{T_{\text{req}} V_A}{2 \pi n \eta_O} = \frac{33.33 \times 1.95}{2 \pi \times 11.5 \times 0.682} = \frac{64.99}{49.27} = 1.319 \ N \cdot m$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):** Two recurring mistakes cost marks every year:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rev/s** before computing $T$ or $Q$. If $n$ is in RPM, you **must** divide by 60: $n_{rev/s} = n_{RPM} / 60$.
> 2. **Confusing $J$** (advance coefficient, dimensionless) with $V_A$ (velocity, $m/s$). The relation is $J = V_A / (nD)$, *not* $J = V_A \cdot n \cdot D$.
> A third subtle pitfall: students often forget that $K_T$ for a B-series propeller *decreases* with $J$, so the iteration direction must be checked carefully — increasing $w$ (decreasing $J$) *increases* $T$, opposite to intuition for some other propellers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **self-propulsion point** is the steady-state condition where propeller thrust equals the effective hull resistance: $T = R / (1 - t)$.
- **Three coordinated model tests** are required: bare-hull resistance, propeller open-water, and self-propulsion.
- The **wake fraction** $w$ accounts for the velocity defect at the propeller disc: $V_A = V_S (1 - w)$.
- The **thrust deduction** $t$ accounts for the resistance increase due to propeller suction: $R = T (1 - t)$.
- The **hull efficiency** is $\eta_H = (1 - t) / (1 - w)$ and can exceed 1 because it is a *frame-of-reference* ratio, not a thermodynamic efficiency.
- The **open-water efficiency** follows $\eta_O = (J / 2 \pi) (K_T / K_Q)$ — the canonical ITTC identity.
- The **quasi-propulsive coefficient** is $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R$, and it is always less than 1.
- **Dimensional checks:** $T$ in newtons, $Q$ in $N \cdot m$, $n$ in $rev/s$ (divide RPM by 60), $V$ in $m/s$, $D$ in $m$, $\rho$ in $kg/m^3$.
- **Standard references** for test procedure: ITTC Recommended Procedures 7.5-04-01-01.1 (resistance), 7.5-04-02-03.1 (propulsion), 7.5-04-01-01.2 (1978 power-prediction method).
- **Typical model scale:** $\lambda = 1/20$ to $1/40$; typical $w = 0.20$ to $0.40$; typical $t = 0.10$ to $0.20$; typical $\eta_D = 0.55$ to $0.75$.
- **Cross-check:** Always verify $\eta_D = EHP / DHP$ at the end of a numerical problem — this single check catches most algebraic errors.
- **Cavitation and pressure-pulse** observations during the self-propulsion run are a valuable bonus deliverable in modern testing, used to validate numerical CFD predictions of the hull–propeller interaction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test Arrangements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Test Arrangements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Test Arrangements** in marine hydrodynamics refer to the systematic and engineered configuration of a physical model, its supporting instrumentation, the towing/measuring carriage, the test basin geometry, fluid medium conditioning, and data-acquisition architecture required to experimentally determine hydrodynamic coefficients (resistance, propulsion, seakeeping, maneuvering) of a ship or marine vehicle under controlled, dynamically similar conditions.

In the context of KTU 2024 scheme (Module 3 of PENST865), the term encompasses:

- The geometric and kinematic mounting of the **ship model** (free-running vs. constrained)
- The selection and positioning of **dynamometric balances** (planar motion mechanism, force balance)
- The orientation and **boundary condition setup** (beach, wavemaker, absorbing paddle)
- The **instrumentation routing** (pressure tappings, wave probes, LDA, PIV)
- The **environmental conditioning** of the towing tank or circulating water channel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> *"Test Arrangements — types (carriage-towed, free-running, PMM, rotatory, cavitation tunnel), model support systems, force balance mounting, instrumentation placement, end-effect and blockage corrections."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surgical Theatre" of Ship Hydrodynamics

Imagine you are a doctor (the researcher) performing open-heart surgery on a patient (the ship model). You cannot simply put the patient in a bathtub; you need:

| Surgical Element | Marine Hydrodynamic Equivalent |
|------------------|-------------------------------|
| Operating theatre | Towing tank / circulating water channel |
| Operating table | Test carriage or PMM arm |
| Anesthesia & vitals monitoring | Dynamometer, wave probes, pressure sensors |
| Sterile conditions | De-aerated, temperature-controlled water |
| Surgical tools alignment | Model attitude control (trim, sinkage) |

A **test arrangement** is the engineering blueprint that ensures the "patient" is properly "anaesthetized" (constrained/conditioned), the "scalpels" (instruments) are accurately placed, and the "vitals" (forces, motions, waves) are precisely measured.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- Standard gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Kinematic viscosity of fresh water at **20 °C: ν = 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
- Density of fresh water at **20 °C: ρ = 998.2 kg/m³**
- Density of seawater: **ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³**
- Standard reference temperature for model testing: **15 °C** (ITTC) or **20 °C** (ISO)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Temperature Matters:** A 1 °C change in water temperature alters viscosity by ~2%, changing the **Reynolds number** by an equivalent margin — a critical correction in **Reynolds number scaling** for high-speed vessels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Test Arrangements

Marine hydrodynamic test arrangements are classified along **five primary axes**:

1. **Constraint Type** — Free vs. Constrained (PMM, rotatory, captive)
2. **Motion Type** — Straight-line towing, circular (rotary), oscillatory (PMM), free-running
3. **Facility Type** — Towing tank, circulating water channel (CWC), wave basin, cavitation tunnel, ice tank
4. **Phenomenon Studied** — Resistance, propulsion, seakeeping, maneuvering, cavitation, slamming
5. **Froude / Reynolds Dominance** — Low-speed vs. high-speed model testing

### 2.2 Component-Level Architecture of a Typical Test Arrangement

#### 2.2.1 The Towing Tank Arrangement (Most Common)

A standard **carriage-towed arrangement** consists of:

- **Main tank** (length 100–400 m, width 3–15 m, depth 3–8 m)
- **Carriage** spanning the tank width on rails, with **in-line dynamometer**
- **Strut** (vertical/horizontal) connecting model to dynamometer
- **Force balance** measuring drag (R_T), side force (Y), and yaw moment (N)
- **Model** typically 1:20 to 1:40 scale for merchant ships
- **Bows wave probes / resistance dynamometer** combined assembly
- **Beach / wave absorber** at the upstream end to prevent wave reflection

#### 2.2.2 The Planar Motion Mechanism (PMM) Arrangement

Used for **manoeuvring derivatives**:

- Model is **constrained** to a vertical strut
- Carriage executes sinusoidal sway (y) and yaw (ψ) motions at controlled frequency ω and amplitude
- The PMM carriage oscillates the model while moving forward
- Forces and moments are measured as time histories, then Fourier-analyzed to obtain **linear** (Y_v, N_v, Y_r, N_r) and **nonlinear** derivatives

#### 2.2.3 The Circulating Water Channel (CWC) Arrangement

- Model is **stationary**; water flows past at controlled velocity U_∞
- Eliminates need for long towing runs
- Higher turbulence intensity; smaller blockage tolerance (≤ 2–5%)
- Used extensively for **propeller open-water tests** and **boundary-layer studies**

#### 2.2.4 The Rotating Arm Basin Arrangement

- Model is attached to an **arm rotating about a vertical axis**
- Effective forward velocity: **U_eff = ω · R** (R = arm radius)
- Generates steady circular motion simulating **turning maneuvers**
- Yaw rate r = ω (constant), drift angle β controllable via arm tilt
- Mainly used for **steady-turning derivatives** (Y_β, N_β, Y_φ=0)

#### 2.2.5 The Cavitation Tunnel Arrangement

- Sealed test section with **de-aerated water**
- Closed-circuit, pressurized flow loop
- Test section usually rectangular (0.5 m × 0.5 m or larger)
- Equipped with **stroboscope + camera system** for cavitation observation
- Velocity up to 15–25 m/s; pressure controlled by throttling valve

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_n = \dfrac{U}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | **Froude number** — gravity/wave scaling | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_n = \dfrac{UL}{\nu}$ | **Reynolds number** — viscous scaling | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S}$ | **Total resistance coefficient** | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10}R_n - 2)^2}$ | **ITTC 1957 frictional line** | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_R = C_T - C_F (1+k)$ | **Residuary resistance coefficient** | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R_T = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S \, C_T$ | **Total resistance** | N |
| 7 | $U_m = U_s \sqrt{\lambda}$ | **Model velocity (Froude scaling)** | m/s |
| 8 | $R_{T,m} = R_{T,s} / \lambda^3$ | **Force scaling** (Newton's 3rd law + cube) | N |
| 9 | $\text{Blockage}_\epsilon = 1 - \left(\dfrac{A_m}{A_t}\right)^{3/2}$ | **Blockage correction factor** | dimensionless |
| 10 | $U_\infty = \omega \cdot R_{arm}$ | **Rotating arm velocity** | m/s |
| 11 | $r = \omega = U_\infty / R_{arm}$ | **Yaw rate in rotating arm** | rad/s |
| 12 | $\beta = \arctan(v/u)$ | **Drift angle (manoeuvring)** | rad |
| 13 | $F_n = 0.4 \cdot \lambda^{-0.5}$ | **Recommended basin depth criterion** | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\Delta T \leq 0.5 °C$ | **Thermal tolerance for model tank** | °C |
| 15 | $h_{beach} \geq 0.5 \lambda_w$ | **Beach length for wave absorption** | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where λ = model scale ratio** (L_ship / L_model), and **S** in C_T formula is the **wetted surface area**.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why Test Arrangements Matter

A well-designed test arrangement provides:

1. **Dimensional similarity** — Ensures forces measured at model scale accurately project to full-scale via Froude/Reynolds scaling laws.
2. **Repeatability** — Standardized arrangement allows comparison between laboratories (ITTC benchmarks).
3. **Boundary isolation** — Reduces wave reflection, blockage, and surface effects that would corrupt data.
4. **Multi-degree-of-freedom capture** — Modern arrangements capture 6-DOF force/moment time histories.

### 2.5 Common Pitfalls in Test Arrangement Selection

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Froude-Reynolds Conflict:** Ship scaling demands simultaneous equality of Froude AND Reynolds numbers, which is physically impossible at one density. Therefore, **Froude is matched and Reynolds is corrected** using frictional form factors or extrapolation methods (e.g., **Hughes' 1954 method**, **ITTC 1978**, **Townsin 1984**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 — Blockage Effect:** A model too close to walls experiences accelerated flow around it, **artificially increasing resistance**. Maximum cross-sectional blockage tolerance is typically **< 5%** (some standards require < 2%).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 — Wave Reflection:** Insufficient beach length produces reflected waves contaminating calm-water resistance readings. Minimum beach length must equal **half the longest wavelength** generated.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Froude Velocity Scaling Law

We derive the relationship between model velocity $U_m$ and ship velocity $U_s$ from the requirement that **Froude numbers are equal**.

**Step 1 — Definition of Froude number:**
The Froude number is the ratio of inertial to gravitational forces.

$$F_n = \dfrac{U}{\sqrt{gL}}$$

**Step 2 — Equality condition at ship and model scales:**
For dynamic similarity, the Froude number at the model must equal the Froude number at full scale.

$$F_{n,m} = F_{n,s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the definition into both sides:**

$$\dfrac{U_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \dfrac{U_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that g is the same in model and ship (Earth-bound test):**

$$\dfrac{U_m}{\sqrt{L_m}} = \dfrac{U_s}{\sqrt{L_s}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for model velocity:**

$$U_m = U_s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{L_m}{L_s}}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the geometric scale ratio $\lambda = L_s / L_m$:**

$$U_m = U_s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\lambda}} = \dfrac{U_s}{\sqrt{\lambda}}$$

**Step 7 — Final working formula:**

$$\boxed{U_m = \dfrac{U_s}{\sqrt{\lambda}}}$$

This is the **Froude velocity scaling law** used universally for ship model testing. The conversion logic: gravitational forces depend on length only (g is constant), so the velocity must scale as the square root of length to preserve the inertial-gravitational balance.

### 3.2 Derivation of Force Scaling (Cube Law)

**Step 1 — Definition of resistance coefficient:**

$$C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S}$$

**Step 2 — Equate coefficients at both scales (dimensional similarity):**

$$C_{T,m} = C_{T,s}$$

**Step 3 — Express model resistance:**

$$R_{T,m} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_m^2 S_m \cdot C_{T,m}$$

**Step 4 — Express full-scale resistance:**

$$R_{T,s} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_s^2 S_s \cdot C_{T,s}$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio R_{T,s} / R_{T,m}:**

$$\dfrac{R_{T,s}}{R_{T,m}} = \dfrac{U_s^2 S_s}{U_m^2 S_m}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute velocity scaling $U_s = U_m \sqrt{\lambda}$ and surface scaling $S_s = S_m \lambda^2$ (area ∝ length²):**

$$\dfrac{R_{T,s}}{R_{T,m}} = \dfrac{(U_m\sqrt{\lambda})^2 \cdot S_m\lambda^2}{U_m^2 \cdot S_m} = \lambda \cdot \lambda^2 = \lambda^3$$

**Step 7 — Therefore:**

$$\boxed{R_{T,s} = \lambda^3 \cdot R_{T,m} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R_{T,m} = \dfrac{R_{T,s}}{\lambda^3}}$$

This is the **force cube law**: resistance scales with the cube of the geometric scale ratio.

### 3.3 Derivation of Blockage Correction (Towing Tank)

**Step 1 — Define blockage ratio ε as the ratio of model submerged cross-section to tank cross-section:**

$$\epsilon = \dfrac{A_m}{A_t}$$

where $A_m$ = submerged frontal area of model, $A_t$ = tank cross-sectional area.

**Step 2 — Empirical correction (Schmied, 1933) — accelerated flow velocity:**
The flow must accelerate around the model, so the effective velocity exceeds the carriage velocity $U_c$:

$$U_\infty = U_c \cdot \left(1 + \epsilon\right)$$

**Step 3 — Higher-order (commonly used) correction — Taylor expansion:**
A widely-used quadratic correction:

$$U_\infty = U_c \cdot \left[1 + \epsilon + \epsilon^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Blockage correction factor for resistance (after substituting back into the dynamic pressure):**

$$C_T^{corrected} = C_T^{measured} \cdot \left(1 + \epsilon\right)^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Practical form using cross-sectional area ratio:**

$$\boxed{C_T^{corrected} = C_T^{measured} \cdot \left(\dfrac{A_t}{A_t + A_m}\right)^{n}}$$

where $n$ is typically 2 (2D correction) or 3 (3D correction with significant lift component).

The conversion logic: since dynamic pressure scales with $U^2$, a $k\%$ flow acceleration produces approximately $2k\%$ excess dynamic pressure, hence $2k\%$ inflated resistance.

### 3.4 Derivation of Reynolds Number at Model Conditions

**Step 1 — Given:** Ship length $L_s$, ship speed $U_s$, model scale $\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Compute model speed via Froude scaling:**

$$U_m = \dfrac{U_s}{\sqrt{\lambda}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute model length:**

$$L_m = \dfrac{L_s}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Compute model Reynolds number:**

$$R_{n,m} = \dfrac{U_m \cdot L_m}{\nu} = \dfrac{(U_s / \sqrt{\lambda}) \cdot (L_s / \lambda)}{\nu} = \dfrac{U_s L_s}{\nu \cdot \lambda^{3/2}}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize full-scale Reynolds number:**

$$R_{n,s} = \dfrac{U_s L_s}{\nu}$$

**Step 6 — Ratio:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{R_{n,m}}{R_{n,s}} = \lambda^{-3/2}}$$

**Numerical example (KTU pattern):** A 200 m tanker at 15 knots tested at 1:50 scale.

$$R_{n,s} = \dfrac{7.72 \times 200}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.544 \times 10^9$$

$$R_{n,m} = 1.544 \times 10^9 \times 50^{-1.5} = 1.544 \times 10^9 / 353.55 \approx 4.37 \times 10^6$$

This mismatch of ~350× in Reynolds number is the **fundamental scaling challenge** of ship model testing.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Test Arrangement

| Phenomena to Measure | Best Arrangement | Reason |
|----------------------|------------------|--------|
| Calm-water resistance | Carriage-towed towing tank | Long steady runs, low turbulence |
| Self-propulsion point | Carriage + propeller + dynamometer | Direct measurement of T, n |
| Wake field | CWC with LDA / PIV | Stationary model, easy probe access |
| Manoeuvring derivatives | PMM or Rotating arm | Controlled imposed motion |
| Wave-added resistance | Wave basin with wavemaker | Direct wave generation |
| Cavitation | Cavitation tunnel | Pressure-controlled de-aerated section |
| Slamming & green water | Wave basin + free-running model | Realistic 6-DOF |
| Ice-hull interaction | Ice tank with model + broken-ice field | Combined solid/fluid mechanics |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Towing Tank Test Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Carriage Rails along Tank] --> B[Main Carriage Assembly]
    B --> C[Dynamometer Mount]
    C --> D[Strut Connector]
    D --> E[Ship Model Hull]
    E --> F[Force Balance XYZ Plane]
    F --> G[Data Acquisition DAQ Unit]
    G --> H[Post-Processing Software]

    I[Wave Probe Upstream] --> G
    J[Pressure Tap Manifold] --> G
    K[Carriage Encoder Speed] --> G
    L[Beach Absorber Upstream End] -.reflects.-> I
    M[Wavemaker Downstream End] -.generates.-> I
    N[Water Conditioning System Temp and De-aeration] --> A
    O[Bridge Crane for Model Installation] --> E
```

### 4.2 Test Arrangement Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Hydrodynamic Problem] --> B{Vessel Operating Condition}
    B -- Steady straight-line --> C[Calm-water Resistance]
    B -- Oscillatory motion --> D[PMM Carriage Setup]
    B -- Pure rotation --> E[Rotating Arm Basin]
    B -- Wave environment --> F[Wave Basin with Wavemaker]

    C --> G{Propeller Involved?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Self-Propulsion Test Arrangement]
    G -- No --> I[Resistance-Only Arrangement]

    D --> J[Linear and Nonlinear Derivatives]
    E --> K[Steady Turning Derivatives]
    F --> L{6-DOF or Constrained?}
    L -- 6-DOF --> M[Free-Running Model with Radio Control]
    L -- Constrained --> N[Wave Basin Carriage System]

    H --> O[Output: Dimensional Coefficients]
    I --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Scale to Full Ship]
```

### 4.3 PMM Test Arrangement Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PMM Carriage Oscillating] --> B[Strut Hub]
    B --> C[Ship Model]
    C --> D[Instrumented Force Strut]
    D --> E[Multi-Component Balance]
    E --> F[Amplifier and Filter Bank]
    F --> G[Oscilloscope and DAQ]
    G --> H[Fourier Decomposition Module]

    I[Servo-Hydraulic Actuator] --> A
    J[Displacement Encoder] --> A
    K[Velocity Reference Signal] --> I
    L[Frequency Controller 0.05 to 0.30 Hz] --> K
```

### 4.4 Circulating Water Channel Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Inlet Contraction Nozzle] --> B[Test Section]
    B --> C[Diffuser]
    C --> D[Corner Turning Vanes]
    D --> E[Return Channel]
    E --> F[Variable Speed Pump]
    F --> G[Flow Straightener]
    G --> A

    B --> H[Stationary Model Mounted on Sting]
    H --> I[Multi-axis Force Balance]
    I --> J[Acquisition PC]

    K[Temperature Sensor] --> B
    L[Turbulence Intensity Probes] --> B
    M[Pressure Taps Test Section Walls] --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **flow loop** in a CWC is closed-circuit; **blockage correction** is critical because the test section is typically only 1–3 m wide, allowing only small models. The accelerated flow past the model produces artificially high resistance readings that must be corrected using the Schmied formula derived in Section 3.3.
