K_T = \frac{38.5}{1000 \times 225 \times 0.0016} = \frac{38.5}{360} = 0.1069
$$

Torque coefficient:
$$
K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D_m^5} = \frac{3.20}{1000 \times 225 \times (0.20)^5}
$$

$$
K_Q = \frac{3.20}{1000 \times 225 \times 0.00032} = \frac{3.20}{72} = 0.04444
$$

Open-water efficiency:
$$
\eta_O = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.833}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{0.1069}{0.04444}
$$

$$
\eta_O = \frac{0.833}{6.2832} \cdot 2.406 = 0.1326 \times 2.406 = 0.3191 = 31.9\%
$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Ship Propeller RPM:**

For the same $J$ on the ship:
$$
J = \frac{V_{A,s}}{n_s D_s} \Rightarrow n_s = \frac{V_{A,s}}{J \cdot D_s}
$$

Assuming the ship's advance velocity $V_{A,s}$ equals $V_s = 12$ m/s (ignoring wake fraction correction at this stage):
$$
n_s = \frac{12}{0.833 \times 5.0} = \frac{12}{4.165} = 2.881 \text{ rps} = 172.9 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- $J = 0.833$, $K_T = 0.1069$, $K_Q = 0.04444$, $\eta_O = 31.9\%$
- Ship rpm = $172.9$ rpm

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in Module 3 "Experiment" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\log_{10}$ in ITTC 78:** Students often write $\log_e$ (natural log) instead of $\log_{10}$. This gives a wildly wrong $C_F$. Examiner deducts **1 full mark**.
>
> 2. **Mixing model and ship fluid properties:** When extrapolating to sea water, use $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³ and $\nu_s = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. **Do not** use model-scale water properties in the ship resistance calculation.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert $C_R$ to ship scale:** $C_R$ is Froude-similar (assumed equal), but the **force** $R_{T,s}$ requires the ship wetted surface area $S_s$, not $S_m$.
>
> 4. **Confusing $L_{PP}$ with $L_{WL}$:** Use the same length reference consistently. Most KTU problems use $L_{PP}$ (between perpendiculars).
>
> 5. **Sign errors in dimensional analysis:** In Buckingham Pi derivation, equate the powers of M, L, T **carefully**. Most common error is in the L-equation. Examiner checks each step.
>
> 6. **In self-propulsion problems, do not confuse $V$ (ship speed) with $V_A$ (advance speed).** $V_A = V(1 - w)$, and $w$ is what we are trying to find.
>
> 7. **Power units:** KTU expects SI (Watts), but answers in kiloWatts (kW) or MegaWatts (MW) are acceptable if clearly stated. **No credit** if unit is missing.
>
> 8. **Show ALL intermediate steps:** A numerical problem in KTU Part B carries 7 marks; the answer alone carries at most **1 mark**. The 6 remaining marks are for intermediate calculations. **Show every line.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Experiment**

- **Froude Number** is the **primary scaling parameter** for ship model testing because wave-making resistance (the dominant component at service speed) is a free-surface gravity-driven phenomenon.
- **Reynolds Number similarity cannot be simultaneously satisfied** with Froude similarity at practical model scales — this is the **Froude-Reynolds scaling dilemma**.
- The **ITTC 1978 friction line** is: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} R_e - 2)^2$.
- **Resistance decomposition:** $C_T = C_F + C_R = (1+k)\,C_F + C_R$ (with form factor).
- **Resistance scales as** $R_m = \lambda^3 R_s$; **Power scales as** $P_m = \lambda^{3.5} P_s$.
- **Froude velocity scaling:** $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$.
- **Buckingham Pi theorem** for ship resistance yields 3 groups: $C_T$, $R_e$, $F_n$ → functional relation $C_T = f(R_e, F_n)$.
- **Advance coefficient:** $J = V_A / (nD)$; **Thrust coefficient:** $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$; **Torque coefficient:** $K_Q = Q / (\rho n^2 D^5)$.
- **Open-water efficiency:** $\eta_O = (J / 2\pi)(K_T / K_Q)$.
- **Self-propulsion test** determines wake fraction $w = (V - V_A)/V$ and thrust deduction $t = (T - R_T)/T$.
- **Hull efficiency:** $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$. **Propulsive efficiency:** $\eta_D = \eta_H \eta_O \eta_R$.
- **Effective power:** $P_E = R_T \cdot V$.
- **Model scale $\lambda$** typically ranges from $1/20$ (small boats, high Re) to $1/50$ (large tankers, low Re).
- **Manoeuvring derivatives** ($Y_v$, $Y_r$, $N_v$, $N_r$) are measured by **PMM tests** and **rotating arm tests**.
- **Weber number** $W_e$ becomes significant for models below ~$L_m = 1$ m (surface tension effects on wave formation).
- **Cavitation number** $\sigma_c = (p_\infty - p_v)/(\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2)$ governs cavitation experiments.
- **ITTC 2017 procedure** refines the 1978 method by accounting for form factor variation with $F_n$; useful for advanced topics.
- **Always state units explicitly** in numerical answers (N, kN, m/s, m², etc.).
- **Prohaska method** determines $(1+k)$ from two resistance tests at different $F_n$ (slope of $C_T F_n^2$ vs $F_n^2$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Results
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUE IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Results # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Experimental Techniques in Marine Hydrodynamics**, the **"Results"** of an experiment constitute the rigorously processed, validated, and interpreted body of quantitative and qualitative information obtained from measurements conducted on physical models of marine systems (hulls, propellers, appendages, offshore structures, etc.) and presented in a form that allows direct comparison with theoretical predictions, empirical correlations, or Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations. The "Results" phase of an experimental campaign is **NOT** the raw data dump from instruments. It is the outcome of a structured chain: **Raw Signal → Calibration Correction → Data Reduction → Uncertainty Quantification → Non-Dimensionalization → Graphical/Tabular Presentation → Physical Interpretation**. Formally, the **Experimentally Derived Result** for any marine hydrodynamic quantity $Q$ is reported as: $$Q = \bar{Q} \pm U_Q$$ where $\bar{Q}$ is the **best estimate** (usually the mean of repeated trials) and $U_Q$ is the **expanded uncertainty** at a stated confidence level (typically **95%** for KTU/ISO-standard marine testing). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, the "Results" module emphasizes three pillars: (1) **Data Reduction & Non-Dimensionalization**, (2) **Uncertainty/Error Analysis**, and (3) **Comparative Validation with Empirical/Theoretical Benchmarks**. Marks are heavily awarded for correct dimensional analysis and proper propagation of uncertainties. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **doctor examining a patient**. - The raw sensor reading (blood pressure gauge showing "135/82") is like the **raw experimental data**. - Adjusting that reading for the patient's age, weight, and the instrument's known calibration drift is like **data reduction and calibration correction**. - Saying "the blood pressure is approximately 135 ± 3 mmHg with 95% confidence" is like a properly reported **experimental result with uncertainty**. - Plotting the patient's BP against a population chart and concluding "the patient is in the high-normal range" is like the **physical interpretation** of the results. **In Marine Hydrodynamics:** - The "patient" is the ship model in the towing tank or circulating water channel. - The "diagnostic tests" are force balances, pressure transducers, Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV), wave probes, etc. - The "doctor's report" is the final **Results** section — with resistance coefficients, wake fractions, propulsive efficiencies, and their associated uncertainties. > [!NOTE] > **Why Results Matter:** No marine engineering design decision (hull form optimization, propeller selection, seakeeping assessment) can be trusted without properly documented experimental results. The **International Towing Tank Conference (ITTC)** and **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** mandate strict uncertainty reporting for all model tests used in ship design verification. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Relevance | |-----------|--------|----------------------|-----------| | Density of fresh water | $\rho_{fw}$ | **1000 kg/m³** | Towing tank calibration | | Density of salt water | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025 kg/m³** | Sea trials correlation | | Kinematic viscosity (fresh, 20°C) | $\nu$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** | Reynolds number calc | | Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Froude number calc | | Standard confidence level | — | **95%** | Uncertainty reporting | | Coverage factor (95%) | $k$ | **2.0** (approx) | Expanded uncertainty | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Results" Pipeline — Structured Logic The transformation from raw measurement to publishable marine hydrodynamic result follows a strict sequence: 1. **Raw Data Acquisition:** Time-series voltage/current signals from load cells, pressure transducers, LDA/PIV systems. 2. **Calibration Correction:** Apply calibration matrices (linear, polynomial) to convert signals to engineering units (Newtons, Pascals, m/s). 3. **Environmental Correction:** Correct for temperature (affecting $\rho$ and $\nu$), blockage in the towing tank, and free-surface effects. 4. **Data Reduction:** Convert measured forces/moments into non-dimensional force coefficients using reference quantities (e.g., $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S$). 5. **Uncertainty Quantification:** Apply Kline-McClintock or GUM (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement) methodology to propagate individual instrument uncertainties to the final result. 6. **Statistical Analysis:** Compute mean, standard deviation, confidence intervals from repeated runs (typically 3–5 runs per condition per ITTC). 7. **Non-Dimensionalization:** Present results as $C_T$, $C_F$, $C_P$, $\eta_0$, etc., versus $Re$, $Fr$, $\sigma$ (slenderness ratio), etc. 8. **Visualization & Interpretation:** Plots, trend lines, comparison with ITTC-1978, Holtrop-Mennen, or CFD predictions. ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step - **Why non-dimensionalize?** Because model-scale results must be extrapolated to full-scale ships. Non-dimensional coefficients (when Reynolds-similarity is maintained) allow this scaling — this is the **cornerstone of ship model testing**. - **Why propagate uncertainty?** Because knowing that $R_T = 0.45$ N is meaningless without knowing whether the true value lies between 0.43 and 0.47. **ITTC Quality System 7.5-04-01-01** mandates uncertainty reporting. - **Why repeat runs?** Random errors (turbulence, slight alignment, electronic noise) only become statistically meaningful through repeated trials. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Re_L = \dfrac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number based on length $L$ and speed $V$ | dimensionless | | 2 | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | Froude number (gravity-inertial ratio) | dimensionless | | 3 | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S}$ | Total resistance coefficient | dimensionless | | 4 | $C_F = \dfrac{R_F}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S}$ | Frictional resistance coefficient | dimensionless | | 5 | $C_P = \dfrac{P}{\rho n^3 D^5}$ | Propeller thrust/power coefficient | dimensionless | | 6 | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | Propeller thrust loading coefficient | dimensionless | | 7 | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{J \cdot K_T}{2\pi K_Q}$ | Propeller open-water efficiency | dimensionless | | 8 | $J = \dfrac{V_A}{n D}$ | Advance coefficient | dimensionless | | 9 | $U_Q = k \cdot u_c = k \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(\dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial x_i} u_{x_i}\right)^2}$ | Combined standard uncertainty (RSS method) | same as $Q$ | | 10 | $U = k \cdot u_c$, with $k=2$ at 95% | Expanded uncertainty | same as $Q$ | | 11 | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Sample mean | same as $x$ | | 12 | $s_x = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Sample standard deviation | same as $x$ | | 13 | $U_{\bar{x}} = t_{95,\,N-1} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Uncertainty of the mean (Student's t) | same as $x$ | | 14 | $C_{F,ITTC} = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$ | ITTC 1957 frictional line (model-ship correlation) | dimensionless | | 15 | $C_{F,ITTC78} = \dfrac{0.455}{(\log_{10} Re)^2.58}$ | ITTC 1978 frictional line | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** You will almost certainly be asked to compute a non-dimensional coefficient from raw force/speed data, OR to propagate uncertainties through a derived quantity. Memorize the Reynolds, Froude, $C_T$, $K_T$, $K_Q$, and uncertainty RSS equations — they are the **highest frequency formulas** in KTU marine exams. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hull Form Optimization:** Resistance curves $C_T$ vs. $Re/Fr$ guide hull form selection during concept design. - **Propeller Design:** Open-water diagrams ($K_T, K_Q, \eta_0$ vs. $J$) are essential inputs to Wageningen B-Series or Kaplan propeller series selection. - **Regulatory Compliance:** Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) require model-test results with documented uncertainties for type approval. - **CFD Validation:** Model test results serve as the **gold standard benchmark** for validating Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) and Large Eddy Simulation (LES) codes. - **Full-Scale Prediction:** Model results are extrapolated to full scale using the **ITTC 1978 correlation procedure** (form factor $k$, roughness allowance $\Delta C_F$). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Uncertainty Propagation (Root-Sum-Square Method) Consider a result $Q$ that is a function of $N$ independent measured variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N$: $$Q = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N)$$ **Step 1 — Taylor Series Expansion (1st order):** We expand $Q$ about the mean values $\bar{x}_i$ using a first-order multivariate Taylor series. For a small perturbation $\delta x_i$ in $x_i$: $$\delta Q = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial x_i} \delta x_i$$ **Step 2 — Variance of Q:** Since the variables are independent, the variance of $Q$ is the sum of squared contributions: $$u_c^2(Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(\dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial x_i}\right)^2 u^2(x_i)$$ **Step 3 — Combined Standard Uncertainty:** $$u_c(Q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(\dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial x_i}\right)^2 u^2(x_i)}$$ This is the famous **Kline-McClintock / RSS (Root-Sum-Square)** formula. The partial derivative $\frac{\partial Q}{\partial x_i}$ is called the **sensitivity coefficient** $\theta_i$. **Step 4 — Expanded Uncertainty:** Multiply by the coverage factor $k$ (for 95% confidence and large $N$, $k \approx 2$): $$U(Q) = k \cdot u_c(Q)$$ **Step 5 — Final Reported Form:** $$Q = \bar{Q} \pm U(Q) \quad \text{(at 95\% confidence)}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Uncertainty in Total Resistance Coefficient Let the result of interest be the **total resistance coefficient**: $$C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S}$$ **Step 1 — Identify the variables:** - $x_1 = R_T$ (measured resistance, N) - $x_2 = \rho$ (water density, kg/m³) - $x_3 = V$ (towing speed, m/s) - $x_4 = S$ (wetted surface area, m²) **Step 2 — Compute sensitivity coefficients:** $$\dfrac{\partial C_T}{\partial R_T} = \dfrac{1}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S} = \dfrac{2 C_T}{R_T}$$ $$\dfrac{\partial C_T}{\partial \rho} = -\dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho^2 V^2 S} = -\dfrac{C_T}{\rho}$$ $$\dfrac{\partial C_T}{\partial V} = -\dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3 S} \cdot 2 = -\dfrac{2 C_T}{V}$$ $$\dfrac{\partial C_T}{\partial S} = -\dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S^2} = -\dfrac{C_T}{S}$$ **Step 3 — Assemble the RSS equation:** $$u_c(C_T) = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{2 C_T}{R_T} u_{R_T}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{C_T}{\rho} u_{\rho}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{2 C_T}{V} u_V\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{C_T}{S} u_S\right)^2}$$ **Step 4 — Relative (Normalized) Form:** Dividing throughout by $C_T$: $$\dfrac{u_c(C_T)}{C_T} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{u_{R_T}}{R_T}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{u_{\rho}}{\rho}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{2 u_V}{V}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{u_S}{S}\right)^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** Notice that the speed $V$ appears with a factor of **2**. This means a 1% error in towing speed translates into a **2% error** in $C_T$. This is why **towing carriage speed calibration** is one of the most critical checks in any model test. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Full Reduction from Raw Data to Final Result **Given (from a model test of a frigate hull at scale $\lambda = 1:30$):** - Measured resistance (mean of 5 runs): $\bar{R}_T = 14.2$ N - Standard deviation of resistance: $s_{R_T} = 0.18$ N - Towing speed: $V = 1.85$ m/s, uncertainty $u_V = 0.005$ m/s - Water density: $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, uncertainty $u_\rho = 1.5$ kg/m³ - Wetted surface area: $S = 4.20$ m², uncertainty $u_S = 0.01$ m² **Step 1 — Compute the mean $C_T$:** $$C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S} = \dfrac{14.2}{0.5 \times 998 \times (1.85)^2 \times 4.20}$$ $$C_T = \dfrac{14.2}{0.5 \times 998 \times 3.4225 \times 4.20} = \dfrac{14.2}{7177.1} = 1.979 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 2 — Uncertainty of the mean of $R_T$ (using $t_{95,4} = 2.776$):** $$U_{\bar{R}_T} = t \cdot \dfrac{s_{R_T}}{\sqrt{N}} = 2.776 \times \dfrac{0.18}{\sqrt{5}} = 2.776 \times 0.0805 = 0.2235 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Compute each term of the RSS uncertainty:** $$\dfrac{U_{R_T}}{R_T} = \dfrac{0.2235}{14.2} = 0.01574 \implies (0.01574)^2 = 2.478 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\dfrac{U_\rho}{\rho} = \dfrac{1.5}{998} = 0.001503 \implies (0.001503)^2 = 2.259 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\dfrac{2 U_V}{V} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.005}{1.85} = 0.005405 \implies (0.005405)^2 = 2.922 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$\dfrac{U_S}{S} = \dfrac{0.01}{4.20} = 0.002381 \implies (0.002381)^2 = 5.669 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 4 — Sum and take the square root:** $$\dfrac{u_c(C_T)}{C_T} = \sqrt{2.478 \times 10^{-4} + 2.259 \times 10^{-6} + 2.922 \times 10^{-5} + 5.669 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$= \sqrt{2.860 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01691$$ **Step 5 — Absolute standard uncertainty:** $$u_c(C_T) = 0.01691 \times 1.979 \times 10^{-3} = 3.347 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 6 — Expanded uncertainty (k = 2):** $$U(C_T) = 2 \times 3.347 \times 10^{-5} = 6.69 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 7 — Final Reported Result:** $$\boxed{C_T = (1.979 \pm 0.067) \times 10^{-3} \text{ at 95\% confidence}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Gold:** Whenever a numerical problem is asked in the KTU examination, the examiner expects you to (a) reduce to a non-dimensional form, (b) propagate uncertainties using RSS, and (c) report with proper confidence statement. Skipping any of the three costs you **3–4 marks per sub-part**. --- ## 3.4 Reynolds Number Extrapolation (Model to Full Scale) The **Froude similarity** used in ship model testing preserves wave-making but **not** viscous similarity. Therefore frictional resistance must be extrapolated using the **ITTC 1978 method**: $$C_{F,s} = \dfrac{0.455}{(\log_{10} Re_s)^{2.58}} \quad \text{(full-scale friction coefficient)}$$ $$C_{F,m} = \dfrac{0.455}{(\log_{10} Re_m)^{2.58}} \quad \text{(model-scale friction coefficient)}$$ The form factor $k$ is determined from a **provisional resistance increase** or **slender-body method**: $$1+k = \dfrac{C_{T,m}}{C_{F,m}} \quad \text{(at low Froude, where wave-making is small)}$$ Full-scale resistance: $$R_{T,s} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_s V_s^2 S_s \cdot \left[\dfrac{C_{F,s} \cdot (1+k) + C_R}{(1+k)}\right]$$ where $C_R$ is the residual resistance coefficient, assumed Froude-similar. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Data-to-Results Processing Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Sensor Signals
Load Cell, PIV, Pressure Tap] --> B[Calibration Correction
Polynomial Matrix] B --> C[Environmental Adjustment
Temperature, Blockage] C --> D[Time-Averaging &
Statistical Reduction] D --> E{Data Reduction} E --> F[Force / Moment
Extraction] E --> G[Velocity / Pressure
Field Extraction] F --> H[Non-Dimensionalization
C_T, K_T, eta_0] G --> H H --> I[Uncertainty Propagation
RSS / Kline-McClintock] I --> J[Graphical Presentation
Curves, Polars, Contours] J --> K[Comparative Validation
ITTC, Holtrop, CFD] K --> L[Final Reported Results
Q-bar plus minus U_Q] style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style L fill:#166534,stroke:#166534,color:#ffffff style I fill:#991b1b,stroke:#991b1b,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Uncertainty Source Hierarchy in a Typical Resistance Test ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Final Result C_T] --> A1[Resistance Force
u_RT] R --> A2[Towing Speed
u_V] R --> A3[Water Density
u_rho] R --> A4[Wetted Surface
u_S] A1 --> A1a[Load Cell Calibration] A1 --> A1b[Alignment Offset] A1 --> A1c[Buoyancy Correction] A2 --> A2a[Carriage Encoder] A2 --> A2b[Temperature Drift] A3 --> A3a[Salinity Probe] A3 --> A3b[Temperature Probe] A4 --> A4a[Hull Form Offset] A4 --> A4b[Scale Measurement] style R fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style A1 fill:#991b1b,stroke:#991b1b,color:#ffffff style A2 fill:#991b1b,stroke:#991b1b,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Validation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR M[Model Test Results] --> P[Plot C_T vs Re/Fr] P --> Q{Cross-Check} Q --> Q1[ITTC 1978 Line] Q --> Q2[Holtrop-Mennen] Q --> Q3[CFD Simulation] Q --> Q4[Empirical Series
Wageningen B-Series] Q1 --> V[Final Validation Report] Q2 --> V Q3 --> V Q4 --> V style M fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style V fill:#14532d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram of the "Results" Deliverable Package ```mermaid flowchart TD OUT[Results Deliverable] --> O1[Tabulated Non-Dim
Coefficients] OUT --> O2[Graphs and Polars
with Error Bars] OUT --> O3[Uncertainty Budget
Table] OUT --> O4[Comparative Curves
vs Theory/CFD] OUT --> O5[Physical Interpretation
and Conclusions] style OUT fill:#1f2937,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff style O1 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff style O2 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff style O3 fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the term "expanded uncertainty" and explain its significance in reporting experimental results in marine hydrodynamics.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** Definition: Expanded uncertainty $U$ is the combined standard uncertainty $u_c$ multiplied by a coverage factor $k$ (typically $k = 2$ for 95% confidence), expressed as $U = k \cdot u_c$. - **(1 mark)** It defines an interval around the measured value within which the true value of the measurand is expected to lie with a stated level of confidence. - **(1 mark)** Significance: It is mandated by ITTC Quality System and ISO/IEC Guide 98-3 (GUM) for all credible marine model tests. Without expanded uncertainty, a reported resistance coefficient (e.g., $C_T = 3.2 \times 10^{-3}$) is **incomplete and unusable** for design verification or CFD validation. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Why are model test results in marine hydrodynamics expressed in non-dimensional form? Give two examples of such non-dimensional coefficients.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1.5 marks)** Reason: Non-dimensional coefficients allow the **scaling of results from model to full scale** and **comparability** across different model sizes and test facilities. They isolate the underlying physical phenomenon (e.g., wave-making, viscous drag) from the absolute size of the test. - **(1.5 marks)** Examples: - $C_T = R_T / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S)$ — total resistance coefficient - $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$ — propeller thrust coefficient - (Also acceptable: $C_F$, $Fr$, $Re$, $\eta_0$, $K_Q$.) --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **(a)** A ship model of length $L_m = 5.0$ m is towed at $V_m = 1.85$ m/s in fresh water ($\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.005 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). The measured mean resistance is $R_T = 14.2$ N with standard deviation $s_{R_T} = 0.18$ N over $N = 5$ runs. The wetted surface area is $S = 4.20$ m² ± 0.01 m². Compute the **Reynolds number** and the **total resistance coefficient** $C_T$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the uncertainties in towing speed and density are $u_V = 0.005$ m/s and $u_\rho = 1.5$ kg/m³ respectively, determine the **expanded uncertainty** in $C_T$ at 95% confidence using the RSS method. State the final result. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Compute Reynolds number (1 mark):** $$Re_m = \dfrac{V_m L_m}{\nu} = \dfrac{1.85 \times 5.0}{1.005 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.204 \times 10^{6}$$ **Step 2 — Write $C_T$ formula (1 mark):** $$C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S}$$ **Step 3 — Compute denominator (1 mark):** $$\tfrac{1}{2} \times 998 \times (1.85)^2 \times 4.20 = 0.5 \times 998 \times 3.4225 \times 4.20 = 7177.1 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $C_T$ (1 mark):** $$C_T = \dfrac{14.2}{7177.1} = 1.979 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 5 — Identify Froude number and physical regime (1 mark):** $$Fr = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \dfrac{1.85}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 5.0}} = \dfrac{1.85}{7.004} = 0.264$$ **Step 6 — State result with units (1 mark):** $$\boxed{Re_m = 9.20 \times 10^{6}, \quad C_T = 1.98 \times 10^{-3}, \quad Fr = 0.264}$$ **Step 7 — Physical interpretation (1 mark):** The model operates in the **subcritical turbulent regime** at moderate Froude number, indicating both frictional and wavemaking contributions are significant. This is typical of a frigate-type hull form at $Fr \approx 0.26$. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Uncertainty of the mean $R_T$ (1 mark):** Using $t_{95,4} = 2.776$: $$U_{R_T} = t \cdot \dfrac{s_{R_T}}{\sqrt{N}} = 2.776 \times \dfrac{0.18}{\sqrt{5}} = 0.2235 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — RSS formula for $C_T$ (1 mark):** $$\dfrac{u_c(C_T)}{C_T} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{U_{R_T}}{R_T}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{u_\rho}{\rho}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{2 u_V}{V}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{u_S}{S}\right)^2}$$ **Step 3 — Compute relative contributions (2 marks):** - $\dfrac{U_{R_T}}{R_T} = \dfrac{0.2235}{14.2} = 0.01574 \implies 2.478 \times 10^{-4}$ - $\dfrac{u_\rho}{\rho} = \dfrac{1.5}{998} = 0.001503 \implies 2.259 \times 10^{-6}$ - $\dfrac{2 u_V}{V} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.005}{1.85} = 0.005405 \implies 2.922 \times 10^{-5}$ - $\dfrac{u_S}{S} = \dfrac{0.01}{4.20} = 0.002381 \implies 5.669 \times 10^{-6}$ **Step 4 — Sum and square root (1 mark):** $$\dfrac{u_c(C_T)}{C_T} = \sqrt{2.860 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01691$$ **Step 5 — Combined standard uncertainty (1 mark):** $$u_c(C_T) = 0.01691 \times 1.979 \times 10^{-3} = 3.347 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 6 — Expanded uncertainty with $k=2$ (1 mark):** $$U(C_T) = 2 \times 3.347 \times 10^{-5} = 6.69 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 7 — Final reported result:** $$\boxed{C_T = (1.98 \pm 0.07) \times 10^{-3} \text{ at 95\% confidence level}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **ITTC 1978 extrapolation procedure** for predicting full-scale resistance from model test results. Clearly state the role of the **form factor $k$** and the **roughness allowance $\Delta C_F$**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A model tested at $Re_m = 9.2 \times 10^6$ gives $C_{F,m} = 3.40 \times 10^{-3}$ and $C_{T,m} = 4.95 \times 10^{-3}$. The full-scale ship operates at $Re_s = 4.0 \times 10^8$. Compute the **form factor** $k$ and the **residual resistance coefficient** $C_R$ (assuming Froude similarity). Predict the **full-scale frictional coefficient** $C_{F,s}$ using the ITTC 1978 line. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)** **Step 1 (2 marks) — ITTC 1978 Procedure Overview:** The ITTC 1978 correlation predicts full-scale resistance by: 1. Running the model in the towing tank (Froude similarity maintained). 2. Determining the form factor $k$ and residual resistance $C_R$ from model data. 3. Recomputing frictional resistance at full-scale Reynolds number. 4. Adding roughness allowance for full-scale hull roughness. 5. Adding residual resistance (assumed Froude-similar) to get total full-scale resistance. **Step 2 (2 marks) — Form Factor $k$:** - $k$ accounts for the viscous pressure resistance of the **3D hull form** that is not captured by the 2D flat-plate frictional line. - Determined by: $\quad 1+k = \dfrac{C_{T,m}}{C_{F,m}}$ evaluated at low $Fr$ (where $C_R \to 0$). - Typical merchant ship: $k \in [0.15, 0.40]$. **Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Roughness Allowance $\Delta C_F$:** - $\Delta C_F$ is the **added frictional resistance** due to full-scale hull roughness (paint degradation, marine fouling, weld seams). - ITTC 1978 formula: $\Delta C_F = \left[\dfrac{0.044}{(\log_{10} Re_s - 2)^2} - 0.000125\right] \times 10^{-3}$ - Typical value: $\Delta C_F \approx 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ for a 5-year-old painted hull. **Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Final full-scale prediction:** $$C_{T,s} = \dfrac{C_{F,s} \cdot (1+k) + C_R + \Delta C_F \cdot S_s / S_{reference}}{(1+k)}$$ or, equivalently: $$C_{T,s} = (1+k) C_{F,s} + C_R + \Delta C_F$$ --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Form factor (2 marks):** $$1 + k = \dfrac{C_{T,m}}{C_{F,m}} = \dfrac{4.95 \times 10^{-3}}{3.40 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.4559$$ $$\boxed{k = 0.456}$$ **Step 2 — Residual resistance $C_R$ at model scale (2 marks):** $$C_R = C_{T,m} - (1+k) \cdot C_{F,m} = 4.95 \times 10^{-3} - 1.4559 \times 3.40 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$C_R = 4.95 \times 10^{-3} - 4.950 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0$$ (At very low $Fr$ the residual is indeed near zero — consistent with assumption.) **Step 3 — Full-scale $C_{F,s}$ using ITTC 1978 (2 marks):** $$C_{F,s} = \dfrac{0.455}{(\log_{10} Re_s)^{2.58}} = \dfrac{0.455}{(\log_{10} 4.0 \times 10^8)^{2.58}}$$ $$\log_{10}(4.0 \times 10^8) = 8.602$$ $$C_{F,s} = \dfrac{0.455}{(8.602)^{2.58}} = \dfrac{0.455}{240.5} = 1.892 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 4 — State final result (1 mark):** $$\boxed{k = 0.456, \quad C_R \approx 0, \quad C_{F,s} = 1.89 \times 10^{-3}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 on speed uncertainty** in $C_T$ propagation — costs 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing $C_{F,ITTC1957}$ with $C_{F,ITTC1978}$** — the 1978 line has a 0.455 coefficient and a 2.58 exponent, not 0.075 / 2. > 3. **Not converting to non-dimensional form before propagating uncertainty** — examiners award marks for setting up the equation symbolically, not just plugging numbers. > 4. **Omitting the coverage factor statement** in the final answer — "at 95% confidence" is mandatory. > 5. **Using $N$ instead of $N-1$** in sample standard deviation — Bessel's correction is required. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Results ≠ Raw data** — Results are *processed, non-dimensionalized, uncertainty-quantified* values. - ✅ **Always report $C_T$, $C_F$, $K_T$ etc. as $Q = \bar{Q} \pm U_Q$** with a stated confidence level. - ✅ **Master the RSS uncertainty equation** — it is the single most-tested formula in this module. - ✅ **Speed uncertainty contributes TWICE** in force-coefficient uncertainty — calibration is critical. - ✅ **Use $k = 2$** for 95% expanded uncertainty unless otherwise specified. - ✅ **ITTC 1978 line:** $C_F = 0.455 / (\log_{10} Re)^{2.58}$ — memorize it. - ✅ **Form factor $k = C_{T,m}/C_{F,m} - 1$** at low $Fr$; **residual $C_R$ is Froude-similar.** - ✅ **Froude similarity** is preserved in ship model testing; **Reynolds similarity is NOT** — hence the need for extrapolation. - ✅ **Towing speed, force balance calibration, and water temperature** are the dominant uncertainty sources. - ✅ **Repeat runs** ($N \geq 3$, typically $N=5$) and use **Student's $t$** to compute mean uncertainty. - ✅ **Comparison with empirical/theoretical/CFD benchmarks** is the final step in any results analysis.

topics.title: Wake Measurements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wake Measurements

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Marine Hydrodynamics** and **KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST865)**, a **Wake** is defined as the region of disturbed fluid flow immediately downstream of a body (such as a ship hull, submarine, propeller, or submerged appendage) where the fluid velocity has been altered—both in magnitude and direction—relative to the free-stream approach velocity. The quantitative measure of this disturbance is known as the **velocity deficit**, expressed as the difference between the undisturbed free-stream velocity ($V_{\infty}$) and the local wake velocity ($V_w$).

**Wake Measurement** refers to the systematic experimental determination of the velocity field, pressure distribution, and turbulence characteristics within this wake region, typically conducted in a **towing tank**, **circulating water channel**, or **cavitation tunnel** using calibrated instrumentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** Wake measurements form a critical experimental input for determining the **propulsive coefficients** of a ship (effective wake fraction $w_T$, thrust deduction $t$, hull efficiency $\eta_H$). These coefficients are essential for matching the propeller to the hull during design.

## 1.2 Standard Terminology (KTU 2024)

| Term | Definition | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Free-Stream Velocity** | Velocity of the undisturbed flow approaching the body | $V_{\infty}$ |
| **Wake Velocity** | Local reduced velocity in the wake | $V_w$ |
| **Velocity Deficit** | $V_{\infty} - V_w$ (m/s) | $\Delta V$ |
| **Wake Fraction** | Non-dimensional velocity deficit at propeller disc | $w$ |
| **Nominal Wake** | Wake at midship section based on model self-propulsion | $w_N$ |
| **Taylor Wake** | Wake measured at a specific longitudinal station (e.g., $x/L$) | — |
| **Effective Wake** | Wake accounting for propeller rotation and non-uniformity | $w_T$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The River Paddle Swimmer:**
> Imagine you are swimming in a calm river, pushing a paddle in front of you. You feel a **drag**. Now look at the water *behind* your body — it is no longer calm. It swirls, eddies form, and the water moves in irregular patterns. The "shadow" of disturbed water trailing behind you is your **wake**.

Applied to a **ship**:
- The ship is the **paddle**, moving through still water (or stationary ship in moving water, as in a towing tank).
- The water just behind the **stern (rear)** is the **wake**.
- This wake has **less velocity** than the surrounding water because the ship "robbed" some momentum from it.
- The propeller of the ship operates *inside* this slower-moving wake.

**Why does this matter for KTU students?**
The slower water at the propeller disc means the propeller works against a lower velocity ($V_A < V_S$), which directly affects **thrust production**, **fuel efficiency**, and **propulsive efficiency** ($\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R$).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Constants for Wake Analysis:**
> - **Reference Velocity ($V_{\infty}$):** typically **2.0–4.0 m/s** in model-scale towing tanks (Froude-scaled).
> - **Model-to-Prototype Scale Ratio ($\lambda$):** typically **1:25 to 1:40** for commercial ship models.
> - **Kinematic Viscosity of Water ($\nu$):** $\approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s at 20°C.
> - **Density of Fresh Water ($\rho$):** **1000 kg/m³** (towing tanks); seawater **1025 kg/m³**.
> - **Wake measurement station:** typically at **0.5L to 0.55L abaft the FP (Forward Perpendicular)** in model tests.
> - **Mean line of wake measurement:** at **0.7R radius** from the shaft centerline, where R = propeller radius.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of the Wake

The wake behind a marine vessel is generated through three primary physical mechanisms operating simultaneously:

1. **Viscous Boundary Layer Separation** — As the ship hull moves through water, the **viscous boundary layer** thickens from bow to stern. Due to the adverse pressure gradient along the afterbody, the boundary layer separates, forming a **turbulent wake** characterized by:
   - Mean velocity deficit
   - Turbulent fluctuations ($u', v', w'$)
   - Vortical structures (horseshoe vortices at bilges, stern vortices)

2. **Wave Generation** — At non-zero Froude numbers, energy is radiated as **surface gravity waves** (Kelvin wave pattern), which absorb energy and create a secondary wave-induced wake.

3. **Three-Dimensional Pressure Field** — The varying cross-section of the hull induces lateral velocity components, generating a **non-axisymmetric** wake.

## 2.2 Types of Wake (Critical for KTU 2024)

| Wake Type | Description | Measurement Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Wake ($w_N$)** | Based on model self-propulsion test | Self-propulsion test (no propeller) |
| **Effective Wake ($w_T$)** | Accounts for propeller-hull interaction | Empirical correlation: $w_T = 0.5 w_N + 0.5 w_P$ |
| **Taylor Wake** | Wake at a specific longitudinal station $x$ | Wake traverse with Pitot tube |
| **Wave Wake** | Surface wave pattern | Wave probes, PIV |
| **Frictional Wake** | Due to viscous shear | Hot-film anemometry |
| **Propeller Slipstream** | Wake of accelerated flow behind propeller | 5-hole Pitot probe |

## 2.3 Mathematical Framework

The fundamental non-dimensional coefficient for wake measurement is the **wake fraction**:

$$w = \frac{V_{\infty} - V_w}{V_{\infty}} = 1 - \frac{V_w}{V_{\infty}}$$

Where:
- $V_{\infty}$ = free-stream velocity (m/s)
- $V_w$ = local wake velocity at the propeller disc plane (m/s)

### 2.3.1 Mean Wake Fraction (Integrated)

When the wake is non-uniform across the propeller disc, we use the **rotor-averaged wake fraction**:

$$\bar{w} = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{R} \left( 1 - \frac{V_w(r,\theta)}{V_{\infty}} \right) r \, dr \, d\theta$$

### 2.3.2 Effective Wake Fraction

The **effective wake fraction** ($w_T$) is the most operationally important quantity, representing the wake at the propeller disc considering the ship's self-propulsion point:

$$w_T = \frac{V_S - V_A}{V_S}$$

Where $V_S$ is ship speed and $V_A$ is the **advance velocity** at the propeller disc.

### 2.3.3 Momentum Thickness of Wake

A key integral parameter used in boundary layer analysis:

$$\theta_{wake} = \int_{0}^{\delta} \frac{u}{V_{\infty}} \left( 1 - \frac{u}{V_{\infty}} \right) dy$$

This is critical for momentum loss analysis in the wake.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — Wake Measurement Equations:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w = 1 - \frac{V_w}{V_{\infty}}$ | Wake fraction (local) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $w_T = 0.5 w_N + 0.5 w_P$ | Effective wake (Harvald formula) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\bar{w} = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int (1 - V_w/V_{\infty}) r dr d\theta$ | Mean wake fraction | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\theta_{wake} = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{V_\infty}(1 - u/V_\infty) dy$ | Momentum thickness | m |
| 5 | $Fr = \frac{V_{\infty}}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude number (wave scaling) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $Re = \frac{V_{\infty} L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (viscous scaling) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_{A} = V_S (1 - w_T)$ | Advance velocity at propeller | m/s |
| 8 | $T = \rho K_T n^2 D^4$ | Thrust from $K_T$ curve | N |
| 9 | $P_D = \rho K_Q n^3 D^5$ | Delivered power (from $K_Q$) | W |
| 10 | $\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w_T}$ | Hull efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $C_P = \frac{\Delta P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^2}$ | Pressure coefficient (Pitot-static) | Dimensionless |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is wake measurement critical in marine engineering?**
> 1. **Propeller-Hull Matching:** The effective wake determines the operating point (advance coefficient $J = V_A/nD$) of the propeller.
> 2. **Fuel Efficiency Optimization:** Minimizing wake non-uniformity reduces blade-rate vibrations.
> 3. **Cavitation Avoidance:** Wake non-uniformity causes sheet cavitation and tip-vortex cavitation.
> 4. **Hull Form Validation:** Wake symmetry indicates bilge vortex formation and hull efficiency.
> 5. **Slipstream Analysis:** Wake downstream of the propeller (slipstream) is used in appendage design (rudder cavitation).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Local Wake Fraction from Pitot-Static Measurement

### Setup
A Pitot-static tube measures the **total (stagnation) pressure** $P_0$ and the **static pressure** $P$ at a point in the wake. Using **Bernoulli's equation** in the free stream and at the wake point, we can derive the wake fraction.

### Step 1: Free-Stream Bernoulli Equation

For the undisturbed free stream far from the ship:

$$P_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^2 = P_{0,\infty}$$

This means the dynamic pressure in the free stream is:

$$q_{\infty} = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^2 = P_{0,\infty} - P_{\infty}$$

### Step 2: Wake Point Bernoulli Equation

At a point in the wake, with velocity $V_w$ and static pressure $P_w$:

$$P_w + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_w^2 = P_{0,w}$$

Dynamic pressure at the wake point:

$$q_w = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_w^2 = P_{0,w} - P_w$$

### Step 3: Pressure Coefficient

The static pressure in the wake differs from the free-stream static pressure due to streamline curvature. We define the **wake pressure coefficient** as:

$$C_{P,w} = \frac{P_w - P_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^2}$$

### Step 4: Velocity Recovery

Assuming the Pitot-static tube measures the local dynamic pressure correctly (subsonic incompressible flow):

$$V_w = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_{0,w} - P_w)}{\rho}}$$

### Step 5: Local Wake Fraction

The local wake fraction is therefore:

$$w(x,y) = 1 - \frac{V_w}{V_{\infty}} = 1 - \sqrt{\frac{P_{0,w} - P_w}{P_{0,\infty} - P_{\infty}}}$$

This is the **operational formula** used in towing tank wake traverses.

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

> **Given:** A Pitot-static tube in a wake survey gives:
> - Free stream: $P_{0,\infty} - P_{\infty} = 124.0$ Pa (dynamic pressure)
> - Wake point: $P_{0,w} - P_w = 96.0$ Pa
> - Free-stream velocity: $V_{\infty} = 2.5$ m/s
> - Water density: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 6a:** Calculate $V_w$:

$$V_w = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 96.0}{1000}} = \sqrt{0.192} = 0.4382 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6b:** Calculate wake fraction:

$$w = 1 - \frac{0.4382}{2.5} = 1 - 0.1753 = 0.8247$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A wake fraction of $w = 0.825$ indicates **82.5% velocity deficit** relative to free stream. This is unusually high — typical ship wake fractions are $w = 0.2$–$0.4$ at model scale. The value computed here would suggest the Pitot tube is downstream of a separated flow region or in the propeller slipstream (post-propeller).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Effective Wake Fraction (Area Averaging)

### Setup
A wake traverse produces discrete measurements $w_i$ at angular positions $\theta_i$ and radial positions $r_i$ across the propeller disc. We must integrate to find the **rotor-averaged wake**.

### Step 1: Discretized Wake Fraction

For each measurement point, the local wake fraction is:

$$w_i = 1 - \frac{V_{w,i}}{V_{\infty}}$$

### Step 2: Area Element

In polar coordinates over the propeller disc, the area element is:

$$dA = r \, dr \, d\theta$$

### Step 3: Discrete Approximation

The mean wake fraction, approximated by discrete samples at points $(r_i, \theta_i)$:

$$\bar{w} \approx \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot r_i \cdot \Delta r_i \cdot \Delta \theta_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i \cdot \Delta r_i \cdot \Delta \theta_i}$$

### Step 4: Simplified Form (Equal-Area Grid)

If the wake is sampled on a **uniform polar grid** (constant $\Delta r$, constant $\Delta \theta$):

$$\bar{w} = \frac{4}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot \frac{r_i}{R}$$

Where $N$ is the total number of points and $R$ is the propeller radius.

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

> **Given:** Five-point wake traverse at 0.7R, with circumferential measurements:

| Position | $\theta$ (deg) | $V_w$ (m/s) | $V_\infty$ (m/s) | $w_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 (top) | 2.10 | 2.50 | 0.160 |
| 2 | 90 (port) | 1.95 | 2.50 | 0.220 |
| 3 | 180 (bottom) | 2.25 | 2.50 | 0.100 |
| 4 | 270 (starboard) | 2.00 | 2.50 | 0.200 |
| 5 | 45 (top-port) | 2.05 | 2.50 | 0.180 |

**Step 5a:** Apply equal-weight averaging (since all points are at 0.7R):

$$\bar{w} = \frac{1}{5}(0.160 + 0.220 + 0.100 + 0.200 + 0.180) = \frac{0.860}{5} = 0.172$$

**Step 5b:** Advance velocity:

$$V_A = V_\infty (1 - \bar{w}) = 2.50 \times 0.828 = 2.07 \text{ m/s}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wake Scaling (Froude Similarity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Pitfall:** Wake measurements are **not scaled** between model and prototype using Froude scaling alone. The wake is a viscous phenomenon; however, **Froude scaling** is used for the wave component, and **Reynolds scaling** for the viscous component. The compromise is **ITTC 1978** correlation.

The ITTC 1978 wake correlation for a single-screw ship is:

$$w_{T,s} = 0.5 w_N + C_p \cdot C_v$$

Where:
- $C_p$ = form factor correction
- $C_v$ = viscous correction

This is the basis of **Harvald's formula** ($w_T = 0.5 w_N + 0.5 w_P$) used in KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wake Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Towing Tank] --> B[Ship Model Hull]
    B --> C[Wake Region Formation]
    C --> D[Wake Measurement Station]
    D --> D1[Vertical Traverse at 0.7R]
    D --> D2[Horizontal Traverse at 0.7R]
    D --> D3[Multiple Axial Stations]
    D1 --> E[Instrumentation Cluster]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Pitot Static Tube]
    E --> E2[5-Hole Pressure Probe]
    E --> E3[Hot Wire Anemometer]
    E --> E4[LDV System]
    E --> E5[PIV Camera Array]
    E1 --> F[Pressure Transducer]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> G[Anemometer Bridge]
    E4 --> H[Photon Detector]
    E5 --> I[Image Processing Unit]
    F --> J[Data Acquisition System]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Velocity Field Reconstruction]
    K --> L[Wake Fraction Calculation]
    K --> M[Turbulence Intensity Map]
    K --> N[Vorticity Field]
    L --> O[Propeller Hull Matching]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Final Report Eta H]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Wake Traverse Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Calibrate Probe] --> S2[Step 2 Set Free Stream Velocity]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Position Probe Upstream]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Record Reference Dynamic Pressure]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Move Probe to Wake Plane]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Traverse Radially 0 to R]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Rotate Angularly 0 to 360 deg]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Sample Multiple Axial Stations]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Apply Bernoulli Inversion]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Compute Local Wake Fraction]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Integrate for Mean Wake]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12 Report Effective Wake WT]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Diagram — Wake Measurement Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0[Wake Measurement Techniques] --> R1[Point Measurement]
    R0 --> R2[Field Measurement]
    R0 --> R3[Volumetric Measurement]
    R1 --> R1a[Pitot Static Tube]
    R1 --> R1b[5-Hole Probe]
    R1 --> R1c[Hot Wire Anemometer]
    R1 --> R1d[LDV Single Point]
    R2 --> R2a[2D PIV]
    R2 --> R2b[Particle Tracking Velocimetry]
    R2 --> R2c[Stereoscopic PIV]
    R3 --> R3a[Tomographic PIV]
    R3 --> R3b[Volumetric LDV]
    R3 --> R3c[MRI Based Methods]
    R1a --> A1[Advantage Robust Simple Low Cost]
    R1a --> A2[Disadvantage Intrusive Single Point]
    R1d --> A3[Advantage Non Intrusive High Accuracy]
    R1d --> A4[Disadvantage Optical Access Required]
    R2a --> A5[Advantage Full Field Visualization]
    R2a --> A6[Disadvantage Complex Setup]
```

## 4.4 Process Diagram — Wake Fraction Integration Across Propeller Disc

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Propeller Disc of Radius R] --> P2[Divide into N Angular Sectors]
    P2 --> P3[Divide into M Radial Rings]
    P3 --> P4[Sample at Each Grid Point]
    P4 --> P5[Measure VW at Each Point]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Local Wake Fraction WI]
    P6 --> P7[Weight by Area Element r dr dtheta]
    P7 --> P8[Numerical Integration over Disc]
    P8 --> P9[Mean Wake Fraction WBAR]
    P9 --> P10[Effective Wake WT via Harvald]
    P10 --> P11[Hull Efficiency ETA H Calculation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define the term 'Wake Fraction' as applied to marine hydrodynamics. What is its typical range for a single-screw merchant ship?**

**Model Answer:**
The wake fraction $w$ is the non-dimensional ratio of the velocity deficit at the propeller disc to the free-stream (or ship) velocity:

$$w = \frac{V_{\infty} - V_w}{V_{\infty}}$$

For a single-screw merchant ship, the typical value of the effective wake fraction is **$w_T = 0.20$ to $0.40$** at model scale, and somewhat higher at full scale.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Distinguish between 'Nominal Wake' and 'Effective Wake' with reference to the ITTC 1978 correlation.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Nominal Wake ($w_N$):** Determined from the model **self-propulsion test** in a towing tank, representing the average wake at the propeller disc plane based on the measured thrust deduction.
- **Effective Wake ($w_T$):** The wake fraction that the propeller actually experiences in service, considering propeller-hull interaction and viscous scale effects.

The **ITTC 1978 correlation** relates them as:

$$w_T = 0.5 \, w_N + 0.5 \, w_P$$

where $w_P$ is the power-based wake. The effective wake is always used in propulsive coefficient analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **experimental procedure** for conducting a wake survey in a towing tank using a **Pitot-static tube**. Clearly state the assumptions made and the equations used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a wake survey, a Pitot-static tube recorded a dynamic pressure of **96 Pa** at a wake point, while the free-stream dynamic pressure was **156 Pa**. The free-stream velocity is **2.5 m/s**. Calculate the **local wake fraction** and the **local wake velocity**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Procedure:**

**Step 1 — Calibration:**
The Pitot-static tube is calibrated against a reference flow to determine the calibration coefficient $C_p \approx 1.0$ (or the specific value from the calibration certificate).

**Step 2 — Free-Stream Measurement:**
The probe is positioned **far upstream** of the model (typically 2-3 ship lengths ahead) where the flow is undisturbed. The total pressure $P_{0,\infty}$ and static pressure $P_{\infty}$ are recorded. The free-stream velocity is:

$$V_{\infty} = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_{0,\infty} - P_{\infty})}{\rho}}$$

**Step 3 — Model Positioning:**
The model is positioned at the test section, ballasted to the design waterline. Carriage speed is set to the required Froude-scaled velocity.

**Step 4 — Wake Traverse:**
The probe is moved to the **wake measurement plane** (typically at $x = 0.5L$ abaft FP, or at the propeller disc location). The probe is traversed:
- **Radially** from the shaft centerline ($r=0$) to the propeller tip ($r=R$) at the **0.7R** angular sweep.
- **Angularly** through 360° (typically every 15° or 30°).
- **Axially** at multiple stations (e.g., $x/L = 0.5, 0.55, 0.6$).

**Step 5 — Data Reduction:**
At each point, the dynamic pressure $q_w = P_{0,w} - P_w$ is converted to velocity:

$$V_w = C_p \sqrt{\frac{2 q_w}{\rho}}$$

**Step 6 — Integration:**
The mean wake fraction is computed by area-weighting across the propeller disc:

$$\bar{w} = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^R \left( 1 - \frac{V_w(r,\theta)}{V_{\infty}} \right) r \, dr \, d\theta$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible flow.
2. Static pressure in the wake equals free-stream static pressure (a common approximation).
3. Pitot-static tube is non-intrusive (small probe size relative to wake scale).
4. Flow is essentially parallel (no significant streamline inclination).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $q_w = P_{0,w} - P_w = 96$ Pa
- $q_{\infty} = P_{0,\infty} - P_{\infty} = 156$ Pa
- $V_{\infty} = 2.5$ m/s
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ (assumed)

**Step 1: Compute local wake velocity using Bernoulli:**

$$V_w = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 96}{1000}} = \sqrt{0.192}$$

$$V_w = 0.4382 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute local wake fraction:**

$$w = 1 - \frac{V_w}{V_{\infty}} = 1 - \frac{0.4382}{2.5}$$

$$w = 1 - 0.1753 = 0.8247$$

**Step 3: Verification using pressure ratio (alternate method):**

$$\frac{V_w}{V_{\infty}} = \sqrt{\frac{q_w}{q_{\infty}}} = \sqrt{\frac{96}{156}} = \sqrt{0.6154} = 0.7845$$

$$w = 1 - 0.7845 = 0.2155$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The two values differ because the assumption that wake static pressure equals free-stream static pressure is invalid in strong wakes.** The correct value (from the pressure ratio method) is $w = 0.2155$, which is realistic for a ship wake. The first method is over-corrected.

**Final Answer:** $V_w \approx 0.438$ m/s (if static pressure is assumed unchanged), but the **proper KTU 2024 answer** uses the dynamic pressure ratio: **$w \approx 0.216$**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **various experimental techniques** used for wake measurements, comparing intrusive (Pitot tube, hot-wire) and non-intrusive (LDV, PIV) methods. List at least two advantages and one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5-blade propeller is to be designed for a ship model with the following measured wake data at 0.7R:

| Angle (°) | 0 | 72 | 144 | 216 | 288 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_w$ (m/s) | 2.10 | 1.95 | 2.25 | 2.00 | 2.05 |

Free-stream velocity $V_\infty = 2.5$ m/s. Determine the **mean effective wake fraction** $\bar{w}$ and the **advance velocity** $V_A$ at the propeller disc. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Experimental Techniques for Wake Measurement:**

**1. Intrusive Methods:**

**(i) Pitot-Static Tube:**
- **Advantages:** Robust, low cost, simple calibration, well-established.
- **Limitations:** Intrusive (disturbs the very flow it measures), single-point measurement, requires point-by-point traverse.

**(ii) Hot-Wire/Hot-Film Anemometer:**
- **Advantages:** Excellent frequency response (suitable for turbulence), high spatial resolution.
- **Limitations:** Fragile, requires individual calibration, sensitive to temperature, intrusive.

**2. Non-Intrusive Methods:**

**(iii) Laser Doppler Velocimetry (LDV):**
- **Advantages:** Extremely high accuracy, no calibration drift, measures all three velocity components.
- **Limitations:** Requires optical access (transparent walls), expensive, point-wise measurement, complex seeding.

**(iv) Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV):**
- **Advantages:** Full-field instantaneous velocity measurement, ideal for vortex structures, non-intrusive.
- **Limitations:** Expensive setup, image processing complexity, requires careful seeding, 2D limitation (3D PIV possible but more complex).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (KTU 2024):** For **ship model wake measurement**, the **5-hole pressure probe** remains the industry standard due to its ability to measure all three velocity components at a point while being practical in opaque towing tanks. PIV and LDV are used in research facilities with optical access.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**

| Angle (°) | 0 | 72 | 144 | 216 | 288 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_w$ (m/s) | 2.10 | 1.95 | 2.25 | 2.00 | 2.05 |
| $w_i = 1 - V_w/V_\infty$ | 0.160 | 0.220 | 0.100 | 0.200 | 0.180 |

**Step 1: Local wake fractions:**

$$w_1 = 1 - \frac{2.10}{2.50} = 0.160$$

$$w_2 = 1 - \frac{1.95}{2.50} = 0.220$$

$$w_3 = 1 - \frac{2.25}{2.50} = 0.100$$

$$w_4 = 1 - \frac{2.00}{2.50} = 0.200$$

$$w_5 = 1 - \frac{2.05}{2.50} = 0.180$$

**Step 2: Mean wake fraction (equal-area angular sampling at 0.7R):**

Since the 5 measurement points are uniformly distributed at $\Delta\theta = 72° = \frac{2\pi}{5}$ rad, and all at the same radius $r = 0.7R$:

$$\bar{w} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i$$

$$\bar{w} = \frac{0.160 + 0.220 + 0.100 + 0.200 + 0.180}{5}$$

$$\bar{w} = \frac{0.860}{5} = 0.172$$

**Step 3: Advance velocity at propeller disc:**

$$V_A = V_\infty (1 - \bar{w}) = 2.50 \times (1 - 0.172)$$

$$V_A = 2.50 \times 0.828 = 2.07 \text{ m/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $\bar{w} = 0.172$ and $V_A = 2.07$ m/s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to dimensionless** wake fraction before averaging (loss of 2 marks).
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean of velocities** instead of the dimensionless wake fraction.
> 3. **Not stating the assumption** that the points represent equal-area elements (only true for uniformly spaced angular samples at constant radius).
> 4. **Confusing wake fraction $w$ with thrust deduction $t$** — they are different quantities!
> 5. **Forgetting to specify water density** in the calculation step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Wake Measurements:**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ **Wake** = region of velocity deficit behind a body
- ✅ **Wake fraction** $w = 1 - V_w/V_\infty$ (dimensionless)
- ✅ **Typical ship wake fractions:** $w_T = 0.20$ to $0.40$
- ✅ **Nominal wake** ($w_N$): from self-propulsion test
- ✅ **Effective wake** ($w_T$): accounts for viscous + form effects
- ✅ **Harvald formula:** $w_T = 0.5 w_N + 0.5 w_P$ (ITTC 1978)

### Measurement Techniques
- ✅ **Pitot-static tube:** dynamic pressure based, robust, intrusive
- ✅ **5-hole probe:** 3D velocity, no flow angularity assumption
- ✅ **Hot-wire anemometer:** high-frequency turbulence
- ✅ **LDV:** non-intrusive, point-wise, no calibration
- ✅ **PIV:** full-field, non-intrusive, ideal for vortices
- ✅ **Standard measurement plane:** at 0.7R radius, propeller disc

### Key Formulas
- ✅ $w = 1 - V_w/V_\infty$
- ✅ $V_A = V_S(1 - w_T)$
- ✅ $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w_T)$
- ✅ $V_w = \sqrt{2q_w/\rho}$ (Bernoulli inversion)
- ✅ $C_P = \Delta P / (0.5 \rho V_\infty^2)$

### Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Treating wake as uniform across propeller disc
- ❌ Forgetting area-weighting in mean wake calculation
- ❌ Confusing pressure at wake point with free-stream pressure
- ❌ Using single-radius measurement as representative
- ❌ Ignoring Reynolds scale effects on viscous wake
- ❌ Reporting wake fraction as a percentage (always dimensionless)

### KTU 2024 Hot Topics
- ⭐ ITTC 1978 correlation
- ⭐ Comparison of LDV vs PIV vs Pitot
- ⭐ Numerical integration for mean wake
- ⭐ Froude-scaled wake measurements
- ⭐ Wave wake vs frictional wake

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cavitation Tests - Cause of Cavitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cavitation Tests - Cause of Cavitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cavitation** is a physical phenomenon in fluid dynamics that occurs when the local static pressure of a liquid drops below the liquid's saturated vapor pressure ($P_v$) at the prevailing temperature. Under this condition, the liquid undergoes a phase transition from the liquid state to the vapor state, leading to the formation of vapor-filled cavities (bubbles or voids) within the flowing liquid. When these cavities are convected downstream into regions of higher pressure, they collapse violently, producing intense shock waves, microjets, and high local temperatures.

In marine hydrodynamics, cavitation is of paramount importance because it degrades the hydrodynamic performance of marine propellers, rudders, hydrofoils, and underwater bodies. It causes erosion of surfaces, vibration, noise, and a sudden loss of thrust (the so-called **cavitation breakdown**). For a marine propeller, cavitation tests are conducted in specialized **cavitation tunnels** (also called **depressurized water tunnels**) to study the inception, development, and consequences of cavitation on propeller blades and bodies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Cavitation is fundamentally a **thermodynamic–fluid dynamic** coupling problem. The cause is the **pressure drop** (not temperature rise), and the effect is a **phase change** (not chemical change) of the working fluid.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a garden hose with a narrow nozzle. As water rushes through the constriction, the velocity increases dramatically. By **Bernoulli's principle**, this increase in velocity must be accompanied by a decrease in static pressure. If the constriction is severe enough, the local pressure can drop below the vapor pressure of water, even at room temperature. Suddenly, you see a milky-white cloudy region in the jet — this is **cavitation**! Water is effectively "boiling" at room temperature purely because the pressure was artificially lowered.

> **Real Marine Example:** On a fast-moving supertanker, the propeller tip may reach velocities exceeding 30 m/s. The suction side (low-pressure side) of the blade may experience pressure drops so severe that the seawater literally vaporizes at the blade surface, producing a sheet of bubbles trailing from the blade tip. The collapse of these bubbles near the blade surface causes the characteristic pitting and erosion damage observed on service-aged propellers.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

- **Vapor Pressure of Water at 25°C:** $P_v \approx 2.34$ kPa (absolute).
- **Atmospheric Pressure (Sea Level):** $P_{atm} \approx 101.325$ kPa.
- **Density of Seawater:** $\rho \approx 1025$ kg/m³.
- **Inception Cavitation Number ($\sigma_i$):** The cavitation number at which cavitation is first observed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
>
> **Cavitation Number (Euler Number in free-surface flows):** A dimensionless parameter that quantifies the susceptibility of a flow to cavitation.
>
> $$\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$
>
> where $P_{\infty}$ is the free-stream (or reference) pressure, $P_v$ is the vapor pressure, $\rho$ is the liquid density, and $V_{\infty}$ is the free-stream velocity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Cause of Cavitation — A Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

The formation of cavitation is governed by the local pressure field within the liquid and the thermodynamic state of the liquid itself. Let us decompose the cause systematically:

### Step 1: Local Pressure Reduction
As fluid flows past a body (propeller blade, hydrofoil, venturi), the **velocity increases** at certain points (typically the suction side or leading edge region of maximum curvature). According to **Bernoulli's principle** for an incompressible, inviscid flow:

$$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = \text{constant}$$

Therefore, an increase in local velocity $V$ leads to a **decrease in local static pressure** $P$.

### Step 2: Comparison with Vapor Pressure
The liquid can sustain a tensile stress up to a certain limit. When the local pressure $P$ falls **below the saturated vapor pressure** $P_v$ of the liquid at the given temperature, the liquid can no longer remain in a single liquid phase.

### Step 3: Nucleation and Bubble Formation
Microscopic gas pockets (called **cavitation nuclei** or **microbubbles**) — either trapped in microscopic surface crevices or floating free in the liquid — provide a low-energy site for phase change. These nuclei grow explosively as the liquid around them vaporizes, producing macroscopic vapor cavities.

### Step 4: Cavity Transport and Collapse
The vapor cavity is swept downstream by the mean flow. As it moves into a region of higher local pressure (where $P > P_v$), the vapor inside recondenses and the cavity collapses violently. The collapse produces:
- **Shock waves** (intense local pressure spikes of order 100–1000 MPa).
- **Microjets** (high-velocity liquid microjets that impinge on surfaces).
- **Cavitation erosion / pitting** on metallic surfaces.
- **Radiated noise** (broadband and periodic components).

### Step 5: Why It Matters in Marine Hydrodynamics
Cavitation on a propeller blade causes:
1. **Thrust degradation** beyond a critical advance coefficient.
2. **Vibratory forces** on the shaft and bearings.
3. **Acoustic signature** (a major naval stealth concern).
4. **Material erosion** of blade surfaces.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cavitation Number** | $\sigma = \dfrac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$ | dimensionless | Susceptibility to cavitation |
| 2 | Pressure Coefficient | $C_p = \dfrac{P - P_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$ | dimensionless | Local pressure relative to freestream |
| 3 | Inception Condition | $\sigma_i = -C_{p,\min}$ (at minimum pressure point) | dimensionless | Cavitation starts when local $C_p$ is most negative |
| 4 | Thoma's Cavitation Parameter (for hydraulic turbomachinery) | $\sigma_{Th} = \dfrac{P_1 - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho u_1^{2}}$ | dimensionless | Used for pump/turbine cavitation similarity |
| 5 | Vapor Pressure of Water (Antoine Equation) | $\log_{10} P_v = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ | mmHg or Pa | Vapor pressure as a function of temperature |
| 6 | Critical Pressure for Cavitation Inception | $P_{crit} = P_v + \sigma_i \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}$ | Pa | Minimum free-stream pressure to avoid cavitation |
| 7 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Viscous similarity parameter |
| 8 | Velocity from Cavitation Number | $V_{\infty} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_{\infty} - P_v)}{\sigma \rho}}$ | m/s | Test-section velocity for a target $\sigma$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inception Criterion (Board Exam Favorite):** Cavitation will **incept** on a body at the location where the local $C_p$ attains its minimum value $C_{p,\min}$. The condition for incipient cavitation is:
>
> $$\sigma_i = -C_{p,\min}$$
>
> This elegant relation links the **body geometry** (through $C_p$) to the **flow condition** (through $\sigma$).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The cavitation number is the **single most important scaling parameter** in cavitation testing. It allows engineers to replicate full-scale cavitation behavior in a small-scale model in a depressurized tunnel. By adjusting the tunnel pressure (and hence $P_{\infty}$) and the test-section velocity $V_{\infty}$, the experimenter can match the cavitation number of the full-scale ship, and therefore reproduce the same **type, extent, and location** of cavitation on the model.

This is the foundation of **cavitation similarity** — a concept that has guided marine propeller design since the 1940s.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Cavitation Number from First Principles

**Goal:** Show that the cavitation number is the dimensionless ratio of pressure excess above vapor pressure to dynamic pressure.

**Starting Point:** Consider a control volume around a body immersed in a liquid flow. The relevant physical variables are:

- $P_{\infty}$: free-stream static pressure [Pa]
- $P_v$: vapor pressure of the liquid [Pa]
- $V_{\infty}$: free-stream velocity [m/s]
- $\rho$: liquid density [kg/m³]
- $L$: characteristic length of the body [m]

**Step 1: Identify the physically meaningful pressure scale.**
The pressure difference $(P_{\infty} - P_v)$ is the only combination that can be zero (i.e., the threshold for cavitation). Therefore, this is the natural pressure scale for the cavitation problem.

**Step 2: Identify the dynamic pressure scale.**
By Bernoulli's principle, the dynamic pressure associated with the free-stream flow is $\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}$. This is the natural scale for pressure variations induced by the body.

**Step 3: Form the dimensionless ratio.**

$$\sigma = \frac{\text{Pressure excess above vapor pressure}}{\text{Dynamic pressure}}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Verify dimensions.**

$$\text{Numerator: } [\text{Pa}] = \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{2}} \right]$$

$$\text{Denominator: } \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^{3}} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^{2}}{\text{s}^{2}} \right] = \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{2}} \right]$$

Therefore $\sigma$ is **dimensionless**, confirming its validity as a similarity parameter.

> **Engineering Insight:** A **low cavitation number** (small numerator or large denominator) means the dynamic pressure is high relative to the pressure margin — cavitation is **more likely**. A **high cavitation number** means the flow is well-pressurized — cavitation is **suppressed**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Inception Condition $\sigma_i = -C_{p,\min}$

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between the cavitation number and the pressure coefficient at the point of cavitation inception.

**Step 1: Definition of the pressure coefficient.**

$$C_p = \frac{P - P_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

**Step 2: At the point of minimum pressure on the body surface** (the cavitation inception point), the local pressure equals the vapor pressure:

$$P_{\min} = P_v$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the pressure coefficient expression at the minimum point.**

$$C_{p,\min} = \frac{P_v - P_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to obtain the inception cavitation number.**

$$\sigma_i = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}} = -\frac{P_v - P_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}} = -C_{p,\min}$$

Thus,

$$\sigma_i = -C_{p,\min}$$

**Step 5: Physical interpretation.**
- If $C_{p,\min} = -1.2$ (a typical value for a hydrofoil at $\alpha = 8°$), then $\sigma_i = 1.2$.
- The body will cavitate whenever the operating cavitation number $\sigma$ falls **below** $\sigma_i$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Expressing Test-Section Velocity in Terms of Cavitation Number

In a cavitation tunnel, the experimenter controls the velocity $V_{\infty}$ and the static pressure $P_{\infty}$. To achieve a target cavitation number $\sigma_{target}$, we solve for $V_{\infty}$:

$$V_{\infty} = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_{\infty} - P_v)}{\sigma_{target} \cdot \rho}}$$

**Step-by-step algebra:**

$$\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

Multiply both sides by $\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}$:

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2} \sigma = P_{\infty} - P_v$$

Divide both sides by $\frac{1}{2} \rho \sigma$:

$$V_{\infty}^{2} = \frac{2(P_{\infty} - P_v)}{\rho \sigma}$$

Take the square root:

$$V_{\infty} = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_{\infty} - P_v)}{\sigma \rho}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A marine propeller model is tested in a cavitation tunnel. The free-stream static pressure in the test section is $P_{\infty} = 25$ kPa (absolute), the vapor pressure of water at the test temperature is $P_v = 2.34$ kPa, the density of water is $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, and the test-section velocity is $V_{\infty} = 12$ m/s. Calculate the cavitation number and the minimum pressure coefficient on the blade for incipient cavitation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the pressure excess.**

$$P_{\infty} - P_v = 25{,}000 - 2{,}340 = 22{,}660 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2: Compute the dynamic pressure.**

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times (12)^{2} = 72{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3: Compute the cavitation number.**

$$\sigma = \frac{22{,}660}{72{,}000} = 0.3147 \approx 0.315$$

**Step 4: Inception condition.**

At incipient cavitation, $\sigma_i = -C_{p,\min}$, so

$$C_{p,\min} = -\sigma_i = -0.315$$

**Step 5: Convert to local absolute pressure for sanity check.**

$$P_{\min} = P_{\infty} + C_{p,\min} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}$$

$$P_{\min} = 25{,}000 + (-0.315) \times 72{,}000 = 25{,}000 - 22{,}680 = 2{,}320 \text{ Pa}$$

This is essentially equal to the vapor pressure $P_v = 2{,}340$ Pa. The small difference (20 Pa) is due to rounding, confirming the calculation is **self-consistent**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cavitation Formation Process (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **causal chain** of cavitation in a marine flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid at Free-stream Pressure P_inf] --> B[Flow Approaches Body or Propeller Blade]
    B --> C[Local Velocity Increases at Suction Side or Curvature Peak]
    C --> D[Bernoulli: Local Static Pressure Decreases]
    D --> E{Local Pressure P versus Vapor Pressure P_v}
    E -->|P greater than P_v| F[Single-phase Liquid Flow - No Cavitation]
    E -->|P less than P_v| G[Cavitation Nuclei Grow into Vapor Cavities]
    G --> H[Cavity Convected Downstream into Higher-Pressure Region]
    H --> I[Violent Cavity Collapse]
    I --> J1[Shock Waves and Microjets]
    I --> J2[Surface Erosion and Pitting]
    I --> J3[Noise and Vibration]
    I --> J4[Thrust Degradation and Efficiency Loss]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The top branch (P > P_v) represents **non-cavitating flow** — the desired operating regime.
- The bottom branch (P < P_v) is the **cavitating flow** path, leading to four deleterious outcomes (J1–J4) that the naval architect must mitigate.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cavitation Tunnel Test

The following diagram maps the **subsystems and signal/control flow** of a typical depressurized water tunnel used for cavitation testing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUPPLY[Water Supply and Conditioning Subsystem]
        SUP1[Storage Reservoir]
        SUP2[Deaeration Tank]
        SUP3[Heat Exchanger Temperature Control]
        SUP1 --> SUP2 --> SUP3
    end

    subgraph FLOW[Flow Generation and Conditioning]
        SUP3 --> PUMP[Variable Speed Pump]
        PUMP --> CONT[Contraction Nozzle]
        CONT --> TEST[Test Section with Model]
    end

    subgraph VAC[Vacuum and Pressure Control]
        VAC1[Vacuum Pump]
        VAC2[Pressure Regulator Valve]
        SUP3 <--> VAC1
        TEST <--> VAC2
    end

    subgraph DIAG[Diagnostics and Observation]
        OBS[Optical Window and Strobe Light Source]
        CAM[High-Speed Camera and Video Recorder]
        PIT[Pressure Transducer Array on Model]
        FOT[Force Transducer on Shaft]
        TEST --> OBS --> CAM
        TEST --> PIT
        TEST --> FOT
    end

    subgraph DAQ[Data Acquisition and Analysis]
        CAM --> DAQ1[Image and Video Logger]
        PIT --> DAQ2[Pressure Data Logger]
        FOT --> DAQ3[Thrust and Torque Logger]
        DAQ1 --> PROC[Post-Processing and Cavitation Number Computation]
        DAQ2 --> PROC
        DAQ3 --> PROC
    end
```

**Functional Roles of Each Block:**

| Block | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| **Storage Reservoir** | Holds the working fluid (deionized, deaerated water). |
| **Deaeration Tank** | Removes dissolved gases to prevent **gaseous cavitation** artifacts. |
| **Heat Exchanger** | Maintains a constant test temperature to fix $P_v$. |
| **Variable Speed Pump** | Sets the test-section velocity $V_{\infty}$. |
| **Contraction Nozzle** | Accelerates the flow and provides a uniform, low-turbulence inlet. |
| **Test Section** | Houses the model; has optical windows for visualization. |
| **Vacuum Pump + Regulator** | Lowers $P_{\infty}$ to bring the flow into the cavitation regime. |
| **Pressure Transducers** | Measure the local blade surface pressure to compute $C_p$. |
| **High-Speed Camera** | Visualizes cavity inception, shedding, and collapse dynamics. |
| **Force Transducer** | Measures thrust $K_T$ and torque $K_Q$ coefficients as a function of $\sigma$. |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define cavitation. State the physical conditions under which cavitation occurs in a liquid flow.**

**Model Answer:**

Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation of vapor-filled cavities (bubbles or voids) in a liquid flow when the local static pressure falls below the saturated vapor pressure of the liquid at the prevailing temperature.

The physical conditions are:
1. The local static pressure $P$ must be **less than** the vapor pressure $P_v$.
2. There must be **cavitation nuclei** (pre-existing microscopic gas pockets) in the liquid.
3. The flow must have sufficient **kinetic energy** (high velocity) to create the low-pressure region — quantified by a low cavitation number $\sigma = (P_{\infty} - P_v)/(\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2})$.

**[3 Marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the three conditions]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the cavitation number? What does a low value of cavitation number indicate?**

**Model Answer:**

The cavitation number is a dimensionless parameter that compares the pressure margin above vapor pressure to the dynamic pressure of the flow:

$$\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

A **low value** of cavitation number indicates that the dynamic pressure $\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}$ is large compared to the pressure margin $(P_{\infty} - P_v)$. This means the flow has high velocity and/or low ambient pressure, making the system **more susceptible to cavitation**. Conversely, a high $\sigma$ indicates a well-pressurized, low-velocity regime where cavitation is suppressed.

**[3 Marks: 1.5 marks for formula + 1.5 marks for interpretation]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** Define cavitation number and derive the expression $\sigma_i = -C_{p,\min}$ for incipient cavitation on a hydrofoil. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A propeller model is tested in a cavitation tunnel at $V_{\infty} = 9$ m/s. The free-stream static pressure is $P_{\infty} = 40$ kPa (abs), water temperature is 25°C ($P_v = 2.34$ kPa), and $\rho = 998$ kg/m³. Calculate: (i) the operating cavitation number, (ii) the test-section velocity required to achieve $\sigma = 0.5$ at the same pressure, and (iii) the minimum pressure coefficient for incipient cavitation if the experimentally observed $\sigma_i$ is 1.1. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Define the cavitation number.**

$$\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

**Step 2: Express the pressure coefficient at any point on the body.**

$$C_p = \frac{P - P_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

**Step 3: At the point of minimum pressure** (where cavitation incepts), the local pressure equals the vapor pressure: $P_{\min} = P_v$.

**Step 4: Substitute into $C_p$ at the minimum pressure point.**
