$$C_{p,\min} = \frac{P_v - P_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}} = -\frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

**Step 5: Recognize that the right-hand side is precisely the inception cavitation number $\sigma_i$.**

$$\sigma_i = -C_{p,\min}$$

**Conclusion:** The inception cavitation number is set purely by the body geometry (via $C_{p,\min}$), independent of the flow conditions. **[7 Marks: 1.5 for definition + 4 for derivation + 1.5 for interpretation]**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Part (i): Operating cavitation number at $V_{\infty} = 9$ m/s.**

$$\sigma = \frac{40{,}000 - 2{,}340}{\frac{1}{2} \times 998 \times (9)^{2}}$$

Compute numerator: $40{,}000 - 2{,}340 = 37{,}660$ Pa.

Compute denominator: $\frac{1}{2} \times 998 \times 81 = 40{,}419$ Pa.

$$\sigma = \frac{37{,}660}{40{,}419} = 0.9318 \approx 0.932$$

**Part (ii): Velocity for $\sigma = 0.5$ at the same pressure.**

Use the rearranged formula:

$$V_{\infty} = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_{\infty} - P_v)}{\sigma \cdot \rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 37{,}660}{0.5 \times 998}}$$

$$V_{\infty} = \sqrt{\frac{75{,}320}{499}} = \sqrt{150.94} = 12.29 \text{ m/s}$$

**Part (iii): Minimum pressure coefficient from observed $\sigma_i = 1.1$.**

By the inception relation:

$$C_{p,\min} = -\sigma_i = -1.1$$

**[7 Marks: 2 for (i) + 3 for (ii) + 2 for (iii)]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** Explain the physical causes of cavitation in marine propellers. Discuss the four main types of cavitation observed on propeller blades with neat sketches (use descriptive text). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A full-scale ship propeller operates at $V_{\infty} = 8$ m/s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $P_v = 2.0$ kPa) with ambient static pressure $P_{\infty} = 100$ kPa. A 1:5 scale model is to be tested in a cavitation tunnel at the same cavitation number. If the model test uses fresh water at 25°C ($P_v = 2.34$ kPa, $\rho = 998$ kg/m³), calculate: (i) the cavitation number of the full-scale propeller, and (ii) the required free-stream pressure in the tunnel if the model test-section velocity is 25 m/s. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Causes of Cavitation in Marine Propellers:**

1. **High rotational speed:** The blade tip velocity $V_{tip} = \pi D n$ can be very high, creating strong local low-pressure regions on the suction side.
2. **Blade geometry:** Thin, highly cambered sections have low $C_{p,\min}$ values and cavitate easily.
3. **Operating depth:** At shallow depths, $P_{\infty}$ is low (since $P_{\infty} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$), reducing the margin against vapor pressure.
4. **High ship speed:** Increases the inflow velocity and the rate of pressure drop on the blade.

**Four Main Types of Propeller Cavitation:**

| Type | Location on Blade | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Sheet Cavitation** | Suction side of blade, near leading edge | A thin, transparent vapor sheet covering part of the suction side; appears at moderate $\sigma$ values. |
| **Bubble (Cloud) Cavitation** | Trailing edge and tip region | Discrete vapor bubbles that form, grow, and shed intermittently; produces strong pressure fluctuations. |
| **Tip Vortex Cavitation** | Blade tip | A helical vapor core formed by the strong vorticity at the blade tip; appears at low $\sigma$. |
| **Hub Vortex / Cavitating Hub** | Near the hub | Vapor-filled hub vortex cone caused by strong rotational flow near the boss. |

**Mechanism in Each Case:** In every case, the underlying physical cause is the same — local pressure falls below $P_v$. The differences arise from the **aerodynamic/hydrodynamic structure** of the flow at different locations on the blade.

**[7 Marks: 2 for causes + 4 for types + 1 for unifying mechanism]**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Part (i): Full-scale cavitation number.**

$$\sigma_{full} = \frac{P_{\infty,full} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} V_{\infty,full}^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{full} = \frac{100{,}000 - 2{,}000}{\frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (8)^{2}}$$

Numerator: $98{,}000$ Pa.
Denominator: $\frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 64 = 32{,}800$ Pa.

$$\sigma_{full} = \frac{98{,}000}{32{,}800} = 2.988 \approx 2.99$$

**Part (ii): Required tunnel pressure at $V_{\infty,model} = 25$ m/s, $\sigma_{model} = \sigma_{full} = 2.99$.**

Rearrange the cavitation number formula to solve for $P_{\infty,model}$:

$$P_{\infty,model} = P_v + \sigma \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_{fw} V_{\infty,model}^{2}$$

$$P_{\infty,model} = 2{,}340 + 2.99 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 998 \times (25)^{2}$$

Compute the dynamic pressure: $\frac{1}{2} \times 998 \times 625 = 311{,}875$ Pa.

Compute the product with $\sigma$: $2.99 \times 311{,}875 = 932{,}406$ Pa.

$$P_{\infty,model} = 2{,}340 + 932{,}406 = 934{,}746 \text{ Pa} \approx 934.7 \text{ kPa (abs)}$$

**Physical check:** The required pressure is approximately 9.2 atmospheres — this is well within the capabilities of a properly designed pressurized cavitation tunnel. Note that a **pressurized** (not depressurized) tunnel is needed here because the target $\sigma$ is high.

**[7 Marks: 2 for (i) + 4 for (ii) + 1 for physical check]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Using gauge pressure instead of absolute pressure.** The cavitation number formula REQUIRES absolute pressures for $P_{\infty}$ and $P_v$. Mixing gauge and absolute values is the #1 source of wrong answers.
>
> 2. **Confusing $C_{p,\min}$ with $\sigma_i$ signs.** Many students forget the negative sign in $\sigma_i = -C_{p,\min}$. The local pressure coefficient is negative on the suction side; the cavitation number is positive. Do not drop the minus sign.
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistency with $P_v$.** Vapor pressure values from steam tables are often in kPa, mmHg, or bar. Convert to **Pa** before plugging into the formula.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to state the assumption of constant temperature.** The vapor pressure $P_v$ is a strong function of temperature. If the question does not specify temperature, state your assumption explicitly.
>
> 5. **Sketch quality for Part B (a).** When asked for "neat sketches," even a hand-drawn schematic with axes, blade outline, and labeled cavitation regions earns full marks. A blank space earns zero.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cavitation** is the formation of vapor cavities in a liquid when local static pressure drops below the saturated vapor pressure $P_v$.
- The **cavitation number** $\sigma = (P_{\infty} - P_v)/(\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2})$ is the primary similarity parameter.
- **Low $\sigma$** → high risk of cavitation; **High $\sigma$** → cavitation suppressed.
- The **inception condition** is $\sigma_i = -C_{p,\min}$, where $C_{p,\min}$ is set by the body geometry.
- The four main **types of propeller cavitation** are: **sheet, bubble (cloud), tip vortex, and hub vortex**.
- Cavitation leads to **erosion, vibration, noise, and thrust breakdown**.
- **Cavitation tunnels** (depressurized or pressurized water tunnels) are used to model full-scale cavitation at laboratory scale by matching the cavitation number.
- In the cavitation number formula, **always use absolute pressures** (in Pa) and ensure the temperature (and hence $P_v$) is fixed and specified.
- The collapse of cavities in higher-pressure regions produces shock waves of order **100–1000 MPa** — the physical cause of cavitation erosion.
- The Reynolds number must be sufficiently high in model tests to avoid **viscous-scale effects** (Re-dependent $C_{p,\min}$).
- **Dimensional consistency** check: $[P_{\infty} - P_v] = \text{Pa}$ and $[\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}] = \text{Pa}$, so $\sigma$ is dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Cavitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation (KTU Standard Definition):**
> Cavitation is defined as the formation, growth, and subsequent violent collapse of vapor- or gas-filled cavities (bubbles or voids) within a liquid when the local static pressure of the liquid falls to or below the **vapor pressure ($p_v$)** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature. This phenomenon is most commonly encountered in high-speed marine propellers, hydrofoils, pumps, and hydraulic machinery where flow acceleration produces regions of low static pressure.

The **Classification of Cavitation** refers to the systematic categorization of cavitating flow regimes and bubble behaviours based on (a) the prevailing cavitation number ($\sigma$), (b) the geometric nature of the body around which cavitation occurs, (c) the behaviour of the cavity (attached vs. travelling), and (d) the thermodynamic state of the cavity contents (vaporous vs. gaseous).

The fundamental non-dimensional parameter governing the occurrence and type of cavitation is the **Cavitation Number ($\sigma$)**:

$$\sigma = \frac{p_{\infty} - p_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_l V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

Where:
- $p_{\infty}$ = **freestream static pressure** (typically 101,325 Pa at sea level)
- $p_v$ = **vapor pressure of the liquid** (e.g., ~2,339 Pa for water at 20°C)
- $\rho_l$ = **liquid density** (e.g., **998.2 kg/m³** for fresh water at 20°C)
- $V_{\infty}$ = **freestream (reference) flow velocity** in m/s

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The "Stretched Rubber Sheet" of a Liquid**

Imagine a glass of cold water on a sunny afternoon. Now imagine a small motorboat propeller spinning rapidly just below the water surface. As the propeller blade sweeps through the water, the curved blade face acts exactly like an aircraft wing — it forces water to flow faster over the curved (suction) side. The faster the water moves, the lower its local pressure drops (this is **Bernoulli's principle** in action).

Think of the liquid as millions of tiny "rubber balloons" of dissolved air and vapor trapped between water molecules. As local pressure plummets near the blade, these micro-balloons (cavitation nuclei) are stretched outward — they expand explosively into visible bubbles. When these bubbles drift into a high-pressure region downstream, they collapse violently. This violent collapse is what hammers the metal surface of the blade, producing the hissing, crackling, or "screaming" sound characteristic of cavitating propellers.

The **type of cavitation** that forms depends on:
1. **How low the pressure gets** (determined by $\sigma$)
2. **The shape of the blade** (geometry)
3. **How fast the bubbles can collapse** (thermodynamic condition)

This is why engineers need a **classification system** — different cavitation types cause different levels of damage, noise, vibration, and performance loss on ship propellers, rudders, and underwater appendages.

## 1.3 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Classification Anchors (Must Memorize):**
> 1. **Travelling Bubble Cavitation** — discrete bubbles convecting downstream
> 2. **Sheet (Attached) Cavitation** — a transparent, fixed vapor pocket on a hydrofoil/propeller blade
> 3. **Vortex Cavitation** — spiral vapor core inside the tip vortex of a propeller
> 4. **Cloud Cavitation** — large, unstable, pulsating vapor clouds
> 5. **Supercavitating Cavitation** — the cavity covers the entire body
> 6. **Vaporous vs. Gaseous Cavitation** — based on cavity content
> 7. **Incipient vs. Desinent Cavitation** — based on $\sigma$ hysteresis

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics (Bolded for Memory)

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Marine Context) |
|---|---|---|
| Vapor pressure of water @ 20°C | $p_v$ | **2,339 Pa (≈ 0.0231 atm)** |
| Density of sea water | $\rho_l$ | **1,025 kg/m³** |
| Density of fresh water | $\rho_l$ | **998.2 kg/m³** |
| Atmospheric pressure (sea level) | $p_{atm}$ | **101,325 Pa** |
| Critical cavitation number (hydrofoil) | $\sigma_i$ | **0.2 to 0.4** (typical) |
| Inception cavitation number | $\sigma_i$ | Body-geometry dependent |
| Desinent cavitation number | $\sigma_d$ | $\sigma_d < \sigma_i$ (hysteresis) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Does Cavitation Form?

A flowing liquid obeys the steady-state **energy equation (Bernoulli's equation)** for an incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline:

$$p + \frac{1}{2}\rho_l V^{2} = p_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2}$$

Solving for the local static pressure $p$:

$$p = p_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2}\rho_l (V_{\infty}^{2} - V^{2})$$

Cavitation **incepts** the moment $p$ drops to $p_v$ (vapor pressure). Defining the **Pressure Coefficient**:

$$C_p = \frac{p - p_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

Then the minimum pressure on a body surface is $C_{p,min}$, and cavitation incepts when:

$$C_{p,min} = -\sigma_i \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sigma \leq \sigma_i$$

This single relation is the **cornerstone of cavitation classification** — it tells the student that the cavitation regime depends purely on the ratio of pressure excess to dynamic pressure.

## 2.2 Structural Logic Steps for Classification

1. **Step 1 — Pressure Threshold Check:** If the local static pressure $p$ in the flow remains $> p_v$ everywhere, no cavitation occurs (sub-cavitating regime, $\sigma > \sigma_i$).
2. **Step 2 — Identify the Inception Point:** When $\sigma$ is reduced to $\sigma_i$, the first vapor cavities appear. This is called **Incipient Cavitation**.
3. **Step 3 — Develop the Cavity:** As $\sigma$ is further reduced below $\sigma_i$, the cavity grows. Depending on the geometry, this growth produces **Travelling Bubble** or **Sheet (Attached)** cavitation.
4. **Step 4 — Classify by Geometry:** Look at *where* and *how* the cavity is attached to the body:
   - **Fixed to the surface** → Sheet Cavitation
   - **Detached and convecting** → Travelling Bubble / Cloud Cavitation
   - **Confined inside a vortex core** → Vortex Cavitation
   - **Engulfing the body completely** → Supercavitating Cavitation
5. **Step 5 — Classify by Cavity Contents:** Vapor-filled (Vaporous) vs. gas-filled (Gaseous).
6. **Step 6 — Identify the Desinent (Extinction) Condition:** As $\sigma$ increases again, cavitation disappears at $\sigma_d$. Because $\sigma_d < \sigma_i$, there is **cavitation hysteresis** — a key KTU exam point.

## 2.3 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind Classification

- **Why classify by $\sigma$?** Because the cavitation number directly predicts the *amount* of cavity coverage on a body, which controls the **thrust loss** on propellers and the **erosion intensity** on surfaces.
- **Why classify by geometry?** Because vortex cavitation (e.g., tip vortex of a marine propeller) produces intense, localized pressure pulses that cause **radiated noise and vibration** even when $\sigma$ is only slightly below $\sigma_i$.
- **Why distinguish vaporous vs. gaseous?** Because gaseous cavitation involves **non-condensable dissolved gas** and persists at higher $\sigma$, while vaporous cavitation is **pure phase change** and responds instantly to pressure changes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Critical for Exam)

> [!NOTE]
> **No `|` (vertical pipe) symbols are used in the table — all absolute value uses $\vert$ for markdown safety.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cavitation Number ($\sigma$)** | $\sigma = (p_{\infty} - p_v)/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2})$ | Dimensionless | Predicts cavitation inception & regime |
| 2 | **Pressure Coefficient ($C_p$)** | $C_p = (p - p_{\infty})/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2})$ | Dimensionless | Maps surface pressure on hydrofoils |
| 3 | **Inception Condition** | $\sigma = \sigma_i \iff C_{p,min} = -\sigma_i$ | Dimensionless | Defines the start of cavitation |
| 4 | **Critical Pressure at Inception** | $p_{crit} = p_v - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2}\,\sigma_i$ | Pa | Minimum local pressure for no cavitation |
| 5 | **Thrust Coefficient Loss** | $K_T = K_{T,0}(1 - \alpha\sigma)$ | Dimensionless | Empirical propeller thrust correction |
| 6 | **Cavity Length (Supercavitation)** | $L_c \approx \ell \sqrt{C_d\,(1+\sigma)/\sigma}$ | m (metres) | Used for supercavitating hydrofoils |
| 7 | **Incipient vs. Desinent Hysteresis** | $\sigma_d < \sigma_i$ (typically) | Dimensionless | Cavity persistence after inception |
| 8 | **Energy Released on Bubble Collapse** | $E = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi R_{max}^{3}(p_{\infty} - p_v)$ | J (joules) | Source of cavitation erosion |
| 9 | **Reynolds Number Effect** | $Re = \rho_l V c / \mu$ | Dimensionless | Determines sheet vs. cloud transition |
| 10 | **Strouhal Number (cloud shedding)** | $St = f L / V_{\infty}$ | Dimensionless | Frequency of cavity oscillation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of This Classification

- **Propeller Design:** Naval architects use $\sigma$ to ensure that ship propellers operate at a "non-cavitating" or "lightly cavitating" envelope to avoid erosion and radiated noise.
- **Pump & Turbine Selection:** Classification helps select pump impeller geometries that avoid vortex cavitation (which causes severe vibration).
- **Submarine Stealth:** Sheet and cloud cavitation on submarine propellers are major sources of acoustic signature; classification guides propeller tip-shape optimization.
- **Hydrofoil Craft & High-Speed Vessels:** Supercavitation classification is used in **supercavitating hydrofoils** to reduce drag at high speeds.
- **Cavitation Tunnel Testing:** The classification framework directly maps to the experimental observation windows used in **Keller, De Laval, and GIT cavitation tunnels** (the focus of the PENST865 course).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — The Cavitation Number from First Principles

We begin with the steady, incompressible Bernoulli equation between a point in the freestream and a point on a body surface:

$$p_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2} = p + \frac{1}{2}\rho_l V^{2}$$

**Step 1:** Isolate the local pressure $p$ on the body:

$$p = p_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2}\rho_l(V_{\infty}^{2} - V^{2})$$

**Step 2:** Factor the dynamic pressure term to expose a non-dimensional ratio. The freestream dynamic pressure is:

$$q_{\infty} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2}$$

Substituting:

$$p = p_{\infty} + q_{\infty}\left(1 - \frac{V^{2}}{V_{\infty}^{2}}\right) = p_{\infty} + q_{\infty}(1 - (V/V_{\infty})^{2})$$

**Step 3:** Define the local velocity ratio $\lambda = V/V_{\infty}$. Then:

$$p = p_{\infty} + q_{\infty}(1 - \lambda^{2})$$

**Step 4:** Cavitation begins at the moment the local pressure reaches the vapor pressure. Therefore, the *cavitation inception condition* is:

$$p_v = p_{\infty} + q_{\infty}(1 - \lambda_{max}^{2})$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the freestream dynamic pressure required for inception. Rearranging:

$$q_{\infty}(1 - \lambda_{max}^{2}) = p_v - p_{\infty}$$

$$q_{\infty} = \frac{p_{\infty} - p_v}{\lambda_{max}^{2} - 1}$$

**Step 6:** Introduce the *minimum* pressure coefficient $C_{p,min}$ corresponding to the location of maximum velocity on the body:

$$C_{p,min} = \frac{p_v - p_{\infty}}{q_{\infty}} = 1 - \lambda_{max}^{2}$$

**Step 7:** The **Cavitation Number $\sigma$** is *defined* as the inverse of the magnitude of $C_{p,min}$ at incipient conditions:

$$\sigma = \frac{p_{\infty} - p_v}{q_{\infty}} = -C_{p,min}$$

**Step 8 (Final Form):**

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{p_{\infty} - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2}}}$$

This is the **fundamental non-dimensional parameter** that the entire classification of cavitation is built upon. When $\sigma$ is small, $q_{\infty}$ is large → easy cavitation. When $\sigma$ is large, $q_{\infty}$ is small → no cavitation.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Cavitation Regimes and Their $\sigma$ Boundaries

We define the four classical regimes observed in cavitation tunnels (these are universally taught in marine hydrodynamics):

| Regime | $\sigma$ Range | Cavity Description | Visual Appearance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **No Cavitation** | $\sigma > \sigma_i$ | No cavity; liquid remains single-phase | Clean flow, no bubbles |
| **Incipient Cavitation** | $\sigma = \sigma_i$ | First transient vapor bubbles appear at $C_{p,min}$ locations | Intermittent flashing |
| **Developed (Sheet/Bubble) Cavitation** | $\sigma_d \leq \sigma < \sigma_i$ | Stable cavity or steady stream of bubbles | Attached sheet OR travelling bubble stream |
| **Supercavitating Cavitation** | $\sigma \ll \sigma_i$ | Cavity engulfs entire body, closes far downstream | Long, elongated cavity |

**Step 1:** Let $\sigma_i = 0.30$ for a typical NACA 66-section hydrofoil in a marine cavitation tunnel.

**Step 2:** Let us determine the freestream velocity $V_{\infty}$ at which cavitation will start, given $p_{\infty} = 101{,}325$ Pa, $p_v = 2{,}339$ Pa, and $\rho_l = 998.2$ kg/m³.

**Step 3:** Rearrange the cavitation number formula:

$$\sigma_i = \frac{p_{\infty} - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

$$V_{\infty}^{2} = \frac{2(p_{\infty} - p_v)}{\sigma_i \rho_l}$$

**Step 4:** Plug numerical values:

$$V_{\infty}^{2} = \frac{2(101{,}325 - 2{,}339)}{0.30 \times 998.2}$$

$$V_{\infty}^{2} = \frac{2 \times 98{,}986}{299.46} = \frac{197{,}972}{299.46}$$

$$V_{\infty}^{2} = 661.06 \;\;\text{(m}^{2}\!/\text{s}^{2}\text{)}$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root:

$$V_{\infty} = \sqrt{661.06} \approx 25.71 \;\;\text{m/s}$$

**Conclusion:** At a freestream velocity of **25.71 m/s** (≈ 50 knots), this hydrofoil will begin to cavitate at $p_{\infty} = 101{,}325$ Pa.

**Step 6 (Practical — Tunnel Pressure Reduction):** In a real cavitation tunnel, $V_{\infty}$ is held constant (e.g., $V_{\infty} = 10$ m/s) and $p_{\infty}$ is *reduced* by a vacuum pump. Compute the tunnel pressure at which cavitation will *just* begin:

$$p_{\infty} = p_v + \sigma_i \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2}$$

$$p_{\infty} = 2{,}339 + 0.30 \times 0.5 \times 998.2 \times 10^{2}$$

$$p_{\infty} = 2{,}339 + 14{,}973 = 17{,}312 \;\;\text{Pa}$$

**Step 7 (Critical Insight):** This means the tunnel operator must reduce the static pressure from **101,325 Pa** (atmospheric) down to **17,312 Pa** — an absolute pressure ratio of approximately **5.85:1** — to observe incipient cavitation in the test section.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Hysteresis (Inception vs. Desinence)

A **critical experimental observation** in cavitation tunnels is that the value of $\sigma$ at which cavitation *starts* (incipient, $\sigma_i$) is **higher** than the value at which it *stops* (desinent, $\sigma_d$).

**Step 1:** Define the experimental procedure:
- **Increasing $\sigma$ (rising pressure):** Cavity collapses at $\sigma_d$
- **Decreasing $\sigma$ (falling pressure):** Cavity appears at $\sigma_i$

**Step 2:** The hysteresis gap is:

$$\Delta\sigma_{hyst} = \sigma_i - \sigma_d > 0$$

**Step 3 (Physical Explanation):** Once a cavity is established, the dissolved gas in the liquid is exsolved at the cavity interface. When pressure rises, this exsolved gas keeps the cavity "alive" for some time. Hence, the cavity survives at higher $\sigma$ than the inception threshold.

**Step 4 (Empirical Result from Keller Tunnel Data):** For a typical marine propeller:

$$\sigma_i \approx 0.32, \quad \sigma_d \approx 0.24 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta\sigma_{hyst} \approx 0.08$$

**Step 5 (Engineering Consequence):** A propeller operating at $\sigma = 0.28$ (between 0.24 and 0.32) is in the **hysteresis zone** — its cavitation state depends on the *history* of $\sigma$ over the last few seconds.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Classification of Cavitation

The KTU examiner expects students to identify *which* type of cavitation forms *under which* conditions. The matrix below consolidates this:

| Classification Criterion | Type 1 | Type 2 | Type 3 | Type 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **By Cavity Attachment** | Sheet (Fixed) Cavitation | Travelling Bubble Cavitation | Vortex Cavitation | Cloud Cavitation |
| **By $\sigma$ Regime** | Incipient ($\sigma = \sigma_i$) | Developed ($\sigma_d \leq \sigma < \sigma_i$) | Supercavitation ($\sigma \ll \sigma_i$) | Desinent ($\sigma = \sigma_d$) |
| **By Cavity Content** | Vaporous (pure vapor) | Gaseous (non-condensable gas) | Mixed (vapor + gas) | — |
| **By Geometry** | Hydrofoil cavitation | Propeller cavitation | Orifice/Venturi cavitation | Vortex-core cavitation |
| **By Reynolds Number** | Type I (low Re, smooth) | Type II (laminar sep.) | Type III (turbulent sep.) | Type IV (cloud shedding) |
| **By Damage Mode** | Silent (no erosion) | Erosive (pitting) | Vibratory (noise) | Super-eroding (severe) |

## 4.4 Schematic — Pressure Distribution Over a Cavitating Hydrofoil

| Region on Hydrofoil Chord | Pressure State | Cavitation Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Leading Edge (Stagnation Point)** | $p = p_{\infty} + q_{\infty}$ (maximum) | No cavitation |
| **Suction Peak (≈ 5–15% chord)** | $p = p_{\infty} + C_{p,min} q_{\infty}$ | Cavity **incepts** here |
| **Cavity Region (≈ 15% – 60% chord)** | $p \approx p_v$ (constant ≈ vapor pressure) | **Sheet cavity** of length $\ell_c$ attached |
| **Cavity Closure Region (≈ 60% chord)** | $p$ rapidly rises from $p_v$ to $p_{\infty}$ | **Cloud cavitation** + pressure pulses |
| **Recovery Region (> 60% chord)** | $p \to p_{\infty}$ (freestream recovery) | No cavitation downstream |
| **Trailing Edge** | $p = p_{\infty}$ | No cavitation |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**Define cavitation number $\sigma$. State the physical significance of $\sigma$ in classifying cavitation regimes on a marine propeller.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Limit: 80–100 words):**
- **(1 Mark)** **Definition:** The cavitation number is the non-dimensional ratio of the pressure available to suppress cavitation to the dynamic pressure of the flow, given by:
- **(1 Mark)** **Formula:** 
- **(1 Mark)** **Physical Significance:** $\sigma$ governs the *type* and *extent* of cavitation. When $\sigma > \sigma_i$ → no cavitation; when $\sigma = \sigma_i$ → incipient; when $\sigma_d \leq \sigma < \sigma_i$ → developed (sheet or bubble); when $\sigma \ll \sigma_i$ → supercavitation. Therefore, $\sigma$ is the master classification parameter used by marine propeller designers to select operating conditions.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**Distinguish between incipient and desinent cavitation. Why does the desinent cavitation number $\sigma_d$ differ from the incipient cavitation number $\sigma_i$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Limit: 80–100 words):**
- **(1 Mark)** **Incipient:** The cavitation number at which cavitation *first* appears when $\sigma$ is gradually reduced — denoted $\sigma_i$.
- **(1 Mark)** **Desinent:** The cavitation number at which cavitation *disappears* when $\sigma$ is gradually increased — denoted $\sigma_d$.
- **(1 Mark)** **Reason for difference (Hysteresis):** $\sigma_d < \sigma_i$ because once a cavity is formed, dissolved non-condensable gas is exsolved at the cavity-liquid interface. This gas keeps the cavity "alive" even after the local pressure rises above $p_v$, until the dissolved gas redissolves into the liquid — a process that takes time, producing the observed hysteresis gap.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

---

### **Question B — Option (a) — 14 Marks [CHOICE A]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the various classifications of cavitation based on **(i)** the type of cavity attachment, **(ii)** the cavity contents, and **(iii)** the geometry of the body. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification by Cavity Attachment (3 Marks)**

1. **Sheet (Fixed/Attached) Cavitation** — A transparent, two-dimensional vapor pocket that is *attached* to the suction side of a hydrofoil or propeller blade. The cavity has a relatively stable leading edge and a violent closure region. **(0.75 M)**
2. **Travelling Bubble Cavitation** — Discrete vapor bubbles that are *not* attached to the body but are convected downstream in the wake. They are often observed on ship propellers at low load conditions. **(0.75 M)**
3. **Vortex Cavitation** — A spiral vapor core formed inside the *tip vortex* of a propeller or in the vortex generated at a hydrofoil tip. Caused by the pressure drop in the vortex core falling below $p_v$. **(0.75 M)**
4. **Cloud Cavitation** — Large, unsteady, pulsating clouds of vapor formed when a sheet cavity breaks down. Produces intense noise and erosion. **(0.75 M)**

**(ii) Classification by Cavity Contents (2 Marks)**

1. **Vaporous Cavitation** — The cavity contains *pure water vapor* produced by phase change when local pressure falls below $p_v$. **(1 M)**
2. **Gaseous Cavitation** — The cavity contains *non-condensable gases* (air, CO₂) exsolved from the dissolved gas content of the liquid. **(0.5 M)**
3. **Mixed Cavitation** — A combination of vapor and gas inside the cavity, common in real marine propellers. **(0.5 M)**

**(iii) Classification by Body Geometry (2 Marks)**

1. **Hydrofoil Section Cavitation** — On 2-D foils (e.g., NACA 66); produces clean sheet cavitation. **(0.5 M)**
2. **Propeller Cavitation** — On 3-D rotating blades; includes sheet, tip-vortex, and root cavitation. **(0.5 M)**
3. **Venturi/Orifice Cavitation** — Inside a converging-diverging nozzle or restriction. **(0.5 M)**
4. **Submarine Appendage Cavitation** — On rudders, struts, and dive planes of submarines. **(0.5 M)**

---

**Q. (b)** A marine propeller is tested in a cavitation tunnel. The freestream velocity is $V_{\infty} = 12$ m/s, water density $\rho_l = 1000$ kg/m³, vapor pressure $p_v = 1.7$ kPa, and the incipient cavitation number for the blade section is $\sigma_i = 0.35$. Determine: **(i)** the tunnel pressure at which cavitation will just begin, and **(ii)** the freestream velocity at which cavitation will *just* start when the tunnel is at atmospheric pressure (101.3 kPa). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_{\infty} = 12$ m/s
- $\rho_l = 1000$ kg/m³
- $p_v = 1.7 \text{ kPa} = 1{,}700 \text{ Pa}$
- $\sigma_i = 0.35$
- $p_{atm} = 101.3 \text{ kPa} = 101{,}300 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1: Calculate freestream dynamic pressure.**

$$q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2} = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 12^{2} = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 144 = 72{,}000 \;\;\text{Pa}$$

**Step 2: Part (i) — Solve for $p_{\infty}$ at incipient cavitation.**

From $\sigma_i = (p_{\infty} - p_v)/q_{\infty}$:

$$p_{\infty} = p_v + \sigma_i \cdot q_{\infty} = 1{,}700 + 0.35 \times 72{,}000$$

$$p_{\infty} = 1{,}700 + 25{,}200 = 26{,}900 \;\;\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{p_{\infty} = 26.9 \;\;\text{kPa}}$$

**Step 3: Part (ii) — Solve for $V_{\infty}$ at incipient cavitation with $p_{\infty} = 101.3$ kPa.**

Rearranging:

$$V_{\infty}^{2} = \frac{2(p_{\infty} - p_v)}{\sigma_i \rho_l} = \frac{2(101{,}300 - 1{,}700)}{0.35 \times 1000}$$

$$V_{\infty}^{2} = \frac{2 \times 99{,}600}{350} = \frac{199{,}200}{350} = 569.14 \;\;\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

$$V_{\infty} = \sqrt{569.14} = 23.86 \;\;\text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\infty} \approx 23.86 \;\;\text{m/s} \;\;(\approx 46.4 \;\;\text{knots})}$$

**Step 4: Engineering Interpretation (for full marks).**

> **(1 Mark bonus)** At $V_{\infty} = 12$ m/s, the tunnel pressure must be reduced from **101.3 kPa to 26.9 kPa** — a 73.5% reduction — to observe incipient cavitation. This is why cavitation tunnels use vacuum pumps to lower the test-section pressure rather than accelerating the flow to unrealistic speeds.

---

### **Question B — Option (b) — 14 Marks [CHOICE B]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the four classical cavitation regimes (no cavitation, incipient, developed, supercavitation) and their corresponding $\sigma$ ranges. State the physical meaning of cavitation hysteresis with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(1) No Cavitation Regime (1 Mark):**
When $\sigma > \sigma_i$, the local minimum pressure on the body remains above $p_v$ everywhere. The flow is single-phase liquid. No cavity is observed. **Visual:** Clean flow, no bubbles.

**(2) Incipient Cavitation Regime (1.5 Marks):**
When $\sigma$ is gradually reduced to $\sigma = \sigma_i$, the local pressure at the $C_{p,min}$ point first reaches $p_v$. Intermittent, transient vapor bubbles appear. **Visual:** Occasional flashing/bubbles at the suction peak.

**(3) Developed Cavitation Regime (1.5 Marks):**
When $\sigma$ is further reduced such that $\sigma_d \leq \sigma < \sigma_i$, the cavity is stable and well-developed. Depending on geometry, this could be **sheet cavitation** (attached transparent pocket) or **travelling bubble cavitation** (discrete bubbles convecting). **Visual:** Steady cavity or bubble stream.

**(4) Supercavitating Cavitation Regime (1 Mark):**
When $\sigma \ll \sigma_i$, the cavity extends so far downstream that it engulfs the entire body. The body is no longer in contact with liquid along most of its length. **Visual:** Long, elongated cavity closing far downstream.

**(5) Cavitation Hysteresis — Diagram (2 Marks):**
On a $\sigma$-vs-cavity-length plot:
- During **decreasing $\sigma$** (inception path): cavity appears at $\sigma_i$ and grows.
- During **increasing $\sigma$** (desinent path): cavity collapses at $\sigma_d < \sigma_i$.
- The two paths form a **hysteresis loop**.

**Physical Reason:** Dissolved non-condensable gas keeps the cavity alive above $p_v$ for some time.

---

**Q. (b)** A hydrofoil section is tested in a cavitation tunnel. The incipient cavitation number is $\sigma_i = 0.28$ and the desinent number is $\sigma_d = 0.22$. The tunnel operates at $V_{\infty} = 8$ m/s, $\rho_l = 998$ kg/m³, and $p_v = 2.3$ kPa. Determine: **(i)** the tunnel pressure at inception, **(ii)** the tunnel pressure at desinence, and **(iii)** the hysteresis pressure gap $\Delta p$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\sigma_i = 0.28$
- $\sigma_d = 0.22$
- $V_{\infty} = 8$ m/s
- $\rho_l = 998$ kg/m³
- $p_v = 2.3 \text{ kPa} = 2{,}300 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1: Compute freestream dynamic pressure.**

$$q_{\infty} = 0.5 \times 998 \times 8^{2} = 0.5 \times 998 \times 64 = 31{,}936 \;\;\text{Pa}$$

**Step 2: Part (i) — Tunnel pressure at inception.**

$$p_{\infty,i} = p_v + \sigma_i \cdot q_{\infty} = 2{,}300 + 0.28 \times 31{,}936$$

$$p_{\infty,i} = 2{,}300 + 8{,}942.08 = 11{,}242.08 \;\;\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{p_{\infty,i} \approx 11.24 \;\;\text{kPa (absolute)}}$$

**Step 3: Part (ii) — Tunnel pressure at desinence.**

$$p_{\infty,d} = p_v + \sigma_d \cdot q_{\infty} = 2{,}300 + 0.22 \times 31{,}936$$

$$p_{\infty,d} = 2{,}300 + 7{,}025.92 = 9{,}325.92 \;\;\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{p_{\infty,d} \approx 9.33 \;\;\text{kPa (absolute)}}$$

**Step 4: Part (iii) — Hysteresis pressure gap.**

$$\Delta p = p_{\infty,i} - p_{\infty,d} = 11{,}242.08 - 9{,}325.92 = 1{,}916.16 \;\;\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta p \approx 1.92 \;\;\text{kPa}}$$

**Step 5: Physical Interpretation (1 Mark for full credit):**

The hysteresis gap of **1.92 kPa** means that if the tunnel operator sets the test-section pressure anywhere between **9.33 kPa and 11.24 kPa**, the cavitation state of the hydrofoil will depend on the *previous history* of pressure. If pressure was recently high (above 11.24 kPa) and is being *lowered* into this zone, no cavitation will appear until 11.24 kPa. If pressure was recently low (below 9.33 kPa) and is being *raised*, cavitation will persist until 9.33 kPa.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Students frequently forget to convert kPa → Pa before substituting into the $\sigma$ formula. *Always* work in SI base units (Pa, m/s, kg/m³) before plugging in.
> 2. **Pressure Reference Confusion:** $p_{\infty}$ is the *absolute* static pressure in the test section, NOT the *gauge* pressure. The vapor pressure $p_v$ is also absolute. Do not mix gauge and absolute.
> 3. **Cavity Length Formula Misuse:** The supercavity length formula $L_c \approx \ell\sqrt{C_d(1+\sigma)/\sigma}$ is valid *only* for supercavitating flows, not for sheet cavitation.
> 4. **Hysteresis Reversal:** Some students incorrectly state $\sigma_i < \sigma_d$. The correct relation is $\sigma_i > \sigma_d$ (inception requires *more* pressure suppression to *initiate*; desinence requires *less*).
> 5. **Vaporous vs. Gaseous Confusion:** Vaporous = pure vapor from *phase change*; Gaseous = non-condensable gas from *exsolution*. Both can coexist in the same cavity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Cavitation — KTU PENST865 Module 4 — Rapid Recap**

- **Cavitation Definition:** Vapor- or gas-filled cavity formation in a liquid when local static pressure falls to/below $p_v$. (1 line)
- **Master Parameter:** **Cavitation Number** $\sigma = (p_{\infty} - p_v)/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_l V_{\infty}^{2})$ — dimensionless.
- **Inception Condition:** Cavity appears when $\sigma = \sigma_i$, equivalently $C_{p,min} = -\sigma_i$.
- **Four Classical Regimes by $\sigma$:** No cavitation → Incipient → Developed → Supercavitating.
- **Classification by Attachment:** Sheet (Fixed), Travelling Bubble, Vortex, Cloud, Supercavitating.
- **Classification by Content:** Vaporous, Gaseous, Mixed.
- **Classification by Geometry:** Hydrofoil, Propeller, Venturi/Orifice, Submarine Appendage.
- **Hysteresis Rule:** $\sigma_d < \sigma_i$ always; typical gap $\Delta\sigma_{hyst} \approx 0.05$–$0.10$.
- **Vortex Cavitation Origin:** Tip vortex of propeller — pressure at vortex core drops below $p_v$.
- **Cloud Cavitation Origin:** Breakdown of sheet cavity at closure region; produces strong pressure pulses.
- **Supercavitation Origin:** $\sigma \ll \sigma_i$ → cavity engulfs entire body; cavity length $L_c \approx \ell\sqrt{C_d(1+\sigma)/\sigma}$.
- **Pressure Reference:** $p_{\infty}$ and $p_v$ are **absolute** pressures (Pa), not gauge.
- **Units Convention:** SI base units throughout — Pa, m/s, kg/m³, N, J.
- **Experimental Source:** All classifications are observable in cavitation tunnels (Keller, De Laval, GIT) used in PENST865 laboratory work.
- **Engineering Impact:** Sheet cavitation → erosion & efficiency loss; Vortex cavitation → noise & vibration; Cloud cavitation → severe erosion & acoustic signature.
- **Defense Application:** Submarine propeller cavitation classification is critical for radiated-noise signature reduction (stealth).
- **Merchant Marine Application:** Cavitation classification guides selection of propeller blade area ratio (BAR) and pitch distribution to avoid sheet and tip-vortex cavitation in service.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation** is a physical phenomenon that occurs in a flowing liquid when the local static pressure falls below the **vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature, resulting in the formation, growth, and subsequent collapse of vapor-filled cavities (bubbles) within the flow field.

In the context of **marine hydrodynamics**, cavitation is principally studied in connection with:
- **Marine propellers** (the dominant application)
- **High-speed underwater bodies** (torpedoes, control surfaces)
- **Pumps, rudders, and shaft brackets**
- **Hydrofoils and high-speed planing craft**

A **Cavitation Test** is a series of carefully controlled model experiments performed in a specialized hydrodynamic facility called a **Cavitation Tunnel** (or **Cavitation Basin**), designed to:

1. Reproduce the **in-service cavitation behavior** of a marine component at model scale.
2. Determine the **onset, extent, and type** of cavitation under varying operating conditions.
3. Quantify the **hydrodynamic performance degradation** (thrust loss, torque fluctuation, efficiency drop, noise, erosion) caused by cavitation.
4. Provide a validated database for **full-scale extrapolation** via similarity laws.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cavitation Number (σ)** — The primary non-dimensional parameter governing cavitation similarity. It is the **single most important quantity** the student must master for Module 4. Its formal expression is given in Section 2.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Cavitation in Plain English

Imagine you are boiling water in a covered pot. You heat the pot, the water gets hot, and bubbles form *because the temperature reaches the boiling point*. That is **boiling** — driven by temperature.

Now imagine a different scenario: you are pumping cold water through a narrow pipe. The water itself is at room temperature, but as it rushes through the narrow section, its **pressure drops dramatically** (Bernoulli's principle: high speed ↔ low pressure). If the local pressure falls below the vapor pressure of cold water (~2.3 kPa absolute at 20 °C), **bubbles spontaneously form** in the cold water. This is **cavitation**.

So:
- **Boiling** = bubbles form because *temperature rises* above saturation.
- **Cavitation** = bubbles form because *pressure falls* below saturation.

> [!TIP]
> The propeller blade tip is the marine equivalent of the "narrow pipe section." As the blade slices through water, the local velocity spikes on the suction side, the pressure plummets, and vapor cavities nucleate — exactly as in the pipe.

**The collapse of these bubbles** is the destructive part. When a cavity travels into a higher-pressure region, it implodes violently, generating:
- Micro-jets and shock waves
- Local pressures of several hundred MPa
- Surface pitting and erosion (cavitation erosion)
- Broadband noise (20 kHz to 1 MHz)
- Vibration and structural fatigue

This is why cavitation tests are mandatory during the design of any high-speed marine propulsor.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Marine Context) |
|---|---|---|
| Vapor pressure of seawater at 15 °C | $p_v$ | $\approx 1.7 \text{ kPa (abs)}$ |
| Vapor pressure of freshwater at 20 °C | $p_v$ | $\approx 2.34 \text{ kPa (abs)}$ |
| Ambient static pressure (full scale, surface) | $p_0$ | $\approx 101.3 \text{ kPa}$ |
| Net inlet pressure (used in σ) | $p_0 - p_v$ | $\approx 99.6 \text{ kPa}$ (typical) |
| Reference velocity (free stream / $nD$) | $V_\infty$ | $5 - 35 \text{ m/s}$ |
| Threshold cavitation number (typical for propellers) | $\sigma_T$ | $0.6 - 2.5$ |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student error is to use the **absolute** ambient pressure instead of the **net** pressure $(p_0 - p_v)$ in the cavitation number formula. The numerator is *always* the **net pressure** — the pressure margin above vapor pressure. Memorize this.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Cavitation — Stepwise Logic

Cavitation is a multi-stage process. Understanding each stage is essential for designing and interpreting cavitation tests.

1. **Nucleation** — Tiny gas/vapor pockets (already present in the liquid or trapped on solid surfaces) act as nucleation sites. The liquid is never perfectly "air-free."
2. **Pressure Drop** — As the fluid accelerates around a body (propeller blade, hydrofoil, etc.), local static pressure falls. The lowest pressure typically occurs on the **suction (low-pressure) side** of the blade near the leading edge.
3. **Critical Condition** — When local static pressure $p_{\min} \le p_v$ (vapor pressure), the liquid locally "boils" at ambient temperature, and a vapor-filled cavity grows.
4. **Cavity Growth** — The cavity enlarges in the low-pressure region, often forming a sheet over the blade surface.
5. **Cavity Collapse** — As the cavity is convected into a region where pressure exceeds $p_v$, the vapor condenses rapidly. The collapse is **violent** and asymmetric, producing damaging micro-jets and shock waves.
6. **Re-initiation** — The cycle repeats with each blade passage, leading to fluctuating loads and noise.

---

## 2.2 Why Cavitation Tests Are Necessary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cavitation **cannot be reliably predicted by potential-flow theory** alone. It depends on viscous effects, turbulence, nuclei distribution, surface roughness, and gas content — all of which are scale-dependent. This is why physical model tests in cavitation tunnels are an **absolute necessity** for propeller design.

Specifically, cavitation tests are conducted to:

- **Identify the operating envelope** (combinations of speed, advance coefficient, and pressure margin) within which cavitation remains acceptable.
- **Classify the type of cavitation** (sheet, bubble, tip-vortex, hub-vortex, leading-edge, etc.) that occurs first as σ is reduced.
- **Quantify performance penalties** — the drop in thrust coefficient $K_T$, rise in torque coefficient $K_Q$, and loss in open-water efficiency $\eta_0$ as a function of σ.
- **Measure unsteady forces and pressures** on the blade (chordwise, side-force fluctuations) for **excited vibration** analysis.
- **Detect cavitation-induced noise signatures** for acoustic signature control of submarines and stealth vessels.
- **Validate CFD codes** for cavitating flows (a modern, increasingly important use).

---

## 2.3 Similarity Parameters — The Four Laws of Cavitation Similitude

To transfer model-test results to full scale, the following dimensionless numbers **must be matched** between model and prototype (or, if impossible, their effect must be quantified):

| # | Parameter | Definition | What It Ensures | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Geometric similarity** | All linear ratios preserved | Same shape | Strictly required |
| 2 | **Cavitation number** $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2}$ | Same pressure / inertia ratio | **Most important** |
| 3 | **Reynolds number** $Re$ | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V_\infty L}{\mu}$ | Same viscous/inertia ratio | Hard to match; effect studied via Re-curve |
| 4 | **Weber number** $We$ | $We = \dfrac{\rho V_\infty^2 L}{\sigma_s}$ (surface tension) | Same inertia / surface-tension ratio | Important for very small models |
| 5 | **Froude number** $Fr$ | $Fr = \dfrac{V_\infty}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Same gravity / inertia ratio (free surface) | Required for cavitation basins, not tunnels |
| 6 | **Nuclei similarity** | Same size/distribution of cavitation nuclei | Same cavity inception behaviour | **Hardest to satisfy** — no easy scaling law |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reynolds-number scaling effect**: Model propellers operate at much lower $Re$ than full-scale ones. The boundary layer is relatively thicker, so the **laminar/turbulent transition location** differs. This changes the cavity extent and onset. ITTC standard procedures require that the test Reynolds number be at least $Re = 2 \times 10^5$ based on chord length, and that results at lower $Re$ be treated as conservative.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cavitation Test Equations

> [!TIP]
> This table is your **examination cheat sheet** for Module 4. Memorize the *form*, not the absolute values.

| # | Formula | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2}$ | Cavitation number (velocity-based) | Primary similarity parameter |
| F2 | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho (nD)^2}$ | Cavitation number (rotational, propeller) | Used when $V_\infty$ is small vs. $nD$ |
| F3 | $J = \dfrac{V_A}{nD}$ | Advance coefficient | $V_A$ = speed of advance |
| F4 | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | Thrust coefficient | $T$ = thrust |
| F5 | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ | Torque coefficient | $Q$ = torque |
| F6 | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q} \cdot \dfrac{J}{2\pi}$ | Open-water efficiency | — |
| F7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V_A D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (advance) | — |
| F8 | $Re_c = \dfrac{\rho c \sqrt{V_A^2 + (\pi n D)^2}}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (blade-section, ITTC 1978) | $c$ = section chord |
| F9 | $p_0 = p_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h$ | Total static pressure at depth $h$ | Used to set test conditions |
| F10 | $p_v(T)$ from steam tables | Vapor pressure of water vs. temperature | Tunes σ by temperature in tunnel |
| F11 | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v - \Delta p_{\text{blade min}}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2}$ | Inception cavitation number (with $\Delta p$ from blade loading) | Theoretically; in practice, $\sigma_i$ is read off the test plot |
| F12 | $\dfrac{T_{\text{cav}}}{T_0} = f(\sigma)$ | Thrust ratio in cavitation | Determined experimentally |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 examinations, examiners **expect** the student to know that:
> 1. $\sigma$ is the **key** non-dimensional number for cavitation.
> 2. $\sigma$ is **inversely proportional** to the square of velocity — doubling the speed reduces σ by a factor of 4.
> 3. The condition for **cavitation inception** is $\sigma = \sigma_i$ (the inception cavitation number).
> 4. **Lower σ → more severe cavitation** (because the available pressure margin shrinks relative to the dynamic pressure).

---

## 2.5 Types of Cavitation Observed in Tests

The most commonly classified types in a propeller test are:

- **Sheet (or Back) Cavitation** — A thin vapor sheet attached to the blade's suction side, typically near the leading edge. The most common and most extensively studied.
- **Bubble Cavitation** — Discrete, transient vapor clouds. Usually observed in the blade-tip region or in turbulent boundary-layer separation zones.
- **Tip Vortex Cavitation** — A helical vapor core formed by the low-pressure core of the blade-tip vortex. Often the **earliest** form to appear on highly-loaded propellers.
- **Hub Vortex Cavitation** — A vortex formed at the hub, often attached to the propeller boss.
- **Leading-Edge (Lifting) Cavitation** — Cavities forming on the leading edge when the angle of attack is high.
- **Cloud Cavitation** — Large, periodic clouds shed from sheet cavities. Major source of unsteady forces.
- **Super-cavitation** — The cavity completely envelops the blade; only the leading edge is in contact with liquid. Relevant to supercavitating torpedoes and high-speed applications.

---

## 2.6 Cavitation Test Facilities — Engineering Context

Two principal facilities are used worldwide:

### 2.6.1 Cavitation Tunnel
- A closed-loop, pressurized/depressurized water tunnel.
- **Advantages:** Precise control of velocity and pressure, easier instrumentation, lower cost.
- **Limitations:** Limited test-section size, walls affect flow, no free-surface effect captured.
- Famous examples: **Göttingen Cavitation Tunnel** (Germany), **EMPA tunnel** (Switzerland), **HSVA tunnel** (Hamburg), **INSEAN tunnel** (Italy), **SRI/KIMM** (Korea).

### 2.6.2 Cavitation Basin
- A large towing tank with a pressurized roof over a section to allow in-air propeller testing.
- **Advantages:** Larger models possible, free-surface effects included, self-propulsion tests feasible.
- **Limitations:** Lower control precision, higher cost, slower test campaign.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask students to **compare** the two facilities. Be ready with at least three valid comparison points.

---

## 2.7 Test Procedure — Generalized Workflow

1. **Model Preparation** — Precision-machined propeller (or component) model, polished surface, paint grid for cavitation observation. Carefully measured geometry.
2. **Installation** — Mounted on the dynamometer shaft at the test section. Shaft aligned, bearings checked.
3. **De-aeration** — Water circulated for several hours to reduce dissolved-gas content and stabilize the **nuclei spectrum**. This is **critical** to obtaining reproducible cavitation.
4. **Calibration** — Pressure transducers, force balances, speed sensors, flowmeters calibrated against standards.
5. **Test Matrix Execution** — Vary $(J, \sigma)$ systematically. For each combination, record:
   - Thrust, torque, RPM, velocity, pressure, temperature.
   - Photograph/video the cavitation pattern.
6. **Data Reduction** — Compute $K_T$, $K_Q$, $\eta_0$, $\sigma$, and plot.
7. **Cavitation Observation** — Visual + photographic + acoustic (hydrophone) + high-speed video for transient events.
8. **Repeatability Check** — Repeat selected runs to verify the consistency of cavitation patterns and forces.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget to include the temperature-correction step. Vapor pressure $p_v$ is a strong function of temperature. For every 1 °C rise, $p_v$ rises by ~170 Pa in water at 20 °C, which materially changes σ. Always state $T$ when reporting σ.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cavitation Number

We start from a fundamental dimensional argument. The condition for incipient cavitation is that the local minimum pressure on the body equals the vapor pressure:

$$p_{\min} = p_v$$

For a body immersed in a flow with free-stream velocity $V_\infty$ and free-stream static pressure $p_0$, we apply a Bernoulli-like argument between the free stream and the body's minimum-pressure point (a streamline point). The dynamic pressure at the minimum-pressure point is captured by a **pressure coefficient** $C_{p,\min}$:

$$p_{\min} = p_0 + C_{p,\min} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2$$

For the inception condition, set $p_{\min} = p_v$ and rearrange:

$$p_0 + C_{p,\min} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = p_v$$

Move the free-stream pressure term to the right:

$$C_{p,\min} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = p_v - p_0$$

The left side is negative (low pressure), so we write:

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 (p_0 - p_v) = -\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 C_{p,\min} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2$$

Cleaner: the **net pressure margin** available above vapor pressure is $(p_0 - p_v)$, while the dynamic pressure is $\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2$. Their ratio is the **cavitation number**:

$$\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $\sigma$ measures the **pressure margin** relative to the **inertial loading**. If $\sigma$ is large, there is ample pressure head to suppress cavitation. If $\sigma$ is small, the dynamic pressure easily overwhelms the available margin, and cavitation forms.

---

## 3.2 Alternative Forms of the Cavitation Number

### 3.2.1 For Propeller Tests (Using $nD$)

For marine propellers, the relevant velocity scale is the **blade-tip speed** (or $nD$), not the free-stream $V_\infty$:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho (nD)^2}$$

This form is useful when the inflow velocity is small (e.g., bollard pull condition, $V_A \to 0$). Note that $V_A$ is generally **not equal** to $nD$ — they are independent reference scales.

### 3.2.2 Composite Cavitation Number

A more general form uses the **relative velocity** seen by the blade section. At radius $r$ and rotational speed $n$, the resultant velocity is:

$$V_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{V_A^2 + (2\pi n r)^2}$$

The local cavitation number at radius $r$ becomes:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\text{rel}}^2}$$

This is the **proper local** form used in modern blade-design analysis.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation — Pressure Margin Required to Suppress Cavitation

**Problem:** A propeller is operating in seawater at 18 °C. The propeller diameter is 5 m, the rotational speed is 120 RPM, the speed of advance is 7 m/s, and the ambient pressure at the shaft centreline is 200 kPa (abs). Determine:
(a) The cavitation number based on $nD$.
(b) The cavitation number based on $V_A$.
(c) The local cavitation number at $r/R = 0.7$.

### Step 1 — Data and Constants

- $p_0 = 200 \text{ kPa} = 200{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $p_v(\text{seawater}, 18\,°\text{C}) \approx 2.07 \text{ kPa} = 2{,}070 \text{ Pa}$
- $\rho_{\text{seawater}} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $D = 5 \text{ m}$, $n = 120 \text{ RPM} = 2 \text{ rev/s}$, $V_A = 7 \text{ m/s}$

### Step 2 — Compute $nD$

$$nD = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 3 — Cavitation number based on $nD$

$$\sigma_n = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho (nD)^2} = \frac{200000 - 2070}{0.5 \times 1025 \times 10^2} = \frac{197930}{51250} \approx 3.86$$

### Step 4 — Cavitation number based on $V_A$

$$\sigma_V = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_A^2} = \frac{197930}{0.5 \times 1025 \times 7^2} = \frac{197930}{25112.5} \approx 7.88$$

### Step 5 — Local cavitation number at $r/R = 0.7$

At $r = 0.7R = 0.7 \times 2.5 = 1.75 \text{ m}$, the tangential velocity is:

$$V_t = 2\pi n r = 2\pi \times 2 \times 1.75 = 21.99 \text{ m/s}$$

The relative velocity:

$$V_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{V_A^2 + V_t^2} = \sqrt{7^2 + 21.99^2} = \sqrt{49 + 483.6} = \sqrt{532.6} \approx 23.08 \text{ m/s}$$

Local cavitation number:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{197930}{0.5 \times 1025 \times 23.08^2} = \frac{197930}{273{,}067} \approx 0.725$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

The fact that $\sigma_n = 3.86$ (high, no cavitation on average) but $\sigma_r = 0.725$ at $r/R = 0.7$ (much lower, possible cavitation) shows that **local** cavitation numbers are far more useful for design than global ones. The blade-tip and outer regions are usually where cavitation appears first.

> [!TIP]
> This worked example is the **exact type of calculation** the KTU examiner loves to set. Practice until you can do it in 4 minutes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cavitation Performance Indicators

The table below summarizes what the student must compute and interpret from a cavitation test.

| Test Output | Equation | Engineering Meaning | Effect of Decreasing σ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thrust coefficient $K_T$ | $K_T = T/(\rho n^2 D^4)$ | Load-carrying capability | Slight decrease in fully cavitating regime |
| Torque coefficient $K_Q$ | $K_Q = Q/(\rho n^2 D^5)$ | Power absorption | Drops sharply at inception |
| Open-water efficiency $\eta_0$ | $\eta_0 = (K_T/K_Q) \cdot (J/2\pi)$ | Propulsive efficiency | Drops due to $K_Q$ decrease |
| Cavity extent $l_c/L$ | Read from photographs | Cavity length relative to chord | Increases |
| Cavity thickness $t_c/c$ | Visual / PIV measurement | Cavity thickness relative to chord | Increases |
| Sheet cavitation onset $\sigma_{\text{sheet}}$ | Read from inception plot | Critical $\sigma$ for sheet cavitation | — (defined value) |
| Tip vortex inception $\sigma_{\text{TV}}$ | Read from inception plot | Critical $\sigma$ for tip-vortex cavitation | — |
| Unsteady blade pressure $C_{p,\text{unsteady}}$ | Measured by pressure transducers | Vibratory load | Increases with σ decrease |
| Spectral noise level $S(f)$ | Measured by hydrophone | Acoustic signature | Strong increase |

---

## 3.5 Power and Efficiency Calculations — Numerical Workflow

A cavitation test delivers the following raw data for each test point:

- $T$ (N), $Q$ (Nm), $n$ (rev/s), $V_A$ (m/s), $p_0$ (Pa), $T_{\text{water}}$ (°C), $D$ (m).

**Step-by-step reduction:**

1. Compute $p_v$ from temperature (steam table or Antoine equation).
2. Compute $J = V_A / (nD)$.
3. Compute $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$.
4. Compute $K_Q = Q / (\rho n^2 D^5)$.
5. Compute $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v) / (0.5 \rho V_A^2)$ (or $\sigma_n$ if using $nD$).
6. Compute $\eta_0 = (K_T / K_Q) \cdot (J / 2\pi)$.
7. Plot $K_T(J,\sigma)$, $K_Q(J,\sigma)$, $\eta_0(J,\sigma)$ as curves at constant $\sigma$.

The resulting **constant-σ curves** are the principal deliverable of a cavitation test campaign.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cavitation Test Facility Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> A physical drawing of a cavitation tunnel is impossible in plain text, so the following **block-level functional architecture** captures all major subsystems and their interaction. This is a **functional schematic** showing the engineering intent.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[reservoir\nStabilization Tank] --> A2[contraction1\nHoneycomb Flow Straightener]
    A2 --> A3[contraction2\nNozzle 4 to 1 Area Ratio]
    A3 --> A4[testsection\nWorking Section]
    A4 --> A5[diffuser1\nPressure Recovery]
    A5 --> A6[pumpunit\nAxial Flow Pump]
    A6 --> A7[flowmeter\nMagnetic Flow Tube]
    A7 --> A8[vacuumline\nVacuum Pump and Pressure Regulator]
    A8 --> A9[presscontrol\nAir Bleed and Pressure Stabilizer]
    A9 --> A1

    A4 --> B1[dynoprop\nDynamometer Mounted Propeller]
    B1 --> B2[loadcell\nThrust and Torque Transducer]
    B2 --> B3[dashaft\nDrive Shaft and Motor Coupling]
    B3 --> B4[drivemotor\nVariable Speed Electric Motor]
    B4 --> B5[controller\nRPM and Torque Controller]

    A4 --> C1[obswin\nOptical Observation Window]
    C1 --> C2[camvid\nHigh Speed Camera and Lighting]
    C1 --> C3[hydrophone\nUnderwater Acoustic Sensor]
    C1 --> C4[presstrans\nBlade Surface Pressure Transducers]

    A4 --> D1[tempprobe\nWater Temperature Probe]
    A4 --> D2[presstransmain\nSection Static Pressure Tap]
    A4 --> D3[pitotprobe\nVelocity Reference Pitot Tube]

    D1 --> E1[databook\nData Acquisition System]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    B2 --> E1
    B4 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    C4 --> E1

    E1 --> F1[reduction\nCompute KT KQ sigma eta0]
    F1 --> F2[plotchart\nPlot KT KQ eta0 versus J for constant sigma]
    F1 --> F3[imagework\nCavitation Pattern Analysis and Classification]
```

**Block-level reading guide:**

- The **horizontal upper loop** (A1 → A2 → A3 → A4 → A5 → A6 → A7 → A8 → A9 → A1) is the **closed-circuit water loop**. It is the heart of the cavitation tunnel. The vacuum line (A8) is what allows the test-section static pressure to be **lowered below atmospheric**, mimicking deep-submergence or high-velocity conditions.
- The **vertical branch** (B1 → B2 → B3 → B4 → B5) is the **propeller drive and dynamometer chain**. The dynamometer is what measures thrust and torque.
- The **right-side branch** (C1 → C2, C3, C4) is the **observation / instrumentation** suite. Optical, acoustic, and pressure measurements feed into the same data acquisition system.
- The **bottom row** (D1, D2, D3) is the **environmental reference** — temperature, pressure, and velocity. Without these, the cavitation number σ cannot be computed.
- The **right-edge column** (E1 → F1 → F2, F3) is the **data reduction and reporting** path. This is what the KTU examiner values most.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Test Procedure Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[start] --> S2[design test matrix\nDefine ranges of J and sigma]
    S2 --> S3[mount model on dynamometer\nVerify alignment]
    S3 --> S4[fill and deaerate tunnel\nRun for 4 to 8 hours]
    S4 --> S5[set test point 1\nChoose V p0 T target J]
    S5 --> S6[stabilize flow\nWait 5 to 10 minutes]
    S6 --> S7[record data\nThrust torque RPM pressure temperature]
    S7 --> S8[observe and photograph cavitation\nUse window and high speed camera]
    S8 --> S9[store in DAQ database]
    S9 --> S10{next test point}
    S10 -- yes --> S5
    S10 -- no --> S11[reduce data\nCompute KT KQ sigma eta0]
    S11 --> S12[plot constant sigma curves]
    S12 --> S13[write report and archive]
    S13 --> S14[end]
```

---

## 4.3 Cavitation Regime Diagram (Logical)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[sigma very high\nNo cavitation\nPure attached flow] --> R2[sigma decreasing\nBubble inception\nDiscrete transient cavities]
    R2 --> R3[sigma lower\nTip vortex cavitation\nSpiral vapor core from blade tip]
    R3 --> R4[sigma lower still\nSheet cavitation onset\nStable vapor sheet on suction side]
    R4 --> R5[sigma very low\nCloud cavitation\nPeriodic shedding of large vapor clouds]
    R5 --> R6[sigma extremely low\nSupercavitation\nBlade fully enveloped by single cavity]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner note:** The "appearance order" of cavitation types as σ decreases is **model- and design-dependent**. For highly skewed propellers, tip-vortex cavitation often appears *before* sheet cavitation. For conventional propellers, sheet cavitation appears first. Always state the design context.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PENST865]

**Define the cavitation number and explain its physical significance in marine propeller testing.**

**Model Answer:**

The cavitation number $\sigma$ is a non-dimensional parameter that compares the net pressure margin above vapor pressure to the dynamic pressure of the flow:

$$\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2}$$

**Physical significance:** $\sigma$ quantifies the propensity of a flow to cavitate. A **large** $\sigma$ means a large pressure margin and therefore **no cavitation**. A **small** $\sigma$ means a small margin relative to the dynamic pressure, so **cavitation is likely**. It is the principal similarity parameter for transferring model cavitation test results to full-scale propellers. For a propeller, the value based on $nD$, denoted $\sigma_n$, is most commonly used.

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PENST865]

**State four different types of cavitation observed on a marine propeller model during a cavitation test, and briefly describe one.**

**Model Answer:**

Four common types of cavitation are:
1. **Sheet (back) cavitation** — A thin vapor sheet attached to the suction side of the blade, starting near the leading edge.
2. **Bubble cavitation** — Small, discrete transient vapor clouds, often in turbulent separation zones.
3. **Tip-vortex cavitation** — A helical vapor core formed by the low-pressure core of the blade-tip leakage vortex.
4. **Hub-vortex cavitation** — A vortex cavity at the hub, extending downstream of the propeller.

**Description of tip-vortex cavitation:** The blade tip generates a strong trailing vortex because of the pressure difference between pressure and suction surfaces. The vortex has a low-pressure core, and when this pressure falls below $p_v$, the core cavitates. Tip-vortex cavitation is often the **earliest** form to appear on highly loaded, skewed propellers, and it is a major source of noise and erosion on the rudder and hull.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Cavitation Test Theory and Analysis [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a pressurized cavitation tunnel used for marine propeller model testing. List the major components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 0.25 m diameter model of a 5 m prototype propeller is tested in a cavitation tunnel at a rotational speed of 18 rev/s and an inflow velocity of 4 m/s. The static pressure in the test section is 35 kPa (abs) and the water temperature is 22 °C. Calculate:
   (i) The cavitation number based on $nD$.
   (ii) The cavitation number based on $V_A$.
   (iii) The Reynolds number based on chord at $r/R = 0.7$ (assume $c/R = 0.18$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Cavitation Tunnel Principle and Components**

A pressurized cavitation tunnel is a closed-circuit water facility in which a propeller model is mounted on a dynamometer in a closed test section. The test section is enclosed by **optical windows** for cavitation observation. The static pressure in the test section is independently controlled by a **vacuum pump and air-bleed valve** to values both above and below atmospheric, so the cavitation number can be varied at fixed velocity.

**Major components:**
1. **Stabilization tank** with stilling baffles — removes swirl and large-scale turbulence.
2. **Honeycomb flow straightener** — breaks up large eddies.
3. **Contraction nozzle** (typical area ratio 4:1 to 9:1) — accelerates and straightens flow.
4. **Test section** — holds the model, with optical windows, pressure taps, and a dynamometer well.
5. **Diffuser** — recovers pressure gradually to prevent cavitation in the line.
6. **Axial-flow pump** with variable-speed drive — controls the flow rate.
7. **Magnetic flowmeter** — measures flow rate.
8. **Vacuum pump and pressure-control system** — sets test-section absolute pressure.
9. **Dynamometer** — measures thrust, torque, and rotational speed.
10. **Data acquisition and high-speed imaging system** — records all data and the cavitation pattern.

**Working principle sketch (text representation):**

```
       [Stilling Tank]
              |
        [Honeycomb]
              |
        [Contraction]
              |
   ======[ TEST SECTION ]======     <-- Pressure = p0, Velocity = V
   |   (  [Propeller + Dyna]  )   |
   |   (  [Optical Windows]    )   |
   |   (  [Pressure Taps]      )   |
   =================================
              |
           [Diffuser]
              |
        [Axial Pump] <---> [Vacuum / Pressure Control]
              |
        [Magnetic Flowmeter]
              |
        (Return to Tank)
```

> [!TIP]
> If the question asks for a "neat sketch," the **above block diagram with annotations** is the minimum acceptable. A real KTU answer may include a simple side-view with arrows.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.25 \text{ m}$, $n = 18 \text{ rev/s}$, $V_A = 4 \text{ m/s}$
- $p_0 = 35{,}000 \text{ Pa}$ (abs)
- $T = 22\,°\text{C}$, so $p_v(22\,°\text{C}) \approx 2{,}645 \text{ Pa}$ (steam table)
- $\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (water at 22 °C)
- $\mu = 9.57 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ at 22 °C

**Step 1 — Cavitation number based on $nD$:**

$$nD = 18 \times 0.25 = 4.5 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\sigma_n = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho (nD)^2} = \frac{35000 - 2645}{0.5 \times 998 \times 4.5^2} = \frac{32355}{10104.75} \approx 3.20$$

**Step 2 — Cavitation number based on $V_A$:**

$$\sigma_V = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_A^2} = \frac{32355}{0.5 \times 998 \times 4^2} = \frac{32355}{7984} \approx 4.05$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds number at $r/R = 0.7$:**

At $r = 0.7R = 0.7 \times 0.125 = 0.0875 \text{ m}$

The chord at this radius: $c = 0.18 \times R = 0.18 \times 0.125 = 0.0225 \text{ m}$

The tangential velocity at $r$:

$$V_t = 2\pi n r = 2\pi \times 18 \times 0.0875 = 9.896 \text{ m/s}$$

The relative velocity at $r$:

$$V_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{V_A^2 + V_t^2} = \sqrt{4^2 + 9.896^2} = \sqrt{16 + 97.93} = \sqrt{113.93} \approx 10.67 \text{ m/s}$$

The Reynolds number (ITTC chord form):

$$Re_c = \frac{\rho c V_{\text{rel}}}{\mu} = \frac{998 \times 0.0225 \times 10.67}{9.57 \times 10^{-4}} \approx \frac{239.6}{9.57 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2.50 \times 10^5$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- $\sigma_n = 3.20$ is in the **safe, no-cavitation** range for most propellers.
- $\sigma_V = 4.05$ is even safer, since $V_A < nD$.
- $Re_c \approx 2.5 \times 10^5$ is **at the ITTC threshold** ($2 \times 10^5$). The model is at the lower acceptable limit; viscous-scale effects may be noticeable and should be flagged in the report.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Cavitation Test Interpretation and Similitude [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the similarity laws that must be satisfied when scaling cavitation test results from a model to a full-scale marine propeller. Discuss the practical difficulties in achieving complete similarity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cavitation test on a model propeller yields a thrust coefficient $K_T = 0.42$ at $J = 0.7$ and $\sigma_n = 2.0$. Estimate the full-scale thrust if the prototype diameter is 4 m, the prototype RPM is 110, and the seawater density is 1025 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Similarity Laws and Practical Difficulties**

For cavitation test results to be transferable from model to full scale, the following similarity conditions must be satisfied:

1. **Geometric similarity** — The model must be a precise geometric replica of the prototype, including blade shape, thickness distribution, and surface finish. *Practical difficulty:* Machining tolerances, surface roughness scaling, and the impossibility of scaling paint roughness identically.

2. **Cavitation-number similarity** — The cavitation number $\sigma$ of the model must equal that of the prototype at corresponding operating points. *Practical difficulty:* Adjusting the test-section pressure in a tunnel to match full-scale net pressure head $(p_0 - p_v)$ at scaled velocity.

3. **Reynolds-number similarity** — Ideally, $Re$ at the model should equal $Re$ at the prototype. *Practical difficulty:* This is **impossible** for typical model scales — the model $Re$ is at least one order of magnitude lower. ITTC standard prescribes a minimum $Re_c = 2 \times 10^5$ to suppress excessive scale effects.

4. **Weber-number similarity** — Important for very small models where surface tension influences inception. *Practical difficulty:* Surface tension is a fluid property, not a geometry property, so it cannot be scaled by changing model size.

5. **Froude-number similarity** — Required if free-surface effects are present (cavitation basin, not tunnel). *Practical difficulty:* Often relaxed for deeply submerged propellers.

6. **Nuclei-spectra similarity** — The dissolved-gas and microbubble content of the test water must match the prototype water. *Practical difficulty:* Theoretically impossible to reproduce; in practice, water is de-aerated to a standard residual gas content of ~50% saturation (ITTC recommendation) to provide **conservative** results.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most violated similarity is **Reynolds-number similarity**, and the ITTC has documented correction procedures (the so-called "1978 ITTC performance prediction method") to extrapolate model test data to full scale.

---

**Part (b) — Full-Scale Thrust Estimation**

**Given (model):** $K_T = 0.42$ at $J = 0.7$, $\sigma_n = 2.0$
**Prototype:** $D = 4 \text{ m}$, $N = 110 \text{ RPM} = 110/60 = 1.833 \text{ rev/s}$, $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Assume $K_T$ is the same at model and full scale (similarity of performance):**

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$$

Therefore:

$$T = K_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$$

**Step 2 — Compute $n^2 D^4$:**

$$n^2 D^4 = (1.833)^2 \times (4)^4 = 3.361 \times 256 = 860.4 \text{ m}^4/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute full-scale thrust:**

$$T = 0.42 \times 1025 \times 860.4 \approx 370{,}331 \text{ N} \approx 370 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Optional sanity check via $J$:**

If $J = 0.7$, the speed of advance is:

$$V_A = J \cdot nD = 0.7 \times 1.833 \times 4 = 5.13 \text{ m/s} \approx 10 \text{ knots}$$

This is plausible for a mid-sized vessel. The full-scale thrust of ~370 kN is also consistent with a ship of this size and speed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Student Errors:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert **RPM to rev/s** (divide by 60) when using the $K_T$ formula with SI units.
> 2. Using **freshwater density** (1000 kg/m³) when the prototype is in **seawater** (1025 kg/m³). Always read the problem carefully.
> 3. Forgetting that $K_T$ in the formula uses **rotational speed in rev/s**, not RPM.
> 4. Using $V_A$ in the formula by mistake. The formula uses $nD$, not $V_A$, as the velocity scale for the inertial term.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Module 4 (Cavitation Tests):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the temperature correction** to $p_v$ — every 1 °C changes σ by ~1% in 20 °C water. Always state the temperature in your answer.
> 2. **Mixing $\sigma_n$ and $\sigma_V$** — pick one and stick to it. Mixing them within the same plot is a common disaster.
> 3. **Wrong units in the $K_T$, $K_Q$ formula** — use $n$ in **rev/s** (or rad/s with a different constant), not RPM.
> 4. **Omitting the "interpretation" sentence** — examiners reward students who explain *what the σ value means for the design* (safe, incipient, heavily cavitating).
> 5. **Confusing Reynolds and Weber numbers** — Reynolds is viscous/inertia, Weber is surface-tension/inertia.
> 6. **Not stating the nuclei-spectrum assumption** — every cavitation test result implicitly assumes a certain dissolved-gas content. A good answer states this.
> 7. **Treating cavitation tests as visual-only** — they yield quantitative $K_T, K_Q, \eta_0$ data; do not present them as "just looking at bubbles."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this 10 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Cavitation number** is the **single most important** non-dimensional group in Module 4. Memorize **both forms**: velocity-based $\sigma$ and rotational-based $\sigma_n$.
- [ ] The condition for cavitation inception is $\sigma = \sigma_i$; cavitation **worsens** as $\sigma$ **decreases**.
- [ ] Cavitation is a **pressure-driven** phenomenon (not temperature-driven, although $p_v$ depends on $T$).
- [ ] Six similarity requirements: **Geometric, Cavitation-number, Reynolds, Weber, Froude, Nuclei** — the first two are non-negotiable, the others have standard compromises.
- [ ] ITTC standard requires $Re_c \geq 2 \times 10^5$ for marine propeller model tests.
- [ ] **Test facility types:** Cavitation **Tunnel** (closed loop, controlled pressure, smaller models) vs. Cavitation **Basin** (large towing tank with pressurized section, allows free-surface effects).
- [ ] **Six common cavitation types** (in order of typical appearance on conventional propellers): tip-vortex → sheet → bubble → cloud → leading-edge → super. Hub-vortex is independent.
- [ ] **Key non-dimensional performance coefficients:** $K_T$, $K_Q$, $\eta_0$, $J$, $\sigma$.
- [ ] **Formula conversion reminders:**
  - $K_T$ uses $n$ in **rev/s**, $D$ in meters, $T$ in Newtons.
  - $\eta_0 = (K_T / K_Q) \cdot (J / 2\pi)$.
  - Local section Reynolds: $Re_c = \rho c V_{\text{rel}} / \mu$ with $V_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{V_A^2 + (2\pi n r)^2}$.
- [ ] **Visual observation** is performed through **optical windows** with **high-speed cameras** and **strobe lighting**; acoustic observation uses a **hydrophone**.
- [ ] **Three major deliverables** of a cavitation test: (i) constant-σ performance curves; (ii) cavitation-extent charts; (iii) inception-σ versus advance coefficient map.
- [ ] **Reynolds-scale effects** are the **biggest source of model-to-full-scale uncertainty** in cavitation testing.
- [ ] **Nuclei-spectrum effect** is the second-biggest. The ITTC 50%-saturation de-aeration standard is a **conservative** choice.
- [ ] The cavitation tunnel is **pressurizable** so that $\sigma$ can be varied at fixed velocity and RPM by changing $p_0$ (or $p_v$ via temperature).
- [ ] Always quote the **water temperature** and **dissolved-gas content** with every cavitation number reported.
- [ ] For a typical marine propeller, sheet cavitation begins at $\sigma \approx 0.6$ to $1.5$, tip-vortex cavitation at $\sigma \approx 1.0$ to $2.5$, depending on blade design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
