> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Prevention of Cavitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛡️ PREVENTION OF CAVITATION — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> **Cavitation** is the formation, growth, and violent collapse of vapor (or gas) bubbles within a liquid when the local static pressure falls below the **vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature. This typically occurs in regions of high velocity (low pressure) such as around marine propellers, pump impellers, hydrofoils, and ship stern appendages.

**Prevention of Cavitation** refers to the set of design, operational, and material strategies employed in marine hydrodynamics to **ensure that the local static pressure within the liquid never drops to or below the saturated vapor pressure**, thereby suppressing bubble formation, mitigating erosion, noise, vibration, and efficiency loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The governing parameter for evaluating cavitation risk in marine systems is the **Thoma's Cavitation Number (σ)**. Prevention strategies fundamentally aim to keep the operating cavitation number **above the critical cavitation number (σc)** of the body:
> **σ ≥ σc**

---

## 💡 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Boiling Water Bottle"

Imagine a sealed plastic water bottle with a small amount of warm water inside. If you shake the bottle vigorously or push hard against its sides, you may notice tiny vapor bubbles forming and collapsing inside the water. Even though the water is far below 100 °C, you momentarily created **low pressure** inside the liquid, causing dissolved vapor to come out of solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cavitation in ships is the same phenomenon:** A propeller blade sweeps through water so fast that the local pressure on the suction (low-pressure) side of the blade **dips below the vapor pressure of seawater**. Tiny vapor cavities nucleate, travel with the flow into a higher-pressure region, and implode violently — creating pitting, noise ("propeller singing"), and loss of thrust.

**Prevention of cavitation = making sure the water around the blade NEVER experiences "boiling" pressure**, either by:
- 🚢 Making the propeller work in **deeper, higher-pressure water** (more submergence)
- 🚢 Slowing the propeller down (lower tip speed)
- 🚢 Designing the blade profile to **avoid sharp pressure drops**
- 🚢 Blowing **air into the low-pressure zone** to cushion the collapse
- 🚢 Using **smooth, polished, erosion-resistant surfaces**

---

## 🔑 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used throughout marine cavitation analysis (Kreitner, H.M. "Cavitation" notes; Carlton, J.S. *Marine Propellers and Propulsion*):

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value/Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------------|
| **p₀** | Local ambient (static) pressure | Pa |
| **pᵥ** | Saturated vapor pressure of liquid | Pa (≈ 1700 Pa at 15 °C for freshwater) |
| **ρ** | Density of liquid | **1025 kg/m³** for seawater |
| **V₀** | Free-stream / reference velocity | m/s |
| **g** | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| **σ** | Thoma's cavitation number | dimensionless |
| **σc** | Critical cavitation number | dimensionless |
| **NPSH** | Net Positive Suction Head | meters of liquid |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 📐 2.1 Governing Physical Principle — The Cavitation Number

The **Thoma's Cavitation Number (σ)** is the most fundamental non-dimensional parameter in marine cavitation analysis. It expresses the ratio between the **pressure margin above vapor pressure** and the **dynamic pressure** of the flow:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{p_{0} - p_{v}}{\dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{0}^{\,2}}$$

### 2.1.1 Physical Meaning of σ

- **High σ (e.g., σ > 1)** → Large pressure margin, **no cavitation** (submerged bodies in deep water at low speed).
- **Low σ (e.g., σ < 0.1)** → Pressure margin is small relative to dynamic head, **cavitation is highly probable** (high-speed surface-piercing propellers).
- **σ = σc** → The **incipient cavitation point**, where the first bubbles appear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Exam Favorite):**
> To prevent cavitation, design the system such that the **operating cavitation number σ_op is at least 1.5 to 2 times the critical cavitation number σc** of the chosen blade section. This provides a safety margin against transient fluctuations in pressure (e.g., rough seas, slamming, acceleration).

---

## 🛠️ 2.2 Classification of Cavitation Prevention Methods

Cavitation prevention in marine hydrodynamics is broadly classified into **three engineering levels**:

### 2.2.1 Design-Level Prevention
1. **Increase submergence depth (h)** — raises p₀ = p_atm + ρgh
2. **Reduce blade tip speed** (lower RPM or smaller diameter)
3. **Streamline blade sections** — use **NACA 66 (thick)** or **NACA 16 (thin)** series sections, designed for delayed cavitation inception
4. **Increase blade chord** to reduce camber-induced pressure gradient
5. **Reduce blade camber and thickness ratio** at the tip
6. **Use end plates / winglets** to suppress tip vortex cavitation
7. **Optimise blade skew** to smooth the pressure distribution
8. **Use ducted / Kort nozzles** (raises σ effectively by accelerating V₀)
9. **Surface polishing** — reduces nucleation sites (roughness > 5 μm acts as cavitation seed)

### 2.2.2 Operational-Level Prevention
1. **Reduce engine RPM** during transit in shallow or warm waters
2. **Avoid prolonged operations in highly aerated flow** (e.g., behind wave-breaking)
3. **Maintain ambient pressure** (avoid venting suction sides to atmosphere unnecessarily)
4. **Cool the working fluid** to lower pᵥ

### 2.2.3 Material & Surface Treatment Prevention
1. **Use cavitation-resistant alloys** — e.g., **Manganese Bronze (HTPB)**, **Nickel-Aluminum Bronze (NAB)**, **Stainless Steel 17-4 PH**, **Titanium grade 5**
2. **Apply elastomeric coatings** (polyurethane, rubber) — absorb impact energy
3. **Use cathodic protection** combined with smooth coating to reduce pitting
4. **Hard chrome plating** in extreme cases

### 2.2.4 Active Prevention — Air Injection / Ventilation
1. **Boundary layer ventilation** — injects air over the low-pressure region, raising local pressure and cushioning bubble collapse
2. **Gas bubble injection upstream** — provides compliant nuclei that absorb energy
3. **Supercavitating section design** — intentionally allows a stable vapor cavity that does not collapse, eliminating erosive collapse

---

## 📊 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|-------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_{0} - p_{v}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{0}^{\,2}}$ | Thoma's cavitation number | dimensionless |
| 2 | $p_{0} = p_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic ambient pressure at depth h | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma_{c} = C_{p,\,min}$ | Critical σ equals minimum pressure coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $NPSH_{a} = \dfrac{p_{atm} - p_{v}}{\rho g} + h_{s} - h_{f}$ | Available Net Positive Suction Head | m |
| 5 | $NPSH_{r} = f(\dot{Q},\,RPM,\,impeller\;geometry)$ | Required NPSH (from manufacturer curves) | m |
| 6 | $NPSH_{a} \geq NPSH_{r} \times 1.2$ | Safety criterion (20% margin) | m |
| 7 | $V_{tip} = \pi D N$ | Propeller tip speed | m/s |
| 8 | $C_{p} = \dfrac{p - p_{0}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{0}^{\,2}}$ | Pressure coefficient | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\sigma_{op} \geq 1.5\,\sigma_{c}$ | Recommended design safety margin | dimensionless |
| 10 | $p_{v}(T) \approx p_{v,0}\,\exp\!\left[\dfrac{L}{R_{v}}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{0}} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]$ | Vapor pressure–temperature (Antoine-like) | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Hook for Exam:**
> *"To PREVENT cavitation, the engineer must BOOST σ."* BOOST = **B**etter streamlined section, **O**perate at lower RPM, **O**ptimum submergence, **S**mooth surface, **T**ough material.

---

## 🌊 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In marine hydrodynamics, cavitation prevention directly impacts:

- **Propulsion efficiency:** Cavitating propellers can lose 15–30% efficiency.
- **Hull resistance:** Tip-vortex cavitation can interact with the hull boundary layer.
- **Underwater radiated noise (URN):** Cavitation is the dominant noise source; suppression is critical for **stealth submarines (anechoic coating integration)**.
- **Structural life:** Pitting erosion on bronze propellers can reach 2–5 mm/year if untreated.
- **Pump and hydraulic systems onboard ships:** Cavitation in seawater cooling pumps causes vibration and impeller failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📘 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thoma's Cavitation Number from Bernoulli's Equation

The cavitation number is fundamentally derived from Bernoulli's equation applied to an incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline. The following exhaustive derivation shows the origin of σ from first principles.

### Step 1: Start with Bernoulli's Equation

For a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow between a free-stream point (index 0) and a point on the body surface (index *s*):

$$\dfrac{p_{0}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{0}^{\,2}}{2g} + z_{0} = \dfrac{p_{s}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{s}^{\,2}}{2g} + z_{s}$$

### Step 2: Neglect Elevation Difference (z₀ ≈ zₛ)

For ship-scale and propeller-scale problems, the elevation term is negligible compared to pressure and velocity terms. Hence:

$$\dfrac{p_{0}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{0}^{\,2}}{2g} = \dfrac{p_{s}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{s}^{\,2}}{2g}$$

### Step 3: Solve for Surface Pressure pₛ

Rearranging to isolate pₛ:

$$p_{s} = p_{0} + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{0}^{\,2} - \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{s}^{\,2}$$

### Step 4: Introduce the Pressure Coefficient

Define the **pressure coefficient** at the surface point:

$$C_{p} = \dfrac{p_{s} - p_{0}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{0}^{\,2}}$$

### Step 5: Substitute the Definition

Rearranging Step 3:

$$p_{s} - p_{0} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho\,(V_{0}^{\,2} - V_{s}^{\,2})$$

Dividing both sides by the dynamic pressure:

$$C_{p} = \dfrac{V_{0}^{\,2} - V_{s}^{\,2}}{V_{0}^{\,2}} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{V_{s}}{V_{0}}\right)^{2}$$

### Step 6: Set the Cavitation Inception Condition

Cavitation begins when the surface pressure falls to the vapor pressure: pₛ = pᵥ.

$$p_{v} = p_{0} + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho\,(V_{0}^{\,2} - V_{s}^{\,2})$$

Rearranging to find the local velocity at inception:

$$V_{s}^{\,2} = V_{0}^{\,2} + \dfrac{2(p_{0} - p_{v})}{\rho}$$

### Step 7: Define the Cavitation Number

Bring the (p₀ − pᵥ) term to one side and express it as a fraction of the dynamic head:

$$\dfrac{2(p_{0} - p_{v})}{\rho V_{0}^{\,2}} = \dfrac{V_{s}^{\,2}}{V_{0}^{\,2}} - 1 = -C_{p,\,min}$$

The left-hand side is, by definition, the **Thoma's cavitation number σ**:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{p_{0} - p_{v}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{0}^{\,2}} = -C_{p,\,min}$$

### Step 8: Critical Cavitation Number

The **critical cavitation number σc** is defined as the value of σ at which cavitation first appears on the body. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{c} = -C_{p,\,min} = -(\text{most negative pressure coefficient on the body})}$$

**Interpretation:** A body with a strongly negative C_p,min (e.g., thin cambered foil at high angle of attack) has a **large σc** and is therefore **more susceptible to cavitation**. Prevention strategies must keep σ_op > σc.

---

## 📘 3.2 Derivation 2 — NPSH Derivation from Bernoulli

The **Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)** is the practical engineering expression of cavitation prevention in pump and marine propulsion systems.

### Step 1: Apply Bernoulli from Suction Reservoir to Pump Impeller Eye

Take point 1 at the free surface of the suction tank (or sea surface) and point 2 at the impeller eye inlet:

$$\dfrac{p_{1}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{1}^{\,2}}{2g} + z_{1} = \dfrac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{2}^{\,2}}{2g} + z_{2} + h_{f}$$

### Step 2: Substitute Boundary Conditions

- V₁ ≈ 0 (large reservoir)
- z₁ − z₂ = hₛ (static suction head; positive if suction is below impeller)
- h_f = friction head loss in suction piping

$$h_{s} + \dfrac{p_{1}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{2}^{\,2}}{2g} + h_{f}$$

### Step 3: Solve for p₂/ρg

$$\dfrac{p_{2}}{\rho g} = h_{s} + \dfrac{p_{1}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{V_{2}^{\,2}}{2g} - h_{f}$$

### Step 4: NPSH Definition

NPSH is the **total head at the impeller eye above the vapor pressure head**. Therefore:

$$NPSH_{a} = \dfrac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{2}^{\,2}}{2g} - \dfrac{p_{v}}{\rho g}$$

### Step 5: Substitute Step 3 Result

Replacing p₂/ρg:

$$NPSH_{a} = \left[h_{s} + \dfrac{p_{1}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{V_{2}^{\,2}}{2g} - h_{f}\right] + \dfrac{V_{2}^{\,2}}{2g} - \dfrac{p_{v}}{\rho g}$$

### Step 6: Simplify

The V₂²/2g terms cancel:

$$\boxed{NPSH_{a} = h_{s} + \dfrac{p_{1} - p_{v}}{\rho g} - h_{f}}$$

For a system open to atmosphere, p₁ = p_atm:

$$NPSH_{a} = h_{s} + \dfrac{p_{atm} - p_{v}}{\rho g} - h_{f}$$

### Step 7: Cavitation Prevention Criterion

The available NPSH must exceed the required NPSH (from pump manufacturer's curves) by a safety margin:

$$NPSH_{a} \geq (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \times NPSH_{r}$$

---

## 📘 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Standard)

**Problem:** A ship operates in seawater (ρ = 1025 kg/m³) with the propeller shaft at 4.0 m below the waterline. The local atmospheric pressure is 101325 Pa, and the vapor pressure of seawater at 25 °C is 2300 Pa. The propeller tip speed is 18 m/s. Determine:

**(a)** The Thoma's cavitation number σ at the shaft centerline depth.

**(b)** Whether cavitation is likely if the blade's σc is 0.35 (assume safety factor 1.5).

### Solution:

**Part (a): Compute p₀ at the shaft depth**

$$p_{0} = p_{atm} + \rho g h$$

$$p_{0} = 101325 + (1025)(9.81)(4.0)$$

$$p_{0} = 101325 + 40221 = 141546\;\text{Pa}$$

**Compute σ:**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{p_{0} - p_{v}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{0}^{\,2}}$$

$$\sigma = \dfrac{141546 - 2300}{\frac{1}{2}(1025)(18)^{2}}$$

$$\sigma = \dfrac{139246}{166050}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma \approx 0.839}$$

**Part (b): Cavitation Check**

Required minimum σ for safe operation:

$$\sigma_{required} = 1.5 \times \sigma_{c} = 1.5 \times 0.35 = 0.525$$

Compare:

$$\sigma_{op} = 0.839 \;\geq\; \sigma_{required} = 0.525 \;\; \text{✅ SAFE — No cavitation expected.}$$

**Recommended Prevention Margin:**

$$\dfrac{\sigma_{op}}{\sigma_{required}} = \dfrac{0.839}{0.525} \approx 1.60$$

The propeller is operating with a **~60% safety margin** above the critical cavitation number.

> [!TIP]
> **What if the answer was UNSAFE?** Then the engineer would need to apply prevention measures: (i) increase shaft depth, (ii) reduce RPM, (iii) add air injection, (iv) select a different blade section with lower σc, or (v) reduce blade tip camber.

---

## 📋 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Prevention Strategy

| Engineering Problem | Root Cause | Recommended Prevention Strategy |
|---------------------|------------|---------------------------------|
| Sheet cavitation on blade suction side | Negative pressure peak > pᵥ | Streamline section (NACA 66), reduce camber |
| Tip vortex cavitation | Strong tip vortex core pressure drop | Skewed blade tips, end plates, winglets |
| Hub vortex cavitation | Pressure drop behind hub | Hub fairing, fillet shaping |
| Erosion pitting on propeller | Violent bubble collapse | NAB alloy, elastomeric coating, polishing |
| Pump cavitation on ship service | NPSHₐ < NPSHᵣ | Increase suction head, reduce temp, larger pipe |
| Rope cavitation (submarine) | High back velocity | Lifting line redesign, suction-side groove |
| Cavitation-induced noise (URN) | Bubble collapse rate | Air injection, supercavitating foil |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🧭 4.1 Block Diagram — Cavitation Prevention Decision Tree

The following functional architecture flow shows how the engineer systematically selects a prevention strategy based on the operating condition and the failure mode identified.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine System: Propeller / Pump / Foil] --> B[Compute Operating Cavitation Number sigma_op]
    B --> C{sigma_op >= 1.5 x sigma_c ?}
    C -- YES --> D[System SAFE - No Action Needed]
    C -- NO --> E[Identify Cavitation Type]
    E --> F[Sheet Cavitation]
    E --> G[Tip Vortex Cavitation]
    E --> H[Hub Vortex Cavitation]
    E --> I[Erosion Pitting]
    E --> J[Pump Inception]
    F --> F1[Use NACA 66 Thick Section]
    F --> F2[Reduce Blade Camber]
    F --> F3[Increase Chord Length]
    G --> G1[Apply Blade Skew]
    G --> G2[Add End Plate / Winglet]
    G --> G3[Polish Tip Surface]
    H --> H1[Apply Hub Fairing]
    H --> H2[Reshape Boss]
    I --> I1[Use NAB Alloy]
    I --> I2[Apply Elastomeric Coating]
    I --> I3[Cathodic Protection]
    J --> J1[Increase Suction Head]
    J --> J2[Enlarge Suction Pipe]
    J --> J3[Reduce Fluid Temperature]
    F1 --> K[Re-evaluate sigma_op]
    F2 --> K
    F3 --> K
    G1 --> K
    G2 --> K
    G3 --> K
    H1 --> K
    H2 --> K
    I1 --> K
    I2 --> K
    I3 --> K
    J1 --> K
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> C
```

---

## 🧩 4.2 Block Diagram — Levels of Cavitation Prevention

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Level 1: DESIGN Prevention] --> L1a[Streamline Blade]
    L1 --> L1b[Optimise Submergence]
    L1 --> L1c[Select Sigma-c Foil]
    L2[Level 2: OPERATIONAL Prevention] --> L2a[Limit RPM]
    L2 --> L2b[Limit Speed in Warm Water]
    L2 --> L2c[Avoid Aerated Flow]
    L3[Level 3: MATERIAL Prevention] --> L3a[NAB / Bronze]
    L3 --> L3b[Elastomeric Coating]
    L3 --> L3c[Hard Chrome Plating]
    L4[Level 4: ACTIVE Prevention] --> L4a[Air Injection]
    L4 --> L4b[Boundary Layer Ventilation]
    L4 --> L4c[Supercavitating Profile]
    L1 --> M[Integrated Cavitation-Free Operation]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    L4 --> M
```

---

## 🧪 4.3 Block Diagram — Cavitation Test Setup in a Marine Hydrodynamic Laboratory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[High-Pressure Water Reservoir] --> A2[Converging Nozzle]
    A2 --> A3[Test Section: Model Propeller / Hydrofoil]
    A3 --> A4[Window for High-Speed Imaging / Schlieren]
    A3 --> A5[Pressure Transducer Array]
    A3 --> A6[Hydrophone for Noise Measurement]
    A3 --> A7[Downstream Diffuser]
    A7 --> A8[Flow Rate Meter]
    A7 --> A9[Vacuum Pump for Pressure Control]
    A5 --> A10[Data Acquisition System]
    A6 --> A10
    A8 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Compute sigma_op vs sigma_c]
    A11 --> A12{Cavitation Incepted?}
    A12 -- YES --> A13[Apply Prevention Strategy]
    A13 --> A1
    A12 -- NO --> A14[Validate Safe Design]
```

# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ✍️ PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Thoma's cavitation number. Why is it important in cavitation prevention?**

**Model Answer:**

Thoma's cavitation number (σ) is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the susceptibility of a hydrodynamic system to cavitation. It is defined as the ratio of the pressure margin above vapor pressure to the dynamic pressure of the flow:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{p_{0} - p_{v}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{0}^{\,2}}$$

It is important in cavitation prevention because it provides a single, experimentally verifiable criterion:

> **If σ_op ≥ σc, the system is safe; if σ_op < σc, cavitation will occur.**

By manipulating σ_op (via submergence, RPM, blade geometry) and σc (via foil selection), the engineer can systematically design cavitation-free marine systems.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four methods used to prevent cavitation in marine propellers.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Increase submergence depth (h)** to raise ambient pressure p₀.
2. **Reduce propeller RPM / tip speed** to lower dynamic pressure.
3. **Use streamlined blade sections** (e.g., NACA 66 series) to minimize pressure drop.
4. **Apply air injection / ventilation** to the low-pressure region.
5. **Use cavitation-resistant materials** like Nickel-Aluminum Bronze (NAB).
6. **Polish blade surfaces** to reduce nucleation sites.
7. **Apply elastomeric coatings** to absorb bubble collapse energy.

---

## ✍️ PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for **Thoma's cavitation number** starting from Bernoulli's equation. State clearly the assumption made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-bladed propeller of diameter 2.4 m rotates at 180 RPM in seawater (ρ = 1025 kg/m³, p_v = 2300 Pa) at a shaft depth of 5 m below the free surface, where p_atm = 101325 Pa. The critical cavitation number of the section is 0.45.

   **(i)** Compute the Thoma's cavitation number σ. **(4 Marks)**
   **(ii)** Check whether cavitation will occur. If unsafe, suggest two prevention strategies. **(3 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Derivation

Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's equation between a free-stream point (0) and a body surface point (s):

$$\dfrac{p_{0}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{0}^{\,2}}{2g} + z_{0} = \dfrac{p_{s}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{s}^{\,2}}{2g} + z_{s}$$

Step 2: **Assumption:** Neglect elevation difference (z₀ ≈ zₛ) and assume incompressible, inviscid flow.

Step 3: Solve for surface pressure:

$$p_{s} = p_{0} + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_{0}^{\,2} - V_{s}^{\,2})$$

Step 4: Cavitation inception occurs when pₛ = pᵥ:

$$p_{v} = p_{0} + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_{0}^{\,2} - V_{s}^{\,2})$$

Step 5: Rearrange:

$$p_{0} - p_{v} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_{s}^{\,2} - V_{0}^{\,2})$$

Step 6: Express the (p₀ − pᵥ) margin as a fraction of the dynamic head:

$$\dfrac{p_{0} - p_{v}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{0}^{\,2}} = \dfrac{V_{s}^{\,2}}{V_{0}^{\,2}} - 1$$

Step 7: The left-hand side is, by definition, the Thoma's cavitation number:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{p_{0} - p_{v}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{0}^{\,2}} = \left(\dfrac{V_{s}}{V_{0}}\right)^{2} - 1 = -C_{p,\,min}}$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Compute σ:**

First, find p₀ at depth h = 5 m:

$$p_{0} = p_{atm} + \rho g h = 101325 + (1025)(9.81)(5)$$

$$p_{0} = 101325 + 50276.25 = 151601.25\;\text{Pa}$$

Compute tip velocity:

$$V_{0} = \pi D N = \pi (2.4) \left(\dfrac{180}{60}\right) = \pi (2.4)(3) = 22.62\;\text{m/s}$$

Compute σ:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{151601.25 - 2300}{\frac{1}{2}(1025)(22.62)^{2}}$$

$$\sigma = \dfrac{149301.25}{(512.5)(511.66)} = \dfrac{149301.25}{262227.5}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma \approx 0.569}$$

**(ii) Cavitation Check:**

Required minimum σ (with 1.5× safety factor):

$$\sigma_{required} = 1.5 \times 0.45 = 0.675$$

Compare:

$$\sigma_{op} = 0.569 < \sigma_{required} = 0.675 \;\;\text{⚠️ UNSAFE — Cavitation will occur.}$$

**Two Prevention Strategies:**

1. **Reduce RPM from 180 to ~150 RPM:**
   New tip speed: V₀ = π(2.4)(2.5) = 18.85 m/s
   New σ = 149301.25 / [(0.5)(1025)(355.4)] = 149301.25 / 182175 ≈ **0.82** ✅ Safe.

2. **Increase submergence from 5 m to 8 m:**
   New p₀ = 101325 + (1025)(9.81)(8) = 101325 + 80442 = 181767 Pa
   New σ = (181767 − 2300) / 262227.5 = 179467 / 262227.5 ≈ **0.68** ✅ Marginally safe.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Define **NPSH**. Derive the expression for **NPSH_available** for a centrifugal pump drawing water from a sump. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A marine seawater pump has NPSH_required = 3.5 m (from manufacturer's curve) and operates at a static suction head of −1.8 m (suction lift) with suction pipe friction loss of 0.6 m. The barometric pressure is 101325 Pa and vapor pressure at 28 °C is 3780 Pa. Take ρ = 1025 kg/m³. **(i)** Compute NPSH_available. **(4 Marks)** **(ii)** Is the pump safe from cavitation? If not, suggest two prevention measures. **(3 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — NPSH Definition and Derivation

**Definition:** NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head) is the total head available at the pump suction (impeller eye) above the vapor pressure head. It is a measure of the margin against cavitation.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: Apply Bernoulli between the free surface of the sump (point 1) and the impeller eye (point 2):

$$\dfrac{p_{1}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{1}^{\,2}}{2g} + z_{1} = \dfrac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{2}^{\,2}}{2g} + z_{2} + h_{f}$$

Step 2: Assume V₁ ≈ 0 (large sump) and let z₁ − z₂ = hₛ:

$$h_{s} + \dfrac{p_{1}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{2}^{\,2}}{2g} + h_{f}$$

Step 3: Solve for p₂/ρg:

$$\dfrac{p_{2}}{\rho g} = h_{s} + \dfrac{p_{1}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{V_{2}^{\,2}}{2g} - h_{f}$$

Step 4: NPSH is the **total head at point 2 above vapor pressure head**:

$$NPSH_{a} = \dfrac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{2}^{\,2}}{2g} - \dfrac{p_{v}}{\rho g}$$

Step 5: Substitute from Step 3 — the V₂²/2g terms cancel:

$$\boxed{NPSH_{a} = h_{s} + \dfrac{p_{1} - p_{v}}{\rho g} - h_{f}}$$

For an open sump, p₁ = p_atm:

$$NPSH_{a} = h_{s} + \dfrac{p_{atm} - p_{v}}{\rho g} - h_{f}$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Compute NPSH_available:**

Given: hₛ = −1.8 m, h_f = 0.6 m, p_atm = 101325 Pa, p_v = 3780 Pa, ρ = 1025 kg/m³.

Compute the atmospheric-to-vapor head term:

$$\dfrac{p_{atm} - p_{v}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{101325 - 3780}{(1025)(9.81)} = \dfrac{97545}{10055.25} \approx 9.70\;\text{m}$$

Now compute NPSHₐ:

$$NPSH_{a} = h_{s} + \dfrac{p_{atm} - p_{v}}{\rho g} - h_{f}$$

$$NPSH_{a} = -1.8 + 9.70 - 0.6$$

$$\boxed{NPSH_{a} = 7.30\;\text{m}}$$

**(ii) Cavitation Check:**

Required NPSH with 20% safety margin:

$$NPSH_{required} = 1.2 \times 3.5 = 4.20\;\text{m}$$

Compare:

$$NPSH_{a} = 7.30\;\text{m} \;\geq\; NPSH_{required} = 4.20\;\text{m} \;\;\text{✅ SAFE}$$

The pump has a **74% safety margin** against cavitation. No prevention measure is required under these operating conditions, but the engineer should monitor:
- Temperature rise (which raises pᵥ and reduces NPSHₐ)
- Pipe fouling (which raises h_f and reduces NPSHₐ)

**If unsafe**, two prevention measures would be:
1. **Reduce static suction lift** (move pump closer to the seawater level) or add a **foot valve with submerged priming**.
2. **Reduce fluid temperature** (lowers pᵥ, increasing NPSHₐ) — or **enlarge suction pipe diameter** (reduces V₂ and h_f).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse σ with C_p:** Examiners frequently test whether students know that **σ_c = −C_p,min**. Many students write σ = C_p,min and lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Always show units** for NPSH calculations; writing "NPSH = 7.3" without "m" loses 1 mark.
> - **Never skip the safety factor (1.5 for σ, 1.2 for NPSH).** Examiners explicitly test for it.
> - **Do not confuse hₛ sign convention:** hₛ is **positive** when the suction source is **above** the pump centerline (gravity feed), and **negative** for **suction lift**. The sign is critical in NPSH derivation.
> - **For numericals**, always compute p₀ from hydrostatic formula **p₀ = p_atm + ρgh** before substituting into σ — skipping this step costs 2–3 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist — Prevention of Cavitation (Module 4)**

- [x] **Cavitation** = local pressure ≤ vapor pressure → vapor bubble formation and collapse.
- [x] **Thoma's Cavitation Number:** $\sigma = \dfrac{p_{0} - p_{v}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{0}^{\,2}}$ — dimensionless, the *master* parameter.
- [x] **Critical σc = −C_p,min** — the negative of the most negative pressure coefficient.
- [x] **Prevention Rule:** Maintain $\sigma_{op} \geq 1.5\,\sigma_{c}$.
- [x] **Hydrostatic pressure at depth:** $p_{0} = p_{atm} + \rho g h$.
- [x] **NPSH_available** = suction head + atmospheric-vapor head − friction loss.
- [x] **NPSH_required** = function of flow rate, RPM, impeller geometry (from catalog).
- [x] **Prevention Criterion:** $NPSH_{a} \geq 1.2 \times NPSH_{r}$.
- [x] **Design Prevention:** streamline section (NACA 66), increase depth, reduce camber, polish surface.
- [x] **Operational Prevention:** reduce RPM, avoid warm shallow water, control aeration.
- [x] **Material Prevention:** NAB alloy, elastomeric polyurethane coating, hard chrome.
- [x] **Active Prevention:** boundary layer ventilation, air injection, supercavitating sections.
- [x] **Memory Hook:** *"BOOST σ"* — **B**etter section, **O**perate slow, **O**ptimum depth, **S**mooth surface, **T**ough material.
- [x] **Key Marine Constants:** ρ_seawater = **1025 kg/m³**, p_v at 15 °C ≈ **1700 Pa**, at 25 °C ≈ **2300 Pa**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seakeeping Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST865 - EXPERIMENTAL TECHNIQUES IN MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Seakeeping Tests ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Seakeeping** is the science and engineering discipline that studies the behavior of a watercraft (ship, offshore platform, or any floating/foating structure) when subjected to the action of **waves**, **wind**, and **currents** in a real sea environment. According to the **ITTC (International Towing Tank Conference)** and **SNAME (Society of Naval Architects and Marine Engineers)** terminology, seakeeping encompasses the prediction, measurement, and evaluation of a vessel's responses — namely **motions**, **accelerations**, **loads**, and **slamming impacts** — in a seaway. A **Seakeeping Test** is therefore an *experimental procedure* carried out either on a **scaled model** in a controlled laboratory environment (a wave basin or an ocean engineering basin) or on the **full-scale prototype vessel** in the open ocean, with the express objective of quantifying how the structure responds to a known or measured wave environment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PENST865 – Module 4):** > *Seakeeping tests are model experiments performed in a wave basin to measure the 6 degrees-of-freedom (6-DOF) motions, accelerations, and global loads of a vessel in regular (monochromatic) and irregular (realistic) sea states. The results are expressed as Response Amplitude Operators (RAOs), motion spectra, and operability indices used by naval architects for ship design verification.* The **six degrees of freedom (6-DOF)** that fully characterize a rigid body's motion in waves are conventionally tabulated as follows: | DOF # | Translational / Rotational | Symbol | Axis | Common Name | |-------|---------------------------|--------|------|-------------| | 1 | Translation | Surge | $x$ | Longitudinal | | 2 | Translation | Sway | $y$ | Transverse | | 3 | Translation | **Heave** | $z$ | Vertical | | 4 | Rotation | **Roll** | $\phi$ | About $x$-axis | | 5 | Rotation | **Pitch** | $\theta$ | About $y$-axis | | 6 | Rotation | Yaw | $\psi$ | About $z$-axis | For most displacement-type monohull vessels, the three **dominant seakeeping motions** are **heave (z)**, **pitch (θ)**, and **roll (φ)** because surge, sway, and yaw are largely restrained by hydrodynamic and metacentric restoring forces. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Floating Bus on a Washboard Road" Imagine you are driving a heavy passenger bus along a road made of corrugated metal sheets (a "washboard" road). Even though the engine pushes the bus forward steadily, the bus will: 1. **Bounce up and down** every time a corrugation passes beneath a wheel → this is analogous to **heave**. 2. **Nod forward and backward** as the front and rear wheels meet successive corrugations → this is analogous to **pitch**. 3. **Rock side-to-side** when one side of the road is bumpier → this is analogous to **roll**. A passenger inside will feel accelerations, get seasick ("motion sickness"), and possibly hit the ceiling during severe bounces ("slamming"). A naval architect performing a seakeeping test is essentially trying to *quantify* all these "bumps, nods, rocks, and discomforts" — but for a ship navigating real ocean waves instead of a bus on a bumpy road. > [!NOTE] > **Why we perform scaled model tests instead of full-scale tests only:** > Full-scale trials are extremely expensive (a single day of ship operation can cost millions of rupees), weather-dependent, and lack repeatability (you cannot generate the same wave twice at sea). Model tests in a basin, by contrast, allow the engineer to reproduce the **same wave** thousands of times under controlled, repeatable, instrumented conditions. --- ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants, Reference Quantities & Terminology | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |----------|--------|----------------------| | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | | Density of fresh water | $\rho_f$ | 1000 kg/m³ | | Density of sea water | $\rho_s$ | **1025 kg/m³** | | Kinematic viscosity (water, 20°C) | $\nu$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s | | Significant wave height (operational) | $H_s$ | 1–12 m | | Peak spectral frequency | $\omega_p$ | 0.3–1.5 rad/s | | Froude scale ratio | $\lambda$ | 1:20 to 1:60 (typical) | | Reynolds scale ratio | $\lambda_R$ | Function of $\lambda$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two Critical Scaling Laws in Seakeeping Tests:** > 1. **Froude Scaling** (gravity/inertia balance): $\lambda_V = \lambda^{1/2}$ for velocities, $\lambda_T = \lambda^{1/2}$ for time. > 2. **Reynolds Similarity** is *deliberately relaxed* in seakeeping model tests because Froude similarity dominates over viscous similarity for wave-induced motions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physics of a Floating Body in Waves When a ship is subjected to a regular (sinusoidal) wave of amplitude $\zeta_a$ and encounter frequency $\omega_e$, the ship is forced to oscillate. In the **frequency domain**, the equation of motion for a single degree of freedom (say, heave) is a **second-order ordinary differential equation with frequency-dependent hydrodynamic coefficients**: $$ \bigl[\,M + A(\omega)\,\bigr]\,\ddot{z} \;+\; B(\omega)\,\dot{z} \;+\; C\,z \;=\; F_0(\omega)\,e^{i\omega t} $$ where the terms have the following physical meaning: - $M$ — **Mass / moment of inertia** of the vessel (rigid-body property, constant). - $A(\omega)$ — **Added mass** (frequency-dependent virtual inertia of the surrounding water that moves with the ship). - $B(\omega)$ — **Damping coefficient** (radiation + viscous + eddy damping). - $C$ — **Restoring force coefficient** (hydrostatic stiffness, e.g., $C = \rho g A_{WP}$ for heave). - $F_0(\omega)$ — **Exciting wave force amplitude**. - $z(t) = z_a\,e^{i\omega t}$ — **Steady-state response**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Intuition for Added Mass $A(\omega)$:** > When a ship heaves up by 1 m, a huge mass of water beneath the hull must also accelerate upward and outward to "make room." This entrained water behaves as if it were additional mass bolted to the hull. At low frequencies, $A(\omega) \to A_\infty$ (constant infinite-frequency limit); at high frequencies, $A(\omega) \to A_0$ (zero-frequency limit, equal to the displaced-water mass for heave). --- ### 2.2 The Response Amplitude Operator (RAO) The single most important result of any seakeeping test is the **RAO** (also called **transfer function** or **frequency-response function**): $$ \text{RAO}(\omega) \;=\; \frac{z_a(\omega)}{\zeta_a(\omega)} \;=\; \bigl\vert\,\text{RAO}(\omega)\,\bigr\vert\,e^{-i\varepsilon(\omega)} $$ It gives, for each encounter frequency $\omega$, the **complex ratio** between the *response amplitude* ($z_a$, e.g., heave in metres or pitch in radians) and the *wave amplitude* ($\zeta_a$, in metres). It therefore is **dimensionless** for translational motions (heave, surge, sway) but **dimensionless** also for rotations when both numerator and denominator are expressed in radians. **The RAO carries two pieces of information at every frequency:** $$ \text{Amplitude RAO: }\;\bigl\vert\,\text{RAO}(\omega)\,\bigr\vert \;=\; \frac{\text{response amplitude}}{\text{wave amplitude}} $$ $$ \text{Phase RAO: }\;\varepsilon(\omega) \;=\; \text{phase lag of response w.r.t. wave crest} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Engineering Utility of RAO:** > - **Predict motions** for any sea state by convolution with the wave spectrum. > - **Compare designs** — a vessel with a lower heave RAO at the dominant wave frequency is a more comfortable ship. > - **Detect resonance** — the frequency at which $\vert \text{RAO}(\omega) \vert$ peaks is the natural heave/pitch/roll frequency. --- ### 2.3 Irregular Sea Representation — Wave Spectra In reality, the ocean is **not** a single sine wave but a superposition of countless waves of different frequencies, directions, and phases. This is represented by a **wave spectrum** $S(\omega)$, with units of m²·s/rad. **Pierson–Moskowitz (PM) Spectrum** (fully developed sea, no fetch limitation): $$ S_{\eta}(\omega) \;=\; \frac{A}{\omega^{5}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{\omega^{4}}\right) $$ with the constants: $$ A \;=\; 0.0081\,g^{2}, \qquad B \;=\; 0.74\,(g/U_{19.5})^{4} $$ where $U_{19.5}$ is the wind speed measured at 19.5 m above the sea surface. **JONSWAP Spectrum** (fetch-limited North Sea, more peaked): $$ S_{\eta}(\omega) \;=\; \alpha\,g^{2}\,\omega^{-5}\,\exp\!\left[-\,\frac{5}{4}\!\left(\frac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^{4}\right]\,\gamma^{\exp\!\left[-(\omega-\omega_p)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}\omega_p^{2})\right]} $$ with typical range of **peak enhancement factor**: $\gamma = 1 \text{ to } 7$ (mean $\approx 3.3$). **Significant wave height** (the average height of the highest 1/3 of waves, as visually observed): $$ H_s \;=\; 4\sqrt{m_0} \;=\; 4\sqrt{\int_{0}^{\infty} S_{\eta}(\omega)\,d\omega} $$ --- ### 2.4 Motion Sickness Incidence (MSI) A practical index of crew comfort is the **Motion Sickness Incidence (MSI)**, defined as the percentage of people likely to vomit after a 2-hour exposure to a given motion: $$ \text{MSI}(\%) \;=\; \frac{100}{N}\sum_{i} n_i $$ It is computed from the **Motion Sickness Dose Value (MSDV)**: $$ \text{MSDV}_v \;=\; \left[\,\int_{0}^{T}\!\bigl[a_v(t)\bigr]^{2}\,dt\,\right]^{1/2} $$ where $a_v(t)$ is the **vertical acceleration** at the location of interest (e.g., the bridge) low-pass filtered with a frequency-weighting $W(f)$ that emphasizes the 0.1–0.5 Hz band where humans are most sensitive. --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Equation | Meaning / Use | |---|----------|---------------| | 1 | $\bigl[M+A(\omega)\bigr]\ddot{z} + B(\omega)\dot{z} + Cz = F_0(\omega)e^{i\omega t}$ | Equation of motion, frequency domain | | 2 | $\text{RAO}(\omega) = z_a/\zeta_a$ | Transfer function (complex) | | 3 | $z_a(\omega) = \text{RAO}(\omega)\cdot\zeta_a(\omega)$ | Regular wave response amplitude | | 4 | $S_z(\omega) = \bigl\vert\text{RAO}(\omega)\bigr\vert^{2}\,S_{\eta}(\omega)$ | Response spectrum from input spectrum | | 5 | $m_n = \int_0^{\infty}\omega^{n}S_z(\omega)\,d\omega$ | Spectral moments (n = 0, 2, 4) | | 6 | $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ | Significant wave height | | 7 | $S_{PM}(\omega) = A\omega^{-5}\exp(-B\omega^{-4})$ | Pierson–Moskowitz spectrum | | 8 | $T_{zn} = 2\pi\sqrt{(M+A(\omega_n))/C}$ | Natural period in mode $n$ | | 9 | $a_{max} = \omega^{2}\,z_a$ | Peak vertical acceleration | | 10 | $MSDV_v = \sqrt{\int_0^{T}a_v^{2}\,dt}$ | Motion sickness dose value | | 11 | $V_s = \omega - k U\cos\beta$ | Encounter frequency (Doppler) | | 12 | $\lambda_t = \sqrt{\lambda_L}$ | Time scale ratio (Froude) | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** > - **Shipyards** (e.g., Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock) use seakeeping test data to issue *Seakeeping Certificates* to buyers. > - **Offshore industry** (ONGC, Shell, Equinor) mandates seakeeping analyses for FPSO/Drillship operability. > - **Naval architects** use MSI to design crew accommodation layouts and bridge ergonomics. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the RAO from the Equation of Motion We wish to find the steady-state heave response $z(t) = z_a\,e^{i\omega t}$ when forced by a regular wave of amplitude $\zeta_a$. **Every algebraic step is shown explicitly below.** **Step 1 — State the equation of motion in time domain.** $$ \bigl[M + A(\omega)\bigr]\ddot{z}(t) + B(\omega)\dot{z}(t) + C\,z(t) = F_0(\omega)\,e^{i\omega t} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the assumed harmonic solution $z(t) = z_a\,e^{i\omega t}$.** Differentiate twice with respect to time: $$ \dot{z}(t) = i\omega\,z_a\,e^{i\omega t}, \qquad \ddot{z}(t) = -\omega^{2}\,z_a\,e^{i\omega t} $$ **Step 3 — Insert derivatives into the equation of motion.** $$ \bigl[M + A(\omega)\bigr](-\omega^{2}z_a\,e^{i\omega t}) + B(\omega)(i\omega\,z_a\,e^{i\omega t}) + C(z_a\,e^{i\omega t}) = F_0(\omega)\,e^{i\omega t} $$ **Step 4 — Cancel the common exponential $e^{i\omega t}$ from both sides (since $e^{i\omega t} \neq 0$).** $$ -\bigl[M + A(\omega)\bigr]\omega^{2}\,z_a + iB(\omega)\omega\,z_a + C\,z_a = F_0(\omega) $$ **Step 5 — Group $z_a$ on the left-hand side.** $$ z_a \cdot \bigl\{- \bigl[M + A(\omega)\bigr]\omega^{2} + i\,B(\omega)\,\omega + C\bigr\} = F_0(\omega) $$ **Step 6 — Solve for $z_a$.** $$ z_a(\omega) = \frac{F_0(\omega)}{C - \bigl[M + A(\omega)\bigr]\omega^{2} + i\,B(\omega)\,\omega} $$ This is the **complex heave response amplitude** for a regular wave. **Step 7 — Express the exciting force in terms of the wave amplitude $\zeta_a$ using the diffraction / Froude–Krylov force coefficient $f_e(\omega)$.** $$ F_0(\omega) = f_e(\omega)\,\zeta_a $$ **Step 8 — Write the RAO as the ratio $z_a / \zeta_a$.** $$ \boxed{\;\text{RAO}_{heave}(\omega) = \frac{f_e(\omega)}{\bigl\{C - [M+A(\omega)]\omega^{2}\bigr\} + i\,B(\omega)\,\omega}\;} $$ **Step 9 — Identify the magnitude and phase.** $$ \bigl\vert\text{RAO}(\omega)\bigr\vert = \frac{\bigl\vert f_e(\omega)\bigr\vert}{\sqrt{\bigl\{C - [M+A(\omega)]\omega^{2}\bigr\}^{2} + \bigl[B(\omega)\omega\bigr]^{2}}} $$ $$ \varepsilon(\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left[\frac{B(\omega)\omega}{C - [M+A(\omega)]\omega^{2}}\right] $$ > [!NOTE] > **Resonance condition:** When $C - [M+A(\omega)]\omega^{2} = 0$ and $B(\omega) \to 0$, the magnitude of the RAO peaks. This defines the **natural heave frequency** $\omega_n$: > $\omega_n = \sqrt{C/[M+A(\omega_n)]}$. > In practice, $A(\omega)$ itself depends on $\omega$, so the resonance is *implicit* and must be solved iteratively. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Motion Sickness Incidence (MSI) from Acceleration Data **Step 1 — Vertical acceleration of a point on the ship.** If heave motion is $z(t) = z_a\sin(\omega t + \varepsilon)$ then: $$ a_v(t) = \ddot{z}(t) = -\omega^{2}z_a\sin(\omega t + \varepsilon) $$ **Step 2 — Frequency weighting $W(f)$.** The ISO 2631 / McCauley weighting that emphasizes 0.1–0.5 Hz is: $$ W(f) = \begin{cases} 0.5\,f^{0.4}, & f \leq 0.125\,\text{Hz} \\[2pt] 1.0, & 0.125 < f < 0.25\,\text{Hz} \\[2pt] 1.0/f^{1.25}, & f \geq 0.25\,\text{Hz} \end{cases} $$ **Step 3 — Weighted acceleration in frequency domain.** $$ a_{v,w}(f) = W(f)\cdot A_v(f) $$ where $A_v(f)$ is the Fourier amplitude of vertical acceleration derived from the heave RAO: $A_v(f) = (2\pi f)^{2}\cdot\bigl\vert\text{RAO}(f)\bigr\vert\cdot\sqrt{S_\eta(f)}$. **Step 4 — Compute MSDV over exposure time $T$.** $$ \text{MSDV}_v = \left[\,\int_{0}^{T}\bigl[a_{v,w}(t)\bigr]^{2}\,dt\,\right]^{1/2} $$ **Step 5 — Apply Lawther's fit to convert MSDV to MSI.** Empirically fitted relation (Lawther & Griffin): $$ \text{MSI}(\%) = 100\left[1 - \exp(-k\,\text{MSDV}_v^{\,n})\right] $$ with $k \approx 0.0182$ and $n \approx 1.84$ (for a 2-hour exposure, vertical motion, unadapted subjects). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical Example:** A vessel with $z_a = 0.4$ m at heave period $T = 8$ s gives $a_{max} = (2\pi/8)^{2}\times 0.4 \approx 0.247$ m/s² ≈ 0.025 g. This is *below* the 0.05 g threshold typically cited as the limit for "comfortable" cruising on small craft. --- ### 3.3 Spectral Response from Input Spectrum — Step by Step **Step 1 — Start with the input wave spectrum $S_\eta(\omega)$ (measured or chosen, e.g., PM with $H_s = 4$ m).** **Step 2 — Compute heave response spectrum using the linear RAO squared:** $$ S_z(\omega) = \bigl\vert\text{RAO}(\omega)\bigr\vert^{2}\,S_\eta(\omega) $$ **Step 3 — Compute spectral moments $m_0$ and $m_2$:** $$ m_0 = \int_0^{\infty} S_z(\omega)\,d\omega, \qquad m_2 = \int_0^{\infty}\omega^{2}S_z(\omega)\,d\omega $$ **Step 4 — Significant heave amplitude (statistical):** $$ Z_{s} = 4\sqrt{m_0} $$ **Step 5 — Average zero-upcrossing period of heave:** $$ T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2} $$ **Step 6 — Expected maximum heave in $N$ cycles (Rayleigh distribution):** $$ Z_{max,N} = Z_s\,\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\ln N} $$ These six steps are the **complete analytical pipeline** by which a single seakeeping test at one sea state yields the full statistical picture of vessel motion. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Seakeeping Test The following Mermaid flowchart shows the **complete instrument chain and signal flow** of a typical seakeeping model test in an ocean engineering basin. Each block represents either a physical subsystem (wave maker, model, sensor) or a computational operation (DAQ, FFT, RAO extraction). ```mermaid flowchart TD A[PC Control Console
Test Matrix] --> B[Wave Maker
Piston / Flap Type] B --> C[Ocean Engineering Basin
L x B x D = 50m x 30m x 5m] C --> D[Scaled Ship Model
Scale 1:40 Froude] D --> E[Motion Capture
Optical Qualisys] D --> F[Onboard IMU Sensors
Accel + Gyro] D --> G[Load Cells
6-Component] E --> H[DAQ System
NI PXIe] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Signal Conditioning
Anti-Alias Filter] I --> J[Time-Series Storage
Raw .tdms Files] J --> K[FFT and Cross-Spectral
Analysis] K --> L[RAO Extraction
RAO = Xzeta] L --> M[Response Spectrum
Sz = RAO^2 x Seta] M --> N[Statistics Engine
Hs Tz MSI] N --> O[Report Generator
Seakeeping Certificate] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style O fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — RAO Computation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR W[Wave Probe
Measures zeta_a] --> X[Reference Signal] M[Motion Sensor
Measures response z_a] --> Y[Response Signal] X --> F1[FFT Block] Y --> F1 F1 --> R[Ratio Computation
Cross / Auto Spectra] R --> S[Smoothing
Hanning Window] S --> P[Plot RAO vs omega] P --> Q[Validation
Reciprocity Check] style W fill:#fff3cd style M fill:#fff3cd style Q fill:#f8d7da ``` ### 4.3 Test Sequence Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Test Planning] --> A2[Model Preparation
Ballast to WL] A2 --> A3[Instrumentation
Calibration] A3 --> A4[Regular Wave Tests
10 Frequencies x 5 Amplitudes] A4 --> A5[Irregular Wave Tests
JONSWAP Spectra] A5 --> A6[Oblique Wave Tests
Head Seas and Beam Seas] A6 --> A7[Data Reduction
and RAO Plotting] A7 --> A8[Validation against
BEM Theory] style A1 fill:#d1ecf1 style A8 fill:#d4edda ``` ### 4.4 Schematic Layout of an Ocean Engineering Basin (Top View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BASIN[Ocean Engineering Basin] WK[Wave Generator
Multi-element Piston] --- TW[Turning Vanes
and Guide Walls] TW --- TM[Test Section
10m x 10m] TM --- AB[Absorber Beach
Slope 1:20] end CO[Control Room
Behind Glass] -. Wireless Link .-> WK TM --- MD[Model at Centre
Towed Carriage Optional] MD --- WV[Wave Probes
x3 Arrays] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why an absorber beach at the far end?** > If waves reflect back from the basin wall they contaminate the test section by creating a *re-reflection* standing-wave pattern. The 1:20 sloping pebble beach dissipates > 95% of incident wave energy, ensuring the model experiences only the forward-propagating wave it is supposed to. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** *Define Response Amplitude Operator (RAO) in the context of seakeeping. Mention its physical significance.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for expression, 1 for significance):** The **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)** is defined as the **complex ratio of the steady-state amplitude of a vessel's motion response to the amplitude of the incident regular wave**, expressed as a function of the encounter frequency $\omega$. $$ \text{RAO}(\omega) = \frac{z_a(\omega)}{\zeta_a(\omega)} = \bigl\vert\text{RAO}(\omega)\bigr\vert\,e^{-i\varepsilon(\omega)} $$ **Physical significance:** - It is the **transfer function** of the floating system. - Its magnitude tells the designer how much motion is generated per metre of wave. - Its phase tells the relative timing of motion with respect to wave passage. - A low RAO in the frequency band of dominant wave energy means a *comfortable, seakindly* vessel. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any three dominant motions of a ship in head seas and the corresponding sensors used to measure them.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 for each motion + 0.5 for each sensor + 0.5 overall):** | Motion | Physical Meaning | Sensor Used | |--------|-----------------|-------------| | **Heave** | Vertical translation ($z$) | Linear potentiometer / LVDT / Accelerometer (double-integrated) | | **Pitch** | Rotation about transverse axis ($\theta$) | Gyroscope / Inclinometer / Optical motion-capture marker | | **Roll** | Rotation about longitudinal axis ($\phi$) | Gyroscope / Clinometer / Pendulum-type roll-pickup | For model tests in a basin, **optical motion-capture systems (Qualisys, Vicon)** and **inertial measurement units (IMUs)** are the most common modern choices. (3 marks) --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Choice-1]* **(a)** Derive the equation of motion of a floating body in heave in the frequency domain. Clearly explain each term. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 100 m long container ship (scale 1:50) is to be tested in a towing basin. Compute **(i)** the model length, **(ii)** the model service speed (full-scale speed = 20 knots), and **(iii)** the wave period to be generated if the full-scale design wave has $T = 10$ s. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Newton's Second Law in heave direction.** For a floating body performing vertical heave $z(t)$: $$ \sum F_z = M\ddot{z} $$ **Step 2 — Identify forces acting on the body in waves.** Four force components act: 1. **Hydrostatic restoring force** (gravity minus buoyancy excursion): $F_R = -C\,z$, where $C = \rho g A_{WP}$ (waterplane area). 2. **Radiation damping force** (energy radiated as waves from oscillating body): $F_D = -B(\omega)\dot{z}$. 3. **Added-mass force** (inertia of entrained water): $F_A = -A(\omega)\ddot{z}$. 4. **Exciting wave force** (Froude–Krylov + diffraction): $F_E = F_0(\omega)\,e^{i\omega t}$. **Step 3 — Assemble Newton's law.** $$ M\ddot{z} = -C z - B(\omega)\dot{z} - A(\omega)\ddot{z} + F_0(\omega)e^{i\omega t} $$ **Step 4 — Collect inertia terms on the left.** $$ \bigl[M + A(\omega)\bigr]\ddot{z} + B(\omega)\dot{z} + C\,z = F_0(\omega)e^{i\omega t} \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$ **Step 5 — Discuss each term (the "explain each term" part).** | Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning | |------|--------|------------------| | $M$ | kg | Ship mass | | $A(\omega)$ | kg | Added mass (virtual mass of co-moving water) | | $B(\omega)$ | kg/s | Radiation damping coefficient | | $C$ | N/m | Hydrostatic restoring stiffness | | $F_0(\omega)$ | N | Exciting wave force amplitude | **Step 6 — Solve for harmonic response.** Assuming $z(t) = z_a\,e^{i\omega t}$ and substituting: $$ z_a = \frac{F_0(\omega)}{C - [M+A(\omega)]\omega^{2} + iB(\omega)\omega} $$ and hence the RAO: $$ \text{RAO}(\omega) = \frac{z_a}{\zeta_a} = \frac{f_e(\omega)}{C - [M+A(\omega)]\omega^{2} + iB(\omega)\omega} $$ **Distribution of marks for (a):** Equation setup (2), term identification (2), harmonic substitution (2), RAO expression (1) = **7 marks**. --- **(b) Scaling calculations (7 marks):** **(i) Model length:** $$ L_m = \frac{L_s}{\lambda} = \frac{100}{50} = 2.0 \text{ m} $$ (1 mark) **(ii) Model service speed (Froude scaling):** Froude number equality: $Fr_s = Fr_m$ → $V_s/\sqrt{gL_s} = V_m/\sqrt{gL_m}$ → $V_m = V_s\sqrt{L_m/L_s} = V_s/\sqrt{\lambda}$. Full-scale speed conversion to m/s: $V_s = 20\text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 10.288$ m/s. $$ V_m = \frac{10.288}{\sqrt{50}} = \frac{10.288}{7.071} = 1.455 \text{ m/s} $$ (2 marks) **(iii) Model wave period:** Time scale: $\lambda_T = \sqrt{\lambda_L} = \sqrt{50}$. $$ T_m = \frac{T_s}{\sqrt{50}} = \frac{10}{7.071} = 1.414 \text{ s} $$ (2 marks) **Verification:** Equivalent encounter frequency check. $$ \omega_m = \frac{2\pi}{1.414} = 4.44 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{10} = 0.628 \text{ rad/s} $$ Ratio $\omega_s/\omega_m = 0.628/4.44 = 0.141 = 1/\sqrt{50}$ ✓ (1 mark) **Bonus (1 mark):** Note that wavelength must also be geometrically scaled: $\lambda_w^L = \lambda_L$ implies $L_{w,m} = L_{w,s}/50$. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Choice-2]* **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the procedure for conducting a regular wave seakeeping test in a model basin. List the instruments used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A ship model heave RAO is given as $\bigl\vert\text{RAO}(\omega)\bigr\vert = 0.45$ at $\omega = 1.2$ rad/s. The ship operates in irregular beam seas described by a Pierson–Moskowitz spectrum with significant wave height $H_s = 3$ m and peak frequency $\omega_p = 0.8$ rad/s. Calculate **(i)** the significant heave amplitude, **(ii)** the RMS heave amplitude, and **(iii)** the peak vertical acceleration. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Procedure (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Test preparation.** - Select Froude scale $\lambda$ (e.g., 1:40 or 1:50). - Manufacture the model to ITTC-Recommended Procedures geometry including all appendages (rudder, propeller, bilge keels). - Ballast the model to the correct displacement and waterline (LCG, VCG, GM measured and matched). **Step 2 — Instrumentation.** | Channel | Instrument | Purpose | |---------|-----------|---------| | Wave height | Resistance-type wave probe | Reference $\zeta_a(t)$ | | Heave | LVDT / Optical marker | $z(t)$ | | Pitch | IMU gyroscope or optical | $\theta(t)$ | | Roll | IMU gyroscope | $\phi(t)$ | | Surge, Sway | Draw-wire potentiometer | $x(t), y(t)$ | | Global forces | 6-component load cell (towed) | Optional | (2 marks) **Step 3 — Test matrix.** - Choose 8–15 encounter frequencies spanning $0.2 < \omega < 3.0$ rad/s. - For each frequency, run 2–3 wave amplitudes to confirm linearity. - Repeat for **head seas, beam seas, quartering seas, following seas**. (1 mark) **Step 4 — Acquisition & processing.** - Run each test for ≥ 30 s model time (≥ 5–10 wave periods). - Acquire motion and wave signals at ≥ 50 Hz. - Compute $\text{RAO}(\omega) = G_{z\zeta}(\omega)/G_{\zeta\zeta}(\omega)$ where $G$ denotes cross/auto-spectral densities. (2 marks) **Step 5 — Reporting.** Plot RAO magnitude and phase as functions of $\omega$, validate against potential-flow (BEM) theory, and certify. (1 mark) **Block Diagram (1 mark):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Wave Maker] --> B[Wave Probe] A --> C[Ship Model] C --> D[Motion Sensors] B --> E[DAQ] D --> E E --> F[FFT Analysis] F --> G[RAO Output] ``` --- **(b) Spectral calculations (7 marks):** **(i) Significant heave amplitude.** The Pierson–Moskowitz spectrum: $$ S_\eta(\omega) = \frac{0.0081\,g^{2}}{\omega^{5}}\,\exp\!\left[-\frac{5}{4}\!\left(\frac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^{4}\right] $$ $m_0 = (H_s/4)^{2} = (3/4)^{2} = 0.5625 \text{ m}^{2}\text{s/rad}$. The response spectrum: $S_z(\omega) = \bigl\vert\text{RAO}(\omega)\bigr\vert^{2}S_\eta(\omega)$. At $\omega = 1.2$ rad/s (narrowband assumption for simplicity): $$ m_0^z = \int_0^{\infty}\bigl\vert\text{RAO}(\omega)\bigr\vert^{2}S_\eta(\omega)\,d\omega \approx \overline{(\text{RAO}^{2})}\cdot m_0 $$ With $\bigl\vert\text{RAO}\bigr\vert^{2} = 0.45^{2} = 0.2025$: $$ m_0^z = 0.2025 \times 0.5625 = 0.1139 \text{ m}^{2}\text{s/rad} $$ Significant heave: $$ Z_s = 4\sqrt{m_0^z} = 4\sqrt{0.1139} = 4 \times 0.3375 = 1.35 \text{ m} $$ (3 marks) **(ii) RMS heave amplitude:** $$ z_{rms} = \sqrt{m_0^z} = \sqrt{0.1139} = 0.337 \text{ m} = 2 \times Z_s/4 \approx Z_s/4 $$ (1 mark) **(iii) Peak vertical acceleration:** Using the dominant encounter frequency $\omega_p = 0.8$ rad/s and corresponding heave amplitude $Z_{1/3} = Z_s/2$: $$ a_{peak} = \omega^{2}\,z_a = (0.8)^{2}\,(1.35/2) = 0.64 \times 0.675 = 0.432 \text{ m/s}^{2} $$ In g units: $0.432/9.81 = 0.044\,g$. (3 marks) **Conclusion:** $0.044\,g < 0.05\,g$ → the vessel is on the borderline of comfort for sustained operations. (1 mark bonus) --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks (KTU Valuation Pattern):** > 1. **Forgetting units** in Froude scaling answers — both m and knots must be quoted. > 2. **Confusing added mass $A(\omega)$ with displaced water mass** — $A(\omega)$ is frequency-dependent. > 3. **Missing the factor of 4** in $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ (or $H_{1/3} = 4\sigma$). > 4. **Treating RAO as a real number** — it is complex; phase carries equally important information. > 5. **Using $\omega$ instead of $\omega_e$** (encounter frequency) when ship is underway — apply the Doppler correction $V_s = \omega - kU\cos\beta$. > 6. **Ignoring Froude scaling of time** when converting test results back to full-scale. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist - **Seakeeping** = study of ship behavior in waves; tests are usually **scaled model experiments** in **wave/ocean engineering basins**. - **6-DOF** motions: **surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw** — of which **heave, pitch, roll** dominate in head/beam seas. - **Equation of motion (frequency domain):** $\bigl[M+A(\omega)\bigr]\ddot{z} + B(\omega)\dot{z} + Cz = F_0(\omega)e^{i\omega t}$. - **RAO** is the complex transfer function $z_a/\zeta_a$; magnitude and phase both essential. - **Froude scaling** governs: $\lambda_T = \sqrt{\lambda_L}$, $\lambda_V = \sqrt{\lambda_L}$. - **Regular waves** → single $\omega$; **irregular waves** → wave spectrum $S_\eta(\omega)$. - **PM spectrum** for fully developed sea; **JONSWAP** for fetch-limited sea (extra peak factor $\gamma$). - **Significant response** $Z_s = 4\sqrt{m_0^z}$. - **MSI** quantifies human discomfort; uses ISO 2631 weighting $W(f)$ peaking at 0.1–0.5 Hz. - **Encounter frequency** $V_s = \omega - kU\cos\beta$ accounts for ship forward motion. - **Peak acceleration** $a = \omega^{2}z_a$; design criterion typically $a < 0.05\,g$ for bridge comfort. - **Test procedure:** ballasting → calibration → regular waves (10 freq × 3 amps) → irregular waves → post-processing via FFT/cross-spectrum → RAO extraction → validation against BEM. - **Key instruments:** wave probe, IMU, optical motion-capture, 6-component load cell, NI-PXIe DAQ. - **Reporting outputs:** RAO plots, motion spectra, operability contours, MSI maps.

topics.title: Tests in Seakeeping and Maneuvering Basin
